the best approach for knowledge bases with multiple long documents. The
following illustration shows both dimensions of context side by side.
--- PAGE 112 ---
A chunk shown with a header band on top providing document-level context (vertical) and neighboring
chunks on either side providing local context (horizontal), forming a cross-shaped enrichment pattern
When to Use This
Best for:
Long documents where ideas span multiple paragraphs (research papers,
legal documents, technical manuals)
Chunk sizes that are small for embedding precision (under 500
characters) where single chunks often cut mid-thought
Systems where answer completeness matters as much as relevance
Overkill when:
Chunks are already large enough to contain full ideas
Documents consist of short, self-contained entries (FAQ pairs, product
listings)
--- PAGE 113 ---
You are already using proposition chunking (which produces self-
contained units by design)
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency Low (neighbor lookup is fast, no LLM call needed)
Complexity Low (simple index-based retrieval with concatenation)
Cost Negligible (no API calls, just local data fetching)
Accuracy gain Moderate to high for long-form documents
Compared to proposition chunking:
Proposition chunking (Chapter 4) solves the incomplete-thought problem
from the other direction: it creates self-contained chunks that never need
expansion because each one is already a complete idea. The context
enrichment window keeps standard chunking but fixes its output at retrieval
time. Proposition chunking is more thorough but requires an LLM call per
chunk during preparation. The context window requires zero LLM calls,
making it the lighter-weight option when preparation cost matters. The
following illustration contrasts the two approaches side by side.
--- PAGE 114 ---
A split comparison showing the context enrichment window approach on one side (small chunks
expanded at retrieval time) versus proposition chunking on the other side (self-contained chunks that
need no expansion)
Key Takeaways
Chunk boundaries are placed by character counts, not by meaning. This
often cuts through the middle of ideas.
The context enrichment window separates the search unit from the
reading unit: small chunks for precise matching, expanded passages for
complete answers.
After retrieving a chunk, grab its neighbors by index and concatenate
them, trimming any overlap to avoid duplication.
Window size is configurable. One to two neighbors per side covers most
use cases.
--- PAGE 115 ---
This technique complements contextual chunk headers. Headers add
document-level context (vertical), while the window adds surrounding-
text context (horizontal).
No LLM calls are needed. The enrichment is a simple index lookup,
making it one of the lowest-cost improvements available.
--- PAGE 116 ---
Chapter 9: Semantic Chunking: Splitting Text
Where the Meaning Shifts
Letting the content itself decide where to split, by measuring the
semantic similarity between consecutive sentences and cutting where
the topic changes.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (chunking, embeddings, vector search). Familiarity
with the chunking limitations discussed in chapters 4 and 8.
The Problem
Every chunking strategy we have seen so far uses external rules to decide
where to split a document. Chapter 1 split text every thousand characters.
Chapter 4 used a language model to decompose chunks into propositions.
Chapter 8 accepted fixed boundaries but expanded the view at retrieval time.
All of these approaches treat the document’s own structure as invisible. The
character counter does not know that sentence 14 is about ocean chemistry
while sentence 15 shifts to deforestation policy. It splits wherever the count
lands.
Consider a document about climate change that flows through several
subtopics. One section discusses the causes of rising temperatures. The next
section, starting mid-paragraph, shifts to the economic consequences. A
fixed-size chunker might place a boundary right in the middle of the causes
discussion, grouping the last two sentences about fossil fuels with the first
three sentences about GDP impacts. The resulting chunk is a semantic blend:
half about causes, half about economics. Its embedding lands somewhere
between the two topics, precisely matching neither. A query about causes
retrieves it weakly. A query about economics retrieves it weakly. A query
about either topic alone would be better served by a chunk that covers just
that topic.
--- PAGE 117 ---
The following illustration shows this: a document with clear topic shifts
getting sliced at fixed intervals that ignore those shifts, producing chunks that
blend unrelated ideas.
A document with colored sections representing distinct topics is sliced at regular fixed intervals, with cut
lines falling in the middle of topics, producing chunks that mix colors and blend unrelated content
The root cause is that fixed-size chunking is blind to meaning. It sees
characters, not ideas. What if the chunking process could sense where one
idea ends and another begins?
The Core Idea
Semantic chunking replaces the character counter with a meaning sensor.
Instead of splitting every N characters, it examines how similar consecutive
sentences are to each other. When two neighboring sentences are about the
same topic, their embeddings are close together and the system keeps them
--- PAGE 118 ---
in the same chunk. When the topic shifts, the embeddings diverge, and the
system places a boundary there.
Think of it like a film editor reviewing raw footage. An amateur editor cuts
every three minutes regardless of what is happening on screen, sometimes
mid-dialogue, sometimes mid-action. A skilled editor watches the footage and
cuts at natural scene transitions: when the location changes, when a new
character starts speaking, when the mood shifts. The scenes that result are
coherent units that make sense on their own. Semantic chunking applies the
same principle to text. It watches the meaning flow between sentences and
cuts when the flow breaks.
The result is chunks that vary in length but are internally coherent. A
dense technical section might produce a short chunk of five sentences, all
tightly related. A flowing narrative section might produce a longer chunk of
fifteen sentences that all develop the same theme. The chunk boundaries are
no longer arbitrary. They reflect the natural structure of the text. The
following diagram illustrates this: consecutive sentences with high similarity
grouped together, and boundaries placed where similarity drops.
--- PAGE 119 ---
A sequence of sentence blocks connected by similarity links, with strong links shown as thick lines and
weak links shown as thin broken lines. Scissors icons appear at the weak links, showing where the text
naturally splits into coherent chunks
The core insight: instead of imposing boundaries from the outside, let
the text reveal where its own boundaries are. Sentences that belong
together stay together. Sentences that shift to a new topic start a new
chunk.
How It Works
Semantic chunking modifies only the splitting step of the preparation phase.
Everything else, embedding, storing, searching, and answering, stays the
same as in chapter 1.
Step 1: Split Into Sentences
--- PAGE 120 ---
The process starts by breaking the document into individual sentences. This
is a much finer split than character-based chunking. Each sentence becomes a
candidate boundary point. The goal is not to keep sentences as individual
chunks (that would be too granular), but to evaluate the connection between
every pair of neighbors and decide which ones belong in the same chunk.
Step 2: Embed Each Sentence
Every sentence is converted into an embedding vector. This is the same
embedding process from chapter 1, but applied at the sentence level rather
than the chunk level. The result is a sequence of vectors, one per sentence,
each capturing that sentence’s meaning.
This step is the most computationally expensive part of semantic
chunking. A document with 500 sentences requires 500 embedding calls. For
comparison, the same document split into 25 chunks of 1,000 characters
requires only 25 embedding calls. This cost is paid once during preparation,
not at query time.
Step 3: Measure Distances Between Consecutive Sentences
For each pair of adjacent sentences, the system computes the distance
between their embeddings. If sentence 7 and sentence 8 are about the same
subtopic, their embeddings will be close and the distance will be small. If
sentence 8 shifts to a new topic, the distance will spike.
The system computes these distances for every consecutive pair across the
entire document, producing a sequence of distance values. The following
diagram visualizes this as a line chart: the x-axis is position in the document,
the y-axis is the distance between consecutive sentence pairs. Valleys
represent stretches of consistent topic, and peaks represent topic transitions.
--- PAGE 121 ---
A line chart showing the distance between consecutive sentence embeddings across a document. The x-
axis represents position and the y-axis represents distance. Most values are low, but several sharp peaks
rise above a horizontal dashed threshold line, with scissors icons at the peaks indicating split points
Step 4: Identify Breakpoints
The distance sequence reveals where the meaning shifts. The question is:
how large must a distance be to count as a genuine topic change rather than
normal sentence-to-sentence variation?
Semantic chunking offers three methods for answering this question, each
using a different statistical approach to define “unusually large.”
Percentile method. Compute all pairwise distances, then set the
threshold at the Nth percentile. For example, with a threshold at the 90th
percentile, only distances in the top 10% of all distances are treated as
breakpoints. This method is simple and works well for most documents. A
higher percentile (95th) produces fewer, larger chunks. A lower percentile
(80th) produces more, smaller chunks.
Standard deviation method. Compute the mean and standard
deviation of all distances. Set the threshold at the mean plus N standard
deviations. A distance that exceeds this threshold is considered an outlier, a
--- PAGE 122 ---
topic shift rather than normal variation. Typical values of N range from 1 to 3.
This method is sensitive to the overall distribution of distances and works
well when topic shifts are genuinely distinct from the baseline.
Interquartile range method. Compute the first and third quartiles of
the distance distribution. The interquartile range (IQR) is the difference
between them. Set the threshold at the third quartile plus 1.5 times the IQR.
This is the same logic used to identify outliers in a box plot, and it is robust to
extreme values. It works well when a few very large distances might skew the
mean and standard deviation.
All three methods answer the same question: “Which distances are
unusually large?” They differ in how they define “unusual.” For most use
cases, the percentile method at a threshold around 90 provides a reliable
starting point.
Algorithm Sketch: Semantic Chunking
1. Split document into individual sentences
2. Embed each sentence into a vector
3. Compute distance between each consecutive pair of sentence vectors
4. Choose a breakpoint method (percentile, standard deviation, or IQR)
5. Set a threshold based on the chosen method
6. Split the text wherever the distance exceeds the threshold
7. Each group of sentences between breakpoints becomes one chunk
8. Embed and store chunks in the vector store
Step 5: Group Sentences Into Chunks
Every sentence between two breakpoints is grouped into a single chunk. The
first group runs from the start of the document to the first breakpoint. The
second group runs from the first breakpoint to the second. And so on. Each
chunk is a semantically coherent unit: a stretch of text where the topic stayed
consistent.
These chunks are then embedded as whole units and stored in the vector
store, just as in chapter 1. The difference is that each chunk now represents a
naturally bounded topic rather than an arbitrary slice of text. The following
diagram shows the complete pipeline from document to semantically
chunked vector store.
--- PAGE 123 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram showing five stages. Stage 1: a document icon. Stage 2: the document
split into many small sentence blocks. Stage 3: arrows showing distances between pairs of adjacent
sentences with a threshold line. Stage 4: sentences grouped into variable-sized chunks with distinct
colors. Stage 5: a vector store with colored dots matching the chunks
Semantic Chunks vs. Fixed Chunks
The practical difference shows up immediately when you compare retrieval
results. Consider a document that discusses three climate change subtopics in
sequence: causes, effects on biodiversity, and economic impacts. Fixed-size
chunking produces chunks that straddle these boundaries, blending content
from two subtopics into single chunks. Semantic chunking produces chunks
that align with the boundaries, keeping each subtopic in its own chunk.
When a user asks about economic impacts, the semantic chunk covering
that subtopic is a clean match. Its embedding precisely captures “economic
impacts of climate change” because that is all it contains. The fixed chunk
that blends economic impacts with biodiversity effects produces a weaker
match for either topic.
The tradeoff is chunk size variability. Fixed chunks are predictable: each is
roughly the same length, which means the context passed to the language
--- PAGE 124 ---
model is consistent. Semantic chunks vary in length. A dense section might
produce a chunk of 200 characters, while a flowing narrative section might
produce one of 3,000 characters. This variability can affect how many chunks
fit in the model’s context window during generation. The following
illustration contrasts the two approaches side by side on the same document.
A split-screen comparison. The left side labeled “Fixed” shows a document sliced into equal-sized chunks
with cut lines falling in the middle of colored topic regions. The right side labeled “Semantic” shows the
same document with variable-sized chunks whose boundaries align with topic changes, each chunk a
single solid color
Where Semantic Chunking Sits Among Chunking Strategies
This book has now covered four approaches to the chunking problem, each
attacking it from a different angle.
Fixed-size chunking (chapter 1) is the simplest. It splits by character
count with overlap to soften boundary effects. It requires no embedding calls
during splitting and works as a reliable baseline.
Proposition chunking (chapter 4) goes to the other extreme. It uses a
language model to decompose text into atomic facts. Each fact is a self-
--- PAGE 125 ---
contained chunk. This gives maximum retrieval precision but loses narrative
context and requires an LLM call per chunk during preparation.
Context enrichment window (chapter 8) does not change the chunking
at all. It keeps fixed chunks but expands the context at retrieval time by
grabbing neighbors. This is the least invasive option, adding no cost during
preparation.
Semantic chunking (this chapter) finds a middle ground. It uses
embedding similarity to place boundaries at natural topic shifts. Chunks vary
in size but are internally coherent. The preparation cost falls between fixed
chunking (cheapest) and proposition chunking (most expensive), since it
requires one embedding per sentence but no LLM calls.
These approaches are not mutually exclusive. You can apply semantic
chunking and then add contextual chunk headers from chapter 7. Or use
semantic chunking during preparation and still apply the context enrichment
window at retrieval time for passages that were split at a subtler shift the
method missed. The following illustration places all four strategies on a
spectrum from simplest to most precise, showing where semantic chunking
fits relative to the alternatives covered so far.
--- PAGE 126 ---
A horizontal arrow pointing from ‘Simple’ on the left to ‘Precise’ on the right, with four icons placed
along the spectrum. From left to right: Fixed (a square block icon), Window (an expandable block icon),
Semantic (a signal/wave icon), and Propositions (a card icon). The arrow transitions from light gray on
the left to warm orange on the right, showing increasing precision.
When to Use This
Best for:
Long documents with clear topic transitions (reports, papers, textbooks,
multi-section articles)
Knowledge bases where fixed-size chunks consistently blend unrelated
topics
Situations where you want better-than-fixed chunking without the LLM
cost of proposition decomposition
Overkill when:
Documents are short or cover a single focused topic throughout
--- PAGE 127 ---
Documents already have clear structural markers (headings, sections)
that a simpler rule-based splitter can use
Your chunking already works well and retrieval quality is not the
bottleneck
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Preparation cost Moderate: one embedding per sentence (more
calls than fixed chunking, no LLM calls)
Query latency Same as standard RAG: no additional work at
query time
Chunk quality High: chunks are internally coherent,
representing single topics
Chunk size Variable: ranges from very short to very long
depending on content
Compared to fixed-size chunking from chapter 1:
Fixed chunking is cheaper to prepare (one embedding per chunk, not per
sentence) and produces predictable chunk sizes. Semantic chunking produces
higher-quality chunks at the cost of more embedding calls during
preparation. For documents with clear multi-topic structure, the investment
pays off through better retrieval precision. For documents that are already
topically consistent throughout, fixed chunking performs nearly as well at
lower cost.
Compared to proposition chunking from chapter 4:
Proposition chunking gives finer granularity (individual facts) and
maximum retrieval precision, but requires expensive LLM calls during
preparation. Semantic chunking provides topic-level granularity using only
embedding calls, making it significantly cheaper. Propositions excel at
pinpoint factual questions. Semantic chunks excel at topical questions where
the answer spans several related sentences. Choose based on the question
patterns your users have.
Key Takeaways
--- PAGE 128 ---
Semantic chunking places split points where the meaning changes, not
where a character counter reaches a limit.
The process works by embedding every sentence, measuring the distance
between consecutive sentence pairs, and splitting where the distance
exceeds a statistical threshold.
Three threshold methods are available: percentile (simplest, reliable
default), standard deviation (sensitive to distribution), and interquartile
range (robust to outliers).
Semantic chunks vary in length but are internally coherent, each
covering a single topic or subtopic.
The preparation cost is moderate: one embedding per sentence, with no
language model calls required for the splitting itself.
Semantic chunking occupies a practical middle ground between cheap
fixed-size chunking and expensive proposition chunking, offering topic-
level coherence without LLM-based decomposition.
--- PAGE 129 ---
Chapter 10: Contextual Compression: Extracting
Only What Matters
After retrieval, an LLM reads each chunk in light of the query and
extracts only the sentences that actually answer the question,
discarding the rest.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (the basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 3 (relevancy
grading as a post-retrieval filter).
The Problem
By this point in the book, the retrieval side of the pipeline has several tools
for finding the right chunks. Better chunking strategies place boundaries at
natural topic shifts. Query transformations rephrase the question to improve
the match. But even when the system retrieves exactly the right chunk, that
chunk is rarely a perfect answer to the question.
Consider a user who asks: “What role does the Paris Agreement play in
limiting global warming?” The retriever does its job and returns a chunk from
a climate policy document. But that chunk is a full paragraph covering the
United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, the history of the
Kyoto Protocol, the Paris Agreement’s temperature targets, and a mention of
nationally determined contributions. Only two sentences in the middle
directly address the user’s question. The rest is context that the chunk
happened to include because it was stored as one unit.
The language model receiving this chunk must now sift through the entire
passage to find the relevant sentences. With one chunk, this is manageable.
But a typical retrieval returns three to five chunks, each carrying its own
freight of tangential information. The model’s context window fills with
material that is topically adjacent but not directly useful. The result is an
answer that may be correct but is diluted: the model weaves in details from
--- PAGE 130 ---
the surrounding sentences because they were present in the context, even
though they do not address the question.
The following illustration shows this: a retrieved chunk where only a small
portion is relevant to the query, while the rest is noise that the model must
wade through.
A retrieved document chunk represented as a tall rectangle filled with horizontal lines of text. A few
lines in the middle are highlighted in warm orange, representing the portion relevant to the query. The
rest of the lines are in light gray, representing irrelevant content. A magnifying glass icon focuses on
the orange lines. A query bubble sits to the left.
The issue is not that the wrong chunk was retrieved. The chunk is relevant at
the topic level. The issue is that relevance is not binary. Within a single
chunk, some sentences are precisely on target and others are merely nearby.
The Core Idea
Contextual compression adds a refinement step between retrieval and
generation. After the base retriever returns its chunks, a language model
--- PAGE 131 ---
reads each chunk alongside the original query and extracts only the portions
that directly address the question. Everything else is discarded. The
generation model then receives a set of compressed excerpts instead of full
chunks.
Think of it like a gold panner working a riverbed. The retriever is the
shovel that scoops up promising sediment from the right part of the river. But
the pan still contains sand, gravel, and silt mixed in with the gold flakes. The
compression step is the panning itself: swirling the material so that the heavy
gold settles to the bottom and the lighter debris washes away. What remains
is concentrated value. The generation model never sees the gravel. The
following illustration shows this three-stage progression from raw sediment
to pure gold.
A three-stage gold panning analogy: a shovel scoops mixed material from a riverbed (retrieval), a pan
swirls to separate gold flakes from debris (compression), and a neat pile of gold dots remains with a
lightbulb glowing above (generation)
The key insight is that relevance depends on the question. The same chunk
might yield different compressed outputs for different queries. A paragraph
about the Paris Agreement might compress to the temperature targets for one
--- PAGE 132 ---
query and to the nationally determined contributions for another. The
compression is contextual because it considers both the chunk and the query
together. The following diagram shows the core concept: full chunks entering
a compression funnel, with only the relevant sentences emerging on the
other side.
A horizontal flow diagram. On the left, three tall rectangles represent retrieved chunks, each with a mix
of orange-highlighted and gray lines. In the center, a funnel icon with a small brain symbol inside it
represents the LLM compressor. On the right, three small rectangles emerge, containing only orange-
highlighted lines. An arrow labeled “Query” points into the funnel from above. The label “Full” appears
under the left rectangles and “Focused” under the right ones.
The core insight: retrieval finds the right chunks, but compression finds
the right sentences within those chunks. Together, they narrow the
focus from document level to sentence level.
How It Works
--- PAGE 133 ---
Contextual compression modifies only the post-retrieval step of the pipeline.
The preparation phase (chunking, embedding, storing) remains unchanged.
The query embedding and similarity search remain unchanged. The
compression layer sits between retrieval and generation, processing each
chunk before it reaches the answer model.
Step 1: Retrieve Chunks Normally
The process begins with a standard retrieval step. The user’s query is
embedded, the vector store returns the top-K most similar chunks, and these
chunks form the initial candidate set. This is the same base retriever from
chapter 1, using whichever chunking strategy the system has in place.
At this point, the chunks are complete passages, exactly as they were
stored during preparation. They are topically relevant but not surgically
precise.
Step 2: Compress Each Chunk Against the Query
Each retrieved chunk is sent to a language model along with the original
query. The model’s task is extraction, not summarization. It reads the chunk,
identifies the specific sentences or phrases that directly address the query,
and returns only those portions. If nothing in the chunk is relevant, the
model returns nothing, and that chunk is effectively filtered out.
This is a fundamentally different operation from the relevancy grading in
chapter 3. Relevancy grading makes a binary decision about each whole
chunk: keep it or discard it. Compression operates at a finer granularity. It
reaches inside the chunk and pulls out only the useful parts, like a surgeon
removing a specific tissue sample rather than deciding whether to keep or
discard the entire organ.
The following illustration shows this step in detail: the LLM receiving a
chunk and a query, then producing a compressed excerpt containing only the
relevant portions.
--- PAGE 134 ---
A close-up diagram of the compression step. On the left, a tall rectangle labeled “Chunk” with mixed
orange and gray horizontal lines. Below it, a rounded rectangle labeled “Query” with a question mark
icon. Both feed into a central rounded square with a brain icon, representing the LLM compressor. On
the right, a shorter rectangle emerges containing only the orange lines from the original chunk. A small
label “Extract” appears below the brain icon.
Step 3: Pass Compressed Results to Generation
The compressed excerpts from all chunks are concatenated and passed to the
generation model as context. Because the irrelevant material has been
stripped away, the context is denser with useful information. The generation
model can focus entirely on answering the question without navigating
around tangential details.
This step is identical to the generation step in chapter 1, with one
difference: the context is shorter and more focused. The generation model
processes less text to produce a better answer.
--- PAGE 135 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Contextual Compression
1. Receive user query
2. Embed query and retrieve top-K chunks from vector store
3. For each retrieved chunk:
   a. Send chunk + query to compressor LLM
   b. LLM extracts only the sentences relevant to the query
   c. If nothing relevant, discard the chunk entirely
4. Concatenate all compressed excerpts
5. Pass compressed context + query to generation LLM
6. Return the answer
The Full Pipeline
The complete pipeline now has an additional stage between retrieval and
generation. The following diagram shows all stages in sequence: the query
triggers retrieval, the retrieved chunks pass through the compression layer,
and only the compressed excerpts reach the generation model.
--- PAGE 136 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram with five stages connected by arrows flowing left to right. Stage 1: a
search icon with the label “Query”. Stage 2: a cylinder database icon with three rectangles emerging
from it, labeled “Retrieve”. Stage 3: a funnel with a brain icon inside, with three shorter rectangles
emerging, labeled “Compress”. Stage 4: an icon of stacked focused excerpts, labeled “Context”. Stage 5: a
lightbulb icon producing text lines, labeled “Answer”. Clean sans-serif labels below each stage.
What Compression Removes
The compressor is effective against several types of noise that commonly
appear in retrieved chunks.
Tangential context. A chunk about the Paris Agreement might include
background on the UNFCCC treaty framework. If the query asks specifically
about temperature targets, the compressor strips the treaty background and
keeps only the target-related sentences.
Transitional prose. Chunks often begin or end with transitional
sentences that connect to the surrounding text: “As discussed in the previous
section…” or “Building on this foundation, the next area to consider is…”
These sentences carry no informational value for the query and are removed.
Duplicate information. When multiple retrieved chunks cover
overlapping content (common with chunk overlap), the compressor naturally
--- PAGE 137 ---
deduplicates by extracting only the distinct relevant portions from each
chunk. Two chunks that share three sentences about temperature targets
might each contribute only the sentences unique to their perspective.
Compression vs. Relevancy Grading
Chapter 3 introduced relevancy grading as a way to filter out noisy chunks.
That technique works at the chunk level: a chunk is either relevant enough to
keep or not. Contextual compression works at the sentence level within each
chunk.
These two approaches address different problems. Relevancy grading
catches chunks that should never have been retrieved at all, for example a
chunk about page formatting that matched by coincidence. Compression
catches chunks that are topically on target but contain a mix of relevant and
irrelevant sentences. The following illustration contrasts the two approaches
side by side.
--- PAGE 138 ---
A split-screen comparison. The left side labeled “Grading” shows three full chunks: one has a green
checkmark (kept whole), one has a red X (discarded whole), one has a green checkmark (kept whole).
The right side labeled “Compression” shows three chunks being processed through small funnels, each
producing a shorter excerpt. One chunk produces a medium excerpt, one produces a tiny excerpt, and
one is fully discarded with nothing remaining. The key difference is visible: grading keeps or discards
whole chunks while compression extracts portions.
In practice, the two techniques can be combined. First, grade each chunk to
filter out the completely irrelevant ones. Then, compress the remaining
chunks to extract only the useful portions. This two-stage approach
minimizes both the number of chunks and the noise within each chunk.
When to Use This
Best for:
Large chunks that cover multiple subtopics within a single passage
Queries that target specific details buried inside broader discussions
--- PAGE 139 ---
Systems where the generation model’s context window is limited and
every token counts
Overkill when:
Chunks are already small and focused (e.g., proposition-chunked as in
chapter 4)
The generation model has a large context window and handles noise well
Latency is the primary concern (compression adds an LLM call per chunk
at query time)
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Query latency High: one LLM call per retrieved chunk at
query time
Preparation cost None: compression happens at query time, not
during indexing
Answer quality Significant improvement when chunks contain
mixed-relevance content
Cost Moderate to high: LLM inference cost scales
with number of retrieved chunks
Compared to relevancy grading from chapter 3:
Relevancy grading is a binary filter: keep or discard the whole chunk. It is
cheaper (a single classification per chunk) and faster, but it cannot salvage
the useful sentences from an otherwise noisy chunk. Compression is more
expensive but more precise, extracting value at the sentence level. For
systems where chunks are well-constructed and topically focused, grading
alone may suffice. For systems with large, multi-topic chunks, compression
provides a meaningful quality improvement.
Compared to proposition chunking from chapter 4:
Proposition chunking solves the mixed-content problem at preparation
time by decomposing text into atomic facts. Each fact is already focused, so
compression at query time would have little to extract. The tradeoff is that
proposition chunking requires LLM calls during preparation (once per
document), while contextual compression requires LLM calls during retrieval
(once per query per chunk). If your documents change rarely but queries are
--- PAGE 140 ---
frequent, proposition chunking front-loads the cost. If your documents
change often, compression avoids reprocessing the entire collection.
Key Takeaways
Retrieved chunks are topically relevant but rarely surgically precise. They
contain a mix of useful and tangential sentences.
Contextual compression adds an LLM-powered extraction step between
retrieval and generation, pulling out only the sentences that address the
specific query.
The compression is contextual: the same chunk produces different
extracts for different queries, because relevance depends on the question
being asked.
Unlike relevancy grading (which keeps or discards whole chunks),
compression operates at the sentence level within each chunk.
The cost is an LLM call per retrieved chunk at query time. This adds
latency and inference cost, making it best suited for systems where
answer precision matters more than speed.
Contextual compression pairs well with other techniques: use relevancy
grading first to remove junk chunks, then compress the survivors. Or
combine with contextual chunk headers to preserve document identity
while still extracting focused content.
--- PAGE 141 ---
Chapter 11: Document Augmentation: Giving Every
Chunk a Set of Questions
During preparation, an LLM generates questions that each chunk could
answer. Those questions are embedded and stored alongside the
original chunks, so user queries match against question-shaped entries
instead of declarative text.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (the basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 6 (HyDE and the
query-document asymmetry problem).
The Problem
Chapter 6 introduced a fundamental tension in retrieval: user queries are
questions, but stored chunks are declarative passages. A user asks “How do
greenhouse gases trap heat?” while the chunk that holds the answer reads
“Greenhouse gases absorb infrared radiation emitted by the Earth’s surface,
re-emitting it in all directions and warming the lower atmosphere.” The
question and the answer describe the same concept, but they are structurally
different. One is an interrogative sentence; the other is an explanatory
statement. Their embeddings land in different regions of the vector space,
even though they are about the same thing.
HyDE solved this by transforming the query side: generating a
hypothetical answer so the search vector looks like a document. But there is
another direction to approach the same problem. Instead of reshaping the
query at search time, what if the documents were reshaped during
preparation to look more like the questions users will eventually ask?
Consider a knowledge base with thousands of chunks about climate
science. A chunk about carbon pricing mechanisms contains the answer to
dozens of potential questions: “What is a carbon tax?”, “How do cap-and-
trade systems work?”, “Why do governments put a price on carbon?” None of
those questions appear in the chunk itself. The chunk is a declarative
--- PAGE 142 ---
explanation. When a user asks one of those questions, the retriever must
bridge the gap between the question’s embedding and the chunk’s
embedding. Sometimes the bridge is short and retrieval succeeds. Sometimes
the gap is wide enough that a less relevant chunk scores higher.
The following illustration shows this shape mismatch: a question-shaped
query trying to find its match among declarative-shaped chunks, with the
embedding distance creating a gap even when the content is a perfect match.
A question mark icon on the left representing a user query, with dotted arrows reaching toward three
rectangular chunks on the right. The chunks contain horizontal lines suggesting declarative text. The
arrows are long and stretched, suggesting distance in embedding space. One chunk has a faint glow
indicating it holds the answer, but the arrow to it is still long. A label “Gap” appears along the stretched
arrows.
What if each chunk came pre-equipped with the questions it could answer?
Then the user’s question would match against other questions, not against
declarative text. The shape mismatch disappears.
The Core Idea
--- PAGE 143 ---
Document augmentation through question generation adds a
preparation step after chunking. For each chunk, a language model reads the
content and generates a set of questions that the chunk could answer. These
generated questions are embedded and stored in the vector store alongside
the original chunk, each pointing back to the parent chunk as its source.
Think of it like a museum curator who writes a “Frequently Asked
Questions” card for each exhibit. The exhibit itself is a detailed description of
a historical artifact, written in declarative prose. Most visitors do not read the
entire description. They scan for answers to specific questions: “When was
this made?”, “What was it used for?”, “Why is it significant?” The FAQ card
anticipates those questions and places them right next to the exhibit. A visitor
scanning the room spots the FAQ card that matches their question and
immediately knows which exhibit to examine in detail. The FAQ cards bridge
the gap between what visitors want to know and how the exhibit presents its
information. The following illustration shows this: a visitor scanning FAQ
cards next to museum exhibits to quickly find the right one.
A museum scene with exhibit pedestals displaying artifacts with detailed descriptive plaques, and small
FAQ cards next to each plaque. A visitor scans the FAQ cards rather than the dense plaques, with a
dotted arrow connecting their thought bubble to a matching FAQ card
--- PAGE 144 ---
In the same way, generated questions bridge the gap between how users
phrase their queries and how chunks store their knowledge. At query time,
the user’s question is far more likely to land near a generated question (same
shape, same intent) than near the original declarative chunk. The following
diagram shows this: each chunk surrounded by a cluster of generated
questions, all stored together in the vector store.
Three rectangular chunks arranged vertically on the left, each in a different muted color (blue, orange,
gray). From each chunk, three to four small rounded bubbles with question mark icons branch out to
the right, representing generated questions. All the chunks and their question bubbles flow into a
cylindrical database icon on the far right. A label “Chunks” appears on the left and “Store” on the right.
The core insight: instead of reshaping the query at search time (like
HyDE), reshape the documents during preparation. Pre-generate the
questions each chunk answers, and let the retriever match question
against question.
--- PAGE 145 ---
How It Works
Document augmentation modifies only the preparation phase of the pipeline.
Once the augmented entries are stored, retrieval and generation work exactly
as in chapter 1.
Step 1: Chunk the Document
The document is split into chunks using any chunking strategy: fixed-size
from chapter 1, semantic from chapter 9, or any other approach. The
augmentation technique is agnostic to how chunks are created. It works with
whatever chunks exist.
Step 2: Generate Questions for Each Chunk
For each chunk, a language model receives the chunk text and a prompt
asking it to generate a set of questions that could be answered by this
content. A typical configuration generates anywhere from five to forty
questions per chunk, depending on the chunk’s length and density.
The generation can happen at two granularity levels. Document-level
generation feeds a larger text passage (potentially spanning multiple
chunks) to the language model and generates questions for the whole
passage. This captures broader themes and cross-sentence relationships.
Fragment-level generation feeds each individual chunk separately,
producing questions tightly scoped to that chunk’s specific content.
Fragment-level generation produces more precise questions but requires
more language model calls.
The generated questions are cleaned and deduplicated. Questions that do
not end with a question mark or that are too vague are filtered out.
Step 3: Store Questions Alongside Chunks
Each generated question becomes its own entry in the vector store, embedded
just like a regular chunk. The critical detail is the metadata: each question
entry carries a pointer back to the parent chunk it was generated from. The
original chunk is also stored in the database as a regular entry.
The vector store now contains two types of entries: original chunks
(declarative text) and generated questions (interrogative text), all living in the
--- PAGE 146 ---
same embedding space. The following pipeline diagram shows the complete
preparation flow.
A horizontal pipeline diagram with four stages connected by smooth arrows flowing left to right. Stage
1: a document page icon, label “Doc” below. Stage 2: the document split into three colored rectangular
chunks, label “Chunks” below. Stage 3: a brain icon with multiple small question mark bubbles
emerging from it, label “Generate” below. Stage 4: a cylindrical database with both rectangular chunk
icons and small question bubble icons inside it, label “Store” below. Clean sans-serif labels.
Step 4: Retrieve via Question Matching
At query time, the user’s question is embedded and matched against the full
vector store. Because the database now contains question-shaped entries, the
user’s question is likely to land nearest to one of the generated questions
rather than to an original chunk. The embeddings of two questions about the
same topic are naturally close: “How do carbon taxes reduce emissions?” and
“What role do carbon pricing mechanisms play in cutting emissions?” are
structurally similar and semantically aligned.
When a generated question is the closest match, the system follows the
metadata pointer to retrieve the parent chunk. This parent chunk, the original
declarative text, is what gets passed to the generation model as context. The
--- PAGE 147 ---
user never sees the generated question. It served only as a matchmaking
intermediary.
Algorithm Sketch: Document Augmentation
1. Split document into chunks
2. For each chunk:
   a. Send chunk text to LLM with question-generation prompt
   b. LLM returns N questions answerable by this chunk
   c. Clean and deduplicate the questions
3. Embed each original chunk and store in vector store
4. Embed each generated question and store with pointer to parent chunk
5. At query time: embed user query, search full database
6. If closest match is a generated question, retrieve its parent chunk
7. Pass parent chunk to generation model as context
The following diagram illustrates the retrieval flow: a user query matching
against a generated question, which then leads back to the parent chunk used
for answer generation.
--- PAGE 148 ---
A retrieval flow diagram. On the left, a rounded rectangle with a question mark labeled “Query”. An
arrow leads to a cylindrical database in the center. Inside the database, the arrow connects to a small
question bubble icon (a generated question) that glows, indicating a match. A curved arrow from the
question bubble points to a rectangular chunk icon, also inside the database, showing the parent-chunk
lookup. The chunk then flows via an arrow to a lightbulb icon on the right, labeled “Answer”. Three
labels total: “Query”, “Match”, “Answer”.
The Mirror Image of HyDE
This technique and HyDE from chapter 6 are mirror images of each other.
They solve the same problem, the shape mismatch between questions and
documents in embedding space, but they attack it from opposite sides.
HyDE transforms the query at search time. It takes the user’s question,
generates a hypothetical answer (document-shaped), and uses that answer’s
embedding to search the vector store. The query is reshaped to look like the
documents.
Document augmentation transforms the documents at preparation time. It
takes each chunk, generates questions (query-shaped), and stores those
questions in the vector store. The documents are reshaped to look like
queries.
--- PAGE 149 ---
The tradeoff is when the work happens. HyDE adds latency at query time
(one language model call per query) but requires no extra preparation.
Document augmentation adds cost at preparation time (language model calls
per chunk) but adds zero latency at query time. For a system with a stable
document collection and high query volume, document augmentation front-
loads the investment. For a system with frequently changing documents and
lower query volume, HyDE avoids the cost of regenerating questions every
time the collection updates. The following illustration places both approaches
side by side.
A split-screen comparison divided by a thin dotted vertical line. Left half labeled “HyDE” at top. Shows a
question mark icon at the top transforming into a document-shaped rectangle via a downward arrow
with a brain icon, then searching against chunk rectangles in a database. Right half labeled “Augment”
at top. Shows chunk rectangles each sprouting question bubble icons via brain icons during preparation,
all stored in a database, then a question mark searching against the question bubbles. The left side has a
label “Query Time” and the right has “Prep Time”.
Choosing the Number of Questions
The number of questions generated per chunk is a tuning parameter. More
questions mean more surface area for matching: a wider net of phrasings that
--- PAGE 150 ---
could catch user queries. But more questions also mean more entries in the
vector store (increasing storage and potentially slowing search) and more
language model calls during preparation.
A practical range is five to forty questions per chunk. Short, dense chunks
(a few sentences about a single fact) might need only five questions because
there are limited ways to ask about the content. Long, rich chunks covering
multiple aspects of a topic benefit from more questions to cover the different
angles a user might approach the content from.
The quality of generated questions matters as much as the quantity.
Questions should be specific enough to uniquely identify their parent chunk.
A question like “What is climate change?” is too broad; it could match dozens
of chunks. A question like “How does the cap-and-trade system incentivize
companies to reduce emissions below their allocated permits?” points clearly
to one specific chunk.
When to Use This
Best for:
Systems with a stable document collection that is updated infrequently,
where the one-time preparation cost is amortized over many queries
High query volumes where any per-query latency (like HyDE’s LLM call)
is unacceptable
Knowledge bases where user queries are consistently question-shaped
and declarative chunks are the norm
Overkill when:
Documents change frequently, forcing constant regeneration of
questions
Chunks are already question-and-answer pairs (FAQ databases), where
the shape mismatch does not exist
The retriever already achieves strong performance without
augmentation, or HyDE is already closing the gap at acceptable latency
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
--- PAGE 151 ---
Factor Impact
Preparation cost High: one LLM call per chunk (or per
document), generating N questions each
Query latency None: all augmentation happens during
preparation
Storage Increased: N extra vector entries per chunk
Retrieval quality Significant improvement when user queries are
question-shaped
Compared to HyDE from chapter 6:
Both solve the query-document shape mismatch. HyDE works at query
time with zero preparation cost; document augmentation works at
preparation time with zero query-time cost. For stable collections with high
traffic, document augmentation is the better investment. For dynamic
collections with lower traffic, HyDE is lighter weight. In some systems, both
can be used together: augment the documents with questions during
preparation, and also apply HyDE at query time for an extra matching boost.
Key Takeaways
User queries are question-shaped, but stored chunks are declarative. This
shape mismatch creates embedding distance even when the content is a
perfect match.
Document augmentation bridges the gap by generating questions from
each chunk during preparation and storing them alongside the original
content in the vector store.
At query time, the user’s question matches against stored questions
(same shape), then the system follows a metadata pointer to retrieve the
parent chunk.
This is the mirror image of HyDE: HyDE transforms the query to look
like a document, while document augmentation transforms documents
to look like queries.
The key tradeoff is when the work happens. Document augmentation
front-loads the cost during preparation, adding zero latency at query
--- PAGE 152 ---
time.
The number of generated questions per chunk is a tuning parameter.
More questions increase matching surface area but also increase storage
and preparation cost.
--- PAGE 153 ---
Chapter 12: Fusion Retrieval: Combining Keyword
and Semantic Search
Running two different retrievers in parallel, one that matches exact
words and one that matches meaning, then merging their results with
weighted scores for broader, more reliable retrieval.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, the basic RAG
pipeline).
The Problem
Semantic search, the vector-based retrieval from chapter 1, understands
meaning. It finds chunks about “greenhouse gas emissions” even when the
query says “carbon dioxide output” because the embeddings capture the
conceptual overlap. This is its strength. But meaning-based matching has a
blind spot: it can overlook exact terminology that matters.
Imagine a climate researcher querying a knowledge base with “BPA
regulation 2019.” The term “BPA” (bisphenol A) is a specific chemical
identifier. The researcher needs chunks that mention this exact acronym. But
the embedding model, trained on broad language patterns, maps “BPA
regulation” into a general region of chemical regulation concepts. It retrieves
chunks about chemical safety standards, environmental regulations, and
plastic manufacturing policies. These are semantically relevant. But the one
chunk that actually discusses BPA by name and cites the 2019 regulatory
update ranks fourth, behind three chunks that discuss similar topics without
ever mentioning BPA.
The reverse problem exists too. A pure keyword search finds every
document containing “BPA” and “2019” but misses a chunk that discusses
“the ban on bisphenol compounds in food-contact plastics enacted last year”
because neither “BPA” nor “2019” appears in its text. The meaning is there.
The words are not. The following illustration shows these two blind spots side

--- PAGE 10 ---
Introduction
A guide to 22 retrieval-augmented generation techniques, explained
through intuition and illustrations instead of code.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation has become one of the most important
patterns in applied AI. The idea is deceptively simple: instead of asking a
language model to answer from memory, you first retrieve relevant
information from your own documents and hand it to the model as context.
The model reads, then answers. No hallucination. No guesswork.
But that simple idea hides a landscape of complexity. How should you split
documents into searchable pieces? What if the user’s question is vague or
ambiguous? What if the retrieved passages are almost-but-not-quite
relevant? What if the model ignores the context and hallucinates anyway?
Each of these failure modes has spawned its own family of techniques, and
the field is growing fast.
This book maps that landscape. It covers 22 distinct RAG techniques, from
the foundational pipeline through advanced strategies like graph-based
retrieval and self-correcting systems. Each chapter extracts the algorithm and
intuition from a real implementation, then teaches it without a single line of
code. The following illustration shows how these techniques span the full
RAG pipeline, from how you prepare documents to how you verify the final
answer.
--- PAGE 11 ---
The 22 techniques span seven stages of the RAG pipeline, from foundational document preparation on
the left through chunking, query improvement, retrieval, post-processing, and advanced strategies, to
evaluation on the right.
--- PAGE 12 ---
Where This Book Comes From
This book is based on the RAG Techniques open-source repository, a
collection of over 20 working notebook implementations covering the full
spectrum of RAG strategies. The repository has become one of the most
widely used RAG references in the community, with millions of developers
using it to learn and build production systems. Each chapter in this book
takes one of those implementations, extracts the underlying algorithm, and
teaches it as a standalone concept without code.
If you want to go deeper after reading a chapter, the repository has a
runnable notebook for every technique covered here. The book gives you the
intuition. The repo gives you the implementation. They are designed to
complement each other.
For ongoing updates on RAG, AI engineering, and new techniques as they
emerge, the DiamantAI Newsletter covers the latest developments in applied
AI with the same focus on practical understanding over hype. New
techniques, architectural patterns, and lessons from production systems are
published regularly.
--- PAGE 13 ---
Who This Book Is For
This book is for developers and technical product managers who want to
understand RAG deeply. Not the API calls. Not the framework boilerplate.
The underlying ideas: why each technique exists, how it works at the
algorithmic level, and when it is the right choice.
If you have tried building a RAG system and found that the basic approach
works sometimes but fails in frustrating, unpredictable ways, this book will
help you understand why and give you a toolkit of solutions. If you are
evaluating RAG techniques for a product and need to understand the
tradeoffs without wading through research papers, this book is your shortcut.
This is not a code cookbook. There are no imports, no function calls, no
framework-specific instructions. Every technique is explained as a process
you could implement in any language, with any library. The goal is
understanding that transfers.
--- PAGE 14 ---
How the Book Is Organized
The 22 chapters follow a natural progression, roughly mirroring the stages of
a RAG pipeline. You can think of them in seven groups.
Foundations (Chapters 1-3) build the core pipeline. Chapter 1
introduces simple RAG: splitting documents, creating embeddings, retrieving
chunks, and generating answers. Chapter 2 extends this to structured data
like spreadsheets. Chapter 3 adds verification, teaching the system to check
its own work.
Smarter Chunking (Chapters 4, 7, 8, 9) tackles the question of how
to break documents into pieces. Proposition chunking (Chapter 4)
decomposes text into atomic facts. Contextual chunk headers (Chapter 7)
attach source metadata to every piece. Context enrichment windows (Chapter
8) expand retrieved passages to include surrounding context. Semantic
chunking (Chapter 9) splits text where the meaning shifts rather than at
arbitrary character counts.
Query Strategies (Chapters 5-6) improve retrieval by transforming the
question itself. Query transformations (Chapter 5) rewrite vague questions
into precise ones. HyDE (Chapter 6) generates a hypothetical answer first,
then uses it to search for real documents.
Post-Retrieval Processing (Chapters 10, 11, 13) refines what comes
back from the search. Contextual compression (Chapter 10) strips retrieved
passages down to only the relevant sentences. Document augmentation
(Chapter 11) pre-generates questions for each chunk so retrieval can match
on intent. Reranking (Chapter 13) uses a second model to re-score and re-
order results by relevance.
Retrieval Strategies (Chapters 12, 14, 15) explore different ways to
search. Fusion retrieval (Chapter 12) combines keyword and semantic search.
Hierarchical indices (Chapter 14) search at multiple levels of granularity,
from summaries down to paragraphs. Dartboard retrieval (Chapter 15) targets
the center of relevance using progressively tighter filters.
Advanced and Specialized (Chapters 16-21) address harder
problems. Multi-modal RAG (Chapter 16) handles images alongside text.
Retrieval with feedback loops (Chapter 17) learns from user interactions to
improve over time. Adaptive retrieval (Chapter 18) dynamically adjusts its
strategy based on query complexity. Explainable retrieval (Chapter 19) shows
--- PAGE 15 ---
its reasoning so users can verify the answer. Corrective RAG (Chapter 20)
detects and repairs its own mistakes mid-pipeline. Graph RAG (Chapter 21)
builds knowledge graphs to capture relationships that flat document chunks
miss.
Measurement (Chapter 22) closes the loop with RAG evaluation: how
to systematically test retrieval quality, faithfulness, and answer relevance so
you can compare techniques with confidence.
The book ends with an Appendix that serves as a decision guide. If you
have a specific problem and want to know which technique to try first, start
there. The following diagram shows how the chapters connect, from
foundations through each layer of sophistication to evaluation.
The book follows a climbing path through seven stages, starting with foundations and progressing
through increasingly sophisticated techniques toward evaluation at the summit.
How to Read This Book
--- PAGE 16 ---
There are two good ways to use this book. The first is to read it front to back.
The chapters build on each other. Chapter 1 introduces vocabulary and
concepts that every later chapter assumes you know. The progression from
simple techniques to advanced ones mirrors the order most teams follow
when evolving their own RAG systems.
The second is to use it as a reference. If you already have a working RAG
pipeline and want to solve a specific problem (poor retrieval for long
documents, hallucinated answers, vague user queries) you can jump directly
to the relevant chapter. The prerequisites line at the top of each chapter tells
you what background it assumes.
Either way, read Chapter 1 first. It establishes the foundational pipeline
that every other technique modifies, extends, or improves.
Every chapter in this book follows the same pattern: start with a
concrete problem, explain the core insight, walk through the algorithm
step by step, and close with tradeoffs and decision criteria. No code. No
framework lock-in. Just the ideas.
A Note on Illustrations
Every chapter includes custom illustrations designed to make abstract
algorithms concrete. Pipeline diagrams show data flowing through stages.
Before-and-after comparisons reveal what a technique actually changes.
Visual analogies bring real-world metaphors to life. These are not decorative.
They are part of the explanation, and each one is tightly connected to the text
around it.
When you encounter an illustration, read the caption. It summarizes the
key takeaway. Then look at the image in the context of the paragraph above it,
which sets up exactly what you are about to see.
Welcome to the book. Chapter 1 starts with the simplest version of RAG: one
document, one question, one answer. Everything builds from there.
--- PAGE 17 ---
Chapter 1: Simple RAG: The Foundation of
Document-Powered AI
Teaching a language model to look things up before answering, instead
of guessing from memory.
Prerequisites: Basic understanding of what a language model (LLM) does.
Familiarity with the idea of a database.
The Problem
Imagine you have a 200-page report about climate change. You need a quick
answer: “What does chapter 2 say about the main cause of climate change?”
So you ask a language model.
The model gives you a generic, textbook-style answer about greenhouse
gases. It sounds reasonable. But it is not drawn from your report. The model
has never seen your document. It is answering from memory, from patterns
learned during training. It might even state facts that sound right but are
nowhere in your source material. This is called hallucination, and it is one
of the biggest risks of using language models for knowledge work.
Now scale this up. Your company has thousands of internal documents:
policy manuals, research papers, product specs, legal contracts. Employees
ask questions about these every day. A language model that answers from
memory alone is useless here. It does not know what is in your documents.
The contrast is stark: without access to your documents, the model guesses
and often gets it wrong. With access, it reads the relevant passage and
answers correctly. The following illustration captures this difference.
--- PAGE 18 ---
Without RAG, a robot answers from memory and gets it wrong. With RAG, the same robot reads the
source document and answers correctly.
The Core Idea
RAG, short for Retrieval-Augmented Generation, solves this by adding a
simple step: before the model answers, it first searches your documents for
relevant information.
Think of it as the difference between a closed-book exam and an open-
book exam. In a closed-book exam, the student relies entirely on memory,
staring at the blank page with growing uncertainty. In an open-book exam,
the student can look up the relevant section, read it, and write an informed
answer with confidence. RAG gives your language model an open book.
--- PAGE 19 ---
A closed-book student struggles with a question mark overhead, while an open-book student writes
confidently with a reference book on their desk and a lightbulb above.
The core insight: you do not need to retrain the model or stuff entire
documents into the prompt. You store your documents in a searchable
format, retrieve only the relevant pieces, and hand those pieces to the
model as context.
The model does what it does best: read the context and generate a clear,
grounded answer. The rest of this chapter explains exactly how this works,
step by step.
How It Works
The simple RAG pipeline has two phases: an offline preparation phase
(done once per document) and an online query phase (done each time a
user asks a question).
--- PAGE 20 ---
Phase 1: Preparing the Knowledge Base
Step 1: Load the Document
The process starts by extracting raw text from your source documents. A
PDF is parsed page by page, converting visual content into plain text. This is a
straightforward conversion step, but it matters. Poor text extraction leads to
poor retrieval downstream.
Think of this as a librarian transcribing a book onto index cards so it can
be cataloged and searched.
Step 2: Split Into Chunks
A 200-page document is far too large to search as a single block. The
system splits the text into smaller pieces called chunks. A common default is
chunks of about 1,000 characters (roughly a long paragraph), with 200
characters of overlap between consecutive chunks.
Why overlap? Imagine a critical explanation that spans two paragraphs. If
you split the text exactly between them, neither chunk captures the full
thought. The overlap ensures that information at the boundaries is preserved.
Each chunk carries the last few lines of the previous chunk and the first few
lines of the next one, so no thought is lost at the seams. The diagram below
shows this: notice how the orange-highlighted bands at the bottom of one
chunk match the top of the next, representing the shared overlap region.
--- PAGE 21 ---
A full document on the left is split into three separate chunks on the right, with orange bands showing
the overlap regions shared between consecutive chunks.
Step 3: Clean the Text
Raw text extracted from PDFs often carries formatting artifacts: stray tab
characters, extra whitespace, broken line endings. A cleaning step normalizes
the text so that the next stages work reliably. This is a small but important
detail. Dirty text leads to noisy embeddings.
Step 4: Create Embeddings
This is the most important transformation in the pipeline. Each text chunk
is converted into an embedding: a list of numbers (a vector) that captures
the meaning of the text. These are not random numbers. They are carefully
computed so that two chunks about similar topics produce vectors that are
close together in mathematical space, while chunks about different topics
produce vectors that are far apart.
Think of embeddings as GPS coordinates for meaning. Just as GPS
coordinates let you find places that are geographically close, embedding
vectors let you find text passages that are semantically close. A chunk about
“greenhouse gas emissions” and a chunk about “carbon dioxide in the
--- PAGE 22 ---
atmosphere” will have nearby vectors, even though they use different words.
The illustration below shows this: the blue and orange text cards map to dots
that cluster together (labeled “similar”) because they cover related topics,
while the gray card maps to a distant dot (labeled “different”).
Three text cards in blue, orange, and gray are mapped into a coordinate grid labeled “Meaning Space.”
The blue and orange dots cluster together labeled “similar,” while the gray dot sits apart labeled
“different.”
Step 5: Store in a Vector Database
All embedding vectors are stored in a specialized database optimized for
similarity search. A popular choice is FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search),
which organizes vectors so that finding the nearest neighbors to any query
vector is extremely fast, even with millions of entries.
This is like organizing a library not alphabetically or by publication date,
but by topic similarity. Books about related subjects sit on adjacent shelves.
When you need information about a specific topic, you go straight to the right
neighborhood and browse nearby books. The image below illustrates this
contrast: a traditional library where a reader walks between distant shelves to
find related books, versus a conceptual library where books on science,
--- PAGE 23 ---
energy, and policy are clustered by meaning, putting relevant material within
arm’s reach.
A traditional library with alphabetical shelves where a reader searches far apart, versus a modern
conceptual library where books on science, energy, and policy are clustered together by topic.
The entire preparation phase can be summarized in five steps:
Algorithm Sketch: Document Preparation
1. Load document and extract raw text
2. Split text into chunks of ~1000 characters with ~200 character overlap
3. Clean each chunk (remove formatting artifacts)
4. Convert each chunk into an embedding vector
5. Store all vectors in a vector store for fast similarity search
Phase 2: Answering a Query
With the knowledge base prepared, the system is ready to answer questions.
This phase runs every time a user submits a query.
Step 6: Embed the Query
--- PAGE 24 ---
When a user asks a question, that question is converted into an embedding
vector using the exact same process applied to the document chunks. This is
critical: the query must live in the same “meaning space” as the stored
chunks so that distances between them are meaningful.
Step 7: Find the Nearest Chunks
The system searches the vector store for the chunks whose embedding
vectors are closest to the query vector. A typical setup retrieves the top 2 to 5
most similar chunks. These represent the parts of the document most likely
to contain the answer.
For example, given the query “What is the main cause of climate change?”
against a climate report, the system might retrieve a chunk discussing
greenhouse gases and fossil fuels, and another covering modern scientific
observations about human-driven climate change. Both are directly relevant
to the question. In the diagram below, the orange dot represents the query in
meaning space, and the blue highlighted dots are the nearest stored chunks
that the system retrieves, connected by dashed lines to show proximity.
A scatter plot of stored document chunks as gray dots in meaning space. An orange query dot appears
with a magnifying glass, and the three nearest chunks are highlighted in blue with dashed lines showing
their proximity.
--- PAGE 25 ---
Step 8: Generate the Answer
The retrieved chunks are passed to the language model along with the
original question. The model reads the relevant context and generates an
answer that is grounded in the actual document content, not in its training
memory. This is the payoff. The model is no longer guessing. It is reading the
relevant paragraphs and summarizing them for you.
The diagram below shows the complete pipeline end-to-end. The top row is
the preparation phase: a document is split, transformed into vectors, and
stored. The bottom row is the query phase: a question is formulated into a
vector, used to search the database, and the retrieved chunks feed into the
final answer.
The full RAG pipeline in two rows. Top row labeled “Prepare” shows document, split, transform, store.
Bottom row labeled “Query” shows question, formulate, search, retrieve, answer.
--- PAGE 26 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Query Processing
1. Receive user question
2. Convert question into an embedding vector
3. Search vector store for top-K nearest chunk vectors
4. Retrieve the corresponding text chunks
5. Pass question + retrieved chunks to the language model
6. Model generates answer grounded in the retrieved context
The Role of Chunk Size
Chunk size is the most important configuration decision in simple RAG, and
there is no single right answer. It is a tradeoff:
Too small (e.g., 100 characters): Each chunk is just a sentence or two. It
may be very specific, but it lacks surrounding context. The model gets a
precise fragment but cannot understand the bigger picture.
Too large (e.g., 5,000 characters): Each chunk covers several pages. The
relevant information is buried in a sea of unrelated text. The retrieval still
works, but the signal is diluted by noise.
Just right (e.g., 1,000 characters with 200 overlap): Each chunk is
roughly a full paragraph. It carries enough context to be meaningful on its
own, but is focused enough to rank well in similarity search. The three panels
below show this visually: tiny scattered fragments with no context, balanced
medium-sized pieces, and oversized blocks where the relevant text is a tiny
fraction of the whole.
--- PAGE 27 ---
Three panels comparing chunk sizes. “Too Small” shows scattered tiny fragments with no context. “Just
Right” shows balanced medium-sized pieces. “Too Large” shows oversized blocks where the relevant
portion is a tiny highlighted area.
Where Simple RAG Falls Short
Simple RAG handles focused, factual questions well. Ask “What is the main
cause of climate change?” and the system retrieves the right paragraphs and
produces an accurate, grounded answer.
But consider a harder question: “Compare the economic impacts of climate
change across different sectors discussed in the report.” This requires
information scattered across multiple sections of the document. A system
that retrieves only two or three chunks will miss most of the relevant
material. The answer will be incomplete or shallow.
Similarly, questions that require reasoning across multiple concepts,
synthesizing definitions, or connecting ideas from different parts of a
document will expose the limits of retrieving a small, fixed number of chunks
from a single search.
These limitations are not bugs. They are the natural boundaries of the
simplest possible RAG design. They are also exactly what motivate the
--- PAGE 28 ---
advanced techniques covered in later chapters: better chunking strategies,
multi-query retrieval, reranking, and query transformation.
When to Use This
Best for:
Answering factual questions about a single document or small collection
Building a quick prototype to validate that RAG helps your use case
Internal knowledge bases with straightforward lookup queries
Situations where simplicity and low cost matter more than perfect
accuracy
Overkill when:
Your documents are short enough to fit entirely in the model’s context
window
You only need keyword search (traditional search engines work fine)
You never update your document collection and could pre-compute all
answers
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency Low: one embedding call + one vector search +
one LLM call
Complexity Low: fewest moving parts of any RAG
approach
Cost Low: embedding is cheap, only one LLM call
per query
Accuracy gain Moderate: strong for focused factual queries,
weak for multi-hop or complex questions
Compared to using an LLM without RAG: Simple RAG grounds the
model’s answers in your actual documents, reducing hallucination and
enabling responses about content the model never saw during training. The
tradeoff is that answer quality depends entirely on retrieval quality. If the
wrong chunks are retrieved, the answer will be wrong or incomplete. And
--- PAGE 29 ---
with a fixed chunk size and a single retrieval strategy, simple RAG has limited
ability to handle nuanced or multi-part questions.
Key Takeaways
RAG adds a “look it up” step before the language model answers,
grounding responses in actual source material instead of training
memory.
Documents are split into overlapping chunks to ensure no information is
lost at the boundaries between pieces.
Embeddings convert text into numerical vectors where similar meanings
are placed close together, enabling search by meaning rather than
keywords.
A vector store organizes these vectors for fast similarity search, even
across millions of chunks.
Simple RAG is the foundation that all advanced techniques build upon.
Its limitations, including fixed chunk sizes, single-strategy retrieval, and
no query refinement, are exactly what the rest of this book addresses.
--- PAGE 30 ---
Chapter 2: RAG for Structured Data: When
Spreadsheets Meet Semantic Search
Adapting the RAG pipeline to work with tabular data like CSV files,
where rows are records and columns are fields.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (Simple RAG). Understanding of how embeddings,
vector stores, and similarity search work.
The Problem
In the previous chapter, we built a RAG pipeline for prose documents. The
system splits continuous text into chunks, embeds them, and retrieves the
most relevant pieces to answer a question. But not all data lives in
documents. A huge amount of business-critical information lives in
spreadsheets and databases: customer records, product catalogs, transaction
logs, employee directories.
Suppose your company maintains a customer database with hundreds of
records. Each record has a name, company, city, country, phone number,
email, and subscription date. A sales manager asks: “Which customers are
based in South American countries?” With a traditional database, you would
need a precise query that lists every South American country by name. Miss
one, and you miss those customers. And what about a fuzzier question like
“Find me customers at companies that sound like they might be in the
consulting business”? No SQL query can answer that, because it requires
understanding what company names imply, not what they exactly match.
The standard RAG pipeline from chapter 1 was designed for running text.
If you try to split a CSV file the same way, treating it as one long string and
chopping it into character-based chunks, you will slice rows in half, mix fields
from different records, and produce chunks that are meaningless. The
illustration below shows this: a table of customer records being blindly
--- PAGE 31 ---
chopped by character count, with rows split mid-record and fields from
different customers jumbled together in a single chunk.
A spreadsheet table on the left is blindly sliced into text chunks on the right, with rows cut in half and
fields from different records mixed together in each chunk, marked with red X symbols.
The Core Idea
The solution is straightforward: treat each row as its own document. Instead
of splitting text by character count, you use the natural boundaries already
present in the data. Every row in a CSV is a self-contained record. Convert
each row into a readable text description, embed it, and store it in a vector
store. Now you can ask natural language questions about your structured data
and retrieve the most relevant records by meaning.
Think of it as giving each row a voice. A row in a spreadsheet is silent data:
columns and values, no context, no narrative. By converting it into a sentence
or paragraph, you let the embedding model understand what the row is about,
not just what it contains. The image below shows a single row of customer
--- PAGE 32 ---
data being transformed into a readable text card, which then maps to a dot in
meaning space alongside other customer records.
A three-stage transformation: a highlighted spreadsheet row labeled ‘Row’ on the left, a warm orange
text card labeled ‘Text’ in the center, and a glowing dot on a coordinate grid labeled ‘Vector’ on the right,
connected by curved arrows.
The key insight: structured data already has natural chunk boundaries.
Each row is a complete unit of information. Use that structure instead
of fighting it.
How It Works
The pipeline follows the same two phases from chapter 1, preparation and
query, but the preparation phase handles tabular data differently.
Phase 1: Preparing the Knowledge Base
--- PAGE 33 ---
Step 1: Load the Structured Data
The process begins by reading the CSV file and parsing it into individual
records. Unlike a PDF, where you get one continuous stream of text, a CSV
gives you cleanly separated rows with labeled columns. Each row is a discrete
unit: one customer, one product, one transaction.
This is a significant advantage. With prose documents, figuring out where
one concept ends and another begins is a real challenge (as we saw with
chunking in chapter 1). With tabular data, the boundaries are already defined
for you.
Step 2: Convert Each Row to Text
This is the step that makes the whole approach work. Each row of
structured data must be converted from a set of field-value pairs into a
human-readable text string. This process is called field serialization.
Consider a customer record with fields like first name, last name,
company, city, and country. The system converts this into something like:
“First Name: Sheryl. Last Name: Baxter. Company: Rasmussen Group. City:
East Leonard. Country: Chile.” This text representation preserves all the
information from the original row, but in a format that an embedding model
can process and understand semantically.
Think of it as translating a form into a paragraph. A filled-out form has the
right information, but it is structured for a computer. The paragraph version
says the same thing, but now a language model (or a human) can read it,
understand context, and make inferences. The illustration below shows this
transformation: a rigid table row with cell values on the left turns into a
flowing text card on the right, with the same information expressed as
readable text.
--- PAGE 34 ---
A rigid spreadsheet grid fragment labeled ‘Grid’ on the left transforms through an arrow with a gear
icon into a friendly rounded card labeled ‘Card’ on the right, showing the same information reorganized
as readable labeled lines.
Step 3: Embed Each Record
