user’s query and then run the transformed query through both the vector and
BM25 retrievers. Each addresses a different source of retrieval failure.
Key Takeaways
Vector search understands meaning but can miss exact keyword matches.
BM25 keyword search matches exact terms but is blind to meaning. Each
has blind spots the other covers.
Fusion retrieval runs both retrievers in parallel on the same query and
merges their results into a single ranked list.
Score normalization is essential because the two retrievers produce
scores on different scales. Distribution-based or min-max normalization
maps both to a common 0-1 range.
--- PAGE 166 ---
The retriever weights control the balance between semantic and keyword
signals. Typical configurations range from 50/50 to 70/30 depending on
query patterns.
BM25 is a keyword-matching algorithm based on term frequency, inverse
document frequency, and length normalization. It requires no
embeddings and complements vector search by catching exact-term
matches that semantic search overlooks.
The additional cost is modest: one extra index during preparation and
one extra retrieval pass at query time. Both retrievers can run in parallel,
so the latency increase is small.
--- PAGE 167 ---
Chapter 13: Reranking
A second, more careful look at retrieved chunks: using specialized
models to re-evaluate and reorder candidates before they reach the
language model.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline)
The Problem
Consider a user who asks: “What is the capital of France?” The vector store
contains several chunks, among them:
“The capital of France is great.”
“The capital of France is beautiful.”
“The capital of France is huge.”
“I really enjoyed my trip to Paris, France. The city is beautiful and the
food is delicious. Such a great capital city.”
Embedding-based similarity search compares the query embedding against
each chunk embedding. The first three chunks are structurally almost
identical to the query. They start with the same words, share the same
sentence pattern, and contain all the same key terms. Their embeddings sit
very close to the query embedding. The fourth chunk talks about visiting
Paris, mentions France only in passing, and uses different vocabulary. Its
embedding is farther away.
The vector store ranks the first three chunks highest. But they are almost
useless as context for answering the question. None of them names Paris.
The fourth chunk, ranked lower by the initial retriever, is the only one that
actually contains the answer.
This is a different problem from the complementary blind spots that
fusion retrieval addresses in chapter 12. There, the issue was that keyword
--- PAGE 168 ---
search and semantic search each miss what the other catches. Here, the
problem lives within a single retriever: embedding similarity is a rough proxy
for relevance, and rough proxies sometimes get the ranking wrong. A chunk
can be semantically close to a query without being a good answer to it. The
following illustration shows a query with its retrieved chunks, where the
most informative result is buried below superficially similar but unhelpful
ones.
A query at the top with a ranked list of retrieved chunks below. The top three chunks glow with high
similarity scores but contain vague, uninformative text (represented by short gray lines). The fourth
chunk, ranked lower with a dimmer score, contains the actual answer (represented by a bright orange
highlight). Red X marks on the top chunks and a green checkmark on the fourth chunk show the
mismatch between similarity ranking and true relevance.
The Core Idea
Reranking adds a second evaluation stage after retrieval. Instead of trusting
the initial similarity ranking as final, the system retrieves a deliberately broad
--- PAGE 169 ---
set of candidates and then re-evaluates each one more carefully using a
model that reads the query and chunk together.
Think of it as a hiring process. A job posting attracts hundreds of
applications. The first screening is fast and coarse: scan each resume for a few
key qualifications and pass anyone who looks plausible. This produces a
shortlist of, say, thirty candidates. Some are excellent fits. Some made it
through because their resume happened to use the right keywords without
having the right experience. The second stage is slower and more thorough:
an interview panel reads each shortlisted resume carefully against the actual
job requirements, scores every candidate on how well they fit the role, and
selects the top three to hire. The following illustration shows this two-stage
process: a wide funnel of many candidates narrowed through careful
evaluation to a final shortlist.
A hiring process scene. On the left, a large stack of many resume icons (small rectangles) enters a wide
funnel labeled “Retrieve”. Below the funnel, a moderate shortlist of candidate icons emerges. These
candidates pass through a second, narrower funnel labeled “Rerank” where a magnifying glass icon
inspects each one. At the bottom, only three candidate icons emerge with green checkmarks,
representing the final top results.
--- PAGE 170 ---
The core insight: initial retrieval optimizes for speed by comparing
chunks independently. Reranking optimizes for accuracy by reading
each chunk alongside the query. The two-stage design gives you both.
How It Works
Reranking inserts a single new step between retrieval and generation. The
preparation phase is unchanged. The generation phase is unchanged. Only
the retrieval phase grows: retrieve broadly, then rerank carefully. The
following diagram shows the full reranking pipeline at a glance.
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right with four stages connected by arrows. Stage 1: a
search icon labeled ‘Query’. Stage 2: a database cylinder labeled ‘Retrieve’ with several document
rectangles emerging. Stage 3: a funnel shape labeled ‘Rerank’ with a magnifying glass, where
documents are filtered and reordered. Stage 4: a lightbulb icon labeled ‘Answer’ with only the top-
ranked documents beside it.
--- PAGE 171 ---
Step 1: Retrieve a Broad Candidate Set
The retriever fetches a larger-than-usual set of candidates. Where a standard
pipeline might retrieve the top three to five chunks, a reranking pipeline
retrieves the top ten to thirty. The exact number depends on how many
candidates the reranker can afford to evaluate. Retrieving more candidates
increases the chance that a truly relevant chunk makes it into the candidate
set, even if the initial retriever did not rank it highly.
Any retriever works for this first stage. Vector search, BM25, or the fusion
retrieval from chapter 12 can all serve as the initial retriever. The reranker
does not care how the candidates were found. It only cares about evaluating
them.
Step 2: Score Each Candidate
This is the heart of reranking. Each candidate chunk is paired with the query
and evaluated to produce a relevance score. Two approaches are common, and
both rely on a key architectural distinction.
Why joint evaluation matters: bi-encoders versus cross-encoders
The initial retrieval in most RAG systems uses a bi-encoder architecture.
A bi-encoder processes the query and each document through the same
embedding model, but independently. The query becomes one vector. Each
document becomes another vector. Relevance is estimated by comparing the
two vectors, typically using cosine similarity. This independence is what
makes vector search fast: document embeddings can be precomputed and
stored, so at query time only the query needs to be embedded.
But independence comes at a cost. Because the model never sees the query
and document at the same time, it cannot detect fine-grained interactions
between them. It cannot notice that a specific phrase in the document directly
answers a specific part of the query. It can only compare two compressed
summaries of what each text means.
A cross-encoder takes the opposite approach. It feeds the query and
document into a single model as one combined input. The model reads both
simultaneously, attending to every word in both texts at once. This joint
processing lets it detect subtle relevance signals: whether a particular
sentence actually answers the question, whether the document’s context
matches the query’s intent, whether key terms are used in the same sense.
The tradeoff is that cross-encoders cannot precompute anything. Every query-
document pair must be processed from scratch, making them too slow for
--- PAGE 172 ---
searching an entire collection but ideal for re-evaluating a small shortlist. The
following diagram compares the two architectures side by side.
A side-by-side comparison. Left side labeled “Bi-Encoder”: a query icon and a document icon each pass
through separate encoder boxes (two parallel paths), producing two separate vector arrows that meet
at a comparison point with a similarity score. Right side labeled “Cross-Encoder”: a query icon and a
document icon enter the same single encoder box together (one combined path), and a single relevance
score emerges from the bottom. The left path is marked with a clock icon suggesting speed. The right
path is marked with a target icon suggesting accuracy.
LLM-based reranking
The first approach uses a general-purpose language model as the
evaluator. The system constructs a prompt containing both the query and the
candidate chunk, and asks the model to rate relevance on a numerical scale,
typically one to ten. The model reads the pair, reasons about how well the
chunk addresses the query, and returns a score.
This approach is the most flexible. The prompt can include custom criteria:
“Rate how directly this passage answers the question. Consider whether it
provides specific facts, not just topically related discussion.” Different
applications can define relevance differently by adjusting the prompt.
--- PAGE 173 ---
The cost is significant. Each candidate requires a full language model call.
With twenty candidates, reranking adds twenty LLM calls to every query. This
can dominate the latency and cost of the entire pipeline. The following
illustration shows the LLM evaluating a query-chunk pair and producing a
relevance score.
A concept close-up showing LLM-based reranking. At the top, a query bubble and a document rectangle
sit side by side. Both feed into a large rounded rectangle in the center representing a language model,
with a small brain icon inside. From the bottom of the model, a single output emerges: a circular badge
showing a score number. Three such query-chunk pairs are shown in a vertical sequence, each
producing its own score badge, suggesting the process repeats for every candidate.
Algorithm Sketch: LLM-Based Reranking
1. Retrieve top-K candidates from any retriever (K = 10-30)
2. For each candidate chunk:
   a. Construct prompt: "Rate relevance of this document to this query (1-10)"
   b. Send query + chunk to language model
   c. Parse returned score
3. Sort all candidates by score (highest first)
4. Select top-N results (N = 3-5)
5. Pass selected chunks to generation model
--- PAGE 174 ---
Cross-encoder reranking
The second approach uses a cross-encoder model specifically trained for
relevance scoring. These models are much smaller than general-purpose
language models. They take a query-document pair as input and output a
single relevance score, typically in milliseconds rather than seconds.
Cross-encoder rerankers are trained on large datasets of query-document
pairs labeled as relevant or not relevant. Through this training, they learn to
recognize patterns that indicate genuine relevance: whether the document
answers the question, whether key entities match, whether the document’s
context aligns with the query’s intent. They perform this evaluation far faster
than a language model because they are purpose-built for this single task.
The tradeoff is rigidity. A cross-encoder reranker scores relevance as it was
defined during training. You cannot add custom criteria through prompting.
If your application has an unusual definition of relevance (for example,
preferring recent documents over older ones regardless of content), the cross-
encoder may not capture it without fine-tuning on domain-specific data.
Algorithm Sketch: Cross-Encoder Reranking
1. Retrieve top-K candidates from any retriever (K = 10-30)
2. Form pairs: [(query, chunk_1), (query, chunk_2), ..., (query, chunk_K)]
3. Feed all pairs through cross-encoder model (batch processing)
4. Receive one relevance score per pair
5. Sort candidates by score (highest first)
6. Select top-N results (N = 3-5)
7. Pass selected chunks to generation model
Step 3: Reorder and Select
Regardless of which scoring method is used, the final step is the same. The
system sorts all candidates by their new relevance scores and selects the top
N. This reordered shortlist replaces the original retrieval results and is passed
to the generation model.
The impact can be substantial. Chunks that the initial retriever ranked
fifth or tenth may jump to first place once a reranker evaluates them against
the actual query. Chunks that ranked first due to surface-level similarity may
drop when the reranker determines they do not actually address the question.
The following illustration shows the same set of chunks before and after
reranking, with the most informative chunk moving from a low initial rank to
the top position.
--- PAGE 175 ---
A split-screen comparison divided by a thin dotted vertical line. Left half labeled “Before” shows a
numbered list of five chunk rectangles. The top three are highlighted in light gray (superficially similar
but uninformative). The fourth chunk is highlighted in warm orange (the truly relevant one), buried at
rank four. Right half labeled “After” shows the same chunks reordered. The orange chunk is now at
rank one at the top. The gray chunks have dropped to lower positions. Arrows between the two halves
show the movement of chunks up and down.
When to Use This
Best for:
Applications where retrieval quality directly affects answer quality and
errors are costly, such as medical, legal, or financial knowledge bases
Complex or nuanced queries where surface-level embedding similarity is
a poor indicator of true relevance
Systems that already retrieve broad candidate sets (ten or more chunks)
and need to select the best subset
Overkill when:
--- PAGE 176 ---
Queries are simple and the vector store reliably returns the right chunks
in the top three to five results
Extreme latency constraints make even cross-encoder evaluation too
slow
The document collection is small enough that retrieval quality is already
high without additional refinement
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Query latency Moderate (cross-encoder) to high (LLM-
based): adds per-candidate evaluation time
Preparation cost None: reranking requires no changes to
indexing or storage
Complexity Low to moderate: one additional step in the
retrieval pipeline
Accuracy gain Significant for complex queries; modest for
simple ones
Choosing between LLM-based and cross-encoder reranking:
Cross-encoder reranking is the default choice for most applications. It
offers the best balance of speed, cost, and accuracy. A cross-encoder can
evaluate twenty candidates in the time it takes an LLM to evaluate one, at a
fraction of the cost. Use LLM-based reranking when you need custom
relevance criteria that a pre-trained cross-encoder cannot capture, or when
your application already makes LLM calls and can absorb the additional
latency.
Compared to relevancy grading from chapter 3:
Relevancy grading makes a binary keep-or-discard decision on each chunk.
Reranking assigns a numerical score and reorders the entire candidate set.
Grading filters noise. Reranking refines ranking. The two are complementary:
you can retrieve broadly, rerank to find the best candidates, and then apply
relevancy grading as a final check to discard any remaining low-quality
chunks.
--- PAGE 177 ---
Key Takeaways
Embedding similarity is a fast approximation of relevance, not a precise
measure. Chunks that are semantically close to a query are not
necessarily good answers to it.
Reranking adds a second evaluation stage that reads each query-chunk
pair jointly, catching relevance signals that independent embeddings
miss.
A bi-encoder embeds query and document separately for fast
comparison. A cross-encoder reads them together for accurate scoring.
Reranking exploits the cross-encoder’s accuracy on a small candidate set
filtered by the bi-encoder’s speed.
Cross-encoder models are purpose-built for relevance scoring: fast,
cheap, and accurate. They are the default choice for most reranking
scenarios.
LLM-based reranking offers maximum flexibility through custom
prompting but at significant cost in latency and expense.
Reranking requires no changes to the preparation phase. It sits between
retrieval and generation, re-evaluating and reordering whatever the
retriever produced.
--- PAGE 178 ---
Chapter 14: Hierarchical Indices
Organizing retrieval into two levels, summaries for finding the right
sections and detailed chunks for finding the right answers, so the
system searches smart before it searches deep.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline)
The Problem
Imagine a company with a knowledge base spanning hundreds of internal
documents: HR policies, engineering specifications, financial reports, product
manuals. A user asks: “What are the parental leave benefits for employees in
Europe?” The vector store contains thousands of chunks from all of these
documents. Some chunks from the engineering specifications mention
“leave” in the context of error handling. Some chunks from the product
manuals discuss “benefits” of a software feature. Some chunks from financial
reports reference “European operations” in a revenue context.
With a flat index, every chunk competes equally. The retriever compares
the query embedding against all chunk embeddings and returns the top
matches by similarity score. A chunk about software error handling that
happens to share the words “leave” and “policy” can score higher than the
actual parental leave policy buried in the HR document. The retriever has no
sense of structure. It does not know that only the HR documents are relevant
to this question. It treats a chunk from page 47 of an engineering spec the
same as a chunk from page 3 of the benefits handbook.
This problem grows with collection size. In a small knowledge base with
twenty chunks, the right answer is usually near the top. In a large knowledge
base with ten thousand chunks from dozens of documents, the relevant
chunks are needles in a haystack. The flat retriever must score every needle
and every piece of hay equally, with no structural shortcut to narrow the
search. The following illustration shows a query competing against thousands

--- PAGE 10 ---
Introduction
A guide to 22 retrieval-augmented generation techniques, explained
through intuition and illustrations instead of code.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation has become one of the most important
patterns in applied AI. The idea is deceptively simple: instead of asking a
language model to answer from memory, you first retrieve relevant
information from your own documents and hand it to the model as context.
The model reads, then answers. No hallucination. No guesswork.
But that simple idea hides a landscape of complexity. How should you split
documents into searchable pieces? What if the user’s question is vague or
ambiguous? What if the retrieved passages are almost-but-not-quite
relevant? What if the model ignores the context and hallucinates anyway?
Each of these failure modes has spawned its own family of techniques, and
the field is growing fast.
This book maps that landscape. It covers 22 distinct RAG techniques, from
the foundational pipeline through advanced strategies like graph-based
retrieval and self-correcting systems. Each chapter extracts the algorithm and
intuition from a real implementation, then teaches it without a single line of
code. The following illustration shows how these techniques span the full
RAG pipeline, from how you prepare documents to how you verify the final
answer.
--- PAGE 11 ---
The 22 techniques span seven stages of the RAG pipeline, from foundational document preparation on
the left through chunking, query improvement, retrieval, post-processing, and advanced strategies, to
evaluation on the right.
--- PAGE 12 ---
Where This Book Comes From
This book is based on the RAG Techniques open-source repository, a
collection of over 20 working notebook implementations covering the full
spectrum of RAG strategies. The repository has become one of the most
widely used RAG references in the community, with millions of developers
using it to learn and build production systems. Each chapter in this book
takes one of those implementations, extracts the underlying algorithm, and
teaches it as a standalone concept without code.
If you want to go deeper after reading a chapter, the repository has a
runnable notebook for every technique covered here. The book gives you the
intuition. The repo gives you the implementation. They are designed to
complement each other.
For ongoing updates on RAG, AI engineering, and new techniques as they
emerge, the DiamantAI Newsletter covers the latest developments in applied
AI with the same focus on practical understanding over hype. New
techniques, architectural patterns, and lessons from production systems are
published regularly.
--- PAGE 13 ---
Who This Book Is For
This book is for developers and technical product managers who want to
understand RAG deeply. Not the API calls. Not the framework boilerplate.
The underlying ideas: why each technique exists, how it works at the
algorithmic level, and when it is the right choice.
If you have tried building a RAG system and found that the basic approach
works sometimes but fails in frustrating, unpredictable ways, this book will
help you understand why and give you a toolkit of solutions. If you are
evaluating RAG techniques for a product and need to understand the
tradeoffs without wading through research papers, this book is your shortcut.
This is not a code cookbook. There are no imports, no function calls, no
framework-specific instructions. Every technique is explained as a process
you could implement in any language, with any library. The goal is
understanding that transfers.
--- PAGE 14 ---
How the Book Is Organized
The 22 chapters follow a natural progression, roughly mirroring the stages of
a RAG pipeline. You can think of them in seven groups.
Foundations (Chapters 1-3) build the core pipeline. Chapter 1
introduces simple RAG: splitting documents, creating embeddings, retrieving
chunks, and generating answers. Chapter 2 extends this to structured data
like spreadsheets. Chapter 3 adds verification, teaching the system to check
its own work.
Smarter Chunking (Chapters 4, 7, 8, 9) tackles the question of how
to break documents into pieces. Proposition chunking (Chapter 4)
decomposes text into atomic facts. Contextual chunk headers (Chapter 7)
attach source metadata to every piece. Context enrichment windows (Chapter
8) expand retrieved passages to include surrounding context. Semantic
chunking (Chapter 9) splits text where the meaning shifts rather than at
arbitrary character counts.
Query Strategies (Chapters 5-6) improve retrieval by transforming the
question itself. Query transformations (Chapter 5) rewrite vague questions
into precise ones. HyDE (Chapter 6) generates a hypothetical answer first,
then uses it to search for real documents.
Post-Retrieval Processing (Chapters 10, 11, 13) refines what comes
back from the search. Contextual compression (Chapter 10) strips retrieved
passages down to only the relevant sentences. Document augmentation
(Chapter 11) pre-generates questions for each chunk so retrieval can match
on intent. Reranking (Chapter 13) uses a second model to re-score and re-
order results by relevance.
Retrieval Strategies (Chapters 12, 14, 15) explore different ways to
search. Fusion retrieval (Chapter 12) combines keyword and semantic search.
Hierarchical indices (Chapter 14) search at multiple levels of granularity,
from summaries down to paragraphs. Dartboard retrieval (Chapter 15) targets
the center of relevance using progressively tighter filters.
Advanced and Specialized (Chapters 16-21) address harder
problems. Multi-modal RAG (Chapter 16) handles images alongside text.
Retrieval with feedback loops (Chapter 17) learns from user interactions to
improve over time. Adaptive retrieval (Chapter 18) dynamically adjusts its
strategy based on query complexity. Explainable retrieval (Chapter 19) shows
--- PAGE 15 ---
its reasoning so users can verify the answer. Corrective RAG (Chapter 20)
detects and repairs its own mistakes mid-pipeline. Graph RAG (Chapter 21)
builds knowledge graphs to capture relationships that flat document chunks
miss.
Measurement (Chapter 22) closes the loop with RAG evaluation: how
to systematically test retrieval quality, faithfulness, and answer relevance so
you can compare techniques with confidence.
The book ends with an Appendix that serves as a decision guide. If you
have a specific problem and want to know which technique to try first, start
there. The following diagram shows how the chapters connect, from
foundations through each layer of sophistication to evaluation.
The book follows a climbing path through seven stages, starting with foundations and progressing
through increasingly sophisticated techniques toward evaluation at the summit.
How to Read This Book
--- PAGE 16 ---
There are two good ways to use this book. The first is to read it front to back.
The chapters build on each other. Chapter 1 introduces vocabulary and
concepts that every later chapter assumes you know. The progression from
simple techniques to advanced ones mirrors the order most teams follow
when evolving their own RAG systems.
The second is to use it as a reference. If you already have a working RAG
pipeline and want to solve a specific problem (poor retrieval for long
documents, hallucinated answers, vague user queries) you can jump directly
to the relevant chapter. The prerequisites line at the top of each chapter tells
you what background it assumes.
Either way, read Chapter 1 first. It establishes the foundational pipeline
that every other technique modifies, extends, or improves.
Every chapter in this book follows the same pattern: start with a
concrete problem, explain the core insight, walk through the algorithm
step by step, and close with tradeoffs and decision criteria. No code. No
framework lock-in. Just the ideas.
A Note on Illustrations
Every chapter includes custom illustrations designed to make abstract
algorithms concrete. Pipeline diagrams show data flowing through stages.
Before-and-after comparisons reveal what a technique actually changes.
Visual analogies bring real-world metaphors to life. These are not decorative.
They are part of the explanation, and each one is tightly connected to the text
around it.
When you encounter an illustration, read the caption. It summarizes the
key takeaway. Then look at the image in the context of the paragraph above it,
which sets up exactly what you are about to see.
Welcome to the book. Chapter 1 starts with the simplest version of RAG: one
document, one question, one answer. Everything builds from there.
--- PAGE 17 ---
Chapter 1: Simple RAG: The Foundation of
Document-Powered AI
Teaching a language model to look things up before answering, instead
of guessing from memory.
Prerequisites: Basic understanding of what a language model (LLM) does.
Familiarity with the idea of a database.
The Problem
Imagine you have a 200-page report about climate change. You need a quick
answer: “What does chapter 2 say about the main cause of climate change?”
So you ask a language model.
The model gives you a generic, textbook-style answer about greenhouse
gases. It sounds reasonable. But it is not drawn from your report. The model
has never seen your document. It is answering from memory, from patterns
learned during training. It might even state facts that sound right but are
nowhere in your source material. This is called hallucination, and it is one
of the biggest risks of using language models for knowledge work.
Now scale this up. Your company has thousands of internal documents:
policy manuals, research papers, product specs, legal contracts. Employees
ask questions about these every day. A language model that answers from
memory alone is useless here. It does not know what is in your documents.
The contrast is stark: without access to your documents, the model guesses
and often gets it wrong. With access, it reads the relevant passage and
answers correctly. The following illustration captures this difference.
--- PAGE 18 ---
Without RAG, a robot answers from memory and gets it wrong. With RAG, the same robot reads the
source document and answers correctly.
The Core Idea
RAG, short for Retrieval-Augmented Generation, solves this by adding a
simple step: before the model answers, it first searches your documents for
relevant information.
Think of it as the difference between a closed-book exam and an open-
book exam. In a closed-book exam, the student relies entirely on memory,
staring at the blank page with growing uncertainty. In an open-book exam,
the student can look up the relevant section, read it, and write an informed
answer with confidence. RAG gives your language model an open book.
--- PAGE 19 ---
A closed-book student struggles with a question mark overhead, while an open-book student writes
confidently with a reference book on their desk and a lightbulb above.
The core insight: you do not need to retrain the model or stuff entire
documents into the prompt. You store your documents in a searchable
format, retrieve only the relevant pieces, and hand those pieces to the
model as context.
The model does what it does best: read the context and generate a clear,
grounded answer. The rest of this chapter explains exactly how this works,
step by step.
How It Works
The simple RAG pipeline has two phases: an offline preparation phase
(done once per document) and an online query phase (done each time a
user asks a question).
--- PAGE 20 ---
Phase 1: Preparing the Knowledge Base
Step 1: Load the Document
The process starts by extracting raw text from your source documents. A
PDF is parsed page by page, converting visual content into plain text. This is a
straightforward conversion step, but it matters. Poor text extraction leads to
poor retrieval downstream.
Think of this as a librarian transcribing a book onto index cards so it can
be cataloged and searched.
Step 2: Split Into Chunks
A 200-page document is far too large to search as a single block. The
system splits the text into smaller pieces called chunks. A common default is
chunks of about 1,000 characters (roughly a long paragraph), with 200
characters of overlap between consecutive chunks.
Why overlap? Imagine a critical explanation that spans two paragraphs. If
you split the text exactly between them, neither chunk captures the full
thought. The overlap ensures that information at the boundaries is preserved.
Each chunk carries the last few lines of the previous chunk and the first few
lines of the next one, so no thought is lost at the seams. The diagram below
shows this: notice how the orange-highlighted bands at the bottom of one
chunk match the top of the next, representing the shared overlap region.
--- PAGE 21 ---
A full document on the left is split into three separate chunks on the right, with orange bands showing
the overlap regions shared between consecutive chunks.
Step 3: Clean the Text
Raw text extracted from PDFs often carries formatting artifacts: stray tab
characters, extra whitespace, broken line endings. A cleaning step normalizes
the text so that the next stages work reliably. This is a small but important
detail. Dirty text leads to noisy embeddings.
Step 4: Create Embeddings
This is the most important transformation in the pipeline. Each text chunk
is converted into an embedding: a list of numbers (a vector) that captures
the meaning of the text. These are not random numbers. They are carefully
computed so that two chunks about similar topics produce vectors that are
close together in mathematical space, while chunks about different topics
produce vectors that are far apart.
Think of embeddings as GPS coordinates for meaning. Just as GPS
coordinates let you find places that are geographically close, embedding
vectors let you find text passages that are semantically close. A chunk about
“greenhouse gas emissions” and a chunk about “carbon dioxide in the
--- PAGE 22 ---
atmosphere” will have nearby vectors, even though they use different words.
The illustration below shows this: the blue and orange text cards map to dots
that cluster together (labeled “similar”) because they cover related topics,
while the gray card maps to a distant dot (labeled “different”).
Three text cards in blue, orange, and gray are mapped into a coordinate grid labeled “Meaning Space.”
The blue and orange dots cluster together labeled “similar,” while the gray dot sits apart labeled
“different.”
Step 5: Store in a Vector Database
All embedding vectors are stored in a specialized database optimized for
similarity search. A popular choice is FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search),
which organizes vectors so that finding the nearest neighbors to any query
vector is extremely fast, even with millions of entries.
This is like organizing a library not alphabetically or by publication date,
but by topic similarity. Books about related subjects sit on adjacent shelves.
When you need information about a specific topic, you go straight to the right
neighborhood and browse nearby books. The image below illustrates this
contrast: a traditional library where a reader walks between distant shelves to
find related books, versus a conceptual library where books on science,
--- PAGE 23 ---
energy, and policy are clustered by meaning, putting relevant material within
arm’s reach.
A traditional library with alphabetical shelves where a reader searches far apart, versus a modern
conceptual library where books on science, energy, and policy are clustered together by topic.
The entire preparation phase can be summarized in five steps:
Algorithm Sketch: Document Preparation
1. Load document and extract raw text
2. Split text into chunks of ~1000 characters with ~200 character overlap
3. Clean each chunk (remove formatting artifacts)
4. Convert each chunk into an embedding vector
5. Store all vectors in a vector store for fast similarity search
Phase 2: Answering a Query
With the knowledge base prepared, the system is ready to answer questions.
This phase runs every time a user submits a query.
Step 6: Embed the Query
--- PAGE 24 ---
When a user asks a question, that question is converted into an embedding
vector using the exact same process applied to the document chunks. This is
critical: the query must live in the same “meaning space” as the stored
chunks so that distances between them are meaningful.
Step 7: Find the Nearest Chunks
The system searches the vector store for the chunks whose embedding
vectors are closest to the query vector. A typical setup retrieves the top 2 to 5
most similar chunks. These represent the parts of the document most likely
to contain the answer.
For example, given the query “What is the main cause of climate change?”
against a climate report, the system might retrieve a chunk discussing
greenhouse gases and fossil fuels, and another covering modern scientific
observations about human-driven climate change. Both are directly relevant
to the question. In the diagram below, the orange dot represents the query in
meaning space, and the blue highlighted dots are the nearest stored chunks
that the system retrieves, connected by dashed lines to show proximity.
A scatter plot of stored document chunks as gray dots in meaning space. An orange query dot appears
with a magnifying glass, and the three nearest chunks are highlighted in blue with dashed lines showing
their proximity.
--- PAGE 25 ---
Step 8: Generate the Answer
The retrieved chunks are passed to the language model along with the
original question. The model reads the relevant context and generates an
answer that is grounded in the actual document content, not in its training
memory. This is the payoff. The model is no longer guessing. It is reading the
relevant paragraphs and summarizing them for you.
The diagram below shows the complete pipeline end-to-end. The top row is
the preparation phase: a document is split, transformed into vectors, and
stored. The bottom row is the query phase: a question is formulated into a
vector, used to search the database, and the retrieved chunks feed into the
final answer.
The full RAG pipeline in two rows. Top row labeled “Prepare” shows document, split, transform, store.
Bottom row labeled “Query” shows question, formulate, search, retrieve, answer.
--- PAGE 26 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Query Processing
1. Receive user question
2. Convert question into an embedding vector
3. Search vector store for top-K nearest chunk vectors
4. Retrieve the corresponding text chunks
5. Pass question + retrieved chunks to the language model
6. Model generates answer grounded in the retrieved context
The Role of Chunk Size
Chunk size is the most important configuration decision in simple RAG, and
there is no single right answer. It is a tradeoff:
Too small (e.g., 100 characters): Each chunk is just a sentence or two. It
may be very specific, but it lacks surrounding context. The model gets a
precise fragment but cannot understand the bigger picture.
Too large (e.g., 5,000 characters): Each chunk covers several pages. The
relevant information is buried in a sea of unrelated text. The retrieval still
works, but the signal is diluted by noise.
Just right (e.g., 1,000 characters with 200 overlap): Each chunk is
roughly a full paragraph. It carries enough context to be meaningful on its
own, but is focused enough to rank well in similarity search. The three panels
below show this visually: tiny scattered fragments with no context, balanced
medium-sized pieces, and oversized blocks where the relevant text is a tiny
fraction of the whole.
--- PAGE 27 ---
Three panels comparing chunk sizes. “Too Small” shows scattered tiny fragments with no context. “Just
Right” shows balanced medium-sized pieces. “Too Large” shows oversized blocks where the relevant
portion is a tiny highlighted area.
Where Simple RAG Falls Short
Simple RAG handles focused, factual questions well. Ask “What is the main
cause of climate change?” and the system retrieves the right paragraphs and
produces an accurate, grounded answer.
But consider a harder question: “Compare the economic impacts of climate
change across different sectors discussed in the report.” This requires
information scattered across multiple sections of the document. A system
that retrieves only two or three chunks will miss most of the relevant
material. The answer will be incomplete or shallow.
Similarly, questions that require reasoning across multiple concepts,
synthesizing definitions, or connecting ideas from different parts of a
document will expose the limits of retrieving a small, fixed number of chunks
from a single search.
These limitations are not bugs. They are the natural boundaries of the
simplest possible RAG design. They are also exactly what motivate the
--- PAGE 28 ---
advanced techniques covered in later chapters: better chunking strategies,
multi-query retrieval, reranking, and query transformation.
When to Use This
Best for:
Answering factual questions about a single document or small collection
Building a quick prototype to validate that RAG helps your use case
Internal knowledge bases with straightforward lookup queries
Situations where simplicity and low cost matter more than perfect
accuracy
Overkill when:
Your documents are short enough to fit entirely in the model’s context
window
You only need keyword search (traditional search engines work fine)
You never update your document collection and could pre-compute all
answers
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency Low: one embedding call + one vector search +
one LLM call
Complexity Low: fewest moving parts of any RAG
approach
Cost Low: embedding is cheap, only one LLM call
per query
Accuracy gain Moderate: strong for focused factual queries,
weak for multi-hop or complex questions
Compared to using an LLM without RAG: Simple RAG grounds the
model’s answers in your actual documents, reducing hallucination and
enabling responses about content the model never saw during training. The
tradeoff is that answer quality depends entirely on retrieval quality. If the
wrong chunks are retrieved, the answer will be wrong or incomplete. And
--- PAGE 29 ---
with a fixed chunk size and a single retrieval strategy, simple RAG has limited
ability to handle nuanced or multi-part questions.
Key Takeaways
RAG adds a “look it up” step before the language model answers,
grounding responses in actual source material instead of training
memory.
Documents are split into overlapping chunks to ensure no information is
lost at the boundaries between pieces.
Embeddings convert text into numerical vectors where similar meanings
are placed close together, enabling search by meaning rather than
keywords.
A vector store organizes these vectors for fast similarity search, even
across millions of chunks.
Simple RAG is the foundation that all advanced techniques build upon.
Its limitations, including fixed chunk sizes, single-strategy retrieval, and
no query refinement, are exactly what the rest of this book addresses.
--- PAGE 30 ---
Chapter 2: RAG for Structured Data: When
Spreadsheets Meet Semantic Search
Adapting the RAG pipeline to work with tabular data like CSV files,
where rows are records and columns are fields.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (Simple RAG). Understanding of how embeddings,
vector stores, and similarity search work.
The Problem
In the previous chapter, we built a RAG pipeline for prose documents. The
system splits continuous text into chunks, embeds them, and retrieves the
most relevant pieces to answer a question. But not all data lives in
documents. A huge amount of business-critical information lives in
spreadsheets and databases: customer records, product catalogs, transaction
logs, employee directories.
Suppose your company maintains a customer database with hundreds of
records. Each record has a name, company, city, country, phone number,
email, and subscription date. A sales manager asks: “Which customers are
based in South American countries?” With a traditional database, you would
need a precise query that lists every South American country by name. Miss
one, and you miss those customers. And what about a fuzzier question like
“Find me customers at companies that sound like they might be in the
consulting business”? No SQL query can answer that, because it requires
understanding what company names imply, not what they exactly match.
The standard RAG pipeline from chapter 1 was designed for running text.
If you try to split a CSV file the same way, treating it as one long string and
chopping it into character-based chunks, you will slice rows in half, mix fields
from different records, and produce chunks that are meaningless. The
illustration below shows this: a table of customer records being blindly
--- PAGE 31 ---
chopped by character count, with rows split mid-record and fields from
different customers jumbled together in a single chunk.
A spreadsheet table on the left is blindly sliced into text chunks on the right, with rows cut in half and
fields from different records mixed together in each chunk, marked with red X symbols.
The Core Idea
The solution is straightforward: treat each row as its own document. Instead
of splitting text by character count, you use the natural boundaries already
present in the data. Every row in a CSV is a self-contained record. Convert
each row into a readable text description, embed it, and store it in a vector
store. Now you can ask natural language questions about your structured data
and retrieve the most relevant records by meaning.
Think of it as giving each row a voice. A row in a spreadsheet is silent data:
columns and values, no context, no narrative. By converting it into a sentence
or paragraph, you let the embedding model understand what the row is about,
not just what it contains. The image below shows a single row of customer
--- PAGE 32 ---
data being transformed into a readable text card, which then maps to a dot in
meaning space alongside other customer records.
A three-stage transformation: a highlighted spreadsheet row labeled ‘Row’ on the left, a warm orange
text card labeled ‘Text’ in the center, and a glowing dot on a coordinate grid labeled ‘Vector’ on the right,
connected by curved arrows.
The key insight: structured data already has natural chunk boundaries.
Each row is a complete unit of information. Use that structure instead
of fighting it.
How It Works
The pipeline follows the same two phases from chapter 1, preparation and
query, but the preparation phase handles tabular data differently.
Phase 1: Preparing the Knowledge Base
--- PAGE 33 ---
Step 1: Load the Structured Data
The process begins by reading the CSV file and parsing it into individual
records. Unlike a PDF, where you get one continuous stream of text, a CSV
gives you cleanly separated rows with labeled columns. Each row is a discrete
unit: one customer, one product, one transaction.
This is a significant advantage. With prose documents, figuring out where
one concept ends and another begins is a real challenge (as we saw with
chunking in chapter 1). With tabular data, the boundaries are already defined
for you.
Step 2: Convert Each Row to Text
This is the step that makes the whole approach work. Each row of
structured data must be converted from a set of field-value pairs into a
human-readable text string. This process is called field serialization.
Consider a customer record with fields like first name, last name,
company, city, and country. The system converts this into something like:
“First Name: Sheryl. Last Name: Baxter. Company: Rasmussen Group. City:
East Leonard. Country: Chile.” This text representation preserves all the
information from the original row, but in a format that an embedding model
can process and understand semantically.
Think of it as translating a form into a paragraph. A filled-out form has the
right information, but it is structured for a computer. The paragraph version
says the same thing, but now a language model (or a human) can read it,
understand context, and make inferences. The illustration below shows this
transformation: a rigid table row with cell values on the left turns into a
flowing text card on the right, with the same information expressed as
readable text.
--- PAGE 34 ---
A rigid spreadsheet grid fragment labeled ‘Grid’ on the left transforms through an arrow with a gear
icon into a friendly rounded card labeled ‘Card’ on the right, showing the same information reorganized
as readable labeled lines.
Step 3: Embed Each Record
Once every row has been converted to text, each text representation is
embedded into a vector, exactly as described in chapter 1. The difference is
that each vector now represents a complete record rather than a passage of
prose. A customer record about someone in Chile will have a vector near
other records from South American countries, even if the word “South
America” never appears in the data.
Step 4: Store in a Vector Database
All record vectors are stored in a vector store for fast similarity search.
Since each record is already a complete, self-contained unit, there is no need
for the overlap strategy that chapter 1 used between chunks. No information
falls between the cracks because there are no cracks. Every row stands on its
own. The diagram below summarizes the full preparation pipeline: a CSV file
is parsed into rows, each row is serialized into text, each text is embedded
into a vector, and all vectors are stored in the database.
--- PAGE 35 ---
A horizontal pipeline showing a CSV spreadsheet on the left, then individual rows separated out, each
row converting to a text card, each card converting to a dot, and all dots flowing into a cylindrical
vector store on the right. Stages are connected by arrows.
Algorithm Sketch: CSV Preparation
1. Load CSV file and parse into rows
2. For each row, serialize all fields into a text string
3. Embed each text string into a vector
4. Store all vectors in a vector store
Phase 2: Answering a Query
The query phase works the same as in chapter 1. A natural language question
is embedded into a vector, the vector store returns the nearest records, and
those records are passed to the language model as context. The model reads
the retrieved customer records and answers the question.
What makes this powerful is the kinds of questions you can now ask.
“Which customers are based in Caribbean nations?” does not require you to
list every Caribbean country. The embedding model understands that Antigua
and Barbuda, for example, is a Caribbean nation, even though the word
--- PAGE 36 ---
“Caribbean” never appears in the data. “Find customers whose companies
sound like they work in group consulting” retrieves records with company
names like “Rasmussen Group” because the embedding captures the
semantic similarity.
This is the real value of applying RAG to structured data. Traditional
database queries require exact matches or carefully constructed filters.
Semantic search understands meaning, inference, and fuzzy relationships.
The illustration below shows this contrast side by side: a traditional query
that requires exact country names and misses matches, versus a semantic
search that understands “South American countries” and finds all relevant
records.
A split-screen comparison. Left side labeled “Exact Query” shows a search box with specific country
names and only two results found. Right side labeled “Semantic Search” shows a natural language
question and five results found, with a lightbulb icon indicating understanding.
--- PAGE 37 ---
Algorithm Sketch: CSV Query Processing
1. Receive natural language question
2. Embed the question into a vector
3. Search vector store for nearest record vectors
4. Retrieve the corresponding text representations
5. Pass question + retrieved records to the language model
6. Model generates answer based on the matching records
How Field Serialization Affects Quality
The way you convert rows to text matters more than you might expect. A
naive approach might just concatenate values: “Sheryl Baxter Rasmussen
Group East Leonard Chile.” This loses the relationship between fields and
values. Is “Rasmussen Group” a company or a city? Is “Chile” a country or a
person’s name?
A better approach includes field names: “First Name: Sheryl. Last Name:
Baxter. Company: Rasmussen Group. City: East Leonard. Country: Chile.”
Now the embedding model understands the structure. It knows “Chile” is a
country, and it can connect that to questions about South American nations.
The most sophisticated approach might construct natural sentences:
“Sheryl Baxter works at Rasmussen Group, located in East Leonard, Chile.”
This reads more naturally and may produce slightly better embeddings, but it
requires more effort to construct and is harder to automate across different
table schemas.
Each approach represents a tradeoff between simplicity and semantic
richness. For most use cases, including field names with values provides the
best balance. The illustration below shows these three approaches side by
side: raw values with no labels, labeled fields, and a natural language
sentence, with quality improving from left to right.
--- PAGE 38 ---
Three cards arranged left to right with increasing quality. Left card labeled ‘Raw’ with a gray border
and messy marks, one star below. Middle card labeled ‘Labeled’ with a blue border and organized rows,
three stars. Right card labeled ‘Natural’ with an orange border and flowing text lines, four stars. A
horizontal arrow below points from left to right.
Handling Large Tables
A customer database with a hundred records is easy. But what happens with a
hundred thousand rows? Each row still becomes one vector, so the vector
store grows linearly. This is where the efficiency of the vector store matters.
Databases like FAISS are designed to handle millions of vectors with sub-
second search times, so the approach scales well in terms of retrieval speed.
The bigger concern is embedding cost. Every row needs to be embedded,
and embedding API calls are charged per token. A table with 100,000 rows
and 10 fields per row will require significantly more embedding calls than a
single document. The preparation phase is a one-time cost, but it is worth
considering for very large datasets.
When to Use This
--- PAGE 39 ---
Best for:
Customer, product, or employee databases where users ask natural
language questions
Records where the meaning of fields matters more than exact values
(company names, locations, descriptions)
Situations where users do not know the exact terms in the data and need
fuzzy, semantic matching
Prototyping a Q&A system over structured data before investing in a
custom query interface
Overkill when:
Your queries are exact lookups (e.g., “find customer with ID 12345”), a
traditional database query is simpler and faster
The data has only numeric fields with no semantic content (e.g., raw
sensor readings)
You need aggregation queries (“what is the average subscription date?”),
which require computation, not retrieval
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency Low: same as simple RAG, one embedding +
one search + one LLM call
Complexity Low: simpler than document RAG because no
chunking decisions needed
Cost Moderate: one embedding per row, can add up
for very large tables
Accuracy gain High for semantic queries, no benefit for exact-
match queries
Compared to simple RAG on documents:
The pipeline is nearly identical, but structured data removes the hardest
part of document RAG: chunking. You never need to decide on chunk size or
overlap because each row is already a natural, self-contained unit. The
tradeoff is that this approach only works for data that fits the row-per-record
pattern. Mixed-format data or data with complex relationships between rows
may need a different strategy.
--- PAGE 40 ---
Key Takeaways
Structured data like CSV files already has natural chunk boundaries: each
row is one record, one document, one chunk.
Field serialization, converting structured fields into readable text, is the
critical step that enables embedding models to understand tabular data.
Including field names in the serialized text significantly improves
retrieval quality by preserving the relationship between labels and
values.
Semantic search on structured data lets users ask fuzzy, meaning-based
questions that traditional exact-match queries cannot handle.
The approach scales well for retrieval speed but requires one embedding
per row, which can become costly for very large datasets.
--- PAGE 41 ---
Chapter 3: Reliable RAG: Building Trust Through
Verification
Adding quality checkpoints to the RAG pipeline so the system filters
irrelevant evidence, catches hallucinated answers, and traces every
claim back to its source.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (Simple RAG). Understanding of the basic retrieval
and generation pipeline.
The Problem
The basic RAG pipeline retrieves chunks and passes them to the language
model. But there is a problem we glossed over: retrieval is not perfect.
Similarity search returns the closest chunks by meaning, but “closest” does
not always mean “relevant.”
Imagine a user asks about agentic design patterns in AI. The system
retrieves four chunks from a collection of articles. Three contain useful
information about specific patterns like reflection, tool use, and planning. But
the fourth chunk is a page footer stuffed with navigation links, subscription
prompts, and generic calls to action. It scored well in similarity search
because it happened to mention the topic in passing, but it contains no
substantive information. The language model now has three good sources
and one piece of noise. At best, the noise is ignored. At worst, it dilutes the
answer or introduces irrelevant information.
And there is a second, deeper problem. Even when all the retrieved chunks
are perfectly relevant, the language model can still hallucinate. It might
generate a claim that sounds plausible but appears nowhere in the provided
context. The user reads the answer, trusts it because they know the system
“looks things up,” and makes a decision based on fabricated information. This
is arguably more dangerous than hallucination without RAG, because the
user has a false sense of security. The illustration below shows both failure
--- PAGE 42 ---
modes side by side: irrelevant chunks sneaking into the context, and the
model adding unsupported claims to its answer.
A four-stage pipeline labeled ‘Query’, ‘Search’, ‘Chunks’, ‘Answer’. The Chunks stage shows four
document rectangles, three with green checkmarks and one with a red X. The Answer stage shows a
card with a red-highlighted bottom line and warning triangle, representing a fabricated claim.
The Core Idea
Reliable RAG adds verification checkpoints to the basic pipeline. Instead of
blindly trusting every retrieved chunk and every generated answer, the
system stops at critical points and asks: “Is this actually good?”
Think of it as adding quality control to a factory assembly line. A basic
factory takes raw materials, runs them through machines, and ships the
product. A reliable factory does the same thing, but adds inspection stations
along the way. One inspector checks incoming materials before they enter
production. Another inspector examines the finished product before it leaves
the building. A third inspector traces the product back to its source materials
--- PAGE 43 ---
for accountability. The illustration below shows this: a factory line with three
inspection checkpoints, each catching a different type of problem.
A factory assembly line with three quality inspection stations. The first station checks incoming
materials and rejects bad ones, the second station examines the finished product, and the third station
traces the product back to source materials with connecting lines.
The core insight: retrieval gives you evidence, but not all evidence is
good. Generation gives you answers, but not all answers are faithful.
Reliable RAG adds the judgment layer that simple RAG is missing.
How It Works
Reliable RAG wraps three verification steps around the basic pipeline from
chapter 1. The retrieval and generation steps remain unchanged. What
changes is what happens between and after them.
--- PAGE 44 ---
Step 1: Retrieve Candidate Documents
The user’s question is embedded and searched against the vector store,
exactly as in chapter 1. The system retrieves a set of candidate chunks,
typically four or more. The word “candidate” matters. In simple RAG,
whatever comes back from the vector store goes straight to the language
model. In reliable RAG, retrieved chunks must pass an inspection before they
are used.
Step 2: Grade Each Document for Relevancy
This is the first verification checkpoint. A language model examines each
retrieved chunk individually and asks a simple question: “Is this chunk
relevant to the user’s question?”
The grading is intentionally lenient. The grader is not looking for a perfect
match. It checks for keyword overlap or semantic connection to the question.
The goal is to filter out obvious noise: pages that scored well in vector search
but contain no useful information about the topic. A chunk that mentions the
topic even briefly passes. A chunk that is entirely off-topic or contains only
boilerplate content does not.
Think of this as a bouncer at a venue entrance. The bouncer is not judging
quality or ranking guests. They are checking one thing: do you belong here or
not? Most guests get in. But the ones wearing the wrong badge, the
navigation menus, the subscription prompts, the unrelated tangents, get
turned away at the door. The following illustration shows retrieved chunks
lining up at a checkpoint, where each is quickly judged as relevant or not.
--- PAGE 45 ---
Four document chunks approach a checkpoint gate. A guard examines each one. Three chunks glow
green and pass through with checkmarks. One chunk glows red and is turned away with an X mark.
The green chunks continue forward along an arrow.
The result is a filtered set of documents. Only the chunks that pass relevancy
grading move forward to the generation step. This simple filter can
meaningfully improve answer quality because it removes noise that would
otherwise distract or mislead the language model.
Algorithm Sketch: Relevancy Grading
1. For each retrieved chunk:
2.   Send chunk and original question to a grading LLM
3.   Ask: "Is this chunk relevant to the question? Yes or no."
4.   If yes, keep the chunk
5.   If no, discard it
6. Pass only the relevant chunks to the generation step
Step 3: Generate an Answer
--- PAGE 46 ---
The language model receives only the filtered, relevant chunks along with the
original question. This step is identical to the generation step in simple RAG,
but the input is cleaner. With the noise removed, the model has a better
signal-to-noise ratio and produces a more focused, accurate answer.
Step 4: Check for Hallucinations
This is the second verification checkpoint, and it addresses the deeper
problem. Even with perfect context, language models can generate
information that is not present in the source material. The hallucination
checker compares the generated answer against the retrieved documents and
asks: “Is every claim in this answer supported by the facts provided?”
This is a fundamentally different question from relevancy grading.
Relevancy grading asks “Is this chunk useful for the question?” Hallucination
checking asks “Is this answer faithful to the chunks?” One looks backward at
the inputs. The other looks forward at the output.
Think of it as a fact-checker at a newspaper. The journalist writes the
article based on interview transcripts and source documents. The fact-checker
reads the article alongside those same sources and verifies that every
statement can be traced back to one of them. If the journalist added a claim
that appears in no source, the fact-checker flags it. The illustration below
shows this process: a generated answer is compared line by line against the
source documents, with supported claims marked as verified and an
unsupported claim flagged.
--- PAGE 47 ---
A generated answer text on the left with three statements. Two statements have green checkmarks and
dotted lines connecting them to matching passages in source documents on the right. One statement has
a red flag icon with no connecting line, indicating it is not grounded in any source.
The hallucination checker returns a simple binary judgment: grounded or not
grounded. In a production system, an answer flagged as not grounded could
trigger several responses. The system might regenerate the answer with
stricter instructions, try a different set of retrieved chunks, or return the
answer with a warning label. The appropriate response depends on the
application. In a medical or legal context, an ungrounded answer should be
blocked entirely. In a casual search tool, a warning might be enough.
Algorithm Sketch: Hallucination Checking
1. Receive the generated answer
2. Receive the set of documents used for generation
3. Send both to a grading LLM
4. Ask: "Is this answer grounded in the provided facts? Yes or no."
5. If yes, proceed to source highlighting
6. If no, flag or regenerate the answer
--- PAGE 48 ---
Step 5: Highlight Source Segments
The final verification step closes the trust loop by tracing the answer back to
its sources. A language model examines the generated answer alongside the
source documents and identifies the exact text segments that support each
claim in the answer. These are not paraphrases or summaries. They are
verbatim excerpts from the source documents, along with references to which
document they came from.
This serves two purposes. First, it gives the user a way to verify the answer
themselves. They can read the original passage in context and decide whether
the answer accurately represents the source. Second, it acts as a final quality
signal. If the system cannot find supporting segments for parts of the answer,
that is a red flag that something may have slipped past the hallucination
checker.
Think of it as adding footnotes to a research paper. Every claim is
annotated with a specific citation that points to the exact passage in the
source material. The reader does not have to take the author’s word for it.
They can check. The illustration below shows an answer with highlighted
claims, each connected by a line to the specific text passage in the source
document that supports it.
--- PAGE 49 ---
A citation diagram labeled ‘Answer’ on the left and ‘Sources’ on the right. The answer card has three
color-coded bands (blue, orange, green) with small circled numbers 1, 2, 3. Two source document cards
on the right have matching colored bands. Thin curved lines connect matching colors between answer
and sources.
Algorithm Sketch: Source Highlighting
1. Receive the generated answer and the source documents
2. Send both to a language model
3. Ask: "Which exact segments from the documents support this answer?"
4. Extract verbatim text segments from each relevant document
5. Return segments with document IDs and source references
6. Present answer to user with linked source attributions
The Complete Pipeline
Putting all the steps together, the reliable RAG pipeline looks like the basic
pipeline from chapter 1 with three verification checkpoints inserted at critical
junctures. After retrieval, the relevancy grader filters the candidate chunks.
After generation, the hallucination checker verifies groundedness. After
verification, the source highlighter traces claims back to exact passages. The
--- PAGE 50 ---
diagram below shows the complete flow from question to verified, attributed
answer.
A horizontal pipeline diagram showing the full reliable RAG flow. Starting with a question on the left,
then retrieve, then a checkpoint labeled “Grade” that filters chunks, then generate, then a checkpoint
labeled “Verify” that checks groundedness, then a checkpoint labeled “Trace” that highlights sources,
ending with a verified answer on the right with source citations.
Choosing Models for Verification
An important practical detail: the verification steps do not need to use the
same large, expensive model used for final answer generation. Relevancy
grading and hallucination checking are binary classification tasks. They ask
simple yes-or-no questions. A smaller, faster model handles these well.
Source highlighting requires more nuance, as the model must identify exact
text matches, but even this can use a mid-sized model.
This layered approach to model selection keeps costs manageable. Use a
small model for the high-volume grading steps (one call per retrieved chunk),
a small model for the single hallucination check, and reserve the most
capable model for the final generation. In practice, the verification steps add
--- PAGE 51 ---
two to four extra LLM calls per query, but the per-call cost can be kept low by
choosing efficient models for each task.
When to Use This
Best for:
Applications where answer accuracy is critical (legal, medical, financial)
Systems serving non-expert users who cannot independently verify
answers
Production RAG systems that need to maintain user trust over time
Any context where “I don’t know” is better than a confident wrong
answer
Overkill when:
You are building a casual Q&A tool where approximate answers are
acceptable
Your document corpus is small and highly focused, making irrelevant
retrievals unlikely
Latency is your primary concern and every millisecond matters
You are in early prototyping and need fast iteration over reliability
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency
High: adds 2-3 extra LLM calls per query for
grading, hallucination check, and source
highlighting
Complexity Medium: three additional verification steps on
top of simple RAG
Cost
Moderate: multiple LLM calls per query,
though verification can use smaller, cheaper
models
Accuracy gain Significant: filters noise, catches hallucinations,
provides source attribution
Compared to simple RAG:
--- PAGE 52 ---
Simple RAG trusts the retrieval and generation steps completely. Reliable
RAG adds a trust-but-verify approach. The cost is additional latency and LLM
calls. The benefit is a system that filters bad evidence, catches fabricated
claims, and lets users trace every answer back to its source. For applications
where accuracy matters more than speed, this tradeoff is almost always worth
making. The diagram below shows this contrast: a simple pipeline that passes
everything through unchecked versus a reliable pipeline with verification
gates at each stage.
A split-screen comparison. Top half labeled “Simple RAG” shows a straight pipeline from question to
retrieve to generate to answer with no checkpoints. Bottom half labeled “Reliable RAG” shows the same
pipeline but with three diamond-shaped checkpoint gates between stages, each labeled Grade, Verify,
and Trace.
Key Takeaways
Not every chunk returned by vector search is relevant. A relevancy
grading step filters out noise before it reaches the language model.
--- PAGE 53 ---
Language models can hallucinate even when given good context. A
hallucination checker verifies that the generated answer is grounded in
the provided facts.
Source highlighting traces every claim in the answer back to exact text
segments in the source documents, giving users a way to verify for
themselves.
These three verification steps, relevancy grading, hallucination checking,
and source highlighting, transform a basic RAG pipeline into a system
that earns user trust.
The tradeoff is additional latency and cost from extra LLM calls. Using
smaller, faster models for verification steps keeps this cost manageable.
--- PAGE 54 ---
Chapter 4: Proposition Chunking: Breaking
Documents Into Atomic Facts
Decomposing text into self-contained, single-fact statements so retrieval
returns precise answers instead of noisy paragraphs.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (Simple RAG). Understanding of chunking,
embeddings, and vector search.
The Problem
In chapter 1, we split documents into chunks of roughly a thousand
characters and embedded each chunk as a single vector. This works well for
broad, thematic questions. But consider what happens when a user asks a
very specific factual question.
Suppose your knowledge base contains an article about the history of
space exploration. One chunk might read: “Neil Armstrong walked on the
Moon in 1969 during the Apollo 11 mission. The mission launched from
Kennedy Space Center in Florida. Buzz Aldrin joined Armstrong on the lunar
surface, while Michael Collins orbited above in the command module.” That
is three distinct facts packed into a single chunk: who walked on the Moon,
where the mission launched from, and who stayed in orbit.
Now a user asks: “Who stayed in the command module during Apollo 11?”
The system retrieves the chunk because it is semantically relevant. But the
answer to the question is just one sentence buried inside a paragraph about
several different facts. The language model must sift through the surrounding
information to find the relevant needle. Worse, the other facts in the chunk
dilute the embedding. The chunk’s vector represents a blend of all three
topics, not any single one precisely. This means a question about launch sites
might also retrieve this same chunk, even though only one sentence is
relevant. The following illustration shows this: a single chunk containing
multiple facts, with a specific question only matching one of them.
--- PAGE 55 ---
A text chunk containing three distinct facts highlighted in blue, orange, and green. A magnifying glass
with a question targets only the orange fact, but the entire chunk is retrieved, with the blue and green
facts shown as noise.
The core issue is granularity. Character-based chunking does not care about
the boundaries between ideas. It slices text at arbitrary points, grouping
unrelated facts together and sometimes splitting a single thought across two
chunks. The result is chunks that are semantically “blurry,” representing a
mix of topics rather than one clear idea.
The Core Idea
Proposition chunking takes a fundamentally different approach to
granularity. Instead of splitting text by character count, it uses a language
model to decompose each chunk into individual propositions: atomic, self-
contained statements that each express exactly one fact.
A proposition has five properties. It expresses a single fact. It can be
understood without any surrounding context. It uses full names instead of
--- PAGE 56 ---
pronouns. It includes relevant dates and qualifiers. And it contains one
subject doing or being one thing.
Think of it as the difference between a paragraph in a textbook and a stack
of flashcards made from that paragraph. The paragraph weaves ideas together
into flowing prose. The flashcards isolate each fact onto its own card. When
you search through flashcards, you find exactly the fact you need. When you
search through paragraphs, you find the neighborhood where the fact lives,
along with everything else in that neighborhood. The illustration below
shows a paragraph being decomposed into individual fact cards, each standing
on its own.
A flowing paragraph of text on the left is broken apart by arrows into five separate rectangular fact
cards on the right, each containing a single short statement. The cards are neatly stacked with small
gaps between them.
The core insight: standard chunks are containers of mixed facts.
Propositions are individual facts extracted from those containers. By
embedding individual facts, you make retrieval as precise as the
questions being asked.
--- PAGE 57 ---
How It Works
Proposition chunking wraps around the preparation phase from chapter 1.
The document is still chunked initially, but those chunks are then refined
into propositions before embedding. The query phase remains unchanged.
Step 1: Initial Chunking
The process begins with standard text splitting, just as in chapter 1. The
document is divided into manageable chunks. These initial chunks are not
the final unit of storage. They serve as input for the next step. Keeping them
at a moderate size ensures the language model can process each one in a
single call without exceeding its context window.
Step 2: Generate Propositions From Each Chunk
This is the step that defines the technique. A language model reads each
chunk and breaks it down into a list of propositions. The model follows
specific rules for what makes a valid proposition.
Consider a chunk that reads: “Graham suggests that founders should
leverage their strengths rather than conform to traditional managerial
practices. Founder Mode is an emerging paradigm that is not yet fully
documented.” The language model decomposes this into individual
statements:
“Graham suggests that founders should leverage their strengths.”
“Founders should not conform to traditional managerial practices,
according to Graham.”
“Founder Mode is an emerging paradigm.”
“Founder Mode is not yet fully documented.”
Notice what happened. The original two sentences became four propositions.
Each one states a single fact. Each one makes sense on its own. Pronouns like
“their” have been resolved to “founders.” Compound sentences with multiple
claims have been split into separate statements. The diagram below shows
this decomposition process: a chunk entering the language model and
emerging as a list of individual propositions.
--- PAGE 58 ---
A rectangular text chunk enters a box labeled with a brain icon representing the language model. From
the box, four separate smaller cards emerge on the right, each containing a single short statement.
Arrows flow left to right showing the decomposition.
The language model is guided by a prompt that specifies the five criteria for
valid propositions: express a single fact, be self-contained, use full names,
include dates and qualifiers, and contain one subject-predicate relationship.
Few-shot examples in the prompt demonstrate the expected output format,
showing the model what good decomposition looks like before it processes
the actual text.
Algorithm Sketch: Proposition Generation
1. Split document into initial text chunks
2. For each chunk:
3.   Send chunk to a language model with decomposition instructions
4.   Receive a list of atomic propositions
5.   Each proposition: one fact, self-contained, no pronouns, includes qualifiers
6. Collect all propositions across all chunks
Step 3: Quality Check Each Proposition
--- PAGE 59 ---
Not every proposition that comes out of the generation step will be good
enough. Language models sometimes produce statements that are vague,
inaccurate, or missing important context. A second language model acts as a
quality gate, evaluating each proposition on four criteria.
Accuracy: Does the proposition faithfully reflect what the original text
said? A score of 1 to 10.
Clarity: Can someone understand this proposition without reading
anything else? A score of 1 to 10.
Completeness: Does the proposition include the necessary details like
dates, names, and qualifiers? A score of 1 to 10.
Conciseness: Is the proposition focused and free of unnecessary words?
A score of 1 to 10.
Each criterion has a threshold, typically around 7 out of 10. A proposition
must meet the threshold on all four criteria to pass. Those that fail are
discarded. This prevents low-quality statements from polluting the vector
store with noisy or misleading embeddings.
Think of this as peer review for individual facts. The first model writes the
propositions, and the second model reviews them. Only statements that are
accurate, clear, complete, and concise survive the review process. The
illustration below shows propositions passing through a quality gate, with
scores displayed for each criterion.
--- PAGE 60 ---
Several proposition cards approach a checkpoint labeled “Quality Gate.” Each card shows four small
score bars for accuracy, clarity, completeness, and conciseness. Cards with all bars above the threshold
line glow green and pass through. One card with a bar below the threshold glows red and is rejected.
This two-model approach, one for generation and one for evaluation, creates
a checks-and-balances system. The generator does not need to be perfect. It
just needs to produce candidates. The evaluator handles quality control. This
separation of concerns is similar in spirit to the relevancy grading step from
chapter 3, but applied earlier in the pipeline, during preparation rather than
at query time.
Algorithm Sketch: Proposition Quality Check
1. For each generated proposition:
2.   Send proposition and its source chunk to a grading LLM
3.   Receive scores for accuracy, clarity, completeness, conciseness
4.   If all scores meet the threshold (e.g., 7/10), keep the proposition
5.   If any score falls below threshold, discard it
6. Only quality-approved propositions proceed to embedding
Step 4: Embed and Store Propositions
--- PAGE 61 ---
The propositions that pass quality checking are embedded into vectors and
stored in a vector store, exactly as described in chapter 1. The crucial
difference is what each vector represents. In standard RAG, each vector
