4. Run vector search independently for each sub-query
5. Collect all retrieved chunks
6. Deduplicate overlapping results
7. Pass original query + combined chunks to the generation model
8. Generate comprehensive answer covering all facets
--- PAGE 77 ---
Choosing the Right Strategy
The three strategies are not interchangeable. Each one fits a different type of
query problem.
Query rewriting works best when the user’s question is on the right
track but too vague. The user knows what they want but has not expressed it
in terms that match the knowledge base well. Rewriting sharpens the query
without changing its fundamental direction.
Step-back prompting works best when the question is specific but the
answer requires foundational context that the user did not ask for. Medical
questions, legal questions, and technical questions often benefit from this
approach because the specific answer only makes sense in the context of
broader background knowledge.
Sub-query decomposition works best when the question is inherently
multi-faceted. Questions that contain “and” or ask about multiple aspects of a
topic are strong candidates. If a single search cannot cover all the ground,
decomposition ensures nothing is missed.
In many real-world systems, these techniques are not used in isolation. A
pipeline might first decompose a complex question into sub-queries, then
rewrite each sub-query for specificity. Or it might run both a step-back search
and a rewritten search in parallel and merge the results. The techniques
compose naturally because they all operate on the same principle: transform
the query before retrieval. The following illustration shows the three
strategies side by side, each applied to the same type of problem, with the
kind of query each handles best.
--- PAGE 78 ---
Three vertical panels side by side. Left panel labeled ‘Rewrite’ shows a small circle becoming a larger
circle via an arrow with a magnifying glass icon, tagged ‘Vague’. Middle panel labeled ‘Step Back’ shows
a circle expanding into concentric circles, tagged ‘Context’. Right panel labeled ‘Split’ shows one circle
branching into three smaller circles with a scissors icon, tagged ‘Complex’.
How Query Transformations Fit the Bigger Picture
Query transformations work at a different point in the pipeline than the
techniques from earlier chapters. Proposition chunking (chapter 4) improves
what gets stored. Relevancy grading (chapter 3) improves what gets passed to
the model after retrieval. Query transformations improve what gets searched
for in the first place.
These approaches are complementary, not competing. You can use
proposition chunking to store precise facts, query rewriting to sharpen the
search, relevancy grading to filter the results, and hallucination checking to
verify the answer. Each technique addresses a different failure mode in the
pipeline. The illustration below positions query transformations at the start
of the RAG pipeline, showing where they operate compared to the techniques
from previous chapters.
--- PAGE 79 ---
A horizontal RAG pipeline from left to right. The first stage, highlighted in warm orange, is labeled
“Transform” and sits before the “Search” stage. Later stages labeled “Grade” and “Verify” appear after
retrieval and generation, shown in muted blue. The highlighted “Transform” stage has a callout
emphasizing that it reshapes the query before retrieval happens.
When to Use This
Best for:
Systems where users ask vague or underspecified questions that need
sharpening
Knowledge bases covering broad topics where a single search misses
important facets
Complex queries that contain multiple parts or require background
context
Situations where retrieval quality is inconsistent due to vocabulary
mismatch between queries and documents
Overkill when:
--- PAGE 80 ---
Your users already ask specific, well-formed questions
Your knowledge base is small and focused enough that simple retrieval
works well
Latency is critical and the extra language model call for transformation is
too slow
Your retrieval already returns consistently relevant results
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency
Moderate: adds one LLM call for
transformation; decomposition adds multiple
retrieval calls
Complexity Low: each technique is a single prompt
template applied before the existing pipeline
Cost Low to moderate: one extra LLM call per
query; decomposition multiplies retrieval calls
Accuracy gain Significant for vague or complex queries;
minimal for already well-formed queries
Compared to searching with the raw query:
Query transformations add a thin intelligence layer between the user and
the vector store. The cost is one additional language model call before
retrieval begins. The benefit is better retrieval coverage, especially for vague,
complex, or multi-faceted questions. For systems where query quality varies
widely, transformations provide a consistent quality floor that catches poorly
framed questions before they produce poor results. For systems where users
already ask precise questions, the improvement may not justify the added
latency.
Key Takeaways
The user’s raw query is often not the best query for retrieval. Vague,
overly specific, or multi-faceted questions all lead to suboptimal search
results.
--- PAGE 81 ---
Query rewriting sharpens a vague question into a more specific, detailed
form that better matches the language of the knowledge base.
Step-back prompting broadens a specific question to retrieve
foundational context that supports a more thorough answer.
Sub-query decomposition splits a complex question into focused sub-
questions, each retrieving results that cover a different facet of the
original question.
These techniques operate before retrieval, complementing post-retrieval
improvements like relevancy grading and hallucination checking from
earlier chapters.
--- PAGE 82 ---
Chapter 6: Hypothetical Document Embedding
Instead of searching with the question, search with a made-up answer.
The fake answer finds the real one.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline),
Chapter 5 (query transformations)
The Problem
Imagine you are searching a knowledge base about climate science. You type:
“How do greenhouse gases trap heat?” The system embeds your seven-word
question, searches the vector store, and returns mediocre results. One chunk
discusses greenhouse gas emissions statistics. Another covers heat waves in
urban areas. A third mentions trapped gases in geological formations. None of
them directly explains the mechanism you asked about.
The frustrating part is that a perfect chunk exists in the database. It is a
detailed paragraph explaining how carbon dioxide and methane absorb
infrared radiation, re-emit it in all directions, and gradually warm the lower
atmosphere. But the embedding of your short question and the embedding of
that detailed explanation sit in different neighborhoods of meaning space.
This is the asymmetry problem. Questions and answers are structurally
different. A question is short, open-ended, and phrased as something
unknown. A document chunk is long, declarative, and phrased as something
known. Even when they are about the exact same topic, their embeddings can
end up surprisingly far apart. A seven-word question and a five-hundred-
character explanation encode different patterns in the embedding model,
even though they are about the same concept. The following diagram
illustrates this gap: a short query sits alone in one region of meaning space
while the relevant document chunks cluster together in another.
--- PAGE 83 ---
A short query shown as a small dot sitting far from a cluster of document chunks in meaning space,
illustrating the structural distance between questions and answers even when they share the same topic
Chapter 5 introduced query transformations as a way to improve retrieval by
reshaping the question. Rewriting sharpens a vague query, step-back
broadening adds context, and decomposition splits complex questions into
smaller ones. But all three strategies still search with a question. The
fundamental asymmetry remains: you are comparing a question-shaped
embedding against document-shaped embeddings. What if you could search
with something that looks like a document instead?
The Core Idea
Hypothetical Document Embedding, or HyDE, flips the search strategy
on its head. Instead of embedding the question and searching for similar
documents, you first ask a language model to generate a hypothetical answer
to the question. Then you embed that hypothetical answer and use it as the
search query. Because the hypothetical answer is structurally similar to real
--- PAGE 84 ---
document chunks (same length, same declarative style, same level of detail),
its embedding lands much closer to the relevant chunks in meaning space.
The key insight is that the hypothetical document does not need to be
correct. It might contain inaccuracies, miss important details, or even get
some facts wrong. That is fine. Its job is not to be the answer. Its job is to be
in the right neighborhood of meaning space so that the real documents can be
found nearby.
Think of it like a police sketch artist. When a witness describes a suspect
(“tall, dark hair, scar on the left cheek”), the detective does not walk through
a crowd holding up a written description. Instead, a sketch artist draws a face
based on those words. The sketch is never a perfect likeness. The nose might
be slightly off, the jawline a bit too wide. But comparing a face to other faces
is far easier than comparing a verbal description to faces. HyDE works the
same way: you create an imperfect but structurally similar representation,
then use it to find the real match. The following illustration shows this
analogy in action.
--- PAGE 85 ---
A sketch artist drawing a portrait from a verbal description on the left, then that sketch being held up
next to a wall of photographs on the right, with one photograph highlighted as a match
--- PAGE 86 ---
How It Works
The HyDE pipeline adds one step to the beginning of the standard retrieval
process: generating a hypothetical document. Everything else, embedding,
search, and answer generation, stays the same.
Step 1: Receive the question. The user asks a question just as in
standard RAG. For example: “What role do oceans play in regulating Earth’s
temperature?”
Step 2: Generate a hypothetical answer. Instead of embedding the
question directly, you send it to a language model with a specific instruction:
“Given this question, write a detailed document that answers it. Make the
document approximately the same length as a typical chunk in the knowledge
base.” The model produces something like:
“Oceans play a critical role in temperature regulation through several
mechanisms. They absorb approximately 90% of excess heat from the
atmosphere due to water’s high specific heat capacity. Ocean currents like the
Gulf Stream redistribute thermal energy from equatorial regions toward the
poles, moderating temperatures across continents. Additionally, oceans serve
as a major carbon sink, absorbing roughly 30% of human-produced carbon
dioxide, which influences the rate of atmospheric warming. Evaporation
from ocean surfaces drives the water cycle, forming clouds that reflect
sunlight and further regulate surface temperatures.”
This hypothetical document is plausible but unverified. Some numbers
might be approximate. Some details might be incomplete. That does not
matter at this stage.
Step 3: Embed the hypothetical document. The system creates an
embedding vector for this generated text, not for the original question.
Because the hypothetical document shares the same length and style as real
chunks in the database, its embedding captures the same structural patterns.
The full pipeline flows from question to hypothetical document to embedding
to search to real chunks to final answer. The following diagram traces this
complete path.
--- PAGE 87 ---
A horizontal pipeline showing six stages connected by arrows: a question mark icon, then a brain icon
generating a document, then a glowing vector dot, then a cylindrical database, then three highlighted
document chunks, and finally a lightbulb producing an answer
Step 4: Search the vector store. Using the hypothetical document’s
embedding as the query, the system performs standard similarity search. The
crucial difference is what is being compared: instead of a question-shaped
vector looking for document-shaped vectors, you now have a document-
shaped vector looking for other document-shaped vectors. Like finds like.
Step 5: Retrieve the top-K real chunks. The vector store returns the
chunks whose embeddings are closest to the hypothetical document’s
embedding. These are real, verified chunks from your knowledge base.
Step 6: Generate the final answer. The retrieved real chunks (not the
hypothetical document) are passed to the language model as context. The
hypothetical document is discarded. It served its purpose as a bridge between
query space and document space. The original question, not the hypothetical
answer, is what the model actually answers using the real retrieved context.
--- PAGE 88 ---
Algorithm Sketch: HyDE Retrieval
1. Receive user question
2. Prompt LLM: "Write a hypothetical answer (~chunk_size characters)"
3. LLM generates hypothetical document
4. Embed the hypothetical document (not the question)
5. Search vector store using hypothetical embedding
6. Retrieve top-K real document chunks
7. Discard hypothetical document
8. Pass real chunks + original question to LLM for final answer
The reason this works lies in how embedding spaces are organized.
Embeddings cluster by topic and by structure. Two paragraphs about ocean
temperature regulation will sit close together even if they use different words,
because they share both meaning and form. A question about ocean
temperature, though topically related, has a different form (interrogative,
short, incomplete) and therefore sits farther away. By transforming the
question into a paragraph first, HyDE bridges the structural gap. The
following visualization shows the hypothetical document’s embedding
landing inside the cluster of relevant real documents, even though the
hypothetical content is not perfectly accurate.
--- PAGE 89 ---
A scatter plot in meaning space with a small blue dot labeled as the query sitting far from a cluster of
orange dots representing real documents, while a green dot representing the hypothetical document
lands right inside the orange cluster
Why Imperfect Is Good Enough
A natural concern is: what if the language model generates a wrong
hypothetical document? If the model hallucinates incorrect facts in the
hypothetical answer, will it retrieve the wrong chunks?
In practice, this is less of a problem than you might expect. The embedding
captures the topic and structure of the hypothetical document, not whether
individual facts are correct. A hypothetical document that says “oceans absorb
85% of excess heat” instead of the correct “93%” will still produce an
embedding very close to chunks about ocean heat absorption. The factual
error barely moves the needle in embedding space.
However, if the language model has no knowledge of the topic at all and
generates something completely off-base, HyDE will steer retrieval in the
wrong direction. This is the technique’s most important limitation. HyDE
works best when the language model has at least surface-level familiarity
--- PAGE 90 ---
with the domain, enough to produce a hypothetical document that lives in the
right region of meaning space.
Matching Chunk Size Matters
One practical detail deserves attention. The prompt that generates the
hypothetical document typically includes an instruction about length: “make
it approximately the same size as chunks in the knowledge base.” This is not
arbitrary. Embedding models encode length-dependent patterns. A fifty-word
hypothesis and a five-hundred-word chunk will produce embeddings with
different structural characteristics even if they discuss the same topic. By
matching the hypothetical document’s length to the actual chunk size, you
ensure the structural similarity that makes HyDE effective.
The following diagram compares direct query search to HyDE on the same
question. With direct search, the short question misses the most relevant
chunks. With HyDE, the hypothetical document’s embedding aligns closely
with the real content, pulling in the right results.
--- PAGE 91 ---
A split-screen comparison with a dotted vertical divider. The left side labeled Direct shows a short query
retrieving documents marked with red X icons for poor relevance. The right side labeled HyDE shows
the same query going through an LLM to produce a longer document, then retrieving documents all
marked with green checkmarks
HyDE and Other Pre-Retrieval Techniques
Chapter 5 explored three query transformation strategies: rewriting, step-
back prompting, and sub-query decomposition. HyDE sits alongside these as
a fourth pre-retrieval technique, but it takes a fundamentally different
approach.
Query transformations stay in “question space.” They reshape the question
to make it more effective, but the search still compares a question to
documents. HyDE crosses into “document space.” It transforms the question
into a document, then compares documents to documents. This distinction
matters because the two approaches have different strengths.
Query rewriting excels when the original question is vague or poorly
worded. Step-back prompting helps when the question is too narrow. Sub-
query decomposition handles multi-part questions. HyDE addresses a
--- PAGE 92 ---
different problem entirely: the structural mismatch between questions and
documents. When the question is clear and well-formed but retrieval still
underperforms, HyDE is often the missing piece.
These techniques are also composable. You can rewrite the question first
for clarity, then generate a hypothetical document from the rewritten
question for search. Or you can decompose a complex question into sub-
queries and apply HyDE to each one. The following illustration compares the
three approaches at a glance: basic RAG searches directly with the question,
query transformation reshapes the question, and HyDE generates an answer-
shaped document for search.
Three parallel vertical paths from a question icon at the top. The left path labeled Basic goes directly to a
search icon. The middle path labeled Transform passes through a reshaping step before search. The
right path labeled HyDE passes through an LLM that generates a document before search. All three
paths converge at a results icon at the bottom
When to Use This
Best for:
--- PAGE 93 ---
Knowledge bases with long, detailed document chunks where the style
gap between queries and documents is large
Academic, legal, or technical domains where documents use formal,
expository language very different from how people ask questions
Complex questions that require synthesizing multiple concepts into a
coherent search
Overkill when:
Simple factual lookups where keyword overlap between question and
answer is high (“What year was the company founded?”)
Real-time applications with strict latency budgets, since HyDE adds a full
language model generation call before search
The knowledge base already uses proposition chunking, which stores
atomic facts that are structurally closer to questions
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency High: adds a full LLM call before every search
Complexity Low: only one extra step, no changes to storage
or indexing
Cost Medium: one additional LLM call per query
Accuracy gain Significant for complex queries in formal
document collections
Compared to query transformations:
Query transformations are lighter and faster. They reshape the question
with a quick LLM call that produces a short rewritten query. HyDE generates
an entire paragraph, which costs more tokens and takes more time. Choose
query transformations when the question itself is the bottleneck (vague, too
specific, or multi-part). Choose HyDE when the question is fine but retrieval
still underperforms due to the structural gap between questions and
documents. In many production systems, both techniques are used together.
--- PAGE 94 ---
The core insight of HyDE: you do not need a correct answer to find the
correct answer. A plausible guess, in the right shape, is enough to
navigate meaning space to where the real answers live.
Key Takeaways
HyDE generates a hypothetical answer to the question and uses that
answer’s embedding for search, bridging the structural gap between short
queries and long documents
The hypothetical document does not need to be factually correct; it only
needs to be in the right neighborhood of meaning space to pull in
relevant real chunks
Matching the hypothetical document’s length to the actual chunk size
improves retrieval quality by aligning structural patterns in the
embeddings
HyDE adds one language model call before search, increasing latency and
cost but requiring no changes to the preparation phase or storage
The technique works best when the language model has surface-level
domain knowledge, and worst when it has no familiarity with the topic at
all
HyDE complements query transformations: transformations reshape the
question, HyDE reshapes the question into an answer, and both can be
combined for maximum retrieval effectiveness
--- PAGE 95 ---
Chapter 7: Contextual Chunk Headers: Giving
Every Chunk Its Origin Story
Prepending document-level context to each chunk so that retrieval
knows where every piece of text came from.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (chunking, embeddings, vector search).
The Problem
Imagine a knowledge base built from hundreds of corporate annual reports.
Each report has been chunked, embedded, and stored using the standard
pipeline from chapter 1. A financial analyst asks: “What are Acme Corp’s
climate change risks?”
The system retrieves several chunks about climate change risks. One is a
perfect match. It reads: “Given the broad and global scope of our operations,
we are particularly vulnerable to the physical risks of climate change, such as
shifts in weather patterns. Extreme weather conditions in the areas in which
our retail stores, suppliers, manufacturers, distribution centers, and
headquarters are located could adversely affect our operating results.”
But which company is this about? The word “Acme” appears nowhere in
the text. The chunk uses “our operations,” “our retail stores,” “our operating
results.” These pronouns made perfect sense inside the original annual
report. Once the paragraph was pulled out and stored as a standalone chunk,
that context vanished. The chunk is topically relevant but contextually
anonymous, and its embedding has no trace of the company’s identity. It
competes equally with identical-sounding paragraphs from dozens of other
companies’ reports. The following illustration shows a chunk severed from its
document, with question marks where its context used to be.
--- PAGE 96 ---
A text chunk floating alone in empty space, with highlighted portions in orange inside it. Question
marks surround the chunk on all sides, representing the missing context about who the pronouns refer
to. A faded document outline sits in the background, showing where the chunk originally came from.
This is not a rare edge case. Most document chunks rely on surrounding
context for their full meaning. Pronouns like “we,” “our,” and “it” are
everywhere in corporate filings, legal contracts, and research papers. Section
headings that establish the topic are left behind when the text beneath them
gets chunked. A paragraph that makes perfect sense on page 47 of a report
becomes ambiguous when it stands alone.
The Core Idea
Contextual chunk headers solve this by prepending a small piece of
document-level context to each chunk before embedding it. The simplest
version is just the document title. Instead of embedding the raw chunk, you
embed: “Document Title: Acme Corp Annual Report 2023” followed by the
chunk text. One line of context, and the chunk carries its identity with it.
--- PAGE 97 ---
Think of it as running headers in a printed book. Every page shows the
book title and chapter name at the top. If you tear out a single page, the
running header still tells you where it came from. Without it, the page is just
an orphaned paragraph. Contextual chunk headers stamp every chunk with
its origin, so that even when it stands alone, it remembers where it belongs.
The following illustration shows this contrast: a plain page versus a page with
a running header that anchors it to its source.
A split-screen comparison. The left side shows a plain page of text with no header, labeled “No Header”
at the top, with a question mark above it. The right side shows the same page with a running header
reading the document title at the top of the page, labeled “With Header,” with a green checkmark above
it. The right page is visually anchored to a small book icon in the corner.
The core insight: chunks lose their identity when separated from their
document. A short header restores it, dramatically improving both
retrieval accuracy and generation quality, with almost no added cost.
--- PAGE 98 ---
How It Works
The technique modifies only the preparation phase. The query phase stays
the same.
Step 1: Generate a Document Title
If your documents already have descriptive titles, use them directly. But
many documents have unhelpful file names like “Q3_report_final_v2.pdf.”
In these cases, send the first few thousand words to a language model with a
simple instruction: “What is the title of this document?” The model returns a
concise, descriptive title like “Acme Corp Annual Report on Form 10-K.” This
is a one-time cost per document, not per chunk.
Step 2: Prepend the Header to Each Chunk
For every chunk, concatenate the header with the chunk text. A raw chunk
that read:
“Given the broad and global scope of our operations, we are particularly
vulnerable to the physical risks of climate change…”
Becomes:
“Document Title: Acme Corp Annual Report 2023. Given the broad and
global scope of our operations, we are particularly vulnerable to the physical
risks of climate change…”
That single line transforms the chunk’s identity. The embedding model
now processes both the title and the content, producing a vector that captures
not just “climate change risks for a large company” but “Acme Corp’s climate
change risks.” The pronouns “our” and “we” are no longer ambiguous.
Headers can carry varying levels of context. The simplest option prepends
just the document title. Richer headers add a document summary or even the
full section path, progressively grounding each chunk in its broader origin.
The following illustration shows three levels of header richness, from
minimal to full context.
--- PAGE 99 ---
Three chunk cards stacked vertically with an arrow labeled ‘More Context’ pointing downward. The top
card labeled ‘Title’ has only an orange title bar. The middle card labeled ‘Title + Summary’ has an
--- PAGE 100 ---
orange bar with both title and summary text. The bottom card labeled ‘Full’ has the orange title and
summary bar plus a section path line, showing the richest contextual header.
Step 3: Embed and Store
Embed the augmented chunks and store them in the vector store, exactly as
in chapter 1. The only difference is a few extra words at the beginning of each
chunk. The following diagram traces the full preparation pipeline.
A horizontal pipeline diagram showing five stages connected by smooth arrows. Stage 1: a document
icon labeled “Doc.” Stage 2: a brain icon generating a title, labeled “Title.” Stage 3: a header template
icon with title and section elements, labeled “Header.” Stage 4: a chunk icon with an orange header
band at the top and gray text below, labeled “Prepend.” Stage 5: a cylindrical database with dots inside,
labeled “Store.” Each stage flows left to right.
Algorithm Sketch: Contextual Chunk Headers
1. Split document into chunks (any chunking strategy)
2. Generate a descriptive document title (or use existing one)
3. For each chunk, prepend the title to the chunk text
4. Embed each augmented chunk
5. Store in vector store
--- PAGE 101 ---
One important detail: keep the header attached at every stage. If you use a
reranker, pass the augmented chunk to it. When presenting chunks to the
language model for answer generation, include the header so the model
knows which document each chunk came from. Stripping the header at any
point loses the disambiguation benefit.
Richer Headers
The document title alone delivers the largest improvement. But you can
include more context when the title is not enough:
Title plus summary. A one-to-two sentence document summary helps
when multiple documents cover similar topics and titles alone cannot
distinguish them.
Title, summary, and section hierarchy. Including the path of
section headings (“Risk Factors > Climate Change”) helps when users
ask about specific sections within a document.
Start with title-only headers. Add more context only if retrieval quality needs
further improvement. The returns diminish with each layer.
The Impact
The effect on retrieval can be dramatic. A chunk about climate change risks
from a corporate annual report, with no mention of the company name,
scores a relevance of roughly 0.1 against the query “Acme Corp climate
change impact.” The same chunk with the document title prepended scores
roughly 0.9. That is not an incremental improvement. It is the difference
between the chunk being buried at the bottom of the results and ranking at
the top.
The header gives the embedding the one piece of information it was
missing: which entity “we” and “our” refer to. The embedding shifts from a
generic “climate risk” region of meaning space into the specific “Acme Corp
climate risk” neighborhood. The following illustration shows this shift.
--- PAGE 102 ---
A meaning space visualization. On the left, labeled “Without,” a broad scattered cloud of gray dots on a
faint grid represents many generic climate-related chunks. On the right, labeled “With,” a dense ring of
orange dots clusters tightly around a central flag icon, representing the specific company neighborhood.
A curved arrow traces the shift from the generic region to the focused cluster.
The improvement is most pronounced when the knowledge base contains
many documents covering similar topics (dozens of annual reports all
discussing risk factors) and when chunks rely heavily on pronouns and
implicit references (legal contracts, research papers, internal memos).
When to Use This
Best for:
Multi-source knowledge bases (multi-company filings, multi-author
papers, mixed document collections)
Documents heavy with pronouns and implicit references (“we,” “our,”
“the company,” “the defendant”)
--- PAGE 103 ---
Any RAG system where retrieval returns topically relevant but
contextually wrong chunks
Overkill when:
Your knowledge base contains only a single document or a few
documents on distinct topics
Chunks are already self-contained (such as propositions from chapter 4
that resolve all pronouns)
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Preparation cost Very low: one LLM call per document, plus
string concatenation
Query latency None: no additional work at query time
Storage Slightly higher: each chunk is a few tokens
longer
Retrieval accuracy Significant, especially for multi-source
knowledge bases
Compared to standard chunking from chapter 1:
The preparation overhead is negligible (one LLM call per document, not
per chunk), query latency is unchanged, and the accuracy improvement can
be substantial. Contextual chunk headers work with any chunking strategy,
including the proposition chunking from chapter 4 and alongside query-phase
techniques like those from chapters 5 and 6. For multi-source knowledge
bases, this should be a default part of the pipeline, not an optional
enhancement.
Key Takeaways
Chunks lose critical context when separated from their documents.
Pronouns and implicit references become ambiguous.
Prepending the document title to each chunk before embedding restores
that context at minimal cost.
--- PAGE 104 ---
Richer headers (summary, section hierarchy) offer diminishing returns
but help with large, overlapping document collections.
Keep the header attached at every stage: embedding, reranking, and
generation.
The technique adds no query-time latency and works with any chunking
strategy. For multi-source knowledge bases, it should be a default.
--- PAGE 105 ---
Chapter 8: Context Enrichment Window
Expand each retrieved chunk with its surrounding text to recover the
full thought that arbitrary boundaries cut apart.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (chunking, overlap, vector search), Chapter 7
(contextual chunk headers)
The Problem
By now you know that chunking splits a document into fixed-size pieces for
embedding and retrieval. You also know that overlap helps preserve
information at the boundaries. But overlap is a partial fix. Consider what
happens when a user asks “What role does deforestation play in climate
change?” and the system retrieves this chunk:
“…and land degradation. Deforestation accounts for approximately 10
percent of global greenhouse gas emissions. When forests are cleared, the
carbon stored in trees is released into the atmosphere as carbon dioxide.
Furthermore, the loss of forest cover reduces the planet’s capacity to…”
The chunk lands right on the most relevant passage, but it starts mid-
paragraph and ends mid-sentence. The reader learns that deforestation
accounts for 10 percent of emissions, but the setup (what causes
deforestation) was in the previous chunk, and the conclusion (what reduced
forest capacity means for climate feedback loops) is in the next chunk. The
answer the language model constructs from this fragment will be technically
accurate but incomplete, missing the cause and the consequence. It delivers
the middle of a thought without the beginning or the end.
This is not a failure of the embedding model or the search algorithm. The
retrieval found exactly the right spot in the document. The problem is that
the chunk boundaries are arbitrary. They were placed by a character counter,
not by a reader who understands where ideas begin and end. The result is a
kind of tunnel vision: the system sees the precise point of relevance but
--- PAGE 106 ---
cannot see the paragraph around it. The following illustration shows how a
chunk retrieved in isolation captures the core fact but loses the reasoning
that surrounds it.
A text chunk floating in isolation, with faded text visible on either side representing the missing context
before and after the retrieved fragment
The Core Idea
The fix is surprisingly simple: after finding the right chunk through similarity
search, widen the view. Go back to the original document and grab the
chunks immediately before and after the retrieved one. Stitch them together
into a single expanded passage. The retrieval stays precise (the small chunk
matched the query), but the context delivered to the language model is broad
enough to contain the complete thought.
Think of it like a spotlight on a theater stage. The similarity search aims
the spotlight at the right actor. But the spotlight beam is narrow, catching
only the actor’s face. The context enrichment window widens the beam to
--- PAGE 107 ---
show the full scene, including the actors on either side who are part of the
same conversation. The search still picks the right spot, but the audience sees
enough to follow the dialogue.
This technique separates the unit of search from the unit of reading. You
search with small, focused chunks (for precision), but you read with wider
passages (for comprehension). The following diagram illustrates how the
narrow search chunk expands into a broader context window by pulling in its
neighbors.
A spotlight beam starting narrow on a single chunk, then widening to encompass the chunks before and
after it, showing the search unit versus the reading unit
How It Works
The context enrichment window requires one extra piece of metadata during
preparation and one extra step during retrieval. Here is the process.
1. Store Position Metadata During Chunking
--- PAGE 108 ---
When you split the document into chunks, tag each chunk with its sequential
index, a simple number recording its position in the original document.
Chunk zero is the first piece of the document, chunk one is the second, and so
on. This index is the thread that lets you find your way back to the neighbors
later.
You also need a way to retrieve any chunk by its index. The simplest
approach is to keep the full set of chunks accessible, whether in memory, in a
side store, or by storing the index as metadata in the vector store itself. The
key requirement is that given an index number, you can look up the
corresponding chunk.
2. Retrieve Relevant Chunks
Run similarity search as usual. The query is embedded, compared against all
chunk embeddings, and the top-K most similar chunks are returned. Nothing
changes here. The chunks are small and focused, which is exactly what gives
you precise matching.
3. Expand Each Result With Neighbors
For each retrieved chunk, read its index from the metadata. Then fetch the
chunks at index minus one, the chunk itself, and index plus one. This gives
you a window of three chunks centered on the match. The window size is
configurable: you might grab one neighbor on each side (window of 3) or two
neighbors on each side (window of 5), depending on how much context you
need.
The following diagram walks through this expansion step by step, showing
how a single retrieved chunk at position four pulls in its neighbors at
positions three and five to form an expanded passage.
--- PAGE 109 ---
A pipeline showing a query matching chunk 4, then arrows reaching left to chunk 3 and right to chunk
5, then all three merging into one expanded passage
4. Concatenate With Overlap Awareness
Here is where a subtle detail matters. If your chunks were created with
overlap (and they usually are), the tail of chunk three and the head of chunk
four share some identical text. Naively joining them would produce a passage
with duplicated sentences in the middle.
To avoid this, the concatenation step trims the overlap region. It takes the
full content of the first chunk, strips off the overlapping tail, and appends the
next chunk. This is repeated for each pair of adjacent chunks in the window.
The result is a clean, continuous passage with no duplication.
--- PAGE 110 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Context Window Expansion
1. Receive retrieved chunk with index I
2. Set window: start = I - N, end = I + N
3. Fetch chunks at indices start through end
4. Sort fetched chunks by index
5. Initialize passage = first chunk's text
6. For each subsequent chunk:
   a. Trim overlap from junction
   b. Append remainder to passage
7. Return expanded passage
The overlap trimming is the difference between a clean paragraph and one
that repeats itself mid-sentence. The following close-up shows how two
adjacent chunks share an overlap region, and how the concatenation trims it
to produce seamless text.
Two adjacent chunks shown side by side, with the overlap region at their boundary highlighted in
orange, and arrows showing how the overlap is trimmed during concatenation to produce clean
continuous text
--- PAGE 111 ---
The key insight: search with small chunks for precision, but read with
expanded windows for comprehension. The two goals do not need the
same unit of text.
Choosing the Window Size
The number of neighbors you grab is a dial you can turn. A window of one
neighbor on each side (three chunks total) roughly triples the context length.
Two neighbors on each side (five chunks) quintuples it. Larger windows
provide more context but also introduce more noise, as the distant neighbors
may drift to a different topic.
In practice, one to two neighbors on each side covers most cases. For
documents with long, flowing arguments (legal briefs, academic papers), a
wider window helps. For documents with short, self-contained sections
(FAQs, product catalogs), even one neighbor may bring in unrelated content.
Match the window size to the document’s natural paragraph length.
How This Relates to Contextual Chunk Headers
Chapter 7 introduced contextual chunk headers, which prepend document-
level information (title, summary, section path) to each chunk. That
technique adds vertical context: it tells the chunk where it lives in the
document hierarchy. The context enrichment window adds horizontal
context: it shows what the surrounding text actually says.
The two techniques solve different aspects of the same problem. Headers
answer “which document is this from and what section?” The window
answers “what was the full thought being expressed here?” They are fully
complementary. You can prepend headers to each chunk during preparation
and still expand the window during retrieval. In fact, combining them is often
the best approach for knowledge bases with multiple long documents. The
following illustration shows both dimensions of context side by side.
--- PAGE 112 ---
A chunk shown with a header band on top providing document-level context (vertical) and neighboring
chunks on either side providing local context (horizontal), forming a cross-shaped enrichment pattern
When to Use This
Best for:
Long documents where ideas span multiple paragraphs (research papers,
legal documents, technical manuals)
Chunk sizes that are small for embedding precision (under 500
characters) where single chunks often cut mid-thought
Systems where answer completeness matters as much as relevance
Overkill when:
Chunks are already large enough to contain full ideas
Documents consist of short, self-contained entries (FAQ pairs, product
listings)
--- PAGE 113 ---
You are already using proposition chunking (which produces self-
contained units by design)
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency Low (neighbor lookup is fast, no LLM call needed)
Complexity Low (simple index-based retrieval with concatenation)
Cost Negligible (no API calls, just local data fetching)
Accuracy gain Moderate to high for long-form documents
Compared to proposition chunking:
Proposition chunking (Chapter 4) solves the incomplete-thought problem
from the other direction: it creates self-contained chunks that never need
expansion because each one is already a complete idea. The context
enrichment window keeps standard chunking but fixes its output at retrieval
time. Proposition chunking is more thorough but requires an LLM call per
chunk during preparation. The context window requires zero LLM calls,
making it the lighter-weight option when preparation cost matters. The
following illustration contrasts the two approaches side by side.
--- PAGE 114 ---
A split comparison showing the context enrichment window approach on one side (small chunks
expanded at retrieval time) versus proposition chunking on the other side (self-contained chunks that
need no expansion)
Key Takeaways
Chunk boundaries are placed by character counts, not by meaning. This
often cuts through the middle of ideas.
The context enrichment window separates the search unit from the
reading unit: small chunks for precise matching, expanded passages for
complete answers.
After retrieving a chunk, grab its neighbors by index and concatenate
them, trimming any overlap to avoid duplication.
Window size is configurable. One to two neighbors per side covers most
use cases.
--- PAGE 115 ---
This technique complements contextual chunk headers. Headers add
document-level context (vertical), while the window adds surrounding-
text context (horizontal).
No LLM calls are needed. The enrichment is a simple index lookup,
making it one of the lowest-cost improvements available.
--- PAGE 116 ---
Chapter 9: Semantic Chunking: Splitting Text
Where the Meaning Shifts
Letting the content itself decide where to split, by measuring the
semantic similarity between consecutive sentences and cutting where
the topic changes.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (chunking, embeddings, vector search). Familiarity
with the chunking limitations discussed in chapters 4 and 8.
The Problem
Every chunking strategy we have seen so far uses external rules to decide
where to split a document. Chapter 1 split text every thousand characters.
Chapter 4 used a language model to decompose chunks into propositions.
Chapter 8 accepted fixed boundaries but expanded the view at retrieval time.
All of these approaches treat the document’s own structure as invisible. The
character counter does not know that sentence 14 is about ocean chemistry
while sentence 15 shifts to deforestation policy. It splits wherever the count
lands.
Consider a document about climate change that flows through several
subtopics. One section discusses the causes of rising temperatures. The next
section, starting mid-paragraph, shifts to the economic consequences. A
fixed-size chunker might place a boundary right in the middle of the causes
discussion, grouping the last two sentences about fossil fuels with the first
three sentences about GDP impacts. The resulting chunk is a semantic blend:
half about causes, half about economics. Its embedding lands somewhere
between the two topics, precisely matching neither. A query about causes
retrieves it weakly. A query about economics retrieves it weakly. A query
about either topic alone would be better served by a chunk that covers just
that topic.
--- PAGE 117 ---
The following illustration shows this: a document with clear topic shifts
getting sliced at fixed intervals that ignore those shifts, producing chunks that
blend unrelated ideas.
A document with colored sections representing distinct topics is sliced at regular fixed intervals, with cut
lines falling in the middle of topics, producing chunks that mix colors and blend unrelated content
The root cause is that fixed-size chunking is blind to meaning. It sees
characters, not ideas. What if the chunking process could sense where one
idea ends and another begins?
The Core Idea
Semantic chunking replaces the character counter with a meaning sensor.
Instead of splitting every N characters, it examines how similar consecutive
sentences are to each other. When two neighboring sentences are about the
same topic, their embeddings are close together and the system keeps them
--- PAGE 118 ---
in the same chunk. When the topic shifts, the embeddings diverge, and the
system places a boundary there.
Think of it like a film editor reviewing raw footage. An amateur editor cuts
every three minutes regardless of what is happening on screen, sometimes
mid-dialogue, sometimes mid-action. A skilled editor watches the footage and
cuts at natural scene transitions: when the location changes, when a new
character starts speaking, when the mood shifts. The scenes that result are
coherent units that make sense on their own. Semantic chunking applies the
same principle to text. It watches the meaning flow between sentences and
cuts when the flow breaks.
The result is chunks that vary in length but are internally coherent. A
dense technical section might produce a short chunk of five sentences, all
tightly related. A flowing narrative section might produce a longer chunk of
fifteen sentences that all develop the same theme. The chunk boundaries are
no longer arbitrary. They reflect the natural structure of the text. The
following diagram illustrates this: consecutive sentences with high similarity
grouped together, and boundaries placed where similarity drops.
--- PAGE 119 ---
A sequence of sentence blocks connected by similarity links, with strong links shown as thick lines and
weak links shown as thin broken lines. Scissors icons appear at the weak links, showing where the text
naturally splits into coherent chunks
The core insight: instead of imposing boundaries from the outside, let
the text reveal where its own boundaries are. Sentences that belong
together stay together. Sentences that shift to a new topic start a new
chunk.
How It Works
Semantic chunking modifies only the splitting step of the preparation phase.
Everything else, embedding, storing, searching, and answering, stays the
same as in chapter 1.
Step 1: Split Into Sentences
--- PAGE 120 ---
The process starts by breaking the document into individual sentences. This
is a much finer split than character-based chunking. Each sentence becomes a
candidate boundary point. The goal is not to keep sentences as individual
chunks (that would be too granular), but to evaluate the connection between
every pair of neighbors and decide which ones belong in the same chunk.
Step 2: Embed Each Sentence
Every sentence is converted into an embedding vector. This is the same
embedding process from chapter 1, but applied at the sentence level rather
than the chunk level. The result is a sequence of vectors, one per sentence,
each capturing that sentence’s meaning.
This step is the most computationally expensive part of semantic
chunking. A document with 500 sentences requires 500 embedding calls. For
comparison, the same document split into 25 chunks of 1,000 characters
requires only 25 embedding calls. This cost is paid once during preparation,
not at query time.
Step 3: Measure Distances Between Consecutive Sentences
For each pair of adjacent sentences, the system computes the distance
between their embeddings. If sentence 7 and sentence 8 are about the same
subtopic, their embeddings will be close and the distance will be small. If
sentence 8 shifts to a new topic, the distance will spike.
The system computes these distances for every consecutive pair across the
entire document, producing a sequence of distance values. The following
diagram visualizes this as a line chart: the x-axis is position in the document,
the y-axis is the distance between consecutive sentence pairs. Valleys
represent stretches of consistent topic, and peaks represent topic transitions.
--- PAGE 121 ---
A line chart showing the distance between consecutive sentence embeddings across a document. The x-
axis represents position and the y-axis represents distance. Most values are low, but several sharp peaks
rise above a horizontal dashed threshold line, with scissors icons at the peaks indicating split points
Step 4: Identify Breakpoints
The distance sequence reveals where the meaning shifts. The question is:
how large must a distance be to count as a genuine topic change rather than
normal sentence-to-sentence variation?
Semantic chunking offers three methods for answering this question, each
using a different statistical approach to define “unusually large.”
Percentile method. Compute all pairwise distances, then set the
threshold at the Nth percentile. For example, with a threshold at the 90th
percentile, only distances in the top 10% of all distances are treated as
breakpoints. This method is simple and works well for most documents. A
higher percentile (95th) produces fewer, larger chunks. A lower percentile
(80th) produces more, smaller chunks.
Standard deviation method. Compute the mean and standard
deviation of all distances. Set the threshold at the mean plus N standard
deviations. A distance that exceeds this threshold is considered an outlier, a
--- PAGE 122 ---
topic shift rather than normal variation. Typical values of N range from 1 to 3.
This method is sensitive to the overall distribution of distances and works
well when topic shifts are genuinely distinct from the baseline.
Interquartile range method. Compute the first and third quartiles of
the distance distribution. The interquartile range (IQR) is the difference
between them. Set the threshold at the third quartile plus 1.5 times the IQR.
This is the same logic used to identify outliers in a box plot, and it is robust to
extreme values. It works well when a few very large distances might skew the
mean and standard deviation.
All three methods answer the same question: “Which distances are
unusually large?” They differ in how they define “unusual.” For most use
cases, the percentile method at a threshold around 90 provides a reliable
starting point.
Algorithm Sketch: Semantic Chunking
1. Split document into individual sentences
2. Embed each sentence into a vector
3. Compute distance between each consecutive pair of sentence vectors
4. Choose a breakpoint method (percentile, standard deviation, or IQR)
5. Set a threshold based on the chosen method
6. Split the text wherever the distance exceeds the threshold
7. Each group of sentences between breakpoints becomes one chunk
8. Embed and store chunks in the vector store
Step 5: Group Sentences Into Chunks
Every sentence between two breakpoints is grouped into a single chunk. The
first group runs from the start of the document to the first breakpoint. The
second group runs from the first breakpoint to the second. And so on. Each
chunk is a semantically coherent unit: a stretch of text where the topic stayed
consistent.
These chunks are then embedded as whole units and stored in the vector
store, just as in chapter 1. The difference is that each chunk now represents a
naturally bounded topic rather than an arbitrary slice of text. The following
diagram shows the complete pipeline from document to semantically
chunked vector store.
--- PAGE 123 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram showing five stages. Stage 1: a document icon. Stage 2: the document
split into many small sentence blocks. Stage 3: arrows showing distances between pairs of adjacent
sentences with a threshold line. Stage 4: sentences grouped into variable-sized chunks with distinct
colors. Stage 5: a vector store with colored dots matching the chunks
Semantic Chunks vs. Fixed Chunks
The practical difference shows up immediately when you compare retrieval
results. Consider a document that discusses three climate change subtopics in
sequence: causes, effects on biodiversity, and economic impacts. Fixed-size
chunking produces chunks that straddle these boundaries, blending content
from two subtopics into single chunks. Semantic chunking produces chunks
that align with the boundaries, keeping each subtopic in its own chunk.
When a user asks about economic impacts, the semantic chunk covering
that subtopic is a clean match. Its embedding precisely captures “economic
impacts of climate change” because that is all it contains. The fixed chunk
that blends economic impacts with biodiversity effects produces a weaker
match for either topic.
The tradeoff is chunk size variability. Fixed chunks are predictable: each is
roughly the same length, which means the context passed to the language
--- PAGE 124 ---
model is consistent. Semantic chunks vary in length. A dense section might
produce a chunk of 200 characters, while a flowing narrative section might
produce one of 3,000 characters. This variability can affect how many chunks
fit in the model’s context window during generation. The following
illustration contrasts the two approaches side by side on the same document.
A split-screen comparison. The left side labeled “Fixed” shows a document sliced into equal-sized chunks
with cut lines falling in the middle of colored topic regions. The right side labeled “Semantic” shows the
same document with variable-sized chunks whose boundaries align with topic changes, each chunk a
single solid color
Where Semantic Chunking Sits Among Chunking Strategies
This book has now covered four approaches to the chunking problem, each
attacking it from a different angle.
Fixed-size chunking (chapter 1) is the simplest. It splits by character
count with overlap to soften boundary effects. It requires no embedding calls
during splitting and works as a reliable baseline.
Proposition chunking (chapter 4) goes to the other extreme. It uses a
language model to decompose text into atomic facts. Each fact is a self-
--- PAGE 125 ---
contained chunk. This gives maximum retrieval precision but loses narrative
context and requires an LLM call per chunk during preparation.
Context enrichment window (chapter 8) does not change the chunking
at all. It keeps fixed chunks but expands the context at retrieval time by
grabbing neighbors. This is the least invasive option, adding no cost during
preparation.
Semantic chunking (this chapter) finds a middle ground. It uses
embedding similarity to place boundaries at natural topic shifts. Chunks vary
in size but are internally coherent. The preparation cost falls between fixed
chunking (cheapest) and proposition chunking (most expensive), since it
requires one embedding per sentence but no LLM calls.
These approaches are not mutually exclusive. You can apply semantic
chunking and then add contextual chunk headers from chapter 7. Or use
semantic chunking during preparation and still apply the context enrichment
window at retrieval time for passages that were split at a subtler shift the
method missed. The following illustration places all four strategies on a
spectrum from simplest to most precise, showing where semantic chunking
fits relative to the alternatives covered so far.
--- PAGE 126 ---
A horizontal arrow pointing from ‘Simple’ on the left to ‘Precise’ on the right, with four icons placed
along the spectrum. From left to right: Fixed (a square block icon), Window (an expandable block icon),
Semantic (a signal/wave icon), and Propositions (a card icon). The arrow transitions from light gray on
the left to warm orange on the right, showing increasing precision.
When to Use This
Best for:
Long documents with clear topic transitions (reports, papers, textbooks,
multi-section articles)
Knowledge bases where fixed-size chunks consistently blend unrelated
topics
Situations where you want better-than-fixed chunking without the LLM
cost of proposition decomposition
Overkill when:
Documents are short or cover a single focused topic throughout
--- PAGE 127 ---
Documents already have clear structural markers (headings, sections)
that a simpler rule-based splitter can use
Your chunking already works well and retrieval quality is not the
bottleneck
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Preparation cost Moderate: one embedding per sentence (more
calls than fixed chunking, no LLM calls)
Query latency Same as standard RAG: no additional work at
query time
Chunk quality High: chunks are internally coherent,
representing single topics
Chunk size Variable: ranges from very short to very long
depending on content
Compared to fixed-size chunking from chapter 1:
Fixed chunking is cheaper to prepare (one embedding per chunk, not per
sentence) and produces predictable chunk sizes. Semantic chunking produces
higher-quality chunks at the cost of more embedding calls during
preparation. For documents with clear multi-topic structure, the investment
pays off through better retrieval precision. For documents that are already
topically consistent throughout, fixed chunking performs nearly as well at
lower cost.
Compared to proposition chunking from chapter 4:
Proposition chunking gives finer granularity (individual facts) and
maximum retrieval precision, but requires expensive LLM calls during
preparation. Semantic chunking provides topic-level granularity using only
embedding calls, making it significantly cheaper. Propositions excel at
pinpoint factual questions. Semantic chunks excel at topical questions where
the answer spans several related sentences. Choose based on the question
patterns your users have.
Key Takeaways
--- PAGE 128 ---
Semantic chunking places split points where the meaning changes, not
where a character counter reaches a limit.
The process works by embedding every sentence, measuring the distance
between consecutive sentence pairs, and splitting where the distance
exceeds a statistical threshold.
Three threshold methods are available: percentile (simplest, reliable
default), standard deviation (sensitive to distribution), and interquartile
range (robust to outliers).
Semantic chunks vary in length but are internally coherent, each
covering a single topic or subtopic.
The preparation cost is moderate: one embedding per sentence, with no
language model calls required for the splitting itself.
Semantic chunking occupies a practical middle ground between cheap
fixed-size chunking and expensive proposition chunking, offering topic-
level coherence without LLM-based decomposition.
--- PAGE 129 ---
Chapter 10: Contextual Compression: Extracting
Only What Matters
After retrieval, an LLM reads each chunk in light of the query and
extracts only the sentences that actually answer the question,
discarding the rest.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (the basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 3 (relevancy
grading as a post-retrieval filter).
The Problem
By this point in the book, the retrieval side of the pipeline has several tools
for finding the right chunks. Better chunking strategies place boundaries at
natural topic shifts. Query transformations rephrase the question to improve
the match. But even when the system retrieves exactly the right chunk, that
chunk is rarely a perfect answer to the question.
Consider a user who asks: “What role does the Paris Agreement play in
limiting global warming?” The retriever does its job and returns a chunk from
a climate policy document. But that chunk is a full paragraph covering the
United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, the history of the
Kyoto Protocol, the Paris Agreement’s temperature targets, and a mention of
nationally determined contributions. Only two sentences in the middle
directly address the user’s question. The rest is context that the chunk
happened to include because it was stored as one unit.
The language model receiving this chunk must now sift through the entire
passage to find the relevant sentences. With one chunk, this is manageable.
But a typical retrieval returns three to five chunks, each carrying its own
freight of tangential information. The model’s context window fills with
material that is topically adjacent but not directly useful. The result is an
answer that may be correct but is diluted: the model weaves in details from
--- PAGE 130 ---
the surrounding sentences because they were present in the context, even
though they do not address the question.
The following illustration shows this: a retrieved chunk where only a small
portion is relevant to the query, while the rest is noise that the model must
wade through.
A retrieved document chunk represented as a tall rectangle filled with horizontal lines of text. A few
lines in the middle are highlighted in warm orange, representing the portion relevant to the query. The
rest of the lines are in light gray, representing irrelevant content. A magnifying glass icon focuses on
the orange lines. A query bubble sits to the left.
The issue is not that the wrong chunk was retrieved. The chunk is relevant at
the topic level. The issue is that relevance is not binary. Within a single
chunk, some sentences are precisely on target and others are merely nearby.
The Core Idea
Contextual compression adds a refinement step between retrieval and
generation. After the base retriever returns its chunks, a language model
--- PAGE 131 ---
reads each chunk alongside the original query and extracts only the portions
that directly address the question. Everything else is discarded. The
generation model then receives a set of compressed excerpts instead of full
chunks.
Think of it like a gold panner working a riverbed. The retriever is the
shovel that scoops up promising sediment from the right part of the river. But
the pan still contains sand, gravel, and silt mixed in with the gold flakes. The
compression step is the panning itself: swirling the material so that the heavy
gold settles to the bottom and the lighter debris washes away. What remains
is concentrated value. The generation model never sees the gravel. The
following illustration shows this three-stage progression from raw sediment
to pure gold.
A three-stage gold panning analogy: a shovel scoops mixed material from a riverbed (retrieval), a pan
swirls to separate gold flakes from debris (compression), and a neat pile of gold dots remains with a
lightbulb glowing above (generation)
The key insight is that relevance depends on the question. The same chunk
might yield different compressed outputs for different queries. A paragraph
about the Paris Agreement might compress to the temperature targets for one
--- PAGE 132 ---
query and to the nationally determined contributions for another. The
compression is contextual because it considers both the chunk and the query
together. The following diagram shows the core concept: full chunks entering
a compression funnel, with only the relevant sentences emerging on the
other side.
A horizontal flow diagram. On the left, three tall rectangles represent retrieved chunks, each with a mix
of orange-highlighted and gray lines. In the center, a funnel icon with a small brain symbol inside it
represents the LLM compressor. On the right, three small rectangles emerge, containing only orange-
highlighted lines. An arrow labeled “Query” points into the funnel from above. The label “Full” appears
under the left rectangles and “Focused” under the right ones.
The core insight: retrieval finds the right chunks, but compression finds
the right sentences within those chunks. Together, they narrow the
focus from document level to sentence level.
How It Works
--- PAGE 133 ---
Contextual compression modifies only the post-retrieval step of the pipeline.
The preparation phase (chunking, embedding, storing) remains unchanged.
The query embedding and similarity search remain unchanged. The
compression layer sits between retrieval and generation, processing each
chunk before it reaches the answer model.
Step 1: Retrieve Chunks Normally
The process begins with a standard retrieval step. The user’s query is
embedded, the vector store returns the top-K most similar chunks, and these
chunks form the initial candidate set. This is the same base retriever from
chapter 1, using whichever chunking strategy the system has in place.
At this point, the chunks are complete passages, exactly as they were
stored during preparation. They are topically relevant but not surgically
precise.
Step 2: Compress Each Chunk Against the Query
Each retrieved chunk is sent to a language model along with the original
query. The model’s task is extraction, not summarization. It reads the chunk,
identifies the specific sentences or phrases that directly address the query,
and returns only those portions. If nothing in the chunk is relevant, the
model returns nothing, and that chunk is effectively filtered out.
This is a fundamentally different operation from the relevancy grading in
chapter 3. Relevancy grading makes a binary decision about each whole
chunk: keep it or discard it. Compression operates at a finer granularity. It
reaches inside the chunk and pulls out only the useful parts, like a surgeon
removing a specific tissue sample rather than deciding whether to keep or
discard the entire organ.
The following illustration shows this step in detail: the LLM receiving a
chunk and a query, then producing a compressed excerpt containing only the
relevant portions.
--- PAGE 134 ---
A close-up diagram of the compression step. On the left, a tall rectangle labeled “Chunk” with mixed
orange and gray horizontal lines. Below it, a rounded rectangle labeled “Query” with a question mark
icon. Both feed into a central rounded square with a brain icon, representing the LLM compressor. On
the right, a shorter rectangle emerges containing only the orange lines from the original chunk. A small
label “Extract” appears below the brain icon.
Step 3: Pass Compressed Results to Generation
The compressed excerpts from all chunks are concatenated and passed to the
generation model as context. Because the irrelevant material has been
stripped away, the context is denser with useful information. The generation
model can focus entirely on answering the question without navigating
around tangential details.
This step is identical to the generation step in chapter 1, with one
difference: the context is shorter and more focused. The generation model
processes less text to produce a better answer.
--- PAGE 135 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Contextual Compression
1. Receive user query
2. Embed query and retrieve top-K chunks from vector store
3. For each retrieved chunk:
   a. Send chunk + query to compressor LLM
   b. LLM extracts only the sentences relevant to the query
   c. If nothing relevant, discard the chunk entirely
4. Concatenate all compressed excerpts
5. Pass compressed context + query to generation LLM
6. Return the answer
The Full Pipeline
The complete pipeline now has an additional stage between retrieval and
generation. The following diagram shows all stages in sequence: the query
triggers retrieval, the retrieved chunks pass through the compression layer,
and only the compressed excerpts reach the generation model.
--- PAGE 136 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram with five stages connected by arrows flowing left to right. Stage 1: a
search icon with the label “Query”. Stage 2: a cylinder database icon with three rectangles emerging
from it, labeled “Retrieve”. Stage 3: a funnel with a brain icon inside, with three shorter rectangles
emerging, labeled “Compress”. Stage 4: an icon of stacked focused excerpts, labeled “Context”. Stage 5: a
lightbulb icon producing text lines, labeled “Answer”. Clean sans-serif labels below each stage.
What Compression Removes
The compressor is effective against several types of noise that commonly
appear in retrieved chunks.
Tangential context. A chunk about the Paris Agreement might include
background on the UNFCCC treaty framework. If the query asks specifically
about temperature targets, the compressor strips the treaty background and
keeps only the target-related sentences.
Transitional prose. Chunks often begin or end with transitional
sentences that connect to the surrounding text: “As discussed in the previous
section…” or “Building on this foundation, the next area to consider is…”
These sentences carry no informational value for the query and are removed.
Duplicate information. When multiple retrieved chunks cover
overlapping content (common with chunk overlap), the compressor naturally
--- PAGE 137 ---
deduplicates by extracting only the distinct relevant portions from each
chunk. Two chunks that share three sentences about temperature targets
might each contribute only the sentences unique to their perspective.
Compression vs. Relevancy Grading
Chapter 3 introduced relevancy grading as a way to filter out noisy chunks.
That technique works at the chunk level: a chunk is either relevant enough to
keep or not. Contextual compression works at the sentence level within each
chunk.
These two approaches address different problems. Relevancy grading
catches chunks that should never have been retrieved at all, for example a
chunk about page formatting that matched by coincidence. Compression
catches chunks that are topically on target but contain a mix of relevant and
irrelevant sentences. The following illustration contrasts the two approaches
side by side.
--- PAGE 138 ---
A split-screen comparison. The left side labeled “Grading” shows three full chunks: one has a green
checkmark (kept whole), one has a red X (discarded whole), one has a green checkmark (kept whole).
The right side labeled “Compression” shows three chunks being processed through small funnels, each
producing a shorter excerpt. One chunk produces a medium excerpt, one produces a tiny excerpt, and
one is fully discarded with nothing remaining. The key difference is visible: grading keeps or discards
whole chunks while compression extracts portions.
In practice, the two techniques can be combined. First, grade each chunk to
filter out the completely irrelevant ones. Then, compress the remaining
chunks to extract only the useful portions. This two-stage approach
minimizes both the number of chunks and the noise within each chunk.
When to Use This
Best for:
Large chunks that cover multiple subtopics within a single passage
Queries that target specific details buried inside broader discussions
--- PAGE 139 ---
Systems where the generation model’s context window is limited and
every token counts
Overkill when:
Chunks are already small and focused (e.g., proposition-chunked as in
chapter 4)
The generation model has a large context window and handles noise well
Latency is the primary concern (compression adds an LLM call per chunk
at query time)
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Query latency High: one LLM call per retrieved chunk at
query time
Preparation cost None: compression happens at query time, not
during indexing
Answer quality Significant improvement when chunks contain
mixed-relevance content
Cost Moderate to high: LLM inference cost scales
with number of retrieved chunks
Compared to relevancy grading from chapter 3:
Relevancy grading is a binary filter: keep or discard the whole chunk. It is
cheaper (a single classification per chunk) and faster, but it cannot salvage
the useful sentences from an otherwise noisy chunk. Compression is more
expensive but more precise, extracting value at the sentence level. For
systems where chunks are well-constructed and topically focused, grading
alone may suffice. For systems with large, multi-topic chunks, compression
provides a meaningful quality improvement.
Compared to proposition chunking from chapter 4:
Proposition chunking solves the mixed-content problem at preparation
time by decomposing text into atomic facts. Each fact is already focused, so
compression at query time would have little to extract. The tradeoff is that
proposition chunking requires LLM calls during preparation (once per
document), while contextual compression requires LLM calls during retrieval
(once per query per chunk). If your documents change rarely but queries are
--- PAGE 140 ---
frequent, proposition chunking front-loads the cost. If your documents
change often, compression avoids reprocessing the entire collection.
Key Takeaways
Retrieved chunks are topically relevant but rarely surgically precise. They
contain a mix of useful and tangential sentences.
Contextual compression adds an LLM-powered extraction step between
retrieval and generation, pulling out only the sentences that address the
specific query.
The compression is contextual: the same chunk produces different
extracts for different queries, because relevance depends on the question
being asked.
Unlike relevancy grading (which keeps or discards whole chunks),
compression operates at the sentence level within each chunk.
The cost is an LLM call per retrieved chunk at query time. This adds
latency and inference cost, making it best suited for systems where
answer precision matters more than speed.
Contextual compression pairs well with other techniques: use relevancy
grading first to remove junk chunks, then compress the survivors. Or
combine with contextual chunk headers to preserve document identity
while still extracting focused content.
--- PAGE 141 ---
Chapter 11: Document Augmentation: Giving Every
Chunk a Set of Questions
During preparation, an LLM generates questions that each chunk could
answer. Those questions are embedded and stored alongside the
original chunks, so user queries match against question-shaped entries
instead of declarative text.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (the basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 6 (HyDE and the
query-document asymmetry problem).
The Problem
Chapter 6 introduced a fundamental tension in retrieval: user queries are
questions, but stored chunks are declarative passages. A user asks “How do
greenhouse gases trap heat?” while the chunk that holds the answer reads
“Greenhouse gases absorb infrared radiation emitted by the Earth’s surface,
re-emitting it in all directions and warming the lower atmosphere.” The
question and the answer describe the same concept, but they are structurally
different. One is an interrogative sentence; the other is an explanatory
statement. Their embeddings land in different regions of the vector space,
even though they are about the same thing.
HyDE solved this by transforming the query side: generating a
hypothetical answer so the search vector looks like a document. But there is
another direction to approach the same problem. Instead of reshaping the
query at search time, what if the documents were reshaped during
preparation to look more like the questions users will eventually ask?
Consider a knowledge base with thousands of chunks about climate
science. A chunk about carbon pricing mechanisms contains the answer to
dozens of potential questions: “What is a carbon tax?”, “How do cap-and-
trade systems work?”, “Why do governments put a price on carbon?” None of
those questions appear in the chunk itself. The chunk is a declarative
--- PAGE 142 ---
explanation. When a user asks one of those questions, the retriever must
bridge the gap between the question’s embedding and the chunk’s
embedding. Sometimes the bridge is short and retrieval succeeds. Sometimes
the gap is wide enough that a less relevant chunk scores higher.
The following illustration shows this shape mismatch: a question-shaped
query trying to find its match among declarative-shaped chunks, with the
embedding distance creating a gap even when the content is a perfect match.
A question mark icon on the left representing a user query, with dotted arrows reaching toward three
rectangular chunks on the right. The chunks contain horizontal lines suggesting declarative text. The
arrows are long and stretched, suggesting distance in embedding space. One chunk has a faint glow
indicating it holds the answer, but the arrow to it is still long. A label “Gap” appears along the stretched
arrows.
What if each chunk came pre-equipped with the questions it could answer?
Then the user’s question would match against other questions, not against
declarative text. The shape mismatch disappears.
The Core Idea
--- PAGE 143 ---
Document augmentation through question generation adds a
preparation step after chunking. For each chunk, a language model reads the
content and generates a set of questions that the chunk could answer. These
generated questions are embedded and stored in the vector store alongside
the original chunk, each pointing back to the parent chunk as its source.
Think of it like a museum curator who writes a “Frequently Asked
Questions” card for each exhibit. The exhibit itself is a detailed description of
a historical artifact, written in declarative prose. Most visitors do not read the
entire description. They scan for answers to specific questions: “When was
this made?”, “What was it used for?”, “Why is it significant?” The FAQ card
anticipates those questions and places them right next to the exhibit. A visitor
scanning the room spots the FAQ card that matches their question and
immediately knows which exhibit to examine in detail. The FAQ cards bridge
the gap between what visitors want to know and how the exhibit presents its
information. The following illustration shows this: a visitor scanning FAQ
cards next to museum exhibits to quickly find the right one.
A museum scene with exhibit pedestals displaying artifacts with detailed descriptive plaques, and small
FAQ cards next to each plaque. A visitor scans the FAQ cards rather than the dense plaques, with a
dotted arrow connecting their thought bubble to a matching FAQ card
--- PAGE 144 ---
In the same way, generated questions bridge the gap between how users
phrase their queries and how chunks store their knowledge. At query time,
the user’s question is far more likely to land near a generated question (same
shape, same intent) than near the original declarative chunk. The following
diagram shows this: each chunk surrounded by a cluster of generated
questions, all stored together in the vector store.
