Three rectangular chunks arranged vertically on the left, each in a different muted color (blue, orange,
gray). From each chunk, three to four small rounded bubbles with question mark icons branch out to
the right, representing generated questions. All the chunks and their question bubbles flow into a
cylindrical database icon on the far right. A label “Chunks” appears on the left and “Store” on the right.
The core insight: instead of reshaping the query at search time (like
HyDE), reshape the documents during preparation. Pre-generate the
questions each chunk answers, and let the retriever match question
against question.
--- PAGE 145 ---
How It Works
Document augmentation modifies only the preparation phase of the pipeline.
Once the augmented entries are stored, retrieval and generation work exactly
as in chapter 1.
Step 1: Chunk the Document
The document is split into chunks using any chunking strategy: fixed-size
from chapter 1, semantic from chapter 9, or any other approach. The
augmentation technique is agnostic to how chunks are created. It works with
whatever chunks exist.
Step 2: Generate Questions for Each Chunk
For each chunk, a language model receives the chunk text and a prompt
asking it to generate a set of questions that could be answered by this
content. A typical configuration generates anywhere from five to forty
questions per chunk, depending on the chunk’s length and density.
The generation can happen at two granularity levels. Document-level
generation feeds a larger text passage (potentially spanning multiple
chunks) to the language model and generates questions for the whole
passage. This captures broader themes and cross-sentence relationships.
Fragment-level generation feeds each individual chunk separately,
producing questions tightly scoped to that chunk’s specific content.
Fragment-level generation produces more precise questions but requires
more language model calls.
The generated questions are cleaned and deduplicated. Questions that do
not end with a question mark or that are too vague are filtered out.
Step 3: Store Questions Alongside Chunks
Each generated question becomes its own entry in the vector store, embedded
just like a regular chunk. The critical detail is the metadata: each question
entry carries a pointer back to the parent chunk it was generated from. The
original chunk is also stored in the database as a regular entry.
The vector store now contains two types of entries: original chunks
(declarative text) and generated questions (interrogative text), all living in the
--- PAGE 146 ---
same embedding space. The following pipeline diagram shows the complete
preparation flow.
A horizontal pipeline diagram with four stages connected by smooth arrows flowing left to right. Stage
1: a document page icon, label “Doc” below. Stage 2: the document split into three colored rectangular
chunks, label “Chunks” below. Stage 3: a brain icon with multiple small question mark bubbles
emerging from it, label “Generate” below. Stage 4: a cylindrical database with both rectangular chunk
icons and small question bubble icons inside it, label “Store” below. Clean sans-serif labels.
Step 4: Retrieve via Question Matching
At query time, the user’s question is embedded and matched against the full
vector store. Because the database now contains question-shaped entries, the
user’s question is likely to land nearest to one of the generated questions
rather than to an original chunk. The embeddings of two questions about the
same topic are naturally close: “How do carbon taxes reduce emissions?” and
“What role do carbon pricing mechanisms play in cutting emissions?” are
structurally similar and semantically aligned.
When a generated question is the closest match, the system follows the
metadata pointer to retrieve the parent chunk. This parent chunk, the original
declarative text, is what gets passed to the generation model as context. The
--- PAGE 147 ---
user never sees the generated question. It served only as a matchmaking
intermediary.
Algorithm Sketch: Document Augmentation
1. Split document into chunks
2. For each chunk:
   a. Send chunk text to LLM with question-generation prompt
   b. LLM returns N questions answerable by this chunk
   c. Clean and deduplicate the questions
3. Embed each original chunk and store in vector store
4. Embed each generated question and store with pointer to parent chunk
5. At query time: embed user query, search full database
6. If closest match is a generated question, retrieve its parent chunk
7. Pass parent chunk to generation model as context
The following diagram illustrates the retrieval flow: a user query matching
against a generated question, which then leads back to the parent chunk used
for answer generation.
--- PAGE 148 ---
A retrieval flow diagram. On the left, a rounded rectangle with a question mark labeled “Query”. An
arrow leads to a cylindrical database in the center. Inside the database, the arrow connects to a small
question bubble icon (a generated question) that glows, indicating a match. A curved arrow from the
question bubble points to a rectangular chunk icon, also inside the database, showing the parent-chunk
lookup. The chunk then flows via an arrow to a lightbulb icon on the right, labeled “Answer”. Three
labels total: “Query”, “Match”, “Answer”.
The Mirror Image of HyDE
This technique and HyDE from chapter 6 are mirror images of each other.
They solve the same problem, the shape mismatch between questions and
documents in embedding space, but they attack it from opposite sides.
HyDE transforms the query at search time. It takes the user’s question,
generates a hypothetical answer (document-shaped), and uses that answer’s
embedding to search the vector store. The query is reshaped to look like the
documents.
Document augmentation transforms the documents at preparation time. It
takes each chunk, generates questions (query-shaped), and stores those
questions in the vector store. The documents are reshaped to look like
queries.
--- PAGE 149 ---
The tradeoff is when the work happens. HyDE adds latency at query time
(one language model call per query) but requires no extra preparation.
Document augmentation adds cost at preparation time (language model calls
per chunk) but adds zero latency at query time. For a system with a stable
document collection and high query volume, document augmentation front-
loads the investment. For a system with frequently changing documents and
lower query volume, HyDE avoids the cost of regenerating questions every
time the collection updates. The following illustration places both approaches
side by side.
A split-screen comparison divided by a thin dotted vertical line. Left half labeled “HyDE” at top. Shows a
question mark icon at the top transforming into a document-shaped rectangle via a downward arrow
with a brain icon, then searching against chunk rectangles in a database. Right half labeled “Augment”
at top. Shows chunk rectangles each sprouting question bubble icons via brain icons during preparation,
all stored in a database, then a question mark searching against the question bubbles. The left side has a
label “Query Time” and the right has “Prep Time”.
Choosing the Number of Questions
The number of questions generated per chunk is a tuning parameter. More
questions mean more surface area for matching: a wider net of phrasings that
--- PAGE 150 ---
could catch user queries. But more questions also mean more entries in the
vector store (increasing storage and potentially slowing search) and more
language model calls during preparation.
A practical range is five to forty questions per chunk. Short, dense chunks
(a few sentences about a single fact) might need only five questions because
there are limited ways to ask about the content. Long, rich chunks covering
multiple aspects of a topic benefit from more questions to cover the different
angles a user might approach the content from.
The quality of generated questions matters as much as the quantity.
Questions should be specific enough to uniquely identify their parent chunk.
A question like “What is climate change?” is too broad; it could match dozens
of chunks. A question like “How does the cap-and-trade system incentivize
companies to reduce emissions below their allocated permits?” points clearly
to one specific chunk.
When to Use This
Best for:
Systems with a stable document collection that is updated infrequently,
where the one-time preparation cost is amortized over many queries
High query volumes where any per-query latency (like HyDE’s LLM call)
is unacceptable
Knowledge bases where user queries are consistently question-shaped
and declarative chunks are the norm
Overkill when:
Documents change frequently, forcing constant regeneration of
questions
Chunks are already question-and-answer pairs (FAQ databases), where
the shape mismatch does not exist
The retriever already achieves strong performance without
augmentation, or HyDE is already closing the gap at acceptable latency
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
--- PAGE 151 ---
Factor Impact
Preparation cost High: one LLM call per chunk (or per
document), generating N questions each
Query latency None: all augmentation happens during
preparation
Storage Increased: N extra vector entries per chunk
Retrieval quality Significant improvement when user queries are
question-shaped
Compared to HyDE from chapter 6:
Both solve the query-document shape mismatch. HyDE works at query
time with zero preparation cost; document augmentation works at
preparation time with zero query-time cost. For stable collections with high
traffic, document augmentation is the better investment. For dynamic
collections with lower traffic, HyDE is lighter weight. In some systems, both
can be used together: augment the documents with questions during
preparation, and also apply HyDE at query time for an extra matching boost.
Key Takeaways
User queries are question-shaped, but stored chunks are declarative. This
shape mismatch creates embedding distance even when the content is a
perfect match.
Document augmentation bridges the gap by generating questions from
each chunk during preparation and storing them alongside the original
content in the vector store.
At query time, the user’s question matches against stored questions
(same shape), then the system follows a metadata pointer to retrieve the
parent chunk.
This is the mirror image of HyDE: HyDE transforms the query to look
like a document, while document augmentation transforms documents
to look like queries.
The key tradeoff is when the work happens. Document augmentation
front-loads the cost during preparation, adding zero latency at query
--- PAGE 152 ---
time.
The number of generated questions per chunk is a tuning parameter.
More questions increase matching surface area but also increase storage
and preparation cost.
--- PAGE 153 ---
Chapter 12: Fusion Retrieval: Combining Keyword
and Semantic Search
Running two different retrievers in parallel, one that matches exact
words and one that matches meaning, then merging their results with
weighted scores for broader, more reliable retrieval.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, the basic RAG
pipeline).
The Problem
Semantic search, the vector-based retrieval from chapter 1, understands
meaning. It finds chunks about “greenhouse gas emissions” even when the
query says “carbon dioxide output” because the embeddings capture the
conceptual overlap. This is its strength. But meaning-based matching has a
blind spot: it can overlook exact terminology that matters.
Imagine a climate researcher querying a knowledge base with “BPA
regulation 2019.” The term “BPA” (bisphenol A) is a specific chemical
identifier. The researcher needs chunks that mention this exact acronym. But
the embedding model, trained on broad language patterns, maps “BPA
regulation” into a general region of chemical regulation concepts. It retrieves
chunks about chemical safety standards, environmental regulations, and
plastic manufacturing policies. These are semantically relevant. But the one
chunk that actually discusses BPA by name and cites the 2019 regulatory
update ranks fourth, behind three chunks that discuss similar topics without
ever mentioning BPA.
The reverse problem exists too. A pure keyword search finds every
document containing “BPA” and “2019” but misses a chunk that discusses
“the ban on bisphenol compounds in food-contact plastics enacted last year”
because neither “BPA” nor “2019” appears in its text. The meaning is there.
The words are not. The following illustration shows these two blind spots side
--- PAGE 154 ---
by side: semantic search missing an exact keyword match, and keyword
search missing a meaning-based match.
A split-screen comparison. The left half labeled “Semantic” shows a query bubble at top with a
magnifying glass, connected to three result chunks below. Two chunks are highlighted in warm orange
(good semantic matches) but a third chunk with the label “BPA” on it is grayed out and pushed aside,
missed despite containing the exact term. The right half labeled “Keyword” shows the same query
bubble connected to chunks below, where the chunk with “BPA” is highlighted but another chunk
without the exact word, shown glowing faintly to suggest it holds the right meaning, is grayed out and
missed.
Neither retrieval method alone covers the full space of relevant results.
Semantic search misses when exact terms matter. Keyword search misses
when meaning matters more than vocabulary. The question is not which one
is better. The question is whether they can work together.
The Core Idea
Fusion retrieval runs two retrievers in parallel on the same query and the
same set of chunks: a vector-based retriever for semantic matching and a
--- PAGE 155 ---
keyword-based retriever for term matching. Each retriever returns its own
ranked list of results. The system then normalizes the scores from both lists,
applies configurable weights, and merges them into a single ranked list.
Think of searching for a recipe in a large cookbook collection. One
approach is to flip to the index at the back of each book and look up exact
ingredient names: “saffron,” “arborio rice,” “parmesan.” The index tells you
exactly which pages mention those words. The other approach is to describe
what you want to an experienced chef who has read every book in the
collection: “something warm and creamy, with an aromatic spice and a slow-
cooked richness.” The chef recommends recipes based on the overall
character you described, even if the recipe calls the spice “the world’s most
precious threads” rather than “saffron.” The index catches what the chef
misses. The chef catches what the index misses. Combining both gives you a
more complete set of matches than either alone. The following illustration
shows this: an index lookup and a chef recommendation producing different
but complementary results.
--- PAGE 156 ---
A cookbook scene. On the left side, a hand flips to an index page at the back of a book, with lines of text
and a finger pointing to a specific entry. Below the hand, two recipe cards emerge as results. On the
right side, a chef figure with a tall hat holds a book open, reading and thinking, with a thought bubble
showing flavor concepts. Below the chef, two different recipe cards emerge. In the center bottom, all
four recipe cards merge into a single neat stack. A label “Index” on the left, “Chef” on the right, and
“Merge” at the bottom center.
The core insight: semantic search and keyword search have
complementary blind spots. Fusion retrieval combines them so that
neither blind spot can cause a relevant chunk to be missed entirely.
How It Works
Fusion retrieval modifies both the preparation phase (one additional index)
and the retrieval phase (two parallel searches plus score merging). The
generation phase stays the same as in chapter 1.
Step 1: Build Two Indexes from the Same Chunks
--- PAGE 157 ---
During preparation, the document is chunked normally. Each chunk is then
indexed twice.
The first index is the vector store from chapter 1. Each chunk is embedded
into a vector, and the vectors are stored in a similarity-search database. This
index supports semantic queries: given a query embedding, it returns chunks
whose embeddings are closest in meaning.
The second index is a BM25 index. BM25, short for “Best Match 25,” is a
keyword-matching algorithm that has been the backbone of text search
engines for decades. Unlike the vector store, BM25 does not use embeddings
at all. It works purely with words: which words appear in the query, which
words appear in each chunk, and how statistically significant those matches
are.
BM25 scores a chunk based on three factors. First, term frequency: how
often each query word appears in the chunk. A chunk that mentions “BPA”
five times scores higher than one that mentions it once. Second, inverse
document frequency: how rare each query word is across the entire
collection. A common word like “the” scores almost nothing because it
appears everywhere. A specific term like “BPA” scores high because it appears
in few chunks, making its presence meaningful. Third, length
normalization: longer chunks naturally contain more words and are more
likely to match by coincidence, so BM25 adjusts scores to prevent long
chunks from dominating the results. The following diagram shows how
BM25 scores a chunk by counting term matches, weighting by rarity, and
adjusting for length.
--- PAGE 158 ---
A concept close-up diagram of BM25 scoring. At the top, a query with two highlighted words. Below, a
single chunk rectangle with horizontal text lines, where two words are circled and highlighted in warm
orange (matching terms). Three annotation arrows point to three factor boxes arranged horizontally
below the chunk. The left box labeled “Frequency” shows a small tally count icon. The middle box
labeled “Rarity” shows a bar chart with one tall bar among short bars, indicating a rare term. The right
box labeled “Length” shows two rectangles of different sizes with a balance scale icon. At the bottom,
these three factors merge via arrows into a single rounded rectangle labeled “Score.”
Both indexes are built from the same chunks during the same preparation
phase. The chunks are identical. Only the indexing method differs. One index
understands meaning. The other counts words.
Step 2: Query Both Retrievers in Parallel
At query time, the user’s query is sent to both retrievers simultaneously. The
vector retriever embeds the query and returns the top-K most semantically
similar chunks with their similarity scores. The BM25 retriever tokenizes the
query into individual words and returns the top-K chunks with the highest
keyword-match scores.
These two result sets often overlap partially. Some chunks rank highly in
both lists because they are both semantically relevant and contain the right
--- PAGE 159 ---
keywords. Some appear in only one list. The chunks unique to the vector
results are the ones that match in meaning but lack the exact query words.
The chunks unique to the BM25 results are the ones that contain the exact
words but are not semantically closest. The following illustration shows two
overlapping result sets: chunks found by vector search, chunks found by
BM25, and the shared chunks where both agree.
Two overlapping circles in a Venn diagram style. The left circle in muted blue is labeled “Vector” and
contains three small rectangle icons representing chunks unique to semantic search. The right circle in
warm orange is labeled “BM25” and contains three small rectangle icons representing chunks unique to
keyword search. The overlapping center region in a blended color contains two rectangle icons
representing chunks found by both. Small arrows from both sides point toward the overlap,
emphasizing shared results.
Step 3: Normalize the Scores
Here is where fusion retrieval requires care. The two retrievers return scores
on completely different scales. A vector similarity score might be 0.87, 0.83,
0.79 (cosine similarity, bounded between -1 and 1). A BM25 score might be
14.2, 9.8, 3.1 (unbounded, based on term statistics). You cannot simply add
--- PAGE 160 ---
0.87 and 14.2 and call it a combined score. The BM25 scores would dominate
by sheer magnitude.
The solution is to normalize both sets of scores onto the same scale before
combining them. The most common approach maps all scores to a range
between 0 and 1, preserving the relative ranking within each retriever while
making the two sets comparable.
Distribution-based normalization computes the mean and standard
deviation of all scores from a single retriever, then defines the effective
minimum as three standard deviations below the mean and the effective
maximum as three standard deviations above. Each score is then rescaled to
fall between 0 and 1 within this range. This method is robust because it bases
the scale on the statistical distribution of the actual scores rather than on the
two most extreme values.
Min-max normalization is simpler: it takes the lowest and highest
scores from a retriever and linearly maps the entire range to 0-1. The lowest-
scoring chunk gets 0, the highest gets 1, and everything else falls
proportionally in between. This works well when the score distribution is
fairly uniform but can be distorted by a single outlier score. The following
illustration shows two sets of scores on different scales being normalized to
the same 0-1 range before merging.
--- PAGE 161 ---
Two horizontal colored bars on the left: a blue bar labeled “Vector” with dots at the 0.7-to-0.9 range
and an orange bar labeled “BM25” with dots at the 0-to-15 range. A large arrow labeled “Scale” points
right to a single unified bar on the right labeled “0-1,” where all six dots (blue and orange) are plotted
together on the same normalized scale.
After normalization, a score of 0.8 from the vector retriever and a score of 0.8
from the BM25 retriever represent the same thing: a strong match according
to that retriever’s criteria.
Step 4: Weight and Merge
With both score sets normalized, the system combines them using
configurable weights. A typical configuration might assign 0.6 to the vector
retriever and 0.4 to BM25. For a chunk that appears in both result sets, its
final score is the weighted sum: (0.6 x vector score) + (0.4 x BM25 score). For
a chunk that appears in only one result set, only that retriever’s weighted
score contributes.
The weights reflect a judgment about which retrieval signal matters more
for a given application. A knowledge base serving technical queries with
specific terminology (product codes, chemical names, legal citations) might
--- PAGE 162 ---
weight BM25 higher because exact terms carry critical information. A
knowledge base serving open-ended research questions might weight the
vector retriever higher because conceptual matching matters more than
vocabulary.
Algorithm Sketch: Fusion Retrieval
1. Preparation: chunk the document
2. Build vector index (embed each chunk, store in vector store)
3. Build BM25 index (tokenize each chunk, build term statistics)
4. At query time: send query to both retrievers in parallel
5. Vector retriever returns top-K chunks with similarity scores
6. BM25 retriever returns top-K chunks with keyword-match scores
7. Normalize both score sets to 0-1 range
8. Combine: final_score = (w_vector x vector_score) + (w_bm25 x bm25_score)
9. Rank all chunks by final_score
10. Pass top results to generation model
The complete pipeline ties all the steps together. The following diagram
shows the full flow: a query entering two parallel retrieval paths, scores being
normalized and merged, and the combined results feeding into generation.
--- PAGE 163 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram. On the far left, a search icon labeled “Query”. From the query, two
parallel arrows split: one upward to a cylinder database icon labeled “Vector” and one downward to a
rectangle index icon labeled “BM25”. Each retriever produces a short ranked list (shown as small
stacked rectangles with score numbers). Both lists feed into a central merge icon (two arrows
converging into a funnel) labeled “Merge”. From the merge, a single ranked list emerges, flowing right
to a lightbulb icon labeled “Answer”. Six labels total: “Query”, “Vector”, “BM25”, “Merge”, “Answer”.
Clean sans-serif font.
Choosing the Weights
The retriever weights are the primary tuning knob of fusion retrieval. They
control how much each signal influences the final ranking.
A 50/50 split gives equal say to both retrievers. This is a safe default when
you have no prior knowledge about query patterns. A split favoring the vector
retriever (such as 60/40 or 70/30) works well for general-purpose question
answering where most queries are natural language questions. A split
favoring BM25 works well for applications where users search for specific
identifiers, technical terms, or proper nouns.
In practice, the optimal weights depend on the query distribution. Some
systems tune them experimentally: run a set of representative queries,
measure retrieval quality at different weight settings, and pick the
--- PAGE 164 ---
configuration that maximizes the number of correct chunks retrieved. Others
adapt the weights dynamically, detecting whether the query contains specific
technical terms (shift toward BM25) or is a broad conceptual question (shift
toward vector search).
Optional: Multi-Query Expansion
Some fusion retrieval implementations also support generating multiple
query variants from the original question, similar to the query
transformations from chapter 5. When enabled, each variant is sent through
both retrievers, broadening the candidate set further. This option is
independent of the core fusion mechanism and adds both cost and
complexity, so many systems keep it disabled and focus on the dual-retriever
fusion alone.
When to Use This
Best for:
Knowledge bases where queries mix natural language questions with
specific terms, acronyms, or proper nouns
Applications where some queries need exact keyword matches (product
codes, legal citations, technical specifications) and others need
conceptual matching
Systems serving a diverse user base with varied query styles
Overkill when:
Queries are consistently one type: all natural language questions (vector
search alone suffices) or all keyword lookups (BM25 alone suffices)
The document collection is small enough that vector search alone
retrieves the right chunks reliably
Latency is extremely constrained and running two retrievers in parallel
adds unacceptable overhead
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
--- PAGE 165 ---
Factor Impact
Preparation cost Moderate: one additional index (BM25)
alongside the vector store
Query latency Low to moderate: two retrievers run in parallel,
plus score merging
Storage Moderate: BM25 index adds storage
proportional to collection size
Retrieval quality Significant improvement when queries contain
both conceptual and keyword signals
Compared to vector-only retrieval from chapter 1:
Vector-only retrieval works well for natural language queries where
meaning matters more than exact terms. Fusion retrieval adds a safety net for
queries where specific words carry critical information. The cost is an
additional index and slightly more complex retrieval logic. For most
production systems serving diverse queries, the improvement in recall
justifies the modest overhead.
Compared to query transformations from chapter 5:
Query transformations reshape the question to improve a single retriever’s
results. Fusion retrieval adds an entirely different retrieval mechanism. These
approaches are complementary: you can apply query transformations to the
user’s query and then run the transformed query through both the vector and
BM25 retrievers. Each addresses a different source of retrieval failure.
Key Takeaways
Vector search understands meaning but can miss exact keyword matches.
BM25 keyword search matches exact terms but is blind to meaning. Each
has blind spots the other covers.
Fusion retrieval runs both retrievers in parallel on the same query and
merges their results into a single ranked list.
Score normalization is essential because the two retrievers produce
scores on different scales. Distribution-based or min-max normalization
maps both to a common 0-1 range.
--- PAGE 166 ---
The retriever weights control the balance between semantic and keyword
signals. Typical configurations range from 50/50 to 70/30 depending on
query patterns.
BM25 is a keyword-matching algorithm based on term frequency, inverse
document frequency, and length normalization. It requires no
embeddings and complements vector search by catching exact-term
matches that semantic search overlooks.
The additional cost is modest: one extra index during preparation and
one extra retrieval pass at query time. Both retrievers can run in parallel,
so the latency increase is small.
--- PAGE 167 ---
Chapter 13: Reranking
A second, more careful look at retrieved chunks: using specialized
models to re-evaluate and reorder candidates before they reach the
language model.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline)
The Problem
Consider a user who asks: “What is the capital of France?” The vector store
contains several chunks, among them:
“The capital of France is great.”
“The capital of France is beautiful.”
“The capital of France is huge.”
“I really enjoyed my trip to Paris, France. The city is beautiful and the
food is delicious. Such a great capital city.”
Embedding-based similarity search compares the query embedding against
each chunk embedding. The first three chunks are structurally almost
identical to the query. They start with the same words, share the same
sentence pattern, and contain all the same key terms. Their embeddings sit
very close to the query embedding. The fourth chunk talks about visiting
Paris, mentions France only in passing, and uses different vocabulary. Its
embedding is farther away.
The vector store ranks the first three chunks highest. But they are almost
useless as context for answering the question. None of them names Paris.
The fourth chunk, ranked lower by the initial retriever, is the only one that
actually contains the answer.
This is a different problem from the complementary blind spots that
fusion retrieval addresses in chapter 12. There, the issue was that keyword
--- PAGE 168 ---
search and semantic search each miss what the other catches. Here, the
problem lives within a single retriever: embedding similarity is a rough proxy
for relevance, and rough proxies sometimes get the ranking wrong. A chunk
can be semantically close to a query without being a good answer to it. The
following illustration shows a query with its retrieved chunks, where the
most informative result is buried below superficially similar but unhelpful
ones.
A query at the top with a ranked list of retrieved chunks below. The top three chunks glow with high
similarity scores but contain vague, uninformative text (represented by short gray lines). The fourth
chunk, ranked lower with a dimmer score, contains the actual answer (represented by a bright orange
highlight). Red X marks on the top chunks and a green checkmark on the fourth chunk show the
mismatch between similarity ranking and true relevance.
The Core Idea
Reranking adds a second evaluation stage after retrieval. Instead of trusting
the initial similarity ranking as final, the system retrieves a deliberately broad
--- PAGE 169 ---
set of candidates and then re-evaluates each one more carefully using a
model that reads the query and chunk together.
Think of it as a hiring process. A job posting attracts hundreds of
applications. The first screening is fast and coarse: scan each resume for a few
key qualifications and pass anyone who looks plausible. This produces a
shortlist of, say, thirty candidates. Some are excellent fits. Some made it
through because their resume happened to use the right keywords without
having the right experience. The second stage is slower and more thorough:
an interview panel reads each shortlisted resume carefully against the actual
job requirements, scores every candidate on how well they fit the role, and
selects the top three to hire. The following illustration shows this two-stage
process: a wide funnel of many candidates narrowed through careful
evaluation to a final shortlist.
A hiring process scene. On the left, a large stack of many resume icons (small rectangles) enters a wide
funnel labeled “Retrieve”. Below the funnel, a moderate shortlist of candidate icons emerges. These
candidates pass through a second, narrower funnel labeled “Rerank” where a magnifying glass icon
inspects each one. At the bottom, only three candidate icons emerge with green checkmarks,
representing the final top results.
--- PAGE 170 ---
The core insight: initial retrieval optimizes for speed by comparing
chunks independently. Reranking optimizes for accuracy by reading
each chunk alongside the query. The two-stage design gives you both.
How It Works
Reranking inserts a single new step between retrieval and generation. The
preparation phase is unchanged. The generation phase is unchanged. Only
the retrieval phase grows: retrieve broadly, then rerank carefully. The
following diagram shows the full reranking pipeline at a glance.
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right with four stages connected by arrows. Stage 1: a
search icon labeled ‘Query’. Stage 2: a database cylinder labeled ‘Retrieve’ with several document
rectangles emerging. Stage 3: a funnel shape labeled ‘Rerank’ with a magnifying glass, where
documents are filtered and reordered. Stage 4: a lightbulb icon labeled ‘Answer’ with only the top-
ranked documents beside it.
--- PAGE 171 ---
Step 1: Retrieve a Broad Candidate Set
The retriever fetches a larger-than-usual set of candidates. Where a standard
pipeline might retrieve the top three to five chunks, a reranking pipeline
retrieves the top ten to thirty. The exact number depends on how many
candidates the reranker can afford to evaluate. Retrieving more candidates
increases the chance that a truly relevant chunk makes it into the candidate
set, even if the initial retriever did not rank it highly.
Any retriever works for this first stage. Vector search, BM25, or the fusion
retrieval from chapter 12 can all serve as the initial retriever. The reranker
does not care how the candidates were found. It only cares about evaluating
them.
Step 2: Score Each Candidate
This is the heart of reranking. Each candidate chunk is paired with the query
and evaluated to produce a relevance score. Two approaches are common, and
both rely on a key architectural distinction.
Why joint evaluation matters: bi-encoders versus cross-encoders
The initial retrieval in most RAG systems uses a bi-encoder architecture.
A bi-encoder processes the query and each document through the same
embedding model, but independently. The query becomes one vector. Each
document becomes another vector. Relevance is estimated by comparing the
two vectors, typically using cosine similarity. This independence is what
makes vector search fast: document embeddings can be precomputed and
stored, so at query time only the query needs to be embedded.
But independence comes at a cost. Because the model never sees the query
and document at the same time, it cannot detect fine-grained interactions
between them. It cannot notice that a specific phrase in the document directly
answers a specific part of the query. It can only compare two compressed
summaries of what each text means.
A cross-encoder takes the opposite approach. It feeds the query and
document into a single model as one combined input. The model reads both
simultaneously, attending to every word in both texts at once. This joint
processing lets it detect subtle relevance signals: whether a particular
sentence actually answers the question, whether the document’s context
matches the query’s intent, whether key terms are used in the same sense.
The tradeoff is that cross-encoders cannot precompute anything. Every query-
document pair must be processed from scratch, making them too slow for
--- PAGE 172 ---
searching an entire collection but ideal for re-evaluating a small shortlist. The
following diagram compares the two architectures side by side.
A side-by-side comparison. Left side labeled “Bi-Encoder”: a query icon and a document icon each pass
through separate encoder boxes (two parallel paths), producing two separate vector arrows that meet
at a comparison point with a similarity score. Right side labeled “Cross-Encoder”: a query icon and a
document icon enter the same single encoder box together (one combined path), and a single relevance
score emerges from the bottom. The left path is marked with a clock icon suggesting speed. The right
path is marked with a target icon suggesting accuracy.
LLM-based reranking
The first approach uses a general-purpose language model as the
evaluator. The system constructs a prompt containing both the query and the
candidate chunk, and asks the model to rate relevance on a numerical scale,
typically one to ten. The model reads the pair, reasons about how well the
chunk addresses the query, and returns a score.
This approach is the most flexible. The prompt can include custom criteria:
“Rate how directly this passage answers the question. Consider whether it
provides specific facts, not just topically related discussion.” Different
applications can define relevance differently by adjusting the prompt.
--- PAGE 173 ---
The cost is significant. Each candidate requires a full language model call.
With twenty candidates, reranking adds twenty LLM calls to every query. This
can dominate the latency and cost of the entire pipeline. The following
illustration shows the LLM evaluating a query-chunk pair and producing a
relevance score.
A concept close-up showing LLM-based reranking. At the top, a query bubble and a document rectangle
sit side by side. Both feed into a large rounded rectangle in the center representing a language model,
with a small brain icon inside. From the bottom of the model, a single output emerges: a circular badge
showing a score number. Three such query-chunk pairs are shown in a vertical sequence, each
producing its own score badge, suggesting the process repeats for every candidate.
Algorithm Sketch: LLM-Based Reranking
1. Retrieve top-K candidates from any retriever (K = 10-30)
2. For each candidate chunk:
   a. Construct prompt: "Rate relevance of this document to this query (1-10)"
   b. Send query + chunk to language model
   c. Parse returned score
3. Sort all candidates by score (highest first)
4. Select top-N results (N = 3-5)
5. Pass selected chunks to generation model
--- PAGE 174 ---
Cross-encoder reranking
The second approach uses a cross-encoder model specifically trained for
relevance scoring. These models are much smaller than general-purpose
language models. They take a query-document pair as input and output a
single relevance score, typically in milliseconds rather than seconds.
Cross-encoder rerankers are trained on large datasets of query-document
pairs labeled as relevant or not relevant. Through this training, they learn to
recognize patterns that indicate genuine relevance: whether the document
answers the question, whether key entities match, whether the document’s
context aligns with the query’s intent. They perform this evaluation far faster
than a language model because they are purpose-built for this single task.
The tradeoff is rigidity. A cross-encoder reranker scores relevance as it was
defined during training. You cannot add custom criteria through prompting.
If your application has an unusual definition of relevance (for example,
preferring recent documents over older ones regardless of content), the cross-
encoder may not capture it without fine-tuning on domain-specific data.
Algorithm Sketch: Cross-Encoder Reranking
1. Retrieve top-K candidates from any retriever (K = 10-30)
2. Form pairs: [(query, chunk_1), (query, chunk_2), ..., (query, chunk_K)]
3. Feed all pairs through cross-encoder model (batch processing)
4. Receive one relevance score per pair
5. Sort candidates by score (highest first)
6. Select top-N results (N = 3-5)
7. Pass selected chunks to generation model
Step 3: Reorder and Select
Regardless of which scoring method is used, the final step is the same. The
system sorts all candidates by their new relevance scores and selects the top
N. This reordered shortlist replaces the original retrieval results and is passed
to the generation model.
The impact can be substantial. Chunks that the initial retriever ranked
fifth or tenth may jump to first place once a reranker evaluates them against
the actual query. Chunks that ranked first due to surface-level similarity may
drop when the reranker determines they do not actually address the question.
The following illustration shows the same set of chunks before and after
reranking, with the most informative chunk moving from a low initial rank to
the top position.
--- PAGE 175 ---
A split-screen comparison divided by a thin dotted vertical line. Left half labeled “Before” shows a
numbered list of five chunk rectangles. The top three are highlighted in light gray (superficially similar
but uninformative). The fourth chunk is highlighted in warm orange (the truly relevant one), buried at
rank four. Right half labeled “After” shows the same chunks reordered. The orange chunk is now at
rank one at the top. The gray chunks have dropped to lower positions. Arrows between the two halves
show the movement of chunks up and down.
When to Use This
Best for:
Applications where retrieval quality directly affects answer quality and
errors are costly, such as medical, legal, or financial knowledge bases
Complex or nuanced queries where surface-level embedding similarity is
a poor indicator of true relevance
Systems that already retrieve broad candidate sets (ten or more chunks)
and need to select the best subset
Overkill when:
--- PAGE 176 ---
Queries are simple and the vector store reliably returns the right chunks
in the top three to five results
Extreme latency constraints make even cross-encoder evaluation too
slow
The document collection is small enough that retrieval quality is already
high without additional refinement
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Query latency Moderate (cross-encoder) to high (LLM-
based): adds per-candidate evaluation time
Preparation cost None: reranking requires no changes to
indexing or storage
Complexity Low to moderate: one additional step in the
retrieval pipeline
Accuracy gain Significant for complex queries; modest for
simple ones
Choosing between LLM-based and cross-encoder reranking:
Cross-encoder reranking is the default choice for most applications. It
offers the best balance of speed, cost, and accuracy. A cross-encoder can
evaluate twenty candidates in the time it takes an LLM to evaluate one, at a
fraction of the cost. Use LLM-based reranking when you need custom
relevance criteria that a pre-trained cross-encoder cannot capture, or when
your application already makes LLM calls and can absorb the additional
latency.
Compared to relevancy grading from chapter 3:
Relevancy grading makes a binary keep-or-discard decision on each chunk.
Reranking assigns a numerical score and reorders the entire candidate set.
Grading filters noise. Reranking refines ranking. The two are complementary:
you can retrieve broadly, rerank to find the best candidates, and then apply
relevancy grading as a final check to discard any remaining low-quality
chunks.
--- PAGE 177 ---
Key Takeaways
Embedding similarity is a fast approximation of relevance, not a precise
measure. Chunks that are semantically close to a query are not
necessarily good answers to it.
Reranking adds a second evaluation stage that reads each query-chunk
pair jointly, catching relevance signals that independent embeddings
miss.
A bi-encoder embeds query and document separately for fast
comparison. A cross-encoder reads them together for accurate scoring.
Reranking exploits the cross-encoder’s accuracy on a small candidate set
filtered by the bi-encoder’s speed.
Cross-encoder models are purpose-built for relevance scoring: fast,
cheap, and accurate. They are the default choice for most reranking
scenarios.
LLM-based reranking offers maximum flexibility through custom
prompting but at significant cost in latency and expense.
Reranking requires no changes to the preparation phase. It sits between
retrieval and generation, re-evaluating and reordering whatever the
retriever produced.
--- PAGE 178 ---
Chapter 14: Hierarchical Indices
Organizing retrieval into two levels, summaries for finding the right
sections and detailed chunks for finding the right answers, so the
system searches smart before it searches deep.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline)
The Problem
Imagine a company with a knowledge base spanning hundreds of internal
documents: HR policies, engineering specifications, financial reports, product
manuals. A user asks: “What are the parental leave benefits for employees in
Europe?” The vector store contains thousands of chunks from all of these
documents. Some chunks from the engineering specifications mention
“leave” in the context of error handling. Some chunks from the product
manuals discuss “benefits” of a software feature. Some chunks from financial
reports reference “European operations” in a revenue context.
With a flat index, every chunk competes equally. The retriever compares
the query embedding against all chunk embeddings and returns the top
matches by similarity score. A chunk about software error handling that
happens to share the words “leave” and “policy” can score higher than the
actual parental leave policy buried in the HR document. The retriever has no
sense of structure. It does not know that only the HR documents are relevant
to this question. It treats a chunk from page 47 of an engineering spec the
same as a chunk from page 3 of the benefits handbook.
This problem grows with collection size. In a small knowledge base with
twenty chunks, the right answer is usually near the top. In a large knowledge
base with ten thousand chunks from dozens of documents, the relevant
chunks are needles in a haystack. The flat retriever must score every needle
and every piece of hay equally, with no structural shortcut to narrow the
search. The following illustration shows a query competing against thousands
--- PAGE 179 ---
of undifferentiated chunks, where relevant results are scattered among
irrelevant ones from unrelated documents.
A query bubble at the top with an arrow pointing down into a dense grid of many small rectangles
representing chunks. Most rectangles are light gray, representing irrelevant chunks from various
documents. A few rectangles scattered throughout the grid are highlighted in warm orange,
representing the actually relevant chunks buried among thousands of irrelevant ones. The overall
impression is of searching for a few needles in a large haystack of chunks.
The Core Idea
Hierarchical indices add structure to the flat search space by creating two
levels of representation for every document. The first level captures what
each document section is about in a broad summary. The second level
contains the detailed chunks that hold specific information. At query time,
the system searches summaries first to find which sections are relevant, then
searches only the detailed chunks within those sections.
Think of it as navigating a department store. You would not wander every
aisle on every floor looking for headphones. You check the floor directory
--- PAGE 180 ---
first: electronics is on the third floor. Then you go to the third floor and
browse only the audio aisle. The directory gives you a fast, coarse answer
(which floor), and the aisle search gives you the precise answer (which
product). Hierarchical indices work the same way: summaries are the floor
directory, and detailed chunks are the individual aisles. The following
illustration shows this two-level navigation, from a broad directory to a
specific search within the right section.
A two-panel department store diagram. Left panel titled “Department Store” shows a building cross-
section with three colored floors: blue with a gear icon, orange with a headphone icon, and gray with a
book icon. A person silhouette at the bottom checks a floor directory, with an arrow pointing to the
orange floor. Right panel titled “Specific Floor Section” shows a zoomed-in view of the orange floor with
clearly separated shelves of items and a magnifying glass icon labeled “Search” hovering over one shelf.
A dashed arrow connects the two panels.
The core insight: instead of searching everything at full detail, first
narrow the field using summaries, then search deeply within the
narrowed results. Coarse-to-fine beats flat-and-exhaustive when the
collection is large.
--- PAGE 181 ---
How It Works
Hierarchical indexing changes both the preparation phase and the retrieval
phase. During preparation, the system builds two separate indexes instead of
one. During retrieval, it performs two searches in sequence instead of one.
Step 1: Summarize Each Document Section
During preparation, the system processes each document at a coarse level
first. Every logical section of the document (a page, a chapter, or a natural
section boundary) gets summarized by a language model. The summary
captures the main topics, key entities, and overall purpose of that section in a
few sentences.
These summaries serve as high-level signposts. A summary of a parental
leave policy page might read: “This section describes parental leave benefits
including duration, eligibility, and regional variations for European and North
American employees.” This summary does not contain the specific benefit
details, but it tells the retriever exactly what kind of information lives in that
section.
Each summary carries metadata linking it back to its source section. This
link is critical. It is what allows the second-stage search to know where to
look. The metadata might be a page number, a section identifier, or a
document-plus-section pair. The following diagram shows how each
document section produces both a summary and a set of detailed chunks,
with metadata connecting them.
--- PAGE 182 ---
A vertical diagram showing the preparation process for one document. At the top, a document icon.
Below it, the document splits into three horizontal sections (shown as stacked rectangles in different
shades). From the left side of each section, an arrow leads to a small condensed rectangle labeled with a
page number, representing the summary. From the right side of each section, arrows lead to multiple
smaller rectangles, also labeled with the same page number, representing detailed chunks. The
summaries collect into a cylinder on the left labeled “Summaries” and the chunks collect into a cylinder
on the right labeled “Chunks”. Thin dotted lines connect each summary to its corresponding chunks,
showing the metadata link.
Step 2: Chunk and Index at the Detail Level
In parallel with summarization, the system chunks each document section
into smaller pieces using any standard chunking strategy. These detailed
chunks are the same kind used in a basic RAG pipeline: they contain the
actual content the generation model needs to formulate an answer.
Each detailed chunk also carries the same section metadata as its parent. A
chunk from page 3 of the HR document is tagged with page 3. A chunk from
page 47 of the engineering spec is tagged with page 47 of that document. This
shared metadata is the bridge between the two levels of the hierarchy.
The system now has two vector stores. The summary store contains one
embedding per document section, each representing the broad topic of that
--- PAGE 183 ---
section. The detailed store contains many embeddings per section, each
representing a specific piece of content. Both stores are searchable
independently, but the hierarchical retrieval strategy queries them in
sequence.
Step 3: Retrieve in Two Stages
At query time, the system performs a coarse-to-fine search. First, it queries
the summary store. The query “What are the parental leave benefits for
employees in Europe?” is compared against all section summaries.
Summaries about HR benefits, employee policies, and European operations
score highest. Summaries about engineering error handling, product features,
and financial revenue score low. The system selects the top few summaries,
typically three to five.
Each selected summary identifies a source section through its metadata.
The system now knows: “Look at pages 2, 3, and 7 of the HR benefits
document.” It takes these section identifiers and uses them as a filter for the
second search.
The second search queries the detailed chunk store, but only considers
chunks whose metadata matches the selected sections. Instead of searching
ten thousand chunks, the system searches perhaps fifty or a hundred. Within
this focused subset, the retriever finds the specific chunks about parental
leave duration, European eligibility rules, and benefit amounts. These are the
chunks that actually answer the question.
Algorithm Sketch: Hierarchical Retrieval
1. Receive user query
2. Search summary vector store for top-K relevant summaries (K = 3-5)
3. Extract section identifiers from each selected summary's metadata
4. Search detailed chunk vector store, filtering by section identifiers
5. Return the top-N detailed chunks from the filtered results (N = 3-5)
6. Pass these chunks to the generation model
The full pipeline connects both stages into a single retrieval flow. The
following diagram shows a query passing through the summary search,
narrowing to specific sections, then searching within those sections for
detailed chunks that feed into the final answer.
--- PAGE 184 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right. On the far left, a search icon labeled “Query”. An
arrow leads to a small cylinder labeled “Summaries”. From the summaries cylinder, a short arrow
leads to a filter icon (a funnel shape) labeled “Filter” with small page number badges falling out of it.
From the filter, an arrow leads to a larger cylinder labeled “Chunks”. From the chunks cylinder, an
arrow carries three small highlighted rectangles to a lightbulb icon on the far right labeled “Answer”.
Above the pipeline, a curved bracket spans from the summaries to the filter, labeled “Coarse”. Below the
pipeline, a curved bracket spans from the filter to the chunks, labeled “Fine”. Five labels total: “Query”,
“Summaries”, “Filter”, “Chunks”, “Answer”.
Why Summaries Work Better Than Raw Text for the First Pass
A natural question is: why not just search the original document pages
directly, without summaries? The answer is that summaries are purpose-built
for matching. A raw document page might spend most of its content on
specific details (exact benefit percentages, eligibility dates, exception clauses)
while only implicitly being “about” parental leave. The embedding of that raw
page reflects all of those details, diluting the topical signal.
A summary distills the page down to its core topic. Its embedding is a
cleaner representation of what the section is about, free from the noise of
specific details. When the query asks a broad topical question, the summary
embedding aligns more naturally with the query embedding than the raw
--- PAGE 185 ---
page embedding would. The summary acts as a semantic label for the section,
making the first-stage search more accurate.
The Role of Metadata
The metadata linking summaries to chunks is the mechanism that makes
hierarchical retrieval possible. Without it, the two vector stores would be
independent and the system would have no way to connect a relevant
summary to its detailed chunks.
The simplest metadata scheme uses page numbers. Each summary stores
the page number it was generated from. Each chunk stores the page number
of the content it was extracted from. When the first stage identifies page 3 as
relevant, the second stage filters the chunk store to only chunks where the
page metadata equals 3. The following illustration shows how matching
metadata values connect a summary to its corresponding chunks across the
two stores.
--- PAGE 186 ---
A concept close-up showing metadata linking. On the left, three summary cards stacked vertically, each
with a small colored tag showing a number (1, 2, 3). The middle summary (number 2) is highlighted in
warm orange. On the right, a column of nine small chunk rectangles, each with a matching numbered
tag. Three chunks tagged with number 2 are highlighted in warm orange, matching the selected
summary. Thin dotted arrows connect the highlighted summary to its three matching chunks. The
non-matching chunks (tagged 1 and 3) remain in light gray. A label “Page 2” appears near the
matching items.
More sophisticated schemes use document identifiers combined with section
numbers, allowing hierarchical retrieval across multi-document collections
where page numbers alone would be ambiguous.
When to Use This
Best for:
Large document collections where flat retrieval returns too many
irrelevant chunks from unrelated sections
Knowledge bases spanning multiple documents on different topics,
where narrowing by document or section dramatically reduces noise
--- PAGE 187 ---
Applications where documents have natural section boundaries
(chapters, pages, numbered sections) that map well to a summary
hierarchy
Overkill when:
The collection is small enough that flat retrieval reliably returns the right
chunks in the top results
Documents are short and homogeneous, so every chunk is roughly
equally relevant or irrelevant
Query latency is extremely constrained, since hierarchical retrieval adds
the cost of LLM summarization during preparation and two sequential
searches at query time
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Preparation cost High: every section needs an LLM-generated
summary and its own embedding
Query latency Moderate: two sequential vector searches
instead of one
Storage Moderate: an additional vector store for
summaries
Accuracy gain Significant for large, multi-topic collections;
modest for small, focused ones
Compared to flat retrieval from chapter 1:
Flat retrieval is simpler, cheaper, and works well for small to medium
collections. Hierarchical retrieval pays off when the collection is large and
diverse enough that flat search consistently pulls in chunks from irrelevant
sections. If your flat retriever already returns the right chunks in the top five,
adding a summary layer adds cost without benefit. If your flat retriever buries
the right chunks under irrelevant noise from other documents, hierarchical
indices solve that directly.
Compared to reranking from chapter 13:
Reranking re-evaluates a broad candidate set to find the best chunks.
Hierarchical retrieval narrows the candidate set before detailed search even
begins. The two approaches are complementary: you can use hierarchical
indices to focus on the right sections, retrieve chunks from those sections,
--- PAGE 188 ---
and then rerank the results for maximum precision. Hierarchical retrieval
reduces noise at the source. Reranking refines whatever remains.
Key Takeaways
Flat retrieval treats every chunk equally, which works for small
collections but struggles when thousands of chunks from unrelated
documents compete for the same top positions.
Hierarchical indices create two levels: document-section summaries for
coarse topic matching and detailed chunks for precise content retrieval.
At query time, summaries identify which sections are relevant. Metadata
links those sections to their detailed chunks. The detailed search is then
filtered to only the relevant sections.
Summaries produce cleaner topical embeddings than raw document
pages because they distill the section’s subject without the noise of
specific details.
The preparation cost is higher than flat indexing because every section
requires an LLM-generated summary. This is a one-time cost that pays
off at query time through more focused retrieval.
Hierarchical retrieval complements other techniques: combine it with
reranking for additional precision, or with fusion retrieval for broader
coverage within the selected sections.
--- PAGE 189 ---
Chapter 15: Dartboard Retrieval
Balancing relevance and diversity in retrieval results so the system
returns chunks that are both on-topic and collectively cover different
aspects of the answer.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline),
Chapter 12 (fusion retrieval and score normalization concepts)
The Problem
Imagine a knowledge base about climate science that has been built from
dozens of overlapping sources: textbook chapters, policy briefs, research
summaries, and encyclopedia entries. Many of these sources cover the same
foundational topics with similar wording. When a user asks “What are the
main causes of climate change?”, the vector store returns the five most
similar chunks. But because several sources explain greenhouse gases in
nearly identical language, the top five results are essentially the same
paragraph repeated. Three of the five chunks contain almost word-for-word
the same content about CO2 emissions and the greenhouse effect.
The system has found relevant content, but it has found the same relevant
content multiple times. The generation model receives a context window
stuffed with redundancy. It has no information about related causes like
deforestation, methane from agriculture, or industrial processes, because
those chunks were pushed out of the top five by near-identical duplicates.
The answer ends up narrow and repetitive, covering one facet of the question
thoroughly while missing others entirely.
This is the redundancy problem. Standard top-K retrieval ranks chunks
purely by similarity to the query. It has no mechanism to notice that the
second result says essentially the same thing as the first. In dense knowledge
bases where multiple documents cover similar ground, pure similarity
ranking produces an echo chamber: the same information echoed multiple
--- PAGE 190 ---
times, squeezing out the diversity the generation model needs. The following
illustration shows a query returning near-identical results while diverse
relevant content sits untouched.
A query bubble at the top connected by arrows to three identical-looking document chunks stacked on
the left side, each with the same blue content pattern indicating duplicate information. On the right side,
three different chunks with varied colors (orange, blue, gray) sit faded and unused, representing diverse
relevant information that was pushed out of the top results by the duplicates.
The Core Idea
Dartboard retrieval solves the redundancy problem by changing how the
system selects its final results. Instead of simply taking the top-K most
similar chunks, it runs a greedy selection process that considers two factors
for each candidate: how relevant it is to the query, and how different it is
from the chunks already selected.
Think of a news editor assembling the evening broadcast. The editor has
twenty candidate stories, all newsworthy. If she picks the five highest-rated
stories and they all happen to be about the same earthquake, the broadcast
--- PAGE 191 ---
wastes four segments repeating the same event. A skilled editor picks the top
earthquake story first, then deliberately looks for the next most important
story that also covers new ground. Maybe a political development, then a
science breakthrough, then an economic report. Each selection balances
newsworthiness with the information it adds beyond what previous
selections already covered. Dartboard retrieval applies this same logic to
chunk selection: relevance is the newsworthiness, and diversity is the new
ground covered. The following illustration shows this editorial balancing act,
choosing stories that are both important and collectively varied.
A split comparison. On the left side labeled “Same”, five story cards stacked vertically all show an
identical wave icon, representing five stories about the same event. On the right side labeled “Varied”,
five story cards each show a different icon (a wave, a building, a beaker, a chart, a globe), representing
five stories covering different topics. An editor figure in the center points toward the varied set with a
thumbs-up icon above.
--- PAGE 192 ---
The core insight: after selecting each chunk, penalize candidates that
are too similar to what has already been chosen. This shifts the
selection toward documents that add new information rather than
restating what the system already has.
How It Works
The algorithm operates in two phases: first, it retrieves a broad candidate pool
from the vector store; then it applies a greedy selection process to pick the
final results from that pool.
Step 1: Oversample the Candidate Pool
Standard retrieval asks for K results. Dartboard retrieval asks for many more,
typically three to five times K. If the system needs five final chunks, it
retrieves fifteen to twenty-five candidates from the vector store using
standard similarity search.
This oversampling is essential. The greedy selection in the next step will
discard candidates that are too similar to already-chosen ones. If the system
starts with only five candidates, it has no room to be selective. A larger pool
gives the algorithm space to find chunks that are both relevant and diverse.
The following diagram shows the oversampling step, where a wide net of
candidates is cast before the refinement begins.
--- PAGE 193 ---
A horizontal flow from left to right. On the left, a search icon labeled “Query” with an arrow pointing to
a cylinder labeled “Store”. From the cylinder, a wide spray of about fifteen small rectangles fans out to
the right, representing the oversampled candidate pool. Most rectangles are light blue, a few are warm
orange. A bracket below labeled “3x” indicates the oversampling factor. On the far right, a dashed
outline contains space for only five rectangles, showing the final selection is much smaller than the
candidate pool.
Step 2: Score the First Selection on Relevance Alone
The algorithm begins by selecting the single most relevant candidate: the
chunk whose embedding is closest to the query embedding. This is the same
choice standard top-K retrieval would make. No diversity consideration
applies yet because there are no previous selections to be diverse from.
Step 3: Iteratively Select Remaining Chunks
For each remaining slot, the algorithm computes a combined score for every
unselected candidate. This score has two components.
The first component is relevance: how similar the candidate is to the
original query. This is the same similarity signal used in standard retrieval.
--- PAGE 194 ---
The second component is diversity: how different the candidate is from
all previously selected chunks. The algorithm measures the distance between
the candidate and each already-selected chunk, then uses the maximum
distance as the diversity signal. A candidate that is far from at least one
selected chunk gets a higher diversity score than one that is close to all of
them.
These two components are combined with configurable weights. A system
that values relevance more than diversity gives a higher weight to the
relevance component. A system that needs maximum coverage gives more
weight to diversity. Typical configurations weight them equally, letting
neither factor dominate.
The candidate with the highest combined score is selected, and the process
repeats. Each iteration, the diversity landscape shifts because there is a new
selected chunk to be different from. A candidate that was too similar to the
first selection might score well on diversity relative to the second and third
selections. The algorithm naturally adapts its preferences as the result set
fills up. The following illustration zooms into the scoring mechanism,
showing how a single candidate is evaluated on both relevance and diversity.
--- PAGE 195 ---
A concept close-up diagram. In the center, a single candidate chunk rectangle in warm orange. Above
it, a downward arrow from a query bubble with a horizontal bar gauge filled about 70% labeled
“Relevance”. Below the candidate, upward arrows from two already-selected chunks in muted blue,
with a second bar gauge filled about 80% labeled “Diversity”. To the right, a combined bar gauge shows
the total score as the weighted sum, labeled “Score”. A small plus symbol sits between the two
component gauges.
Algorithm Sketch: Dartboard Selection
1. Retrieve N candidates from vector store (N = K * oversampling_factor)
2. Compute distance from query to each candidate
3. Compute pairwise distances between all candidates
4. Select the candidate closest to query as first result
5. For each remaining slot:
   a. Compute relevance_score (similarity to query) for each unselected candidate
   b. Compute diversity_score (max distance from any selected chunk)
   c. Combined score = relevance_weight * relevance + diversity_weight *
diversity
   d. Select the candidate with the highest combined score
6. Return the K selected chunks
The Smoothing Parameter
--- PAGE 196 ---
The raw distances between embeddings can be noisy. A small difference in
cosine distance might not reflect a meaningful difference in content. The
algorithm uses a smoothing parameter called sigma that converts raw
distances into probability-like scores through a log-normal function. A
smaller sigma makes the algorithm more sensitive to small distance
differences, creating sharper distinctions between candidates. A larger sigma
smooths out these differences, treating candidates with similar distances
more equally. A typical starting value is around 0.1, adjustable based on the
embedding model and collection characteristics.
Putting It All Together
The full pipeline starts with a user query, oversamples from the vector store,
then feeds the candidate pool through the greedy dartboard selection. The
output is a set of chunks that collectively cover more informational ground
than a pure similarity ranking would. The technique also works naturally
with other retrieval approaches: if you use fusion retrieval from chapter 12,
the fused similarity scores can serve as the relevance input, and if you use
cross-encoder reranking from chapter 13, the reranker scores can replace raw
embedding distances. The following diagram shows the complete flow from
query to diversified results.
--- PAGE 197 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right in five stages connected by smooth arrows. Stage 1: a
search icon labeled “Query”. Stage 2: a cylinder labeled “Store” with a wide arrow indicating broad
retrieval. Stage 3: a rounded box containing many small rectangles labeled “Pool”. Stage 4: a funnel
shape labeled “Select” with a small target icon and a spread-pattern icon inside it. Stage 5: five
rectangles in different colors (blue, orange, gray, green, teal) labeled “Result”. Clean sans-serif font.
When to Use This
Best for:
Knowledge bases with significant content overlap, where multiple
documents cover the same topics in similar language
Dense collections where the same information appears in multiple forms
(summaries, full articles, and FAQs all about the same subject)
Questions that benefit from multi-faceted answers, where covering
different aspects of a topic matters more than finding the single best
chunk
Overkill when:
--- PAGE 198 ---
The collection has minimal redundancy, with each chunk covering
distinct content
The retrieval pipeline already handles diversity through other means,
such as fusion retrieval from chapter 12 which naturally diversifies by
combining keyword and semantic results
The system only needs one or two chunks, where redundancy has little
room to manifest
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Query latency Low: pairwise distance computation is fast on
small candidate sets
Implementation complexity Low: the core algorithm is a short greedy loop
over distance matrices
Preparation cost None: operates at query time on standard
embeddings with no index changes
Accuracy gain Significant when redundancy is the primary
failure mode; minimal when it is not
Compared to standard top-K from chapter 1:
Standard top-K is simpler and faster. If your knowledge base has minimal
redundancy, top-K already returns diverse results naturally, and dartboard
selection adds computation without benefit. Dartboard retrieval specifically
targets the failure mode where the top results are near-duplicates. If you
observe that your retriever frequently returns repetitive chunks, dartboard
selection is a lightweight fix that requires no changes to the index or
preparation pipeline. The following illustration contrasts the two approaches:
standard top-K returns near-identical results, while dartboard retrieval selects
a diverse set that covers more ground.
--- PAGE 199 ---
A split comparison with a dotted vertical divider. Left side labeled ‘Top-K’ shows a query bubble above
five identical blue document rectangles stacked vertically, each with a red circular icon indicating
redundancy. Right side labeled ‘Dartboard’ shows the same query bubble above five document
rectangles in varied colors (blue, orange, gray, teal, orange) with green checkmarks on three and
orange dots on two, indicating a diverse selection.
Compared to reranking from chapter 13:
Reranking re-scores candidates based on deeper relevance analysis but
does not explicitly optimize for diversity. A reranker might still place five
similar-but-relevant chunks at the top. Dartboard selection and reranking
solve different problems and can be combined: rerank the oversampled
candidates first for better relevance scores, then apply dartboard selection to
ensure the final set is also diverse.
Key Takeaways
Standard top-K retrieval optimizes only for relevance, which leads to
redundant results when knowledge bases contain overlapping content.
--- PAGE 200 ---
Dartboard retrieval adds a diversity objective to the selection process,
ensuring each chosen chunk adds new information beyond what
previous selections already cover.
The algorithm is a greedy loop: select the most relevant chunk first, then
iteratively select chunks that score highest on a weighted combination of
relevance and diversity.
Oversampling the initial candidate pool gives the algorithm room to
trade off between relevance and diversity without sacrificing too much of
either.
The technique requires no changes to the index, embedding model, or
preparation pipeline. It operates entirely at query time as a post-retrieval
selection layer.
Configurable weights let you tune the balance between relevance and
diversity based on your collection’s characteristics and your application’s
needs.
--- PAGE 201 ---
Chapter 16: Multi-Modal RAG with Captioning
Extending retrieval beyond text by converting images, tables, and
charts into searchable descriptions so the system can find answers that
live in visual content.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline)
The Problem
Imagine a company’s engineering team uses a knowledge base built from
technical PDFs: architecture diagrams, performance benchmark tables,
deployment flowcharts, and design specifications. An engineer asks: “What
does the system architecture look like for the data processing pipeline?” The
answer is right there in the document, on page 12, in a detailed diagram
showing microservices, message queues, and data flows. But the RAG system
never sees it.
Standard RAG extracts text from documents and ignores everything else.
When the system processes a PDF, it reads the paragraphs and headings but
skips the figures, charts, tables rendered as images, and diagrams. Those
visual elements are invisible to the pipeline. They never get chunked, never
get embedded, never enter the vector store. The information they contain is
unreachable.
This is not a minor gap. In many professional documents, the most
information-dense elements are visual. A single architecture diagram conveys
what would take pages of prose to describe. A benchmark table compresses
dozens of data points into a compact grid. When RAG treats documents as
text-only, it operates with partial information, answering queries about the
prose while remaining blind to the visuals. The following illustration shows a
document where text content is searchable but image content sits locked
away, invisible to the retrieval system.
--- PAGE 202 ---
A document page with text paragraphs highlighted in blue as searchable content on one side, while an
image and a table on the other side are grayed out with lock icons, showing they are invisible to the
retrieval system
The Core Idea
Multi-modal RAG with captioning bridges the gap between visual and text-
based retrieval by adding a translation step. Before indexing, a vision-capable
language model examines every image, table, and diagram extracted from the
document and writes a text description of what it sees. These descriptions,
called captions, are then chunked and embedded alongside the regular text,
entering the same vector store.
Think of it like a radio sports announcer calling a live game. The audience
at home cannot see the field. The announcer watches the action unfold and
translates it into vivid words: “The striker cuts left past the defender, sends a
cross into the box…” Listeners can now follow the game entirely through the
announcer’s descriptions. The captioning model plays exactly this role. It
watches the visual content and translates it into text so the search system,
--- PAGE 203 ---
which only works with words, can find and retrieve it. The following
illustration shows this translation in action, where visual content is described
in words to make it accessible to a text-based system.
A visual-to-text translation scene: on the left a colorful image representing visual content, in the center
a figure with a microphone icon acting as the announcer or captioner, and on the right text lines
representing the generated description, with an arrow flowing left to right showing the translation
The core insight: you do not need a special visual search engine. You
convert visual content into text, and then your existing text-based
retrieval pipeline handles it naturally.
How It Works
The technique modifies only the preparation phase of the RAG pipeline. The
query phase remains unchanged. Once images are captioned and indexed,
they are just text chunks like any other.
--- PAGE 204 ---
Step 1: Extract Text and Images Separately
The first step processes the source document and separates its content into
two streams. A PDF parser reads each page, extracting the raw text into one
collection and the embedded images into another. Each extracted image is
tagged with metadata noting which page it came from, so the system can trace
any caption back to its source location.
This dual extraction is the foundation of the multi-modal approach.
Standard RAG performs only the text extraction and discards the images.
Here, both streams are preserved for processing. The following diagram
shows a document splitting into its text and image components.
A document icon on the left with two diverging arrows: the top arrow leads to a stack of text rectangles
in muted blue labeled Text, the bottom arrow leads to a row of image frames in warm orange labeled
Images, showing the dual extraction of content types
Step 2: Caption Every Image with a Vision Model
Each extracted image is sent to a vision-capable language model with a
prompt asking for a concise, retrieval-optimized summary. The model
--- PAGE 205 ---
examines the image and produces a text description capturing the key
information it contains.
