Semantic search on structured data lets users ask fuzzy, meaning-based
questions that traditional exact-match queries cannot handle.
The approach scales well for retrieval speed but requires one embedding
per row, which can become costly for very large datasets.
--- PAGE 41 ---
Chapter 3: Reliable RAG: Building Trust Through
Verification
Adding quality checkpoints to the RAG pipeline so the system filters
irrelevant evidence, catches hallucinated answers, and traces every
claim back to its source.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (Simple RAG). Understanding of the basic retrieval
and generation pipeline.
The Problem
The basic RAG pipeline retrieves chunks and passes them to the language
model. But there is a problem we glossed over: retrieval is not perfect.
Similarity search returns the closest chunks by meaning, but “closest” does
not always mean “relevant.”
Imagine a user asks about agentic design patterns in AI. The system
retrieves four chunks from a collection of articles. Three contain useful
information about specific patterns like reflection, tool use, and planning. But
the fourth chunk is a page footer stuffed with navigation links, subscription
prompts, and generic calls to action. It scored well in similarity search
because it happened to mention the topic in passing, but it contains no
substantive information. The language model now has three good sources
and one piece of noise. At best, the noise is ignored. At worst, it dilutes the
answer or introduces irrelevant information.
And there is a second, deeper problem. Even when all the retrieved chunks
are perfectly relevant, the language model can still hallucinate. It might
generate a claim that sounds plausible but appears nowhere in the provided
context. The user reads the answer, trusts it because they know the system
“looks things up,” and makes a decision based on fabricated information. This
is arguably more dangerous than hallucination without RAG, because the
user has a false sense of security. The illustration below shows both failure
--- PAGE 42 ---
modes side by side: irrelevant chunks sneaking into the context, and the
model adding unsupported claims to its answer.
A four-stage pipeline labeled ‘Query’, ‘Search’, ‘Chunks’, ‘Answer’. The Chunks stage shows four
document rectangles, three with green checkmarks and one with a red X. The Answer stage shows a
card with a red-highlighted bottom line and warning triangle, representing a fabricated claim.
The Core Idea
Reliable RAG adds verification checkpoints to the basic pipeline. Instead of
blindly trusting every retrieved chunk and every generated answer, the
system stops at critical points and asks: “Is this actually good?”
Think of it as adding quality control to a factory assembly line. A basic
factory takes raw materials, runs them through machines, and ships the
product. A reliable factory does the same thing, but adds inspection stations
along the way. One inspector checks incoming materials before they enter
production. Another inspector examines the finished product before it leaves
the building. A third inspector traces the product back to its source materials
--- PAGE 43 ---
for accountability. The illustration below shows this: a factory line with three
inspection checkpoints, each catching a different type of problem.
A factory assembly line with three quality inspection stations. The first station checks incoming
materials and rejects bad ones, the second station examines the finished product, and the third station
traces the product back to source materials with connecting lines.
The core insight: retrieval gives you evidence, but not all evidence is
good. Generation gives you answers, but not all answers are faithful.
Reliable RAG adds the judgment layer that simple RAG is missing.
How It Works
Reliable RAG wraps three verification steps around the basic pipeline from
chapter 1. The retrieval and generation steps remain unchanged. What
changes is what happens between and after them.
--- PAGE 44 ---
Step 1: Retrieve Candidate Documents
The user’s question is embedded and searched against the vector store,
exactly as in chapter 1. The system retrieves a set of candidate chunks,
typically four or more. The word “candidate” matters. In simple RAG,
whatever comes back from the vector store goes straight to the language
model. In reliable RAG, retrieved chunks must pass an inspection before they
are used.
Step 2: Grade Each Document for Relevancy
This is the first verification checkpoint. A language model examines each
retrieved chunk individually and asks a simple question: “Is this chunk
relevant to the user’s question?”
The grading is intentionally lenient. The grader is not looking for a perfect
match. It checks for keyword overlap or semantic connection to the question.
The goal is to filter out obvious noise: pages that scored well in vector search
but contain no useful information about the topic. A chunk that mentions the
topic even briefly passes. A chunk that is entirely off-topic or contains only
boilerplate content does not.
Think of this as a bouncer at a venue entrance. The bouncer is not judging
quality or ranking guests. They are checking one thing: do you belong here or
not? Most guests get in. But the ones wearing the wrong badge, the
navigation menus, the subscription prompts, the unrelated tangents, get
turned away at the door. The following illustration shows retrieved chunks
lining up at a checkpoint, where each is quickly judged as relevant or not.
--- PAGE 45 ---
Four document chunks approach a checkpoint gate. A guard examines each one. Three chunks glow
green and pass through with checkmarks. One chunk glows red and is turned away with an X mark.
The green chunks continue forward along an arrow.
The result is a filtered set of documents. Only the chunks that pass relevancy
grading move forward to the generation step. This simple filter can
meaningfully improve answer quality because it removes noise that would
otherwise distract or mislead the language model.
Algorithm Sketch: Relevancy Grading
1. For each retrieved chunk:
2.   Send chunk and original question to a grading LLM
3.   Ask: "Is this chunk relevant to the question? Yes or no."
4.   If yes, keep the chunk
5.   If no, discard it
6. Pass only the relevant chunks to the generation step
Step 3: Generate an Answer
--- PAGE 46 ---
The language model receives only the filtered, relevant chunks along with the
original question. This step is identical to the generation step in simple RAG,
but the input is cleaner. With the noise removed, the model has a better
signal-to-noise ratio and produces a more focused, accurate answer.
Step 4: Check for Hallucinations
This is the second verification checkpoint, and it addresses the deeper
problem. Even with perfect context, language models can generate
information that is not present in the source material. The hallucination
checker compares the generated answer against the retrieved documents and
asks: “Is every claim in this answer supported by the facts provided?”
This is a fundamentally different question from relevancy grading.
Relevancy grading asks “Is this chunk useful for the question?” Hallucination
checking asks “Is this answer faithful to the chunks?” One looks backward at
the inputs. The other looks forward at the output.
Think of it as a fact-checker at a newspaper. The journalist writes the
article based on interview transcripts and source documents. The fact-checker
reads the article alongside those same sources and verifies that every
statement can be traced back to one of them. If the journalist added a claim
that appears in no source, the fact-checker flags it. The illustration below
shows this process: a generated answer is compared line by line against the
source documents, with supported claims marked as verified and an
unsupported claim flagged.
--- PAGE 47 ---
A generated answer text on the left with three statements. Two statements have green checkmarks and
dotted lines connecting them to matching passages in source documents on the right. One statement has
a red flag icon with no connecting line, indicating it is not grounded in any source.
The hallucination checker returns a simple binary judgment: grounded or not
grounded. In a production system, an answer flagged as not grounded could
trigger several responses. The system might regenerate the answer with
stricter instructions, try a different set of retrieved chunks, or return the
answer with a warning label. The appropriate response depends on the
application. In a medical or legal context, an ungrounded answer should be
blocked entirely. In a casual search tool, a warning might be enough.
Algorithm Sketch: Hallucination Checking
1. Receive the generated answer
2. Receive the set of documents used for generation
3. Send both to a grading LLM
4. Ask: "Is this answer grounded in the provided facts? Yes or no."
5. If yes, proceed to source highlighting
6. If no, flag or regenerate the answer
--- PAGE 48 ---
Step 5: Highlight Source Segments
The final verification step closes the trust loop by tracing the answer back to
its sources. A language model examines the generated answer alongside the
source documents and identifies the exact text segments that support each
claim in the answer. These are not paraphrases or summaries. They are
verbatim excerpts from the source documents, along with references to which
document they came from.
This serves two purposes. First, it gives the user a way to verify the answer
themselves. They can read the original passage in context and decide whether
the answer accurately represents the source. Second, it acts as a final quality
signal. If the system cannot find supporting segments for parts of the answer,
that is a red flag that something may have slipped past the hallucination
checker.
Think of it as adding footnotes to a research paper. Every claim is
annotated with a specific citation that points to the exact passage in the
source material. The reader does not have to take the author’s word for it.
They can check. The illustration below shows an answer with highlighted
claims, each connected by a line to the specific text passage in the source
document that supports it.
--- PAGE 49 ---
A citation diagram labeled ‘Answer’ on the left and ‘Sources’ on the right. The answer card has three
color-coded bands (blue, orange, green) with small circled numbers 1, 2, 3. Two source document cards
on the right have matching colored bands. Thin curved lines connect matching colors between answer
and sources.
Algorithm Sketch: Source Highlighting
1. Receive the generated answer and the source documents
2. Send both to a language model
3. Ask: "Which exact segments from the documents support this answer?"
4. Extract verbatim text segments from each relevant document
5. Return segments with document IDs and source references
6. Present answer to user with linked source attributions
The Complete Pipeline
Putting all the steps together, the reliable RAG pipeline looks like the basic
pipeline from chapter 1 with three verification checkpoints inserted at critical
junctures. After retrieval, the relevancy grader filters the candidate chunks.
After generation, the hallucination checker verifies groundedness. After
verification, the source highlighter traces claims back to exact passages. The
--- PAGE 50 ---
diagram below shows the complete flow from question to verified, attributed
answer.
A horizontal pipeline diagram showing the full reliable RAG flow. Starting with a question on the left,
then retrieve, then a checkpoint labeled “Grade” that filters chunks, then generate, then a checkpoint
labeled “Verify” that checks groundedness, then a checkpoint labeled “Trace” that highlights sources,
ending with a verified answer on the right with source citations.
Choosing Models for Verification
An important practical detail: the verification steps do not need to use the
same large, expensive model used for final answer generation. Relevancy
grading and hallucination checking are binary classification tasks. They ask
simple yes-or-no questions. A smaller, faster model handles these well.
Source highlighting requires more nuance, as the model must identify exact
text matches, but even this can use a mid-sized model.
This layered approach to model selection keeps costs manageable. Use a
small model for the high-volume grading steps (one call per retrieved chunk),
a small model for the single hallucination check, and reserve the most
capable model for the final generation. In practice, the verification steps add
--- PAGE 51 ---
two to four extra LLM calls per query, but the per-call cost can be kept low by
choosing efficient models for each task.
When to Use This
Best for:
Applications where answer accuracy is critical (legal, medical, financial)
Systems serving non-expert users who cannot independently verify
answers
Production RAG systems that need to maintain user trust over time
Any context where “I don’t know” is better than a confident wrong
answer
Overkill when:
You are building a casual Q&A tool where approximate answers are
acceptable
Your document corpus is small and highly focused, making irrelevant
retrievals unlikely
Latency is your primary concern and every millisecond matters
You are in early prototyping and need fast iteration over reliability
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency
High: adds 2-3 extra LLM calls per query for
grading, hallucination check, and source
highlighting
Complexity Medium: three additional verification steps on
top of simple RAG
Cost
Moderate: multiple LLM calls per query,
though verification can use smaller, cheaper
models
Accuracy gain Significant: filters noise, catches hallucinations,
provides source attribution
Compared to simple RAG:
--- PAGE 52 ---
Simple RAG trusts the retrieval and generation steps completely. Reliable
RAG adds a trust-but-verify approach. The cost is additional latency and LLM
calls. The benefit is a system that filters bad evidence, catches fabricated
claims, and lets users trace every answer back to its source. For applications
where accuracy matters more than speed, this tradeoff is almost always worth
making. The diagram below shows this contrast: a simple pipeline that passes
everything through unchecked versus a reliable pipeline with verification
gates at each stage.
A split-screen comparison. Top half labeled “Simple RAG” shows a straight pipeline from question to
retrieve to generate to answer with no checkpoints. Bottom half labeled “Reliable RAG” shows the same
pipeline but with three diamond-shaped checkpoint gates between stages, each labeled Grade, Verify,
and Trace.
Key Takeaways
Not every chunk returned by vector search is relevant. A relevancy
grading step filters out noise before it reaches the language model.
--- PAGE 53 ---
Language models can hallucinate even when given good context. A
hallucination checker verifies that the generated answer is grounded in
the provided facts.
Source highlighting traces every claim in the answer back to exact text
segments in the source documents, giving users a way to verify for
themselves.
These three verification steps, relevancy grading, hallucination checking,
and source highlighting, transform a basic RAG pipeline into a system
that earns user trust.
The tradeoff is additional latency and cost from extra LLM calls. Using
smaller, faster models for verification steps keeps this cost manageable.
--- PAGE 54 ---
Chapter 4: Proposition Chunking: Breaking
Documents Into Atomic Facts
Decomposing text into self-contained, single-fact statements so retrieval
returns precise answers instead of noisy paragraphs.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (Simple RAG). Understanding of chunking,
embeddings, and vector search.
The Problem
In chapter 1, we split documents into chunks of roughly a thousand
characters and embedded each chunk as a single vector. This works well for
broad, thematic questions. But consider what happens when a user asks a
very specific factual question.
Suppose your knowledge base contains an article about the history of
space exploration. One chunk might read: “Neil Armstrong walked on the
Moon in 1969 during the Apollo 11 mission. The mission launched from
Kennedy Space Center in Florida. Buzz Aldrin joined Armstrong on the lunar
surface, while Michael Collins orbited above in the command module.” That
is three distinct facts packed into a single chunk: who walked on the Moon,
where the mission launched from, and who stayed in orbit.
Now a user asks: “Who stayed in the command module during Apollo 11?”
The system retrieves the chunk because it is semantically relevant. But the
answer to the question is just one sentence buried inside a paragraph about
several different facts. The language model must sift through the surrounding
information to find the relevant needle. Worse, the other facts in the chunk
dilute the embedding. The chunk’s vector represents a blend of all three
topics, not any single one precisely. This means a question about launch sites
might also retrieve this same chunk, even though only one sentence is
relevant. The following illustration shows this: a single chunk containing
multiple facts, with a specific question only matching one of them.
--- PAGE 55 ---
A text chunk containing three distinct facts highlighted in blue, orange, and green. A magnifying glass
with a question targets only the orange fact, but the entire chunk is retrieved, with the blue and green
facts shown as noise.
The core issue is granularity. Character-based chunking does not care about
the boundaries between ideas. It slices text at arbitrary points, grouping
unrelated facts together and sometimes splitting a single thought across two
chunks. The result is chunks that are semantically “blurry,” representing a
mix of topics rather than one clear idea.
The Core Idea
Proposition chunking takes a fundamentally different approach to
granularity. Instead of splitting text by character count, it uses a language
model to decompose each chunk into individual propositions: atomic, self-
contained statements that each express exactly one fact.
A proposition has five properties. It expresses a single fact. It can be
understood without any surrounding context. It uses full names instead of
--- PAGE 56 ---
pronouns. It includes relevant dates and qualifiers. And it contains one
subject doing or being one thing.
Think of it as the difference between a paragraph in a textbook and a stack
of flashcards made from that paragraph. The paragraph weaves ideas together
into flowing prose. The flashcards isolate each fact onto its own card. When
you search through flashcards, you find exactly the fact you need. When you
search through paragraphs, you find the neighborhood where the fact lives,
along with everything else in that neighborhood. The illustration below
shows a paragraph being decomposed into individual fact cards, each standing
on its own.
A flowing paragraph of text on the left is broken apart by arrows into five separate rectangular fact
cards on the right, each containing a single short statement. The cards are neatly stacked with small
gaps between them.
The core insight: standard chunks are containers of mixed facts.
Propositions are individual facts extracted from those containers. By
embedding individual facts, you make retrieval as precise as the
questions being asked.
--- PAGE 57 ---
How It Works
Proposition chunking wraps around the preparation phase from chapter 1.
The document is still chunked initially, but those chunks are then refined
into propositions before embedding. The query phase remains unchanged.
Step 1: Initial Chunking
The process begins with standard text splitting, just as in chapter 1. The
document is divided into manageable chunks. These initial chunks are not
the final unit of storage. They serve as input for the next step. Keeping them
at a moderate size ensures the language model can process each one in a
single call without exceeding its context window.
Step 2: Generate Propositions From Each Chunk
This is the step that defines the technique. A language model reads each
chunk and breaks it down into a list of propositions. The model follows
specific rules for what makes a valid proposition.
Consider a chunk that reads: “Graham suggests that founders should
leverage their strengths rather than conform to traditional managerial
practices. Founder Mode is an emerging paradigm that is not yet fully
documented.” The language model decomposes this into individual
statements:
“Graham suggests that founders should leverage their strengths.”
“Founders should not conform to traditional managerial practices,
according to Graham.”
“Founder Mode is an emerging paradigm.”
“Founder Mode is not yet fully documented.”
Notice what happened. The original two sentences became four propositions.
Each one states a single fact. Each one makes sense on its own. Pronouns like
“their” have been resolved to “founders.” Compound sentences with multiple
claims have been split into separate statements. The diagram below shows
this decomposition process: a chunk entering the language model and
emerging as a list of individual propositions.
--- PAGE 58 ---
A rectangular text chunk enters a box labeled with a brain icon representing the language model. From
the box, four separate smaller cards emerge on the right, each containing a single short statement.
Arrows flow left to right showing the decomposition.
The language model is guided by a prompt that specifies the five criteria for
valid propositions: express a single fact, be self-contained, use full names,
include dates and qualifiers, and contain one subject-predicate relationship.
Few-shot examples in the prompt demonstrate the expected output format,
showing the model what good decomposition looks like before it processes
the actual text.
Algorithm Sketch: Proposition Generation
1. Split document into initial text chunks
2. For each chunk:
3.   Send chunk to a language model with decomposition instructions
4.   Receive a list of atomic propositions
5.   Each proposition: one fact, self-contained, no pronouns, includes qualifiers
6. Collect all propositions across all chunks
Step 3: Quality Check Each Proposition
--- PAGE 59 ---
Not every proposition that comes out of the generation step will be good
enough. Language models sometimes produce statements that are vague,
inaccurate, or missing important context. A second language model acts as a
quality gate, evaluating each proposition on four criteria.
Accuracy: Does the proposition faithfully reflect what the original text
said? A score of 1 to 10.
Clarity: Can someone understand this proposition without reading
anything else? A score of 1 to 10.
Completeness: Does the proposition include the necessary details like
dates, names, and qualifiers? A score of 1 to 10.
Conciseness: Is the proposition focused and free of unnecessary words?
A score of 1 to 10.
Each criterion has a threshold, typically around 7 out of 10. A proposition
must meet the threshold on all four criteria to pass. Those that fail are
discarded. This prevents low-quality statements from polluting the vector
store with noisy or misleading embeddings.
Think of this as peer review for individual facts. The first model writes the
propositions, and the second model reviews them. Only statements that are
accurate, clear, complete, and concise survive the review process. The
illustration below shows propositions passing through a quality gate, with
scores displayed for each criterion.
--- PAGE 60 ---
Several proposition cards approach a checkpoint labeled “Quality Gate.” Each card shows four small
score bars for accuracy, clarity, completeness, and conciseness. Cards with all bars above the threshold
line glow green and pass through. One card with a bar below the threshold glows red and is rejected.
This two-model approach, one for generation and one for evaluation, creates
a checks-and-balances system. The generator does not need to be perfect. It
just needs to produce candidates. The evaluator handles quality control. This
separation of concerns is similar in spirit to the relevancy grading step from
chapter 3, but applied earlier in the pipeline, during preparation rather than
at query time.
Algorithm Sketch: Proposition Quality Check
1. For each generated proposition:
2.   Send proposition and its source chunk to a grading LLM
3.   Receive scores for accuracy, clarity, completeness, conciseness
4.   If all scores meet the threshold (e.g., 7/10), keep the proposition
5.   If any score falls below threshold, discard it
6. Only quality-approved propositions proceed to embedding
Step 4: Embed and Store Propositions
--- PAGE 61 ---
The propositions that pass quality checking are embedded into vectors and
stored in a vector store, exactly as described in chapter 1. The crucial
difference is what each vector represents. In standard RAG, each vector
represents a paragraph-sized chunk containing multiple facts. In proposition
chunking, each vector represents a single fact.
This changes the geometry of the vector space. Instead of having a few
hundred vectors that each cover broad topics, you have many more vectors
that each pinpoint a specific piece of information. The vectors cluster more
tightly around precise meanings rather than spreading across blended topics.
When a query arrives, the nearest vectors in the database are not just “in the
same neighborhood” as the question. They are direct answers to it.
Step 5: Retrieve and Answer
The query phase is unchanged from chapter 1. The user’s question is
embedded, the nearest proposition vectors are retrieved, and those
propositions are passed to the language model as context. But the quality of
the context is different. Instead of receiving paragraphs that happen to
contain the answer somewhere inside them, the model receives individual
facts that directly address the question.
For example, the query “Who stayed in the command module during
Apollo 11?” would retrieve the proposition “Michael Collins orbited above in
the command module during the Apollo 11 mission.” That is the complete
answer, with no surrounding noise. The model simply reads it and responds.
The following diagram shows the complete pipeline from document to
proposition-based retrieval.
--- PAGE 62 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram in two rows. Top row labeled “Prepare” shows: document, then split into
chunks, then LLM decomposes chunks into small fact cards, then quality gate filters cards, then embed
remaining cards as dots in a database. Bottom row labeled “Query” shows: question, embed as a dot,
search database, retrieve matching fact cards, generate answer.
Proposition Retrieval vs. Standard Retrieval
The difference becomes clear when you compare retrieval results side by side.
Take the question “When was the essay Founder Mode published?” With
standard chunk-based retrieval, the system returns a full paragraph that
mentions the publication date but also discusses the essay’s thesis, its
contrast with conventional management wisdom, and the concept of founder
abilities. The answer is in there, but it is one detail among many.
With proposition-based retrieval, the system returns: “Paul Graham
published an essay called Founder Mode in September 2024.” That is the fact
the user asked about, nothing more and nothing less.
This precision has a flip side. Ask a broader question like “What is Founder
Mode about?” and proposition retrieval returns several disconnected facts:
“Founder Mode is an emerging paradigm.” “Founder Mode is not yet fully
documented.” “Paul Graham’s essay challenges conventional wisdom about
--- PAGE 63 ---
scaling startups.” Each is accurate, but they lack the narrative flow that a
longer chunk provides. The standard chunk gives the reader a coherent
paragraph that tells a story. The propositions give precise data points that
must be synthesized.
The illustration below shows this tradeoff: a specific question getting a
precise answer from propositions versus a broad question getting fragmented
responses.
A split-screen comparison. Left side labeled “Specific Question” shows a targeted question with
proposition retrieval returning one precise matching fact card with a green checkmark. Right side
labeled “Broad Question” shows a thematic question with proposition retrieval returning several
disconnected fact cards that lack narrative flow, with a yellow caution icon.
When Propositions Shine and When They Struggle
Proposition chunking excels at questions with clear, factual answers. “Who
founded the company?” “What year did the merger happen?” “Which
countries were involved?” These questions map directly to individual
propositions, and the retrieval is both precise and efficient.
--- PAGE 64 ---
The technique struggles with questions that require understanding
relationships across multiple facts or grasping the overall argument of a
passage. “How does the author’s argument develop?” or “Compare the two
approaches described in the document” require contextual understanding
that individual propositions, by design, do not carry.
This is not a flaw in the technique. It is the defining tradeoff. You are
trading context for precision. The right choice depends on what kinds of
questions your users ask most often.
When to Use This
Best for:
Knowledge bases where users ask specific factual questions (who, what,
when, where)
Documents packed with dense factual information like reports,
encyclopedias, or technical specifications
Systems where retrieval precision matters more than narrative context
Reducing noise in retrieval when standard chunks consistently return
too much irrelevant information
Overkill when:
Your documents are already structured as short, focused paragraphs that
naturally contain one idea each
Users primarily ask broad, thematic questions that benefit from
contextual paragraphs
The added latency and cost of proposition generation during preparation
is prohibitive
Your document collection is small enough that standard chunking
already retrieves well
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Preparation cost High: requires LLM calls to decompose and
quality-check every chunk
--- PAGE 65 ---
Factor Impact
Query latency Same as standard RAG: one embedding + one
search + one LLM call
Storage Higher: many more vectors stored
(propositions outnumber chunks significantly)
Retrieval precision High: each vector represents one fact, enabling
exact-match retrieval
Context richness Lower: individual propositions lack the
narrative flow of paragraph-sized chunks
Compared to standard chunking from chapter 1:
Standard chunking is simpler, cheaper, and preserves narrative context.
Proposition chunking trades that simplicity for precision. The preparation
phase is more expensive because it requires multiple LLM calls per chunk
(one for generation, one for quality checking). But the query phase is
identical in cost and speed. If your users ask specific factual questions and
standard chunking consistently retrieves chunks with too much noise,
proposition chunking is a targeted fix. If your users ask broad questions and
value coherent context, standard chunking may serve you better. The
following illustration shows where each approach falls on the spectrum
between context preservation and retrieval precision.
--- PAGE 66 ---
A horizontal spectrum with “Context” on the left end and “Precision” on the right end. A marker labeled
“Standard Chunks” sits toward the left, showing high context but lower precision. A marker labeled
“Propositions” sits toward the right, showing high precision but lower context. Icons illustrate each: a
paragraph block for context and a single fact card for precision.
Key Takeaways
Proposition chunking uses a language model to decompose text chunks
into atomic, self-contained statements that each express a single fact.
Each proposition uses full names (no pronouns), includes relevant dates
and qualifiers, and can be understood without any surrounding context.
A quality-checking step evaluates every proposition on accuracy, clarity,
completeness, and conciseness, discarding those that fall below
threshold.
Embedding individual propositions instead of paragraph-sized chunks
makes retrieval more precise for specific factual questions.
--- PAGE 67 ---
The tradeoff is context versus precision: propositions excel at pinpoint
factual retrieval but lose the narrative flow that longer chunks provide
for broad or thematic questions.
--- PAGE 68 ---
Chapter 5: Query Transformations: Reshaping the
Question Before the Search
Improving retrieval by rewriting, broadening, or decomposing the
user’s query before it ever reaches the vector store.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (Simple RAG). Understanding of embeddings and
vector search.
The Problem
In every chapter so far, we have focused on improving what happens after the
user asks a question: better chunking strategies, relevancy filtering,
hallucination checking. But there is a fundamental assumption we have not
questioned: that the user’s original query is a good search query.
It often is not.
Consider a user who asks: “What are the impacts of climate change on the
environment?” This seems reasonable. But it is enormously broad. Climate
change affects biodiversity, ocean chemistry, weather patterns, agriculture,
human health, and dozens of other systems. A vector search with this query
returns a few chunks that mention “climate change” and “environment” in
general terms, but misses the detailed paragraphs about specific impacts like
coral bleaching or soil erosion. The query is too vague to surface the best
evidence.
Now consider the opposite problem. A user asks: “What specific molecular
mechanisms allow CRISPR-Cas9 to achieve targeted gene editing in
eukaryotic cells?” This query is so specific and technical that the vector
search struggles to find chunks that match all its facets. The knowledge base
might contain excellent explanations of CRISPR, but they use different
terminology or cover the topic at a different level of detail. The query is too
narrow for the language the documents use.
--- PAGE 69 ---
Both problems have the same root cause: the user’s words do not match
the documents’ words well enough for vector search to bridge the gap. The
meaning might be there, but the framing is wrong. The illustration below
shows a vague query casting a wide net that catches only generic results,
while a well-shaped query lands on precise, relevant evidence.
A vague search query on the left retrieves scattered, generic chunks marked with question marks, while
a focused, reshaped query on the right retrieves precise, relevant chunks marked with checkmarks.
The Core Idea
Query transformation attacks the problem at its source: the query itself.
Instead of searching with the user’s raw question, the system first reshapes
the question into a form that retrieves better results. The original question
stays the same for the final answer. Only the search query changes.
Think of it as the difference between asking a librarian your question
exactly as it pops into your head versus taking a moment to think about how
to phrase it for the best results. A skilled researcher does not walk into a
--- PAGE 70 ---
library and say “tell me about climate change.” They think about which angle
they need, what level of detail, and whether they should search broadly or
narrowly. Query transformation automates that thinking step.
This chapter covers three complementary strategies, each reshaping the
query in a different direction. Query rewriting makes the question more
specific and detailed. Step-back prompting makes it more general to retrieve
foundational context. Sub-query decomposition splits a complex question
into focused parts. The diagram below shows these three directions: a single
question branching into three different reshaping paths.
A central circle representing the original query labeled ‘Query’ with three arrows pointing outward.
One arrow points right to a detailed rectangle labeled ‘Rewrite’, one arrow points upward to a broader
circle labeled ‘Step Back’, and one arrow points downward to three smaller rectangles labeled ‘Split’.
The core insight: the quality of your retrieval depends as much on how
you ask the question as on how you store the answers. A well-shaped
query finds evidence that a raw query misses entirely.
--- PAGE 71 ---
How It Works
All three techniques share the same structure. A language model reads the
user’s original query and generates one or more transformed versions. These
transformed queries are used for retrieval instead of (or in addition to) the
original. The retrieved results are then passed to the generation step along
with the original question, so the final answer addresses what the user
actually asked.
Strategy 1: Query Rewriting
Query rewriting takes a vague or underspecified question and makes it more
specific, more detailed, and more likely to match the language used in the
knowledge base.
The process is simple. The user’s original query goes to a language model
with instructions to reformulate it into a version that is more precise and
includes details that the original left implicit.
For example:
Original: “What are the impacts of climate change on the
environment?”
Rewritten: “What are the specific effects of climate change on various
ecosystems, including changes in temperature, precipitation patterns, sea
levels, and biodiversity?”
Notice what happened. The rewritten query unpacks the word “environment”
into concrete dimensions: ecosystems, temperature, precipitation, sea levels,
biodiversity. Each of these terms is more likely to match a specific passage in
the knowledge base. The vector search now has five hooks into the document
collection instead of one.
Think of it as adjusting the zoom on a camera. The original query is at a
wide angle, capturing a blurry overview of the whole landscape. The rewritten
query zooms in, bringing specific details into sharp focus. The following
diagram shows a compact query on the left being expanded through a
language model into a richer, more detailed query on the right.
--- PAGE 72 ---
A horizontal flow showing a small rounded rectangle with a few short lines inside labeled “Vague” on
the left, an arrow pointing to a box with a brain icon labeled “LLM” in the center, and a larger rounded
rectangle with many detailed lines inside labeled “Specific” on the right. A subtle zoom-in icon below
illustrates the focusing effect.
The rewritten query replaces the original for the vector search step. The
original question is still used when generating the final answer, so the
response addresses what the user actually asked, not the expanded version.
Algorithm Sketch: Query Rewriting
1. Receive the user's original query
2. Send query to a language model with reformulation instructions
3. LLM generates a more specific, detailed version
4. Use the rewritten query for vector search
5. Retrieve matching chunks
6. Pass original query + retrieved chunks to the generation model
7. Generate answer that addresses the original question
Strategy 2: Step-Back Prompting
--- PAGE 73 ---
Step-back prompting does the opposite of query rewriting. Instead of making
the query more specific, it makes it more general. The idea is that sometimes
you need background context before you can answer a specific question well.
Consider the same query: “What are the impacts of climate change on the
environment?” A step-back version might be: “What are the general effects of
climate change?” This broader query retrieves chunks that provide
foundational context about climate change as a whole, including passages
that explain underlying mechanisms, historical trends, and general principles.
Why would you want less specific results? Because some questions cannot
be answered well without understanding the broader topic first. If a user asks
about a specific policy’s impact on agriculture, the system might benefit from
first retrieving general information about how that policy works, what
agriculture means in this context, and what kinds of impacts are possible. The
specific answer then builds on this foundation.
Think of it as the difference between asking “How does insulin resistance
affect Type 2 diabetes?” and first asking “What is diabetes?” The first
question is specific but assumes knowledge. The step-back question provides
the foundation needed to give a thorough answer. The following illustration
shows a specific question being expanded outward into a broader question
that captures more foundational context from the knowledge base.
--- PAGE 74 ---
Two concentric circles. The inner small circle is labeled “Specific” and represents the original narrow
query. The outer large circle is labeled “Broad” and represents the step-back query. An arrow points
outward from the inner circle to the outer circle. The outer circle connects to more diverse document
chunks around it.
In practice, the system can use both the step-back query and the original
query for retrieval. The step-back results provide context. The original results
provide direct answers. The language model receives both sets of retrieved
chunks and synthesizes a response that is both well-grounded and
contextually informed.
Algorithm Sketch: Step-Back Prompting
1. Receive the user's original query
2. Send query to a language model with instructions to generalize
3. LLM generates a broader "step-back" version of the query
4. Run vector search with both the original and step-back queries
5. Merge retrieved chunks from both searches
6. Pass original query + all retrieved chunks to the generation model
7. Generate answer with both specific evidence and broader context
--- PAGE 75 ---
Strategy 3: Sub-Query Decomposition
Sub-query decomposition tackles a different problem entirely: queries that
are too complex for a single search to handle. Some questions contain
multiple parts, or they require connecting information from several different
topics. A single vector search returns results that are close to the overall
theme but may miss important facets.
The technique is straightforward. A language model reads the complex
query and breaks it into two to four simpler sub-queries. Each sub-query
targets a specific aspect of the original question. Each runs as an independent
vector search. The results from all searches are collected, deduplicated, and
passed to the language model for final answer generation.
For example:
Original: “What are the impacts of climate change on the
environment?”
Sub-query 1: “How does climate change affect biodiversity and
ecosystems?”
Sub-query 2: “What are the impacts of climate change on oceans and
marine life?”
Sub-query 3: “How does climate change influence weather patterns and
extreme weather?”
Sub-query 4: “What are the effects of climate change on forests and
deserts?”
Each sub-query is focused enough to retrieve precise, relevant chunks about
one aspect of the original question. Together, they cover the topic
comprehensively. No single search would have retrieved all of this
information, because the original question was too broad for any one search
to capture all four dimensions.
Think of it as the difference between sending one person to research
everything about a topic versus sending four specialists, each assigned to a
specific angle. The specialist approach produces more thorough coverage
because each search is targeted enough to find the best evidence for its
particular facet. The following diagram shows a complex query at the top
splitting into four focused sub-queries, each retrieving its own set of results
that flow together into a combined collection.
--- PAGE 76 ---
A wide query rectangle at the top splits downward through dotted lines into four smaller query
rectangles. Each small query has a downward arrow to its own cluster of retrieved document chunks.
All chunks flow together into a combined collection at the bottom labeled “Combined Results.”
The key design decision is how many sub-queries to generate. Too few and
you miss important facets. Too many and you waste retrieval calls on
overlapping searches. Two to four sub-queries is a practical range for most
questions. The language model’s decomposition prompt should specify this
range to keep the approach efficient.
Algorithm Sketch: Sub-Query Decomposition
1. Receive the user's original query
2. Send query to a language model with decomposition instructions
3. LLM generates 2-4 focused sub-queries
4. Run vector search independently for each sub-query
5. Collect all retrieved chunks
6. Deduplicate overlapping results
7. Pass original query + combined chunks to the generation model
8. Generate comprehensive answer covering all facets
--- PAGE 77 ---
Choosing the Right Strategy
The three strategies are not interchangeable. Each one fits a different type of
query problem.
Query rewriting works best when the user’s question is on the right
track but too vague. The user knows what they want but has not expressed it
in terms that match the knowledge base well. Rewriting sharpens the query
without changing its fundamental direction.
Step-back prompting works best when the question is specific but the
answer requires foundational context that the user did not ask for. Medical
questions, legal questions, and technical questions often benefit from this
approach because the specific answer only makes sense in the context of
broader background knowledge.
Sub-query decomposition works best when the question is inherently
multi-faceted. Questions that contain “and” or ask about multiple aspects of a
topic are strong candidates. If a single search cannot cover all the ground,
decomposition ensures nothing is missed.
In many real-world systems, these techniques are not used in isolation. A
pipeline might first decompose a complex question into sub-queries, then
rewrite each sub-query for specificity. Or it might run both a step-back search
and a rewritten search in parallel and merge the results. The techniques
compose naturally because they all operate on the same principle: transform
the query before retrieval. The following illustration shows the three
strategies side by side, each applied to the same type of problem, with the
kind of query each handles best.
--- PAGE 78 ---
Three vertical panels side by side. Left panel labeled ‘Rewrite’ shows a small circle becoming a larger
circle via an arrow with a magnifying glass icon, tagged ‘Vague’. Middle panel labeled ‘Step Back’ shows
a circle expanding into concentric circles, tagged ‘Context’. Right panel labeled ‘Split’ shows one circle
branching into three smaller circles with a scissors icon, tagged ‘Complex’.
How Query Transformations Fit the Bigger Picture
Query transformations work at a different point in the pipeline than the
techniques from earlier chapters. Proposition chunking (chapter 4) improves
what gets stored. Relevancy grading (chapter 3) improves what gets passed to
the model after retrieval. Query transformations improve what gets searched
for in the first place.
These approaches are complementary, not competing. You can use
proposition chunking to store precise facts, query rewriting to sharpen the
search, relevancy grading to filter the results, and hallucination checking to
verify the answer. Each technique addresses a different failure mode in the
pipeline. The illustration below positions query transformations at the start
of the RAG pipeline, showing where they operate compared to the techniques
from previous chapters.
--- PAGE 79 ---
A horizontal RAG pipeline from left to right. The first stage, highlighted in warm orange, is labeled
“Transform” and sits before the “Search” stage. Later stages labeled “Grade” and “Verify” appear after
retrieval and generation, shown in muted blue. The highlighted “Transform” stage has a callout
emphasizing that it reshapes the query before retrieval happens.
When to Use This
Best for:
Systems where users ask vague or underspecified questions that need
sharpening
Knowledge bases covering broad topics where a single search misses
important facets
Complex queries that contain multiple parts or require background
context
Situations where retrieval quality is inconsistent due to vocabulary
mismatch between queries and documents
Overkill when:
--- PAGE 80 ---
Your users already ask specific, well-formed questions
Your knowledge base is small and focused enough that simple retrieval
works well
Latency is critical and the extra language model call for transformation is
too slow
Your retrieval already returns consistently relevant results
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency
Moderate: adds one LLM call for
transformation; decomposition adds multiple
retrieval calls
Complexity Low: each technique is a single prompt
template applied before the existing pipeline
Cost Low to moderate: one extra LLM call per
query; decomposition multiplies retrieval calls
Accuracy gain Significant for vague or complex queries;
minimal for already well-formed queries
Compared to searching with the raw query:
Query transformations add a thin intelligence layer between the user and
the vector store. The cost is one additional language model call before
retrieval begins. The benefit is better retrieval coverage, especially for vague,
complex, or multi-faceted questions. For systems where query quality varies
widely, transformations provide a consistent quality floor that catches poorly
framed questions before they produce poor results. For systems where users
already ask precise questions, the improvement may not justify the added
latency.
Key Takeaways
The user’s raw query is often not the best query for retrieval. Vague,
overly specific, or multi-faceted questions all lead to suboptimal search
results.
--- PAGE 81 ---
Query rewriting sharpens a vague question into a more specific, detailed
form that better matches the language of the knowledge base.
Step-back prompting broadens a specific question to retrieve
foundational context that supports a more thorough answer.
Sub-query decomposition splits a complex question into focused sub-
questions, each retrieving results that cover a different facet of the
original question.
These techniques operate before retrieval, complementing post-retrieval
improvements like relevancy grading and hallucination checking from
earlier chapters.
--- PAGE 82 ---
Chapter 6: Hypothetical Document Embedding
Instead of searching with the question, search with a made-up answer.
The fake answer finds the real one.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline),
Chapter 5 (query transformations)
The Problem
Imagine you are searching a knowledge base about climate science. You type:
“How do greenhouse gases trap heat?” The system embeds your seven-word
question, searches the vector store, and returns mediocre results. One chunk
discusses greenhouse gas emissions statistics. Another covers heat waves in
urban areas. A third mentions trapped gases in geological formations. None of
them directly explains the mechanism you asked about.
The frustrating part is that a perfect chunk exists in the database. It is a
detailed paragraph explaining how carbon dioxide and methane absorb
infrared radiation, re-emit it in all directions, and gradually warm the lower
atmosphere. But the embedding of your short question and the embedding of
that detailed explanation sit in different neighborhoods of meaning space.
This is the asymmetry problem. Questions and answers are structurally
different. A question is short, open-ended, and phrased as something
unknown. A document chunk is long, declarative, and phrased as something
known. Even when they are about the exact same topic, their embeddings can
end up surprisingly far apart. A seven-word question and a five-hundred-
character explanation encode different patterns in the embedding model,
even though they are about the same concept. The following diagram
illustrates this gap: a short query sits alone in one region of meaning space
while the relevant document chunks cluster together in another.
--- PAGE 83 ---
A short query shown as a small dot sitting far from a cluster of document chunks in meaning space,
illustrating the structural distance between questions and answers even when they share the same topic
Chapter 5 introduced query transformations as a way to improve retrieval by
reshaping the question. Rewriting sharpens a vague query, step-back
broadening adds context, and decomposition splits complex questions into
smaller ones. But all three strategies still search with a question. The
fundamental asymmetry remains: you are comparing a question-shaped
embedding against document-shaped embeddings. What if you could search
with something that looks like a document instead?
The Core Idea
Hypothetical Document Embedding, or HyDE, flips the search strategy
on its head. Instead of embedding the question and searching for similar
documents, you first ask a language model to generate a hypothetical answer
to the question. Then you embed that hypothetical answer and use it as the
search query. Because the hypothetical answer is structurally similar to real
--- PAGE 84 ---
document chunks (same length, same declarative style, same level of detail),
its embedding lands much closer to the relevant chunks in meaning space.
The key insight is that the hypothetical document does not need to be
correct. It might contain inaccuracies, miss important details, or even get
some facts wrong. That is fine. Its job is not to be the answer. Its job is to be
in the right neighborhood of meaning space so that the real documents can be
found nearby.
Think of it like a police sketch artist. When a witness describes a suspect
(“tall, dark hair, scar on the left cheek”), the detective does not walk through
a crowd holding up a written description. Instead, a sketch artist draws a face
based on those words. The sketch is never a perfect likeness. The nose might
be slightly off, the jawline a bit too wide. But comparing a face to other faces
is far easier than comparing a verbal description to faces. HyDE works the
same way: you create an imperfect but structurally similar representation,
then use it to find the real match. The following illustration shows this
analogy in action.
--- PAGE 85 ---
A sketch artist drawing a portrait from a verbal description on the left, then that sketch being held up
next to a wall of photographs on the right, with one photograph highlighted as a match
--- PAGE 86 ---
How It Works
The HyDE pipeline adds one step to the beginning of the standard retrieval
process: generating a hypothetical document. Everything else, embedding,
search, and answer generation, stays the same.
Step 1: Receive the question. The user asks a question just as in
standard RAG. For example: “What role do oceans play in regulating Earth’s
temperature?”
Step 2: Generate a hypothetical answer. Instead of embedding the
question directly, you send it to a language model with a specific instruction:
“Given this question, write a detailed document that answers it. Make the
document approximately the same length as a typical chunk in the knowledge
base.” The model produces something like:
“Oceans play a critical role in temperature regulation through several
mechanisms. They absorb approximately 90% of excess heat from the
atmosphere due to water’s high specific heat capacity. Ocean currents like the
Gulf Stream redistribute thermal energy from equatorial regions toward the
poles, moderating temperatures across continents. Additionally, oceans serve
as a major carbon sink, absorbing roughly 30% of human-produced carbon
dioxide, which influences the rate of atmospheric warming. Evaporation
from ocean surfaces drives the water cycle, forming clouds that reflect
sunlight and further regulate surface temperatures.”
This hypothetical document is plausible but unverified. Some numbers
might be approximate. Some details might be incomplete. That does not
matter at this stage.
Step 3: Embed the hypothetical document. The system creates an
embedding vector for this generated text, not for the original question.
Because the hypothetical document shares the same length and style as real
chunks in the database, its embedding captures the same structural patterns.
The full pipeline flows from question to hypothetical document to embedding
to search to real chunks to final answer. The following diagram traces this
complete path.
--- PAGE 87 ---
A horizontal pipeline showing six stages connected by arrows: a question mark icon, then a brain icon
generating a document, then a glowing vector dot, then a cylindrical database, then three highlighted
document chunks, and finally a lightbulb producing an answer
Step 4: Search the vector store. Using the hypothetical document’s
embedding as the query, the system performs standard similarity search. The
crucial difference is what is being compared: instead of a question-shaped
vector looking for document-shaped vectors, you now have a document-
shaped vector looking for other document-shaped vectors. Like finds like.
Step 5: Retrieve the top-K real chunks. The vector store returns the
chunks whose embeddings are closest to the hypothetical document’s
embedding. These are real, verified chunks from your knowledge base.
Step 6: Generate the final answer. The retrieved real chunks (not the
hypothetical document) are passed to the language model as context. The
hypothetical document is discarded. It served its purpose as a bridge between
query space and document space. The original question, not the hypothetical
answer, is what the model actually answers using the real retrieved context.
--- PAGE 88 ---
Algorithm Sketch: HyDE Retrieval
1. Receive user question
2. Prompt LLM: "Write a hypothetical answer (~chunk_size characters)"
3. LLM generates hypothetical document
4. Embed the hypothetical document (not the question)
5. Search vector store using hypothetical embedding
6. Retrieve top-K real document chunks
7. Discard hypothetical document
8. Pass real chunks + original question to LLM for final answer
The reason this works lies in how embedding spaces are organized.
Embeddings cluster by topic and by structure. Two paragraphs about ocean
temperature regulation will sit close together even if they use different words,
because they share both meaning and form. A question about ocean
temperature, though topically related, has a different form (interrogative,
short, incomplete) and therefore sits farther away. By transforming the
question into a paragraph first, HyDE bridges the structural gap. The
following visualization shows the hypothetical document’s embedding
landing inside the cluster of relevant real documents, even though the
hypothetical content is not perfectly accurate.
--- PAGE 89 ---
A scatter plot in meaning space with a small blue dot labeled as the query sitting far from a cluster of
orange dots representing real documents, while a green dot representing the hypothetical document
lands right inside the orange cluster
Why Imperfect Is Good Enough
A natural concern is: what if the language model generates a wrong
hypothetical document? If the model hallucinates incorrect facts in the
hypothetical answer, will it retrieve the wrong chunks?
In practice, this is less of a problem than you might expect. The embedding
captures the topic and structure of the hypothetical document, not whether
individual facts are correct. A hypothetical document that says “oceans absorb
85% of excess heat” instead of the correct “93%” will still produce an
embedding very close to chunks about ocean heat absorption. The factual
error barely moves the needle in embedding space.
However, if the language model has no knowledge of the topic at all and
generates something completely off-base, HyDE will steer retrieval in the
wrong direction. This is the technique’s most important limitation. HyDE
works best when the language model has at least surface-level familiarity
--- PAGE 90 ---
with the domain, enough to produce a hypothetical document that lives in the
right region of meaning space.
Matching Chunk Size Matters
One practical detail deserves attention. The prompt that generates the
hypothetical document typically includes an instruction about length: “make
it approximately the same size as chunks in the knowledge base.” This is not
arbitrary. Embedding models encode length-dependent patterns. A fifty-word
hypothesis and a five-hundred-word chunk will produce embeddings with
different structural characteristics even if they discuss the same topic. By
matching the hypothetical document’s length to the actual chunk size, you
ensure the structural similarity that makes HyDE effective.
The following diagram compares direct query search to HyDE on the same
question. With direct search, the short question misses the most relevant
chunks. With HyDE, the hypothetical document’s embedding aligns closely
with the real content, pulling in the right results.
--- PAGE 91 ---
A split-screen comparison with a dotted vertical divider. The left side labeled Direct shows a short query
retrieving documents marked with red X icons for poor relevance. The right side labeled HyDE shows
the same query going through an LLM to produce a longer document, then retrieving documents all
marked with green checkmarks
HyDE and Other Pre-Retrieval Techniques
Chapter 5 explored three query transformation strategies: rewriting, step-
back prompting, and sub-query decomposition. HyDE sits alongside these as
a fourth pre-retrieval technique, but it takes a fundamentally different
approach.
Query transformations stay in “question space.” They reshape the question
to make it more effective, but the search still compares a question to
documents. HyDE crosses into “document space.” It transforms the question
into a document, then compares documents to documents. This distinction
matters because the two approaches have different strengths.
Query rewriting excels when the original question is vague or poorly
worded. Step-back prompting helps when the question is too narrow. Sub-
query decomposition handles multi-part questions. HyDE addresses a
--- PAGE 92 ---
different problem entirely: the structural mismatch between questions and
documents. When the question is clear and well-formed but retrieval still
underperforms, HyDE is often the missing piece.
These techniques are also composable. You can rewrite the question first
for clarity, then generate a hypothetical document from the rewritten
question for search. Or you can decompose a complex question into sub-
queries and apply HyDE to each one. The following illustration compares the
three approaches at a glance: basic RAG searches directly with the question,
query transformation reshapes the question, and HyDE generates an answer-
shaped document for search.
Three parallel vertical paths from a question icon at the top. The left path labeled Basic goes directly to a
search icon. The middle path labeled Transform passes through a reshaping step before search. The
right path labeled HyDE passes through an LLM that generates a document before search. All three
paths converge at a results icon at the bottom
When to Use This
Best for:
--- PAGE 93 ---
Knowledge bases with long, detailed document chunks where the style
gap between queries and documents is large
Academic, legal, or technical domains where documents use formal,
expository language very different from how people ask questions
Complex questions that require synthesizing multiple concepts into a
coherent search
Overkill when:
Simple factual lookups where keyword overlap between question and
answer is high (“What year was the company founded?”)
Real-time applications with strict latency budgets, since HyDE adds a full
language model generation call before search
The knowledge base already uses proposition chunking, which stores
atomic facts that are structurally closer to questions
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency High: adds a full LLM call before every search
Complexity Low: only one extra step, no changes to storage
or indexing
Cost Medium: one additional LLM call per query
Accuracy gain Significant for complex queries in formal
document collections
Compared to query transformations:
Query transformations are lighter and faster. They reshape the question
with a quick LLM call that produces a short rewritten query. HyDE generates
an entire paragraph, which costs more tokens and takes more time. Choose
query transformations when the question itself is the bottleneck (vague, too
specific, or multi-part). Choose HyDE when the question is fine but retrieval
still underperforms due to the structural gap between questions and
documents. In many production systems, both techniques are used together.
--- PAGE 94 ---
The core insight of HyDE: you do not need a correct answer to find the
correct answer. A plausible guess, in the right shape, is enough to
navigate meaning space to where the real answers live.
Key Takeaways
HyDE generates a hypothetical answer to the question and uses that
answer’s embedding for search, bridging the structural gap between short
queries and long documents
The hypothetical document does not need to be factually correct; it only
needs to be in the right neighborhood of meaning space to pull in
relevant real chunks
Matching the hypothetical document’s length to the actual chunk size
improves retrieval quality by aligning structural patterns in the
embeddings
HyDE adds one language model call before search, increasing latency and
cost but requiring no changes to the preparation phase or storage
The technique works best when the language model has surface-level
domain knowledge, and worst when it has no familiarity with the topic at
all
HyDE complements query transformations: transformations reshape the
question, HyDE reshapes the question into an answer, and both can be
combined for maximum retrieval effectiveness
--- PAGE 95 ---
Chapter 7: Contextual Chunk Headers: Giving
Every Chunk Its Origin Story
Prepending document-level context to each chunk so that retrieval
knows where every piece of text came from.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (chunking, embeddings, vector search).
The Problem
Imagine a knowledge base built from hundreds of corporate annual reports.
Each report has been chunked, embedded, and stored using the standard
pipeline from chapter 1. A financial analyst asks: “What are Acme Corp’s
climate change risks?”
The system retrieves several chunks about climate change risks. One is a
perfect match. It reads: “Given the broad and global scope of our operations,
we are particularly vulnerable to the physical risks of climate change, such as
shifts in weather patterns. Extreme weather conditions in the areas in which
our retail stores, suppliers, manufacturers, distribution centers, and
headquarters are located could adversely affect our operating results.”
But which company is this about? The word “Acme” appears nowhere in
the text. The chunk uses “our operations,” “our retail stores,” “our operating
results.” These pronouns made perfect sense inside the original annual
report. Once the paragraph was pulled out and stored as a standalone chunk,
that context vanished. The chunk is topically relevant but contextually
anonymous, and its embedding has no trace of the company’s identity. It
competes equally with identical-sounding paragraphs from dozens of other
companies’ reports. The following illustration shows a chunk severed from its
document, with question marks where its context used to be.
--- PAGE 96 ---
A text chunk floating alone in empty space, with highlighted portions in orange inside it. Question
marks surround the chunk on all sides, representing the missing context about who the pronouns refer
to. A faded document outline sits in the background, showing where the chunk originally came from.
This is not a rare edge case. Most document chunks rely on surrounding
context for their full meaning. Pronouns like “we,” “our,” and “it” are
everywhere in corporate filings, legal contracts, and research papers. Section
headings that establish the topic are left behind when the text beneath them
gets chunked. A paragraph that makes perfect sense on page 47 of a report
becomes ambiguous when it stands alone.
The Core Idea
Contextual chunk headers solve this by prepending a small piece of
document-level context to each chunk before embedding it. The simplest
version is just the document title. Instead of embedding the raw chunk, you
embed: “Document Title: Acme Corp Annual Report 2023” followed by the
chunk text. One line of context, and the chunk carries its identity with it.
--- PAGE 97 ---
Think of it as running headers in a printed book. Every page shows the
book title and chapter name at the top. If you tear out a single page, the
running header still tells you where it came from. Without it, the page is just
an orphaned paragraph. Contextual chunk headers stamp every chunk with
its origin, so that even when it stands alone, it remembers where it belongs.
The following illustration shows this contrast: a plain page versus a page with
a running header that anchors it to its source.
A split-screen comparison. The left side shows a plain page of text with no header, labeled “No Header”
at the top, with a question mark above it. The right side shows the same page with a running header
reading the document title at the top of the page, labeled “With Header,” with a green checkmark above
it. The right page is visually anchored to a small book icon in the corner.
The core insight: chunks lose their identity when separated from their
document. A short header restores it, dramatically improving both
retrieval accuracy and generation quality, with almost no added cost.
--- PAGE 98 ---
How It Works
The technique modifies only the preparation phase. The query phase stays
the same.
Step 1: Generate a Document Title
If your documents already have descriptive titles, use them directly. But
many documents have unhelpful file names like “Q3_report_final_v2.pdf.”
In these cases, send the first few thousand words to a language model with a
simple instruction: “What is the title of this document?” The model returns a
concise, descriptive title like “Acme Corp Annual Report on Form 10-K.” This
is a one-time cost per document, not per chunk.
Step 2: Prepend the Header to Each Chunk
For every chunk, concatenate the header with the chunk text. A raw chunk
that read:
“Given the broad and global scope of our operations, we are particularly
vulnerable to the physical risks of climate change…”
Becomes:
“Document Title: Acme Corp Annual Report 2023. Given the broad and
global scope of our operations, we are particularly vulnerable to the physical
risks of climate change…”
That single line transforms the chunk’s identity. The embedding model
now processes both the title and the content, producing a vector that captures
not just “climate change risks for a large company” but “Acme Corp’s climate
change risks.” The pronouns “our” and “we” are no longer ambiguous.
Headers can carry varying levels of context. The simplest option prepends
just the document title. Richer headers add a document summary or even the
full section path, progressively grounding each chunk in its broader origin.
The following illustration shows three levels of header richness, from
minimal to full context.
--- PAGE 99 ---
Three chunk cards stacked vertically with an arrow labeled ‘More Context’ pointing downward. The top
card labeled ‘Title’ has only an orange title bar. The middle card labeled ‘Title + Summary’ has an
--- PAGE 100 ---
orange bar with both title and summary text. The bottom card labeled ‘Full’ has the orange title and
summary bar plus a section path line, showing the richest contextual header.
Step 3: Embed and Store
Embed the augmented chunks and store them in the vector store, exactly as
in chapter 1. The only difference is a few extra words at the beginning of each
chunk. The following diagram traces the full preparation pipeline.
A horizontal pipeline diagram showing five stages connected by smooth arrows. Stage 1: a document
icon labeled “Doc.” Stage 2: a brain icon generating a title, labeled “Title.” Stage 3: a header template
icon with title and section elements, labeled “Header.” Stage 4: a chunk icon with an orange header
band at the top and gray text below, labeled “Prepend.” Stage 5: a cylindrical database with dots inside,
labeled “Store.” Each stage flows left to right.
Algorithm Sketch: Contextual Chunk Headers
1. Split document into chunks (any chunking strategy)
2. Generate a descriptive document title (or use existing one)
3. For each chunk, prepend the title to the chunk text
4. Embed each augmented chunk
5. Store in vector store
--- PAGE 101 ---
One important detail: keep the header attached at every stage. If you use a
reranker, pass the augmented chunk to it. When presenting chunks to the
language model for answer generation, include the header so the model
knows which document each chunk came from. Stripping the header at any
point loses the disambiguation benefit.
Richer Headers
The document title alone delivers the largest improvement. But you can
include more context when the title is not enough:
Title plus summary. A one-to-two sentence document summary helps
when multiple documents cover similar topics and titles alone cannot
distinguish them.
Title, summary, and section hierarchy. Including the path of
section headings (“Risk Factors > Climate Change”) helps when users
ask about specific sections within a document.
Start with title-only headers. Add more context only if retrieval quality needs
further improvement. The returns diminish with each layer.
The Impact
The effect on retrieval can be dramatic. A chunk about climate change risks
from a corporate annual report, with no mention of the company name,
scores a relevance of roughly 0.1 against the query “Acme Corp climate
change impact.” The same chunk with the document title prepended scores
roughly 0.9. That is not an incremental improvement. It is the difference
between the chunk being buried at the bottom of the results and ranking at
the top.
The header gives the embedding the one piece of information it was
missing: which entity “we” and “our” refer to. The embedding shifts from a
generic “climate risk” region of meaning space into the specific “Acme Corp
climate risk” neighborhood. The following illustration shows this shift.
--- PAGE 102 ---
A meaning space visualization. On the left, labeled “Without,” a broad scattered cloud of gray dots on a
faint grid represents many generic climate-related chunks. On the right, labeled “With,” a dense ring of
orange dots clusters tightly around a central flag icon, representing the specific company neighborhood.
A curved arrow traces the shift from the generic region to the focused cluster.
The improvement is most pronounced when the knowledge base contains
many documents covering similar topics (dozens of annual reports all
discussing risk factors) and when chunks rely heavily on pronouns and
implicit references (legal contracts, research papers, internal memos).
When to Use This
Best for:
Multi-source knowledge bases (multi-company filings, multi-author
papers, mixed document collections)
Documents heavy with pronouns and implicit references (“we,” “our,”
“the company,” “the defendant”)
--- PAGE 103 ---
Any RAG system where retrieval returns topically relevant but
contextually wrong chunks
Overkill when:
Your knowledge base contains only a single document or a few
documents on distinct topics
Chunks are already self-contained (such as propositions from chapter 4
that resolve all pronouns)
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Preparation cost Very low: one LLM call per document, plus
string concatenation
Query latency None: no additional work at query time
Storage Slightly higher: each chunk is a few tokens
longer
Retrieval accuracy Significant, especially for multi-source
knowledge bases
Compared to standard chunking from chapter 1:
The preparation overhead is negligible (one LLM call per document, not
per chunk), query latency is unchanged, and the accuracy improvement can
be substantial. Contextual chunk headers work with any chunking strategy,
including the proposition chunking from chapter 4 and alongside query-phase
techniques like those from chapters 5 and 6. For multi-source knowledge
bases, this should be a default part of the pipeline, not an optional
enhancement.
Key Takeaways
Chunks lose critical context when separated from their documents.
Pronouns and implicit references become ambiguous.
Prepending the document title to each chunk before embedding restores
that context at minimal cost.
--- PAGE 104 ---
Richer headers (summary, section hierarchy) offer diminishing returns
but help with large, overlapping document collections.
Keep the header attached at every stage: embedding, reranking, and
generation.
The technique adds no query-time latency and works with any chunking
strategy. For multi-source knowledge bases, it should be a default.
--- PAGE 105 ---
Chapter 8: Context Enrichment Window
Expand each retrieved chunk with its surrounding text to recover the
full thought that arbitrary boundaries cut apart.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (chunking, overlap, vector search), Chapter 7
(contextual chunk headers)
The Problem
By now you know that chunking splits a document into fixed-size pieces for
embedding and retrieval. You also know that overlap helps preserve
information at the boundaries. But overlap is a partial fix. Consider what
happens when a user asks “What role does deforestation play in climate
change?” and the system retrieves this chunk:
“…and land degradation. Deforestation accounts for approximately 10
percent of global greenhouse gas emissions. When forests are cleared, the
carbon stored in trees is released into the atmosphere as carbon dioxide.
Furthermore, the loss of forest cover reduces the planet’s capacity to…”
The chunk lands right on the most relevant passage, but it starts mid-
paragraph and ends mid-sentence. The reader learns that deforestation
accounts for 10 percent of emissions, but the setup (what causes
deforestation) was in the previous chunk, and the conclusion (what reduced
forest capacity means for climate feedback loops) is in the next chunk. The
answer the language model constructs from this fragment will be technically
accurate but incomplete, missing the cause and the consequence. It delivers
the middle of a thought without the beginning or the end.
This is not a failure of the embedding model or the search algorithm. The
retrieval found exactly the right spot in the document. The problem is that
the chunk boundaries are arbitrary. They were placed by a character counter,
not by a reader who understands where ideas begin and end. The result is a
kind of tunnel vision: the system sees the precise point of relevance but
--- PAGE 106 ---
cannot see the paragraph around it. The following illustration shows how a
chunk retrieved in isolation captures the core fact but loses the reasoning
that surrounds it.
A text chunk floating in isolation, with faded text visible on either side representing the missing context
before and after the retrieved fragment
The Core Idea
The fix is surprisingly simple: after finding the right chunk through similarity
search, widen the view. Go back to the original document and grab the
chunks immediately before and after the retrieved one. Stitch them together
into a single expanded passage. The retrieval stays precise (the small chunk
matched the query), but the context delivered to the language model is broad
enough to contain the complete thought.
Think of it like a spotlight on a theater stage. The similarity search aims
the spotlight at the right actor. But the spotlight beam is narrow, catching
only the actor’s face. The context enrichment window widens the beam to
--- PAGE 107 ---
show the full scene, including the actors on either side who are part of the
