output is a set of chunks that collectively cover more informational ground
than a pure similarity ranking would. The technique also works naturally
with other retrieval approaches: if you use fusion retrieval from chapter 12,
the fused similarity scores can serve as the relevance input, and if you use
cross-encoder reranking from chapter 13, the reranker scores can replace raw
embedding distances. The following diagram shows the complete flow from
query to diversified results.
--- PAGE 197 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right in five stages connected by smooth arrows. Stage 1: a
search icon labeled “Query”. Stage 2: a cylinder labeled “Store” with a wide arrow indicating broad
retrieval. Stage 3: a rounded box containing many small rectangles labeled “Pool”. Stage 4: a funnel
shape labeled “Select” with a small target icon and a spread-pattern icon inside it. Stage 5: five
rectangles in different colors (blue, orange, gray, green, teal) labeled “Result”. Clean sans-serif font.
When to Use This
Best for:
Knowledge bases with significant content overlap, where multiple
documents cover the same topics in similar language
Dense collections where the same information appears in multiple forms
(summaries, full articles, and FAQs all about the same subject)
Questions that benefit from multi-faceted answers, where covering
different aspects of a topic matters more than finding the single best
chunk
Overkill when:
--- PAGE 198 ---
The collection has minimal redundancy, with each chunk covering
distinct content
The retrieval pipeline already handles diversity through other means,
such as fusion retrieval from chapter 12 which naturally diversifies by
combining keyword and semantic results
The system only needs one or two chunks, where redundancy has little
room to manifest
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Query latency Low: pairwise distance computation is fast on
small candidate sets
Implementation complexity Low: the core algorithm is a short greedy loop
over distance matrices
Preparation cost None: operates at query time on standard
embeddings with no index changes
Accuracy gain Significant when redundancy is the primary
failure mode; minimal when it is not
Compared to standard top-K from chapter 1:
Standard top-K is simpler and faster. If your knowledge base has minimal
redundancy, top-K already returns diverse results naturally, and dartboard
selection adds computation without benefit. Dartboard retrieval specifically
targets the failure mode where the top results are near-duplicates. If you
observe that your retriever frequently returns repetitive chunks, dartboard
selection is a lightweight fix that requires no changes to the index or
preparation pipeline. The following illustration contrasts the two approaches:
standard top-K returns near-identical results, while dartboard retrieval selects
a diverse set that covers more ground.
--- PAGE 199 ---
A split comparison with a dotted vertical divider. Left side labeled ‘Top-K’ shows a query bubble above
five identical blue document rectangles stacked vertically, each with a red circular icon indicating
redundancy. Right side labeled ‘Dartboard’ shows the same query bubble above five document
rectangles in varied colors (blue, orange, gray, teal, orange) with green checkmarks on three and
orange dots on two, indicating a diverse selection.
Compared to reranking from chapter 13:
Reranking re-scores candidates based on deeper relevance analysis but
does not explicitly optimize for diversity. A reranker might still place five
similar-but-relevant chunks at the top. Dartboard selection and reranking
solve different problems and can be combined: rerank the oversampled
candidates first for better relevance scores, then apply dartboard selection to
ensure the final set is also diverse.
Key Takeaways
Standard top-K retrieval optimizes only for relevance, which leads to
redundant results when knowledge bases contain overlapping content.
--- PAGE 200 ---
Dartboard retrieval adds a diversity objective to the selection process,
ensuring each chosen chunk adds new information beyond what
previous selections already cover.
The algorithm is a greedy loop: select the most relevant chunk first, then
iteratively select chunks that score highest on a weighted combination of
relevance and diversity.
Oversampling the initial candidate pool gives the algorithm room to
trade off between relevance and diversity without sacrificing too much of
either.
The technique requires no changes to the index, embedding model, or
preparation pipeline. It operates entirely at query time as a post-retrieval
selection layer.
Configurable weights let you tune the balance between relevance and
diversity based on your collection’s characteristics and your application’s
needs.
--- PAGE 201 ---
Chapter 16: Multi-Modal RAG with Captioning
Extending retrieval beyond text by converting images, tables, and
charts into searchable descriptions so the system can find answers that
live in visual content.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline)
The Problem
Imagine a company’s engineering team uses a knowledge base built from
technical PDFs: architecture diagrams, performance benchmark tables,
deployment flowcharts, and design specifications. An engineer asks: “What
does the system architecture look like for the data processing pipeline?” The
answer is right there in the document, on page 12, in a detailed diagram
showing microservices, message queues, and data flows. But the RAG system
never sees it.
Standard RAG extracts text from documents and ignores everything else.
When the system processes a PDF, it reads the paragraphs and headings but
skips the figures, charts, tables rendered as images, and diagrams. Those
visual elements are invisible to the pipeline. They never get chunked, never
get embedded, never enter the vector store. The information they contain is
unreachable.
This is not a minor gap. In many professional documents, the most
information-dense elements are visual. A single architecture diagram conveys
what would take pages of prose to describe. A benchmark table compresses
dozens of data points into a compact grid. When RAG treats documents as
text-only, it operates with partial information, answering queries about the
prose while remaining blind to the visuals. The following illustration shows a
document where text content is searchable but image content sits locked
away, invisible to the retrieval system.
--- PAGE 202 ---
A document page with text paragraphs highlighted in blue as searchable content on one side, while an
image and a table on the other side are grayed out with lock icons, showing they are invisible to the
retrieval system
The Core Idea
Multi-modal RAG with captioning bridges the gap between visual and text-
based retrieval by adding a translation step. Before indexing, a vision-capable
language model examines every image, table, and diagram extracted from the
document and writes a text description of what it sees. These descriptions,
called captions, are then chunked and embedded alongside the regular text,
entering the same vector store.
Think of it like a radio sports announcer calling a live game. The audience
at home cannot see the field. The announcer watches the action unfold and
translates it into vivid words: “The striker cuts left past the defender, sends a
cross into the box…” Listeners can now follow the game entirely through the
announcer’s descriptions. The captioning model plays exactly this role. It
watches the visual content and translates it into text so the search system,
--- PAGE 203 ---
which only works with words, can find and retrieve it. The following
illustration shows this translation in action, where visual content is described
in words to make it accessible to a text-based system.
A visual-to-text translation scene: on the left a colorful image representing visual content, in the center
a figure with a microphone icon acting as the announcer or captioner, and on the right text lines
representing the generated description, with an arrow flowing left to right showing the translation
The core insight: you do not need a special visual search engine. You
convert visual content into text, and then your existing text-based
retrieval pipeline handles it naturally.
How It Works
The technique modifies only the preparation phase of the RAG pipeline. The
query phase remains unchanged. Once images are captioned and indexed,
they are just text chunks like any other.
--- PAGE 204 ---
Step 1: Extract Text and Images Separately
The first step processes the source document and separates its content into
two streams. A PDF parser reads each page, extracting the raw text into one
collection and the embedded images into another. Each extracted image is
tagged with metadata noting which page it came from, so the system can trace
any caption back to its source location.
This dual extraction is the foundation of the multi-modal approach.
Standard RAG performs only the text extraction and discards the images.
Here, both streams are preserved for processing. The following diagram
shows a document splitting into its text and image components.
A document icon on the left with two diverging arrows: the top arrow leads to a stack of text rectangles
in muted blue labeled Text, the bottom arrow leads to a row of image frames in warm orange labeled
Images, showing the dual extraction of content types
Step 2: Caption Every Image with a Vision Model
Each extracted image is sent to a vision-capable language model with a
prompt asking for a concise, retrieval-optimized summary. The model
--- PAGE 205 ---
examines the image and produces a text description capturing the key
information it contains.
For a table image, the caption might describe the column headers, the
range of values, and the key comparisons the table enables. For an
architecture diagram, the caption might list the components shown, their
connections, and the overall flow. For a chart, it might describe the trend, the
axes, and the notable data points.
The quality of these captions directly affects retrieval performance. A
vague caption like “a table with numbers” provides little semantic signal for
embedding. A specific caption like “performance benchmark comparing six
model variants on translation quality, showing BLEU scores ranging from
25.8 to 41.0” creates a rich embedding that aligns well with queries about
model performance or translation scores. The captioning prompt should
instruct the model to produce summaries optimized for search, not generic
image descriptions.
Algorithm Sketch: Image Captioning
1. For each extracted image:
   a. Send image to a vision-capable language model
   b. Prompt: "Summarize this table, image, or chart for retrieval.
      Give a concise description optimized for search."
   c. Store the returned text caption with source page metadata
2. Collect all captions into a document list
The captioning step is the computational heart of this technique. It runs once
during preparation, not at query time, so the latency cost is paid upfront. The
following illustration zooms into the captioning process, showing a vision
model examining an image and producing a text description.
--- PAGE 206 ---
A concept close-up: on the left an image frame containing abstract colored bars representing a chart, in
the center a rounded box with an eye icon representing the vision model, and on the right a text card
with lines representing the generated caption labeled Caption, with arrows flowing left to right
Step 3: Chunk and Embed Both Streams
With the captions generated, the system now has two collections of text: the
original extracted text and the image captions. Both are processed identically
from this point forward. Each collection is split into chunks using standard
text splitting, and every chunk is converted into an embedding vector.
The text chunks and caption chunks then enter the same vector store.
Once embedded, there is no distinction between a chunk that originated from
a paragraph on page 5 and a chunk that originated from a caption of a
diagram on page 12. They are all vectors in the same space, searchable by the
same similarity metric. A query about system architecture will find text
chunks that discuss architecture and caption chunks that describe
architecture diagrams, ranked purely by semantic relevance. The following
illustration shows both streams merging into a single unified store where
text-derived and image-derived content become indistinguishable.
--- PAGE 207 ---
Two input streams converging into one store: a top stream of blue text rectangles and a bottom stream
of orange caption cards both pass through chunking and embedding steps, then merge into a single
cylinder labeled Store where blue and orange dots mix together
Step 4: Retrieve and Answer as Usual
At query time, nothing changes. The user’s query is embedded, the vector
store returns the top-K most similar chunks, and the language model
generates an answer from the retrieved context. The only difference is that
the retrieved chunks might include a caption describing a relevant table or
diagram, giving the model access to information that would have been
invisible in a text-only pipeline.
If an engineer asks “What is the BLEU score of the base model?”, the
retriever might return a caption chunk that summarizes a benchmark table:
“Performance comparison table showing the base Transformer model
achieving a BLEU score of 27.3 on English-to-German translation.” The
language model reads this and produces a grounded answer, even though the
original information existed only in a table image.
The following diagram shows the complete pipeline from document
ingestion through dual extraction, captioning, unified storage, and standard
--- PAGE 208 ---
retrieval.
A wide horizontal pipeline in six stages: a document icon labeled Doc splits into two parallel paths for
Text and Images, the images path passes through a box with an eye icon labeled Caption, both paths
merge into a cylinder labeled Store, a query bubble labeled Query searches the store, and a lightbulb
icon labeled Answer appears on the far right
When to Use This
Best for:
Documents that contain significant visual content: research papers with
tables and figures, technical manuals with diagrams, reports with charts
and graphs
Knowledge bases where answers to common queries live in images,
tables, or figures rather than in the body text
PDF-heavy collections where tables are embedded as images rather than
as structured data
--- PAGE 209 ---
Overkill when:
Documents are text-only with no meaningful visual content
Tables are already extracted as structured text by the PDF parser, making
captioning redundant
The knowledge base is small enough to manually transcribe visual
content into text
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Preparation cost Moderate: each image requires a vision model
API call during indexing
Query latency None: query-time behavior is identical to
standard RAG
Storage Slightly higher: additional caption chunks
increase the vector store size
Accuracy gain High when queries target visual content; no
change for text-only queries
Compared to text-only RAG from chapter 1:
Text-only RAG is simpler and cheaper to prepare. If your documents are
predominantly text with few or no important images, adding captioning
increases preparation cost without improving retrieval. Multi-modal
captioning pays off when visual elements carry information that users
actually query about. If no one asks questions about the tables or diagrams,
captioning them adds cost without benefit.
Compared to hierarchical indices from chapter 14:
Hierarchical indices organize retrieval by document structure, narrowing
the search to relevant sections. Multi-modal captioning expands retrieval
coverage to include visual content. The two techniques solve orthogonal
problems and combine naturally: use hierarchical indices to find the right
sections, and within those sections, retrieve from both text chunks and image
captions.
Key Takeaways
--- PAGE 210 ---
Standard RAG extracts only text from documents, leaving images, tables,
charts, and diagrams invisible to the retrieval system.
Multi-modal RAG with captioning uses a vision-capable language model
to generate text descriptions of every extracted image before indexing.
Captions are chunked and embedded alongside regular text, entering the
same vector store with no special handling needed at query time.
Caption quality matters. Specific, retrieval-optimized summaries produce
better embeddings than vague descriptions.
The technique modifies only the preparation pipeline. Query-time
behavior and latency remain unchanged.
This approach is most valuable for document collections where
significant information lives in visual content rather than body text.
--- PAGE 211 ---
Chapter 17: Retrieval with Feedback Loops
Closing the loop: using explicit user feedback to continuously improve
what a RAG system retrieves and how it responds.
Prerequisites: Chapters 1-3 (basic RAG pipeline, vector stores, relevance
scoring)
The Problem
Picture a RAG system that answers employee questions about company
policy. An HR manager asks, “What is our parental leave policy for adoptive
parents?” The system retrieves three chunks about general leave policies,
none of which mention adoption specifically. The manager marks the answer
as unhelpful and moves on.
A week later, a different manager asks essentially the same question. The
system retrieves the exact same irrelevant chunks and produces the same
incomplete answer. Nobody told the system it got things wrong last time. It
has no memory of failure.
This is the statelessness problem. Every query starts from zero. The
system never learns which documents actually helped users and which ones
wasted their time. A thousand queries could pass through the pipeline, and
the thousand-and-first would be no better than the first. All the signal about
what worked and what did not vanishes the moment each interaction ends.
The following illustration shows the difference between a memoryless system
that repeats its mistakes and one that improves from feedback.
--- PAGE 212 ---
A split-screen comparison: on the left, the same query arrives three times and receives the same poor
result each time, with red X marks on the responses; on the right, a system that remembers past
feedback, with each successive response improving, shown by responses shifting from red to yellow to
green checkmarks
The Core Idea
A feedback loop turns a static RAG system into one that improves over time.
After each response, the system collects explicit feedback from the user: was
the answer relevant? Was it high quality? These ratings get stored
persistently. On subsequent queries, the system consults past feedback to
adjust how it ranks retrieved documents. And periodically, it enriches its own
index by folding the best question-answer pairs back into the vector store as
new searchable content.
Think of a personal fitness trainer who keeps a detailed training log.
Without one, every session starts from scratch: the trainer might assign
exercises that proved too difficult last time, or repeat drills the client has
already mastered. With a training log, the trainer reviews what worked
(exercises the client rated highly) and adjusts the next session’s plan
--- PAGE 213 ---
accordingly. Over time, the trainer also compiles the most effective routines
into a go-to collection of proven workouts, making future session planning
faster and more reliably effective. The feedback loop in RAG works the same
way: it remembers, adjusts, and accumulates knowledge.
The following diagram shows the continuous cycle at the heart of this
technique. A query enters the system, documents are retrieved, an answer is
generated, the user provides feedback, and that feedback flows back to
influence the next retrieval.
A circular feedback loop diagram with five stages connected by smooth arrows: Query at the top, then
Retrieve, then Respond, then Feedback from the user, then Store, with an arrow looping back to
influence the Retrieve stage on the next cycle
How It Works
The technique has two complementary mechanisms that operate on different
timescales: real-time score adjustment on every query, and periodic index
enrichment at scheduled intervals.
--- PAGE 214 ---
Step 1: Collect and Store Feedback
After the system generates a response, it presents the answer to the user
along with a simple feedback form. The user rates two dimensions on a scale
of one to five.
Relevance
Was the retrieved information actually about the question?
Quality
Was the final answer accurate, complete, and useful?
The user can also leave free-text comments explaining what went wrong or
what they expected instead. Each feedback entry bundles the original query,
the generated response, the two numerical scores, and any comments into a
single record. These records are appended to a persistent feedback store,
whether that is a JSON file, a database table, or any other durable storage.
The following diagram shows a user receiving a response, providing
ratings, and having that feedback stored for future use.
--- PAGE 215 ---
A horizontal three-stage flow with arrows pointing left to right. Stage 1 labeled “Receive” shows a
person silhouette next to a document card. Stage 2 labeled “Rate” shows a person with four out of five
stars above and a thumbs-up icon below. Stage 3 labeled “Store” shows an orange database cylinder
representing persistent storage.
The feedback store grows over time into a structured memory of what worked
and what did not. Its value compounds: early feedback may be sparse, but
after hundreds of interactions, the system has a rich history to draw from.
Step 2: Adjust Relevance Scores at Query Time
When a new query arrives, the system retrieves documents through the
normal vector search process. But before passing those documents to the
language model, it adds a new step: consulting the feedback history.
For each retrieved document, the system scans the feedback store and asks
a language model a targeted question: “Is this past feedback entry relevant to
the current query and this document?” The language model acts as a judge,
comparing the old query, old response, new query, and current document
content. It returns a simple yes or no.
--- PAGE 216 ---
When relevant feedback entries are found, the system adjusts the
document’s relevance score. It calculates the average relevance rating from all
applicable feedback entries and uses it as a multiplier. On a one-to-five scale,
a score of three is neutral, meaning the document’s rank stays roughly the
same. Scores above three boost the document higher in the results. Scores
below three push it down. After all adjustments, the documents are re-ranked
by their new scores.
The following diagram shows how past feedback entries are evaluated
against the current query and used to adjust document scores. Each candidate
document gets its ranking adjusted based on the accumulated wisdom from
earlier interactions.
A diagram showing score adjustment: on the left, four retrieved documents stacked vertically with
initial scores; in the center, a set of past feedback cards being checked against each document by a small
judge icon; on the right, the same documents re-ordered with adjusted scores, some moved up with
green arrows and some moved down with orange arrows
--- PAGE 217 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Feedback-Adjusted Retrieval
1. Receive new query
2. Retrieve candidate documents via vector search
3. Load all stored feedback entries
4. For each candidate document:
   a. For each feedback entry, ask LLM: "Is this feedback relevant?"
   b. Collect all relevant feedback entries
   c. If relevant feedback exists:
      - Compute average relevance score
      - Multiply document score by (average / neutral midpoint)
5. Re-rank documents by adjusted scores
6. Pass top results to language model for answer generation
This process means that documents which consistently received positive
feedback in similar contexts get promoted, while documents associated with
negative feedback get demoted. The system develops preferences based on
proven track records.
Step 3: Enrich the Index Periodically
The second mechanism operates on a longer timescale. At scheduled
intervals, perhaps daily or weekly, the system reviews accumulated feedback
and identifies high-quality interactions: those where both relevance and
quality were rated four or above.
For each high-quality interaction, the system concatenates the original
query with its verified response to create a new synthetic document. This
combined text captures both the question pattern and a proven-good answer.
These synthetic documents are then encoded into embeddings and added to
the vector store alongside the original content.
This is like the fitness trainer distilling months of session notes into a
curated collection of proven workout routines. The next time a similar
question arrives, the vector store now contains not just the raw source
material but also the distilled wisdom of successful past interactions. The
synthetic document will often match better than the original chunks because
it already contains the question-answer pattern the user is looking for.
The following diagram shows how high-quality feedback is filtered and
distilled into new index entries. Only interactions where both scores meet the
threshold qualify, ensuring the index absorbs proven knowledge rather than
noise.
--- PAGE 218 ---
A funnel-shaped diagram: on the left, many feedback entries of various colors flow into a filter labeled
with a star icon; entries with low scores are filtered out and fall away in gray; entries with high scores
pass through and emerge on the right as new document icons in warm orange, which then flow into a
vector store cylinder, joining the existing blue document dots inside
How the Two Mechanisms Work Together
Real-time score adjustment handles the short game: it biases each query
toward documents that worked in similar situations before. Index enrichment
handles the long game: it creates entirely new retrieval targets that capture
proven question-answer patterns.
Together, they create a system that improves on two fronts
simultaneously. Score adjustment makes existing content more findable
when it matters. Index enrichment makes past successes directly retrievable.
The result is a RAG pipeline that gets measurably better with use, turning
every user interaction into a learning opportunity.
The complete architecture shows both mechanisms operating in parallel.
The fast path adjusts scores on every query. The slow path enriches the index
on a schedule. Both draw from the same feedback store, and both feed into
the same vector store that serves future queries.
--- PAGE 219 ---
A wide architecture diagram showing the complete system: at the top, a query flows through retrieval,
score adjustment using the feedback store, and response generation in a horizontal pipeline; at the
bottom, a separate periodic process reads from the same feedback store, filters for high-quality entries,
and injects new documents into the vector store; both paths share the central vector store and feedback
store
The core insight: most RAG improvements happen before or during
retrieval. A feedback loop is unique because it improves the system after
retrieval, using the one signal no algorithm can manufacture on its
own: whether the human found the answer actually useful.
When to Use This
Best for:
Internal knowledge bases with repeat users who can provide ratings
Support systems where response quality has measurable business impact
Domains where the same types of questions recur frequently
--- PAGE 220 ---
Applications with enough query volume to accumulate meaningful
feedback
Overkill when:
The system handles mostly one-off queries from anonymous users
Query volume is too low to build a useful feedback history
The underlying document collection changes so rapidly that historical
feedback becomes stale quickly
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency Medium: LLM calls to evaluate feedback
relevance add time per query
Complexity Medium: requires feedback storage, evaluation
logic, and periodic maintenance
Cost Medium: each query triggers additional LLM
calls proportional to feedback history size
Accuracy gain Cumulative: improves steadily as feedback
accumulates
Compared to static retrieval with reranking:
Reranking (Chapter 13) applies a smarter second pass to every query, but it
starts fresh each time. A feedback loop carries forward what it learned from
past interactions, meaning it can correct systematic biases that reranking
alone cannot detect. If your system consistently retrieves a particular chunk
that users mark as unhelpful, reranking may keep scoring it highly because it
looks relevant on paper. Feedback-adjusted retrieval will learn to demote it.
The tradeoff is that feedback loops require infrastructure for collecting and
storing ratings, and they need a critical mass of feedback before they show
meaningful improvement.
Key Takeaways
--- PAGE 221 ---
RAG systems are stateless by default: they repeat the same mistakes
because each query starts fresh with no memory of past interactions
A feedback loop collects explicit user ratings on relevance and quality
after each response, building a persistent record of what worked
At query time, the system checks whether past feedback applies to the
current retrieval and adjusts document scores accordingly, promoting
documents with strong track records
Periodically, the system enriches its vector index by adding synthetic
documents built from high-quality question-answer pairs, making
proven knowledge directly searchable
The technique is most valuable in environments with recurring query
patterns and engaged users willing to provide feedback, and its benefits
compound over time
--- PAGE 222 ---
Chapter 18: Adaptive Retrieval
Match the retrieval strategy to the question, not the other way around.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline),
Chapter 5 (query transformations: rewriting and sub-query decomposition)
The Problem
Imagine an enterprise knowledge base serving a customer support team. One
agent asks “What is the refund policy for damaged goods?” and needs a
single, precise passage from the company handbook. Another agent asks
“How does our return policy compare across product categories?” and needs a
broad sweep across multiple documents to assemble an analytical picture. A
third asks “What do customers generally feel about our return process?” and
needs diverse viewpoints from survey responses and complaint logs.
A standard RAG pipeline treats all three queries identically. It embeds the
question, retrieves the top-K nearest chunks, and hands them to the language
model. But these three questions have fundamentally different shapes. The
factual question needs precision. The analytical question needs breadth. The
opinion question needs diversity. A single retrieval strategy cannot optimize
for all three at once.
The result is predictable. The factual question drowns in loosely related
passages when it only needed one. The analytical question gets a narrow slice
when it needed a panoramic view. The opinion question surfaces only the
majority sentiment when it should have captured the full range. The
following diagram shows how a uniform pipeline produces mediocre results
across different query types, while specialized paths give each question what
it actually needs.
--- PAGE 223 ---
A uniform retrieval pipeline on the left produces mediocre results for different query types, while
specialized retrieval paths on the right match each query type to an appropriate strategy
The Core Idea
Adaptive retrieval adds a classification step before retrieval begins. An LLM
reads the incoming query, determines what type of question it is, and routes
it to a retrieval strategy designed specifically for that type. Factual queries get
precision-focused retrieval with relevance ranking. Analytical queries get
decomposed into sub-questions for comprehensive coverage. Opinion queries
get viewpoint-aware retrieval that actively seeks diversity. Contextual queries
get reformulated with user-specific information before searching.
Think of it as a hospital triage system. When a patient arrives at the
emergency department, a triage nurse does not send everyone to the same
specialist. She assesses the patient first: chest pain goes to cardiology, a
broken bone goes to orthopedics, a skin rash goes to dermatology. Each
specialist then follows their own diagnostic protocol tailored to the type of
problem. Adaptive retrieval works the same way: classify first, then apply the
--- PAGE 224 ---
right protocol. The following diagram shows this triage-style routing in
action.
A triage-style routing diagram where incoming queries are assessed and directed to specialized retrieval
strategies, like patients being routed to the right medical specialist
How It Works
The system has two main components: a query classifier that acts as the
router, and a set of specialized retrieval strategies that each handle one query
type. Every query passes through the classifier first, then flows into exactly
one strategy.
Step 1: Classify the Query
The first component is a query classifier. It receives the raw user query and
asks an LLM to categorize it into one of four types:
--- PAGE 225 ---
Factual: seeks specific, verifiable information (“What is the maximum
upload size?”)
Analytical: requires comprehensive analysis across multiple aspects
(“How do our caching strategies affect latency under different load
patterns?”)
Opinion: asks about subjective matters or seeks diverse viewpoints
(“What are the main arguments for and against microservices?”)
Contextual: depends on user-specific circumstances (“Based on my
team’s current architecture, which database should we migrate to?”)
The classifier is a single LLM call with a structured output format that
returns one of these four labels. It runs once per query, adding minimal
latency. The following diagram shows how a query enters the classifier and
emerges tagged with its type, ready for routing to the appropriate strategy.
A query entering a classification box and being routed to one of four labeled output paths: Factual,
Analytical, Opinion, and Contextual
Step 2: Execute the Matched Strategy
--- PAGE 226 ---
Once the query is classified, it is handed to the strategy that matches its type.
Each strategy follows a different protocol, but they all end at the same place:
a set of retrieved documents ready for the language model to synthesize into
an answer.
Factual Strategy: Precision Through Enhancement and Ranking
Factual queries need the right passage, not many passages. This strategy
sharpens the query first, then filters aggressively.
The LLM rewrites the original query into a more precise form optimized
for retrieval. This is the same query rewriting idea from Chapter 5, applied
specifically to tighten a factual question. The enhanced query then runs
against the vector store, but retrieves twice as many candidates as needed (2K
instead of K). Each candidate is then scored individually by an LLM that rates
its relevance to the query on a numeric scale. The top K by relevance score are
kept.
The key insight is the two-stage narrowing: cast a wider initial net, then
use an LLM judge to keep only the most directly relevant passages. This
catches the precise answer even if it was not the closest vector match. The
following diagram traces a factual query through enhancement, over-
retrieval, and LLM-based ranking.
--- PAGE 227 ---
The factual retrieval strategy: a query is enhanced by an LLM, then retrieves double the needed
candidates, which are individually scored and filtered to keep only the most relevant
Algorithm Sketch: Factual Retrieval Strategy
1. Receive classified factual query
2. Ask LLM to rewrite query for precision
3. Retrieve 2K candidates using enhanced query
4. For each candidate, ask LLM to score relevance (1-10)
5. Sort by score descending
6. Return top K candidates
Analytical Strategy: Breadth Through Decomposition and Diversity
Analytical queries need coverage across multiple facets of a topic. A single
search cannot capture the full picture when the question spans several sub-
topics.
This strategy uses sub-query decomposition, another technique from
Chapter 5, but applies it in a targeted way. An LLM breaks the analytical
question into several focused sub-questions, each covering a different angle.
Each sub-question runs its own retrieval, producing a pool of candidates from
different corners of the knowledge base.
--- PAGE 228 ---
The challenge is that these candidate pools may overlap. Two sub-
questions might retrieve the same popular passages. So an LLM reviews the
full pool and selects a final set that maximizes both relevance and diversity,
ensuring each selected document contributes something distinct. The result
is broad, non-redundant coverage of the analytical question. The following
illustration shows how a single analytical query expands into multiple
retrieval paths that converge on a diverse result set.
The analytical retrieval strategy: a query splits into multiple sub-queries, each retrieving different
documents, then an LLM selects the most diverse and relevant subset from the combined pool
Opinion Strategy: Diversity Through Viewpoint Identification
Opinion queries need balance. If the knowledge base contains ten documents
supporting one viewpoint and two supporting another, standard retrieval will
surface a lopsided picture.
This strategy starts by asking an LLM to identify the distinct viewpoints or
perspectives that exist on the topic. Each identified viewpoint becomes a
search term, combined with the original query. Retrieval runs separately for
--- PAGE 229 ---
each viewpoint, ensuring that even minority perspectives get representation
in the candidate pool.
An LLM then reviews the combined pool and selects documents that
represent the broadest range of opinions. This is not about finding the most
relevant passages. It is about finding the most representative set of
perspectives.
Contextual Strategy: Personalization Through Context Incorporation
Contextual queries depend on information about the user that is not in the
query itself. “Which plan should I upgrade to?” means nothing without
knowing the user’s current plan, usage patterns, and budget constraints.
This strategy takes user context (which must be provided alongside the
query) and asks an LLM to reformulate the query so that the context is woven
into the search terms. The contextualized query then runs against the vector
store with over-retrieval, and each candidate is ranked by an LLM that
considers both relevance to the query and alignment with the user’s specific
situation.
Step 3: Generate the Response
Regardless of which strategy was used, the final set of retrieved documents is
passed to the language model alongside the original query. The generation
step is identical across all four paths. Only the retrieval path differed.
The core insight: the same question asked in a different way requires a
different search process. A system that recognizes this and adapts its
retrieval accordingly will outperform one that treats every question the
same.
Algorithm Sketch: Adaptive Retrieval
1. Receive query
2. Classify query type (Factual, Analytical, Opinion, Contextual)
3. Route to matching retrieval strategy
4. Execute strategy-specific retrieval
5. Pass retrieved documents + original query to LLM
6. Return generated response
--- PAGE 230 ---
The following diagram shows the complete adaptive retrieval architecture
with the classifier routing to all four strategies, each feeding into the shared
response generation step.
The complete adaptive retrieval pipeline: a query enters the classifier, gets routed to one of four
specialized strategies, and all paths converge at the response generation step
When to Use This
Best for:
Knowledge bases that serve diverse query patterns (factual lookups,
analytical exploration, opinion gathering)
Customer-facing systems where users ask fundamentally different types
of questions
Enterprise search platforms spanning multiple document types and use
cases
Overkill when:
--- PAGE 231 ---
All queries are the same type (e.g., a FAQ system where every question is
factual)
The knowledge base is small enough that top-K retrieval already captures
everything relevant
Latency budget is very tight, since classification adds an extra LLM call
before retrieval begins
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency
Medium to High: one classification call plus
strategy-specific LLM calls (ranking,
decomposition, or viewpoint identification)
Complexity High: four separate retrieval strategies to build,
test, and maintain
Cost
High: multiple LLM calls per query for
classification, query enhancement, and
document ranking
Accuracy gain Significant for mixed-type workloads; marginal
if queries are mostly one type
Compared to standard retrieval with query transformations
(Chapter 5):
Query transformations apply a fixed enhancement to every query. Adaptive
retrieval first decides what kind of enhancement is appropriate, then applies a
strategy-specific protocol. If your queries are homogeneous, the fixed
approach from Chapter 5 is simpler and nearly as effective. If your queries
span multiple types with genuinely different retrieval needs, adaptive
retrieval delivers better results at the cost of higher complexity and more
LLM calls.
Key Takeaways
Not all queries benefit from the same retrieval strategy; factual questions
need precision, analytical questions need breadth, opinion questions
need diversity, and contextual questions need personalization
--- PAGE 232 ---
A lightweight LLM-based classifier can route each query to the right
strategy with a single additional call
Each strategy applies a different combination of familiar techniques:
query rewriting, sub-query decomposition, LLM-based ranking, and
diversity selection
The cost is real: classification plus strategy-specific LLM calls add latency
and expense to every query
Adaptive retrieval is most valuable when a system serves genuinely
diverse query types; for homogeneous workloads, simpler approaches
suffice
--- PAGE 233 ---
Chapter 19: Explainable Retrieval
Pairing every retrieved document with a natural language explanation
of why it is relevant to the query.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 3 (verification and
source attribution)
The Problem
A compliance officer at a financial firm types a question into the company’s
internal knowledge base: “What are our obligations for reporting suspicious
transactions?” The system returns five documents. Three look relevant based
on their titles. Two seem unrelated at first glance. The officer opens each one,
skims through paragraphs of dense regulatory text, and tries to figure out
why the system chose these particular documents. After fifteen minutes of
reading, she discovers that one of the seemingly unrelated documents
contains a buried clause about cross-border transaction thresholds that
directly addresses her question. But she almost skipped it.
This is the transparency gap in standard retrieval. The system found the
right documents but gave the user no help understanding why. The user is
left to reconstruct the system’s reasoning on her own, one document at a
time. In low-stakes settings this is merely annoying. In high-stakes domains
like legal research, medical information, or financial compliance, it erodes
trust and wastes expert time.
The situation gets worse when results seem wrong. Without explanations,
users cannot tell whether the system retrieved a document for a good reason
they have not yet considered, or whether the retrieval genuinely missed the
mark. They have no basis for trusting or challenging the results. The
following illustration shows the contrast between opaque results that leave
users guessing and annotated results that make the reasoning visible.
--- PAGE 234 ---
A split screen: on the left, a user stares at a plain list of five documents with question marks floating
above, looking confused. On the right, the same five documents each have a small explanation card
attached, and the user looks confident with a checkmark above.
The Core Idea
The fix is straightforward: after retrieving documents, ask a language model
to explain why each one is relevant. For every query-document pair, the
system generates a short explanation connecting the query’s intent to the
document’s content. The user sees not just what was retrieved, but why.
Think of it like the difference between a waiter who silently places a dish
on your table and a sommelier who explains why a particular wine
complements your meal. The sommelier does not change what is served.
They add a layer of reasoning that helps you appreciate and trust the
selection. In the same way, explainable retrieval does not alter the retrieved
documents. It wraps each result with a reason, turning a list of results into a
list of reasoned results. The following illustration brings this analogy to life.
--- PAGE 235 ---
A visual analogy with two scenes side by side. Left scene: a waiter silently places a wine bottle on a
table, the diner has a question mark above their head. Right scene: a sommelier presents the same
bottle with a small speech bubble containing a note icon, the diner smiles with a lightbulb above their
head.
How It Works
The architecture wraps around any existing retrieval system. The retriever
itself does not change. What changes is what happens after retrieval.
Step 1: Retrieve as Usual
The query enters the vector store and the system retrieves the top-K most
similar documents, exactly as in a standard RAG pipeline. Nothing about the
retrieval mechanism changes. Whether you use cosine similarity, maximum
marginal relevance, or fusion retrieval from Chapter 12, the retrieval step
remains the same.
Step 2: Pair Each Result with the Query
--- PAGE 236 ---
For each retrieved document, the system constructs a prompt that includes
both the original query and the document’s content. This pairing is the key
input to the explanation step. The prompt asks the language model to analyze
the relationship between the two and articulate why this particular document
matters for this particular question.
Step 3: Generate Explanations
The language model reads both the query and the document, then produces a
natural language explanation. A good explanation identifies which specific
aspects of the document address the query’s intent. It might note that the
document contains a definition the user is looking for, provides supporting
evidence for a claim, or covers a related subtopic that broadens the answer.
The diagram below shows how a single query-document pair flows through
the explanation step.
A concept close-up diagram: on the left, a speech bubble icon labeled ‘Query’ and a document icon
labeled ‘Doc’ both feed arrows into a central rounded rectangle with a brain icon labeled ‘LLM’. From
the right side of the LLM box, a larger speech bubble emerges containing three short horizontal lines
representing explanation text.
--- PAGE 237 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Explainable Retrieval
1. Receive user query
2. Retrieve top-K documents from vector store
3. For each retrieved document:
   a. Build prompt with query and document content
   b. Ask LLM: "Why is this document relevant?"
   c. Capture the explanation
4. Return list of (document, explanation) pairs
Step 4: Return Annotated Results
The final output is a list where each entry contains the original document
content alongside its generated explanation. Users can scan the explanations
to quickly understand the retrieval logic without reading each document in
full. When an explanation does not make sense or feels like a stretch, it
signals that the retrieval may have returned a false positive, giving the user
an immediate basis for filtering.
The complete pipeline adds an explanation loop after the standard retrieval
step, as the following diagram shows.
--- PAGE 238 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right with four stages connected by arrows. Stage 1: a
speech bubble icon on the far left. Stage 2: a cylinder icon labeled ‘Search’ representing the vector store.
Stage 3: a rounded rectangle labeled ‘Explain’ with a loop arrow icon, showing multiple document-
explanation pairs being generated. Stage 4: on the far right, a stack of documents each with a small
attached card, representing annotated results.
Crafting the Explanation Prompt
The quality of explanations depends entirely on how you instruct the
language model. Three principles produce consistently useful output.
Specificity over generality. Ask the model to identify which parts of the
document address which aspects of the query. An explanation that says “this
document is about transactions, which relates to your question about
transactions” adds almost nothing. An explanation that says “this document
defines the reporting threshold for cross-border transactions exceeding a
certain amount, which directly addresses the obligation criteria in your
query” is actionable.
Query-awareness. The explanation should reflect the user’s actual
intent, not just the document’s topic. Two different queries might retrieve the
--- PAGE 239 ---
same document for different reasons. The explanation should capture the
specific reason for this particular query.
Brevity. Explanations that run longer than two or three sentences defeat
the purpose. The goal is to save the user time, not add more text to read.
Instruct the model to keep explanations concise and focused on the single
strongest connection between query and document.
The power of explainable retrieval is not in changing what is retrieved.
It is in making the retrieval reasoning visible, turning a black box into a
glass box.
When to Use This
Best for:
High-stakes domains where users need to trust and verify retrieval
results (legal, medical, compliance)
User-facing search interfaces where non-technical users interact with the
system
Debugging and quality assurance during RAG system development
Audit trails where decisions need documented reasoning
Overkill when:
The retrieved documents feed directly into generation without user
review
Latency is the primary constraint and every millisecond counts
The retrieval results are simple and self-explanatory (e.g., FAQ matching)
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency High: adds one LLM call per retrieved
document
Complexity Low: wraps existing retriever with no
architectural changes
--- PAGE 240 ---
Factor Impact
Cost Medium: K additional LLM calls per query
Accuracy gain None: does not change what is retrieved, only
adds transparency
Compared to source attribution (Chapter 3):
Source attribution highlights which passages in a document support the
generated answer. Explainable retrieval explains why each document was
retrieved in the first place. They operate at different stages: attribution works
after generation, while explainable retrieval works between retrieval and
generation. The key difference is shown below. For maximum transparency in
critical applications, you can use both together.
A comparison diagram with two horizontal rows. Top row labeled ‘Explain’ shows: Query arrow to
Search arrow to documents with attached explanation cards arrow to LLM arrow to Answer. The
explanation cards appear between Search and LLM. Bottom row labeled ‘Attribute’ shows: Query
arrow to Search arrow to documents arrow to LLM arrow to Answer with highlighted source spans.
The highlights appear after the Answer. A bracket on the right labels the top row ‘Why retrieved?’ and
the bottom row ‘What supports answer?’.
--- PAGE 241 ---
Key Takeaways
Explainable retrieval adds a language model explanation step after
standard retrieval, pairing each document with a reason for its relevance
The retrieval mechanism itself does not change; explanations are a post-
retrieval annotation layer
Explanation quality depends on prompt design: ask for specific
connections between query intent and document content, not generic
relevance statements
The main cost is latency and token usage: one additional LLM call per
retrieved document
This technique is most valuable in high-stakes, user-facing applications
where trust and transparency matter more than raw speed
--- PAGE 242 ---
Chapter 20: Corrective RAG
Adding a quality gate after retrieval that evaluates each document’s
relevance and dynamically switches to web search when the local
knowledge base falls short.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 5 (query
transformations)
The Problem
A product manager asks the company knowledge base: “What are the new
data privacy regulations announced last quarter?” The system retrieves three
chunks from an internal policy document written two years ago. The chunks
mention data privacy in general terms but say nothing about last quarter’s
announcements. The language model, trusting what it received, weaves those
outdated paragraphs into a confident-sounding answer that is entirely wrong.
The user has no way to know the system served stale information, and the
system has no way to know it failed.
This is the blind confidence problem. Standard retrieval pipelines have no
self-awareness. They retrieve the closest matches from the vector store and
pass them straight to the language model, regardless of whether those
matches actually answer the question. When the knowledge base covers the
topic well, this works fine. When it does not, the pipeline silently produces
hallucinated or irrelevant answers built on a weak foundation.
The gap is especially dangerous for questions that fall in a gray zone. The
retrieved documents are not completely off-topic, so cosine similarity scores
look reasonable, but they do not contain the specific information the user
needs. The system has no mechanism to recognize this mismatch and no
fallback plan when it occurs. The following illustration shows how a standard
pipeline confidently serves a poor answer because it never checks whether its
retrieval actually succeeded.
--- PAGE 243 ---
A split screen. Left side labeled ‘Blind’: a query arrow goes to a vector store cylinder, three document
rectangles come out, and flow straight into a lightbulb that generates an answer. A red X appears on the
answer. Right side labeled ‘Aware’: the same flow, but between the documents and the lightbulb sits a
checkpoint shield icon that inspects the documents. One path leads to the lightbulb with a green
checkmark, another path leads to a globe icon for web search.
The Core Idea
Corrective RAG inserts a quality gate between retrieval and generation. After
the vector store returns its results, a language model evaluates how well each
document actually addresses the query. Based on that evaluation, the system
takes one of three paths: use the document directly if it is highly relevant,
discard it and search the web if it is clearly irrelevant, or combine the
document with web results if the relevance is ambiguous.
Think of it like an editor at a news desk reviewing a reporter’s source
material before the story goes to print. If the sources are strong and directly
support the story, the editor approves publication. If the sources are weak or
off-topic, the editor sends the reporter back out to find better material from
external sources. And if the sources are partially relevant but not sufficient on
--- PAGE 244 ---
their own, the editor runs with a blended approach: use what is good from the
internal sources and supplement with outside reporting. The editor does not
change how the reporter gathers initial sources. They add a checkpoint that
prevents weak material from reaching the reader. The following illustration
brings this editorial analogy to life.
A visual analogy with a news desk theme. On the left, a reporter figure hands a stack of documents to
an editor figure seated at a desk. Three arrows branch from the editor to three outcomes. Top arrow
labeled ‘Use’ leads to a newspaper icon with a green check. Middle arrow labeled ‘Mix’ leads to the
documents plus a globe icon merging together. Bottom arrow labeled ‘Search’ leads to just a globe icon.
The editor acts as the quality checkpoint.
How It Works
Step 1: Retrieve Documents
The query enters the vector store and the system retrieves the top-K most
similar documents, exactly as in standard retrieval. Nothing about this step
--- PAGE 245 ---
changes. Whether you use cosine similarity, fusion retrieval from Chapter 12,
or any other search strategy, the initial retrieval remains the same.
Step 2: Score Each Document for Relevance
Here is where Corrective RAG diverges. For each retrieved document, a
language model evaluates how relevant it is to the query. The evaluator
assigns a numerical score between zero and one. A score near one means the
document directly addresses the query. A score near zero means it is off-topic.
Scores in the middle indicate partial relevance.
This evaluation is more nuanced than the raw similarity score from the
vector store. Vector similarity tells you how close two text fragments are in
embedding space. The relevance evaluator asks a deeper question: does this
document actually contain information that helps answer this specific query?
A document about data privacy might have high cosine similarity to a query
about new privacy regulations, but the evaluator can recognize that the
document discusses privacy principles in general without mentioning any
recent regulatory changes.
Step 3: Route Based on Confidence
The maximum relevance score across all retrieved documents determines
which path the system takes. This three-way routing is the heart of the
technique. The following diagram shows the decision logic and the three
resulting paths.
--- PAGE 246 ---
A concept close-up of the three-way routing decision. At the top, three small document rectangles with
score badges showing ‘0.9’, ‘0.5’, and ‘0.2’. Below them, a diamond-shaped decision node. Three arrows
branch out from the diamond. The left arrow labeled ‘High’ leads to a single document icon with a
green border. The middle arrow labeled ‘Mixed’ leads to a document icon plus a globe icon side by side.
The right arrow labeled ‘Low’ leads to just a globe icon with an orange border.
High confidence (score above 0.7). The most relevant document is
strong enough to stand on its own. The system uses it directly as the
knowledge source for generation. No additional processing is needed.
Low confidence (score below 0.3). None of the retrieved documents
adequately address the query. The local knowledge base does not have what
the user needs. The system rewrites the query to be more suitable for web
search and retrieves fresh information from the internet. The web results are
then refined to extract key points before being passed to generation.
Ambiguous confidence (score between 0.3 and 0.7). The retrieved
documents contain some relevant information but are not sufficient alone.
The system hedges its bets by combining the best local document with web
search results. Both sources go through knowledge refinement, and the
combined output provides a more complete answer than either source could
alone.
--- PAGE 247 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Corrective RAG Routing
1. Retrieve top-K documents from vector store
2. Score each document for relevance (0 to 1)
3. Find maximum relevance score across all documents
4. If max score > 0.7: use the best document directly
5. If max score < 0.3: rewrite query, search the web, refine results
6. If 0.3 ≤ max score ≤ 0.7: combine best document with refined web results
7. Generate response from the selected knowledge source
Step 4: Refine and Supplement Knowledge
When the system triggers a web search, either for low-confidence or
ambiguous cases, two additional processing steps occur. First, the original
query is rewritten into a form better suited for web search engines. Web
searches work differently from vector retrieval. A query that performs well
against an embedding index might be too specific or too abstract for a search
engine. The rewriter reshapes the query to match web search expectations.
Second, the raw web search results go through knowledge refinement. The
system extracts key points from the search results, discarding noise,
advertisements, and tangential information. What remains is a clean set of
bullet points that capture the essential facts. In ambiguous cases, these
refined web points are merged with the content from the best local document,
giving the language model a richer knowledge base to work from.
The complete pipeline, from query to evaluated retrieval to dynamic
routing to response generation, is shown in the following diagram.
--- PAGE 248 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right. Stage 1: a speech bubble icon on the far left
representing the query. Stage 2: a cylinder icon labeled ‘Retrieve’. Stage 3: a shield icon labeled ‘Score’
with a small gauge indicator. Stage 4: a diamond shape labeled ‘Route’ with three forking arrows. The
top fork leads directly to Stage 5 (a lightbulb labeled ‘Generate’). The middle fork passes through both a
document icon and a globe icon merging before reaching Stage 5. The bottom fork passes through a
globe icon and a funnel icon labeled ‘Refine’ before reaching Stage 5.
Step 5: Generate the Response
With the appropriate knowledge source selected and refined, the language
model generates the final answer. The generation step itself is identical to
standard RAG. What changes is the quality and appropriateness of the
knowledge it receives. By dynamically choosing between local documents,
web results, or a combination, the system ensures the language model always
works with the best available information.
The core insight of Corrective RAG: retrieval is not a fire-and-forget
step. By evaluating what comes back and switching strategies when the
results fall short, the system turns a fragile one-shot retrieval into a
resilient, self-correcting process.
--- PAGE 249 ---
When to Use This
Best for:
Knowledge bases with uneven coverage, where some topics are well-
documented and others have gaps
Applications that need current information that may not yet be in the
local knowledge base
High-stakes systems where a confidently wrong answer is worse than a
slightly slower correct one
Domains where questions frequently fall outside the scope of the
indexed documents
Overkill when:
The knowledge base is comprehensive and regularly updated, covering
virtually all expected queries
External web access is not available or not permitted (regulated
environments)
Latency is the primary constraint and every query must return within
milliseconds
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency High: adds K evaluation calls plus potential
web search
Complexity Medium: requires relevance scoring, query
rewriting, and web search integration
Cost Medium to High: multiple LLM calls for
evaluation plus potential web API costs
Accuracy gain Significant for queries outside local knowledge
base coverage
Compared to standard RAG:
Standard RAG trusts whatever the vector store returns. Corrective RAG
verifies before trusting. The cost is additional latency from the evaluation
step and potential web search. The benefit is dramatically better answers
when the local knowledge base does not cover the query well. If your
--- PAGE 250 ---
knowledge base has comprehensive coverage and is regularly updated,
standard RAG may be sufficient. If there are coverage gaps or if queries
frequently touch on recent events, Corrective RAG provides a meaningful
safety net.
Compared to explainable retrieval (Chapter 19):
Explainable retrieval tells the user why documents were retrieved but does
not change what is retrieved. Corrective RAG changes what is used for
generation by dynamically switching sources. They solve different problems:
transparency versus accuracy. In systems where both matter, Corrective RAG
can ensure the right sources are selected, while explainable retrieval can
explain the selection to the user. The following diagram contrasts the
straight-line standard pipeline with the branching corrective pipeline.
A comparison diagram with two rows. Top row labeled ‘Standard’: query arrow to vector store cylinder
arrow to documents arrow to lightbulb, a straight line with no checkpoint. Bottom row labeled
‘Corrective’: query arrow to vector store cylinder arrow to a shield checkpoint arrow that branches
three ways, all converging at the lightbulb. The bottom row is visibly more robust with the branching
paths.
--- PAGE 251 ---
Key Takeaways
Corrective RAG adds a relevance evaluation step between retrieval and
generation, turning blind retrieval into assessed retrieval
A three-way routing mechanism directs the pipeline based on confidence:
use the local document, search the web, or combine both sources
The relevance evaluator provides deeper assessment than raw vector
similarity by checking whether the document actually addresses the
specific query
Web search acts as a fallback when local knowledge is insufficient, with
query rewriting and knowledge refinement to bridge the gap between
vector retrieval and web search formats
The technique is most valuable when knowledge base coverage is uneven
or when queries may require information not yet indexed locally
The main costs are additional latency from evaluation calls and the
infrastructure needed for web search integration
--- PAGE 252 ---
Chapter 21: Graph RAG
Building a knowledge graph from documents, clustering it into
communities, and using graph structure to answer both specific and
big-picture questions that chunk-based retrieval cannot handle.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 4 (proposition
chunking concepts)
The Problem
A policy analyst loads five hundred pages of internal reports into a RAG
system and asks: “What are the three biggest risks facing the organization
this year?” The system retrieves the five chunks most similar to that query. It
finds a paragraph mentioning cybersecurity threats, another about supply
chain delays, and three more that are vaguely related but not quite on point.
The generated answer covers cybersecurity and supply chains but misses the
budget shortfalls discussed in a different set of reports, the leadership
transitions mentioned in the HR documents, and the regulatory changes
scattered across legal memos.
The problem is structural. The answer to a big-picture question is never
sitting in any single chunk. It is distributed across dozens of documents,
woven into entity relationships that span the entire knowledge base. Standard
retrieval finds chunks that look similar to the query. It cannot synthesize
themes that emerge only when you connect information across many
documents.
The gap is equally painful for connection questions. “How is the marketing
team’s new campaign related to the product recall last quarter?” requires
tracing a chain of relationships: the recall affected customer trust, which
triggered a rebranding initiative, which the marketing team then built their
campaign around. Each link in that chain lives in a different document. No
single chunk contains the full story, and no amount of cosine similarity will

--- PAGE 10 ---
Introduction
A guide to 22 retrieval-augmented generation techniques, explained
through intuition and illustrations instead of code.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation has become one of the most important
patterns in applied AI. The idea is deceptively simple: instead of asking a
language model to answer from memory, you first retrieve relevant
information from your own documents and hand it to the model as context.
The model reads, then answers. No hallucination. No guesswork.
But that simple idea hides a landscape of complexity. How should you split
documents into searchable pieces? What if the user’s question is vague or
ambiguous? What if the retrieved passages are almost-but-not-quite
relevant? What if the model ignores the context and hallucinates anyway?
Each of these failure modes has spawned its own family of techniques, and
the field is growing fast.
This book maps that landscape. It covers 22 distinct RAG techniques, from
the foundational pipeline through advanced strategies like graph-based
retrieval and self-correcting systems. Each chapter extracts the algorithm and
intuition from a real implementation, then teaches it without a single line of
code. The following illustration shows how these techniques span the full
RAG pipeline, from how you prepare documents to how you verify the final
answer.
--- PAGE 11 ---
The 22 techniques span seven stages of the RAG pipeline, from foundational document preparation on
the left through chunking, query improvement, retrieval, post-processing, and advanced strategies, to
evaluation on the right.
--- PAGE 12 ---
Where This Book Comes From
This book is based on the RAG Techniques open-source repository, a
collection of over 20 working notebook implementations covering the full
spectrum of RAG strategies. The repository has become one of the most
widely used RAG references in the community, with millions of developers
using it to learn and build production systems. Each chapter in this book
takes one of those implementations, extracts the underlying algorithm, and
teaches it as a standalone concept without code.
If you want to go deeper after reading a chapter, the repository has a
runnable notebook for every technique covered here. The book gives you the
intuition. The repo gives you the implementation. They are designed to
complement each other.
For ongoing updates on RAG, AI engineering, and new techniques as they
emerge, the DiamantAI Newsletter covers the latest developments in applied
AI with the same focus on practical understanding over hype. New
techniques, architectural patterns, and lessons from production systems are
published regularly.
--- PAGE 13 ---
Who This Book Is For
This book is for developers and technical product managers who want to
understand RAG deeply. Not the API calls. Not the framework boilerplate.
The underlying ideas: why each technique exists, how it works at the
algorithmic level, and when it is the right choice.
If you have tried building a RAG system and found that the basic approach
works sometimes but fails in frustrating, unpredictable ways, this book will
help you understand why and give you a toolkit of solutions. If you are
evaluating RAG techniques for a product and need to understand the
tradeoffs without wading through research papers, this book is your shortcut.
This is not a code cookbook. There are no imports, no function calls, no
framework-specific instructions. Every technique is explained as a process
you could implement in any language, with any library. The goal is
understanding that transfers.
--- PAGE 14 ---
How the Book Is Organized
The 22 chapters follow a natural progression, roughly mirroring the stages of
a RAG pipeline. You can think of them in seven groups.
Foundations (Chapters 1-3) build the core pipeline. Chapter 1
introduces simple RAG: splitting documents, creating embeddings, retrieving
chunks, and generating answers. Chapter 2 extends this to structured data
like spreadsheets. Chapter 3 adds verification, teaching the system to check
its own work.
Smarter Chunking (Chapters 4, 7, 8, 9) tackles the question of how
to break documents into pieces. Proposition chunking (Chapter 4)
decomposes text into atomic facts. Contextual chunk headers (Chapter 7)
attach source metadata to every piece. Context enrichment windows (Chapter
8) expand retrieved passages to include surrounding context. Semantic
chunking (Chapter 9) splits text where the meaning shifts rather than at
arbitrary character counts.
Query Strategies (Chapters 5-6) improve retrieval by transforming the
question itself. Query transformations (Chapter 5) rewrite vague questions
into precise ones. HyDE (Chapter 6) generates a hypothetical answer first,
then uses it to search for real documents.
Post-Retrieval Processing (Chapters 10, 11, 13) refines what comes
back from the search. Contextual compression (Chapter 10) strips retrieved
passages down to only the relevant sentences. Document augmentation
(Chapter 11) pre-generates questions for each chunk so retrieval can match
on intent. Reranking (Chapter 13) uses a second model to re-score and re-
order results by relevance.
Retrieval Strategies (Chapters 12, 14, 15) explore different ways to
search. Fusion retrieval (Chapter 12) combines keyword and semantic search.
Hierarchical indices (Chapter 14) search at multiple levels of granularity,
from summaries down to paragraphs. Dartboard retrieval (Chapter 15) targets
the center of relevance using progressively tighter filters.
Advanced and Specialized (Chapters 16-21) address harder
problems. Multi-modal RAG (Chapter 16) handles images alongside text.
Retrieval with feedback loops (Chapter 17) learns from user interactions to
improve over time. Adaptive retrieval (Chapter 18) dynamically adjusts its
strategy based on query complexity. Explainable retrieval (Chapter 19) shows
--- PAGE 15 ---
its reasoning so users can verify the answer. Corrective RAG (Chapter 20)
detects and repairs its own mistakes mid-pipeline. Graph RAG (Chapter 21)
builds knowledge graphs to capture relationships that flat document chunks
miss.
Measurement (Chapter 22) closes the loop with RAG evaluation: how
to systematically test retrieval quality, faithfulness, and answer relevance so
you can compare techniques with confidence.
The book ends with an Appendix that serves as a decision guide. If you
have a specific problem and want to know which technique to try first, start
there. The following diagram shows how the chapters connect, from
foundations through each layer of sophistication to evaluation.
The book follows a climbing path through seven stages, starting with foundations and progressing
through increasingly sophisticated techniques toward evaluation at the summit.
How to Read This Book
--- PAGE 16 ---
There are two good ways to use this book. The first is to read it front to back.
The chapters build on each other. Chapter 1 introduces vocabulary and
concepts that every later chapter assumes you know. The progression from
simple techniques to advanced ones mirrors the order most teams follow
when evolving their own RAG systems.
The second is to use it as a reference. If you already have a working RAG
pipeline and want to solve a specific problem (poor retrieval for long
documents, hallucinated answers, vague user queries) you can jump directly
to the relevant chapter. The prerequisites line at the top of each chapter tells
you what background it assumes.
Either way, read Chapter 1 first. It establishes the foundational pipeline
that every other technique modifies, extends, or improves.
Every chapter in this book follows the same pattern: start with a
concrete problem, explain the core insight, walk through the algorithm
step by step, and close with tradeoffs and decision criteria. No code. No
framework lock-in. Just the ideas.
A Note on Illustrations
Every chapter includes custom illustrations designed to make abstract
algorithms concrete. Pipeline diagrams show data flowing through stages.
Before-and-after comparisons reveal what a technique actually changes.
Visual analogies bring real-world metaphors to life. These are not decorative.
They are part of the explanation, and each one is tightly connected to the text
around it.
When you encounter an illustration, read the caption. It summarizes the
key takeaway. Then look at the image in the context of the paragraph above it,
which sets up exactly what you are about to see.
Welcome to the book. Chapter 1 starts with the simplest version of RAG: one
document, one question, one answer. Everything builds from there.
--- PAGE 17 ---
Chapter 1: Simple RAG: The Foundation of
Document-Powered AI
Teaching a language model to look things up before answering, instead
of guessing from memory.
Prerequisites: Basic understanding of what a language model (LLM) does.
Familiarity with the idea of a database.
The Problem
Imagine you have a 200-page report about climate change. You need a quick
answer: “What does chapter 2 say about the main cause of climate change?”
So you ask a language model.
The model gives you a generic, textbook-style answer about greenhouse
gases. It sounds reasonable. But it is not drawn from your report. The model
has never seen your document. It is answering from memory, from patterns
learned during training. It might even state facts that sound right but are
nowhere in your source material. This is called hallucination, and it is one
of the biggest risks of using language models for knowledge work.
Now scale this up. Your company has thousands of internal documents:
policy manuals, research papers, product specs, legal contracts. Employees
ask questions about these every day. A language model that answers from
memory alone is useless here. It does not know what is in your documents.
The contrast is stark: without access to your documents, the model guesses
and often gets it wrong. With access, it reads the relevant passage and
answers correctly. The following illustration captures this difference.
--- PAGE 18 ---
Without RAG, a robot answers from memory and gets it wrong. With RAG, the same robot reads the
source document and answers correctly.
The Core Idea
RAG, short for Retrieval-Augmented Generation, solves this by adding a
simple step: before the model answers, it first searches your documents for
relevant information.
Think of it as the difference between a closed-book exam and an open-
book exam. In a closed-book exam, the student relies entirely on memory,
staring at the blank page with growing uncertainty. In an open-book exam,
the student can look up the relevant section, read it, and write an informed
answer with confidence. RAG gives your language model an open book.
--- PAGE 19 ---
A closed-book student struggles with a question mark overhead, while an open-book student writes
confidently with a reference book on their desk and a lightbulb above.
The core insight: you do not need to retrain the model or stuff entire
documents into the prompt. You store your documents in a searchable
format, retrieve only the relevant pieces, and hand those pieces to the
model as context.
The model does what it does best: read the context and generate a clear,
grounded answer. The rest of this chapter explains exactly how this works,
step by step.
How It Works
The simple RAG pipeline has two phases: an offline preparation phase
(done once per document) and an online query phase (done each time a
user asks a question).
--- PAGE 20 ---
Phase 1: Preparing the Knowledge Base
Step 1: Load the Document
The process starts by extracting raw text from your source documents. A
PDF is parsed page by page, converting visual content into plain text. This is a
straightforward conversion step, but it matters. Poor text extraction leads to
poor retrieval downstream.
Think of this as a librarian transcribing a book onto index cards so it can
be cataloged and searched.
Step 2: Split Into Chunks
A 200-page document is far too large to search as a single block. The
system splits the text into smaller pieces called chunks. A common default is
chunks of about 1,000 characters (roughly a long paragraph), with 200
characters of overlap between consecutive chunks.
Why overlap? Imagine a critical explanation that spans two paragraphs. If
you split the text exactly between them, neither chunk captures the full
thought. The overlap ensures that information at the boundaries is preserved.
Each chunk carries the last few lines of the previous chunk and the first few
