feedback
Overkill when:
The system handles mostly one-off queries from anonymous users
Query volume is too low to build a useful feedback history
The underlying document collection changes so rapidly that historical
feedback becomes stale quickly
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency Medium: LLM calls to evaluate feedback
relevance add time per query
Complexity Medium: requires feedback storage, evaluation
logic, and periodic maintenance
Cost Medium: each query triggers additional LLM
calls proportional to feedback history size
Accuracy gain Cumulative: improves steadily as feedback
accumulates
Compared to static retrieval with reranking:
Reranking (Chapter 13) applies a smarter second pass to every query, but it
starts fresh each time. A feedback loop carries forward what it learned from
past interactions, meaning it can correct systematic biases that reranking
alone cannot detect. If your system consistently retrieves a particular chunk
that users mark as unhelpful, reranking may keep scoring it highly because it
looks relevant on paper. Feedback-adjusted retrieval will learn to demote it.
The tradeoff is that feedback loops require infrastructure for collecting and
storing ratings, and they need a critical mass of feedback before they show
meaningful improvement.
Key Takeaways
--- PAGE 221 ---
RAG systems are stateless by default: they repeat the same mistakes
because each query starts fresh with no memory of past interactions
A feedback loop collects explicit user ratings on relevance and quality
after each response, building a persistent record of what worked
At query time, the system checks whether past feedback applies to the
current retrieval and adjusts document scores accordingly, promoting
documents with strong track records
Periodically, the system enriches its vector index by adding synthetic
documents built from high-quality question-answer pairs, making
proven knowledge directly searchable
The technique is most valuable in environments with recurring query
patterns and engaged users willing to provide feedback, and its benefits
compound over time
--- PAGE 222 ---
Chapter 18: Adaptive Retrieval
Match the retrieval strategy to the question, not the other way around.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (embeddings, vector search, basic RAG pipeline),
Chapter 5 (query transformations: rewriting and sub-query decomposition)
The Problem
Imagine an enterprise knowledge base serving a customer support team. One
agent asks “What is the refund policy for damaged goods?” and needs a
single, precise passage from the company handbook. Another agent asks
“How does our return policy compare across product categories?” and needs a
broad sweep across multiple documents to assemble an analytical picture. A
third asks “What do customers generally feel about our return process?” and
needs diverse viewpoints from survey responses and complaint logs.
A standard RAG pipeline treats all three queries identically. It embeds the
question, retrieves the top-K nearest chunks, and hands them to the language
model. But these three questions have fundamentally different shapes. The
factual question needs precision. The analytical question needs breadth. The
opinion question needs diversity. A single retrieval strategy cannot optimize
for all three at once.
The result is predictable. The factual question drowns in loosely related
passages when it only needed one. The analytical question gets a narrow slice
when it needed a panoramic view. The opinion question surfaces only the
majority sentiment when it should have captured the full range. The
following diagram shows how a uniform pipeline produces mediocre results
across different query types, while specialized paths give each question what
it actually needs.
--- PAGE 223 ---
A uniform retrieval pipeline on the left produces mediocre results for different query types, while
specialized retrieval paths on the right match each query type to an appropriate strategy
The Core Idea
Adaptive retrieval adds a classification step before retrieval begins. An LLM
reads the incoming query, determines what type of question it is, and routes
it to a retrieval strategy designed specifically for that type. Factual queries get
precision-focused retrieval with relevance ranking. Analytical queries get
decomposed into sub-questions for comprehensive coverage. Opinion queries
get viewpoint-aware retrieval that actively seeks diversity. Contextual queries
get reformulated with user-specific information before searching.
Think of it as a hospital triage system. When a patient arrives at the
emergency department, a triage nurse does not send everyone to the same
specialist. She assesses the patient first: chest pain goes to cardiology, a
broken bone goes to orthopedics, a skin rash goes to dermatology. Each
specialist then follows their own diagnostic protocol tailored to the type of
problem. Adaptive retrieval works the same way: classify first, then apply the
--- PAGE 224 ---
right protocol. The following diagram shows this triage-style routing in
action.
A triage-style routing diagram where incoming queries are assessed and directed to specialized retrieval
strategies, like patients being routed to the right medical specialist
How It Works
The system has two main components: a query classifier that acts as the
router, and a set of specialized retrieval strategies that each handle one query
type. Every query passes through the classifier first, then flows into exactly
one strategy.
Step 1: Classify the Query
The first component is a query classifier. It receives the raw user query and
asks an LLM to categorize it into one of four types:
--- PAGE 225 ---
Factual: seeks specific, verifiable information (“What is the maximum
upload size?”)
Analytical: requires comprehensive analysis across multiple aspects
(“How do our caching strategies affect latency under different load
patterns?”)
Opinion: asks about subjective matters or seeks diverse viewpoints
(“What are the main arguments for and against microservices?”)
Contextual: depends on user-specific circumstances (“Based on my
team’s current architecture, which database should we migrate to?”)
The classifier is a single LLM call with a structured output format that
returns one of these four labels. It runs once per query, adding minimal
latency. The following diagram shows how a query enters the classifier and
emerges tagged with its type, ready for routing to the appropriate strategy.
A query entering a classification box and being routed to one of four labeled output paths: Factual,
Analytical, Opinion, and Contextual
Step 2: Execute the Matched Strategy
--- PAGE 226 ---
Once the query is classified, it is handed to the strategy that matches its type.
Each strategy follows a different protocol, but they all end at the same place:
a set of retrieved documents ready for the language model to synthesize into
an answer.
Factual Strategy: Precision Through Enhancement and Ranking
Factual queries need the right passage, not many passages. This strategy
sharpens the query first, then filters aggressively.
The LLM rewrites the original query into a more precise form optimized
for retrieval. This is the same query rewriting idea from Chapter 5, applied
specifically to tighten a factual question. The enhanced query then runs
against the vector store, but retrieves twice as many candidates as needed (2K
instead of K). Each candidate is then scored individually by an LLM that rates
its relevance to the query on a numeric scale. The top K by relevance score are
kept.
The key insight is the two-stage narrowing: cast a wider initial net, then
use an LLM judge to keep only the most directly relevant passages. This
catches the precise answer even if it was not the closest vector match. The
following diagram traces a factual query through enhancement, over-
retrieval, and LLM-based ranking.
--- PAGE 227 ---
The factual retrieval strategy: a query is enhanced by an LLM, then retrieves double the needed
candidates, which are individually scored and filtered to keep only the most relevant
Algorithm Sketch: Factual Retrieval Strategy
1. Receive classified factual query
2. Ask LLM to rewrite query for precision
3. Retrieve 2K candidates using enhanced query
4. For each candidate, ask LLM to score relevance (1-10)
5. Sort by score descending
6. Return top K candidates
Analytical Strategy: Breadth Through Decomposition and Diversity
Analytical queries need coverage across multiple facets of a topic. A single
search cannot capture the full picture when the question spans several sub-
topics.
This strategy uses sub-query decomposition, another technique from
Chapter 5, but applies it in a targeted way. An LLM breaks the analytical
question into several focused sub-questions, each covering a different angle.
Each sub-question runs its own retrieval, producing a pool of candidates from
different corners of the knowledge base.
--- PAGE 228 ---
The challenge is that these candidate pools may overlap. Two sub-
questions might retrieve the same popular passages. So an LLM reviews the
full pool and selects a final set that maximizes both relevance and diversity,
ensuring each selected document contributes something distinct. The result
is broad, non-redundant coverage of the analytical question. The following
illustration shows how a single analytical query expands into multiple
retrieval paths that converge on a diverse result set.
The analytical retrieval strategy: a query splits into multiple sub-queries, each retrieving different
documents, then an LLM selects the most diverse and relevant subset from the combined pool
Opinion Strategy: Diversity Through Viewpoint Identification
Opinion queries need balance. If the knowledge base contains ten documents
supporting one viewpoint and two supporting another, standard retrieval will
surface a lopsided picture.
This strategy starts by asking an LLM to identify the distinct viewpoints or
perspectives that exist on the topic. Each identified viewpoint becomes a
search term, combined with the original query. Retrieval runs separately for
--- PAGE 229 ---
each viewpoint, ensuring that even minority perspectives get representation
in the candidate pool.
An LLM then reviews the combined pool and selects documents that
represent the broadest range of opinions. This is not about finding the most
relevant passages. It is about finding the most representative set of
perspectives.
Contextual Strategy: Personalization Through Context Incorporation
Contextual queries depend on information about the user that is not in the
query itself. “Which plan should I upgrade to?” means nothing without
knowing the user’s current plan, usage patterns, and budget constraints.
This strategy takes user context (which must be provided alongside the
query) and asks an LLM to reformulate the query so that the context is woven
into the search terms. The contextualized query then runs against the vector
store with over-retrieval, and each candidate is ranked by an LLM that
considers both relevance to the query and alignment with the user’s specific
situation.
Step 3: Generate the Response
Regardless of which strategy was used, the final set of retrieved documents is
passed to the language model alongside the original query. The generation
step is identical across all four paths. Only the retrieval path differed.
The core insight: the same question asked in a different way requires a
different search process. A system that recognizes this and adapts its
retrieval accordingly will outperform one that treats every question the
same.
Algorithm Sketch: Adaptive Retrieval
1. Receive query
2. Classify query type (Factual, Analytical, Opinion, Contextual)
3. Route to matching retrieval strategy
4. Execute strategy-specific retrieval
5. Pass retrieved documents + original query to LLM
6. Return generated response
--- PAGE 230 ---
The following diagram shows the complete adaptive retrieval architecture
with the classifier routing to all four strategies, each feeding into the shared
response generation step.
The complete adaptive retrieval pipeline: a query enters the classifier, gets routed to one of four
specialized strategies, and all paths converge at the response generation step
When to Use This
Best for:
Knowledge bases that serve diverse query patterns (factual lookups,
analytical exploration, opinion gathering)
Customer-facing systems where users ask fundamentally different types
of questions
Enterprise search platforms spanning multiple document types and use
cases
Overkill when:
--- PAGE 231 ---
All queries are the same type (e.g., a FAQ system where every question is
factual)
The knowledge base is small enough that top-K retrieval already captures
everything relevant
Latency budget is very tight, since classification adds an extra LLM call
before retrieval begins
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency
Medium to High: one classification call plus
strategy-specific LLM calls (ranking,
decomposition, or viewpoint identification)
Complexity High: four separate retrieval strategies to build,
test, and maintain
Cost
High: multiple LLM calls per query for
classification, query enhancement, and
document ranking
Accuracy gain Significant for mixed-type workloads; marginal
if queries are mostly one type
Compared to standard retrieval with query transformations
(Chapter 5):
Query transformations apply a fixed enhancement to every query. Adaptive
retrieval first decides what kind of enhancement is appropriate, then applies a
strategy-specific protocol. If your queries are homogeneous, the fixed
approach from Chapter 5 is simpler and nearly as effective. If your queries
span multiple types with genuinely different retrieval needs, adaptive
retrieval delivers better results at the cost of higher complexity and more
LLM calls.
Key Takeaways
Not all queries benefit from the same retrieval strategy; factual questions
need precision, analytical questions need breadth, opinion questions
need diversity, and contextual questions need personalization
--- PAGE 232 ---
A lightweight LLM-based classifier can route each query to the right
strategy with a single additional call
Each strategy applies a different combination of familiar techniques:
query rewriting, sub-query decomposition, LLM-based ranking, and
diversity selection
The cost is real: classification plus strategy-specific LLM calls add latency
and expense to every query
Adaptive retrieval is most valuable when a system serves genuinely
diverse query types; for homogeneous workloads, simpler approaches
suffice
--- PAGE 233 ---
Chapter 19: Explainable Retrieval
Pairing every retrieved document with a natural language explanation
of why it is relevant to the query.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 3 (verification and
source attribution)
The Problem
A compliance officer at a financial firm types a question into the company’s
internal knowledge base: “What are our obligations for reporting suspicious
transactions?” The system returns five documents. Three look relevant based
on their titles. Two seem unrelated at first glance. The officer opens each one,
skims through paragraphs of dense regulatory text, and tries to figure out
why the system chose these particular documents. After fifteen minutes of
reading, she discovers that one of the seemingly unrelated documents
contains a buried clause about cross-border transaction thresholds that
directly addresses her question. But she almost skipped it.
This is the transparency gap in standard retrieval. The system found the
right documents but gave the user no help understanding why. The user is
left to reconstruct the system’s reasoning on her own, one document at a
time. In low-stakes settings this is merely annoying. In high-stakes domains
like legal research, medical information, or financial compliance, it erodes
trust and wastes expert time.
The situation gets worse when results seem wrong. Without explanations,
users cannot tell whether the system retrieved a document for a good reason
they have not yet considered, or whether the retrieval genuinely missed the
mark. They have no basis for trusting or challenging the results. The
following illustration shows the contrast between opaque results that leave
users guessing and annotated results that make the reasoning visible.
--- PAGE 234 ---
A split screen: on the left, a user stares at a plain list of five documents with question marks floating
above, looking confused. On the right, the same five documents each have a small explanation card
attached, and the user looks confident with a checkmark above.
The Core Idea
The fix is straightforward: after retrieving documents, ask a language model
to explain why each one is relevant. For every query-document pair, the
system generates a short explanation connecting the query’s intent to the
document’s content. The user sees not just what was retrieved, but why.
Think of it like the difference between a waiter who silently places a dish
on your table and a sommelier who explains why a particular wine
complements your meal. The sommelier does not change what is served.
They add a layer of reasoning that helps you appreciate and trust the
selection. In the same way, explainable retrieval does not alter the retrieved
documents. It wraps each result with a reason, turning a list of results into a
list of reasoned results. The following illustration brings this analogy to life.
--- PAGE 235 ---
A visual analogy with two scenes side by side. Left scene: a waiter silently places a wine bottle on a
table, the diner has a question mark above their head. Right scene: a sommelier presents the same
bottle with a small speech bubble containing a note icon, the diner smiles with a lightbulb above their
head.
How It Works
The architecture wraps around any existing retrieval system. The retriever
itself does not change. What changes is what happens after retrieval.
Step 1: Retrieve as Usual
The query enters the vector store and the system retrieves the top-K most
similar documents, exactly as in a standard RAG pipeline. Nothing about the
retrieval mechanism changes. Whether you use cosine similarity, maximum
marginal relevance, or fusion retrieval from Chapter 12, the retrieval step
remains the same.
Step 2: Pair Each Result with the Query
--- PAGE 236 ---
For each retrieved document, the system constructs a prompt that includes
both the original query and the document’s content. This pairing is the key
input to the explanation step. The prompt asks the language model to analyze
the relationship between the two and articulate why this particular document
matters for this particular question.
Step 3: Generate Explanations
The language model reads both the query and the document, then produces a
natural language explanation. A good explanation identifies which specific
aspects of the document address the query’s intent. It might note that the
document contains a definition the user is looking for, provides supporting
evidence for a claim, or covers a related subtopic that broadens the answer.
The diagram below shows how a single query-document pair flows through
the explanation step.
A concept close-up diagram: on the left, a speech bubble icon labeled ‘Query’ and a document icon
labeled ‘Doc’ both feed arrows into a central rounded rectangle with a brain icon labeled ‘LLM’. From
the right side of the LLM box, a larger speech bubble emerges containing three short horizontal lines
representing explanation text.
--- PAGE 237 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Explainable Retrieval
1. Receive user query
2. Retrieve top-K documents from vector store
3. For each retrieved document:
   a. Build prompt with query and document content
   b. Ask LLM: "Why is this document relevant?"
   c. Capture the explanation
4. Return list of (document, explanation) pairs
Step 4: Return Annotated Results
The final output is a list where each entry contains the original document
content alongside its generated explanation. Users can scan the explanations
to quickly understand the retrieval logic without reading each document in
full. When an explanation does not make sense or feels like a stretch, it
signals that the retrieval may have returned a false positive, giving the user
an immediate basis for filtering.
The complete pipeline adds an explanation loop after the standard retrieval
step, as the following diagram shows.
--- PAGE 238 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right with four stages connected by arrows. Stage 1: a
speech bubble icon on the far left. Stage 2: a cylinder icon labeled ‘Search’ representing the vector store.
Stage 3: a rounded rectangle labeled ‘Explain’ with a loop arrow icon, showing multiple document-
explanation pairs being generated. Stage 4: on the far right, a stack of documents each with a small
attached card, representing annotated results.
Crafting the Explanation Prompt
The quality of explanations depends entirely on how you instruct the
language model. Three principles produce consistently useful output.
Specificity over generality. Ask the model to identify which parts of the
document address which aspects of the query. An explanation that says “this
document is about transactions, which relates to your question about
transactions” adds almost nothing. An explanation that says “this document
defines the reporting threshold for cross-border transactions exceeding a
certain amount, which directly addresses the obligation criteria in your
query” is actionable.
Query-awareness. The explanation should reflect the user’s actual
intent, not just the document’s topic. Two different queries might retrieve the
--- PAGE 239 ---
same document for different reasons. The explanation should capture the
specific reason for this particular query.
Brevity. Explanations that run longer than two or three sentences defeat
the purpose. The goal is to save the user time, not add more text to read.
Instruct the model to keep explanations concise and focused on the single
strongest connection between query and document.
The power of explainable retrieval is not in changing what is retrieved.
It is in making the retrieval reasoning visible, turning a black box into a
glass box.
When to Use This
Best for:
High-stakes domains where users need to trust and verify retrieval
results (legal, medical, compliance)
User-facing search interfaces where non-technical users interact with the
system
Debugging and quality assurance during RAG system development
Audit trails where decisions need documented reasoning
Overkill when:
The retrieved documents feed directly into generation without user
review
Latency is the primary constraint and every millisecond counts
The retrieval results are simple and self-explanatory (e.g., FAQ matching)
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency High: adds one LLM call per retrieved
document
Complexity Low: wraps existing retriever with no
architectural changes
--- PAGE 240 ---
Factor Impact
Cost Medium: K additional LLM calls per query
Accuracy gain None: does not change what is retrieved, only
adds transparency
Compared to source attribution (Chapter 3):
Source attribution highlights which passages in a document support the
generated answer. Explainable retrieval explains why each document was
retrieved in the first place. They operate at different stages: attribution works
after generation, while explainable retrieval works between retrieval and
generation. The key difference is shown below. For maximum transparency in
critical applications, you can use both together.
A comparison diagram with two horizontal rows. Top row labeled ‘Explain’ shows: Query arrow to
Search arrow to documents with attached explanation cards arrow to LLM arrow to Answer. The
explanation cards appear between Search and LLM. Bottom row labeled ‘Attribute’ shows: Query
arrow to Search arrow to documents arrow to LLM arrow to Answer with highlighted source spans.
The highlights appear after the Answer. A bracket on the right labels the top row ‘Why retrieved?’ and
the bottom row ‘What supports answer?’.
--- PAGE 241 ---
Key Takeaways
Explainable retrieval adds a language model explanation step after
standard retrieval, pairing each document with a reason for its relevance
The retrieval mechanism itself does not change; explanations are a post-
retrieval annotation layer
Explanation quality depends on prompt design: ask for specific
connections between query intent and document content, not generic
relevance statements
The main cost is latency and token usage: one additional LLM call per
retrieved document
This technique is most valuable in high-stakes, user-facing applications
where trust and transparency matter more than raw speed
--- PAGE 242 ---
Chapter 20: Corrective RAG
Adding a quality gate after retrieval that evaluates each document’s
relevance and dynamically switches to web search when the local
knowledge base falls short.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 5 (query
transformations)
The Problem
A product manager asks the company knowledge base: “What are the new
data privacy regulations announced last quarter?” The system retrieves three
chunks from an internal policy document written two years ago. The chunks
mention data privacy in general terms but say nothing about last quarter’s
announcements. The language model, trusting what it received, weaves those
outdated paragraphs into a confident-sounding answer that is entirely wrong.
The user has no way to know the system served stale information, and the
system has no way to know it failed.
This is the blind confidence problem. Standard retrieval pipelines have no
self-awareness. They retrieve the closest matches from the vector store and
pass them straight to the language model, regardless of whether those
matches actually answer the question. When the knowledge base covers the
topic well, this works fine. When it does not, the pipeline silently produces
hallucinated or irrelevant answers built on a weak foundation.
The gap is especially dangerous for questions that fall in a gray zone. The
retrieved documents are not completely off-topic, so cosine similarity scores
look reasonable, but they do not contain the specific information the user
needs. The system has no mechanism to recognize this mismatch and no
fallback plan when it occurs. The following illustration shows how a standard
pipeline confidently serves a poor answer because it never checks whether its
retrieval actually succeeded.
--- PAGE 243 ---
A split screen. Left side labeled ‘Blind’: a query arrow goes to a vector store cylinder, three document
rectangles come out, and flow straight into a lightbulb that generates an answer. A red X appears on the
answer. Right side labeled ‘Aware’: the same flow, but between the documents and the lightbulb sits a
checkpoint shield icon that inspects the documents. One path leads to the lightbulb with a green
checkmark, another path leads to a globe icon for web search.
The Core Idea
Corrective RAG inserts a quality gate between retrieval and generation. After
the vector store returns its results, a language model evaluates how well each
document actually addresses the query. Based on that evaluation, the system
takes one of three paths: use the document directly if it is highly relevant,
discard it and search the web if it is clearly irrelevant, or combine the
document with web results if the relevance is ambiguous.
Think of it like an editor at a news desk reviewing a reporter’s source
material before the story goes to print. If the sources are strong and directly
support the story, the editor approves publication. If the sources are weak or
off-topic, the editor sends the reporter back out to find better material from
external sources. And if the sources are partially relevant but not sufficient on
--- PAGE 244 ---
their own, the editor runs with a blended approach: use what is good from the
internal sources and supplement with outside reporting. The editor does not
change how the reporter gathers initial sources. They add a checkpoint that
prevents weak material from reaching the reader. The following illustration
brings this editorial analogy to life.
A visual analogy with a news desk theme. On the left, a reporter figure hands a stack of documents to
an editor figure seated at a desk. Three arrows branch from the editor to three outcomes. Top arrow
labeled ‘Use’ leads to a newspaper icon with a green check. Middle arrow labeled ‘Mix’ leads to the
documents plus a globe icon merging together. Bottom arrow labeled ‘Search’ leads to just a globe icon.
The editor acts as the quality checkpoint.
How It Works
Step 1: Retrieve Documents
The query enters the vector store and the system retrieves the top-K most
similar documents, exactly as in standard retrieval. Nothing about this step
--- PAGE 245 ---
changes. Whether you use cosine similarity, fusion retrieval from Chapter 12,
or any other search strategy, the initial retrieval remains the same.
Step 2: Score Each Document for Relevance
Here is where Corrective RAG diverges. For each retrieved document, a
language model evaluates how relevant it is to the query. The evaluator
assigns a numerical score between zero and one. A score near one means the
document directly addresses the query. A score near zero means it is off-topic.
Scores in the middle indicate partial relevance.
This evaluation is more nuanced than the raw similarity score from the
vector store. Vector similarity tells you how close two text fragments are in
embedding space. The relevance evaluator asks a deeper question: does this
document actually contain information that helps answer this specific query?
A document about data privacy might have high cosine similarity to a query
about new privacy regulations, but the evaluator can recognize that the
document discusses privacy principles in general without mentioning any
recent regulatory changes.
Step 3: Route Based on Confidence
The maximum relevance score across all retrieved documents determines
which path the system takes. This three-way routing is the heart of the
technique. The following diagram shows the decision logic and the three
resulting paths.
--- PAGE 246 ---
A concept close-up of the three-way routing decision. At the top, three small document rectangles with
score badges showing ‘0.9’, ‘0.5’, and ‘0.2’. Below them, a diamond-shaped decision node. Three arrows
branch out from the diamond. The left arrow labeled ‘High’ leads to a single document icon with a
green border. The middle arrow labeled ‘Mixed’ leads to a document icon plus a globe icon side by side.
The right arrow labeled ‘Low’ leads to just a globe icon with an orange border.
High confidence (score above 0.7). The most relevant document is
strong enough to stand on its own. The system uses it directly as the
knowledge source for generation. No additional processing is needed.
Low confidence (score below 0.3). None of the retrieved documents
adequately address the query. The local knowledge base does not have what
the user needs. The system rewrites the query to be more suitable for web
search and retrieves fresh information from the internet. The web results are
then refined to extract key points before being passed to generation.
Ambiguous confidence (score between 0.3 and 0.7). The retrieved
documents contain some relevant information but are not sufficient alone.
The system hedges its bets by combining the best local document with web
search results. Both sources go through knowledge refinement, and the
combined output provides a more complete answer than either source could
alone.
--- PAGE 247 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Corrective RAG Routing
1. Retrieve top-K documents from vector store
2. Score each document for relevance (0 to 1)
3. Find maximum relevance score across all documents
4. If max score > 0.7: use the best document directly
5. If max score < 0.3: rewrite query, search the web, refine results
6. If 0.3 ≤ max score ≤ 0.7: combine best document with refined web results
7. Generate response from the selected knowledge source
Step 4: Refine and Supplement Knowledge
When the system triggers a web search, either for low-confidence or
ambiguous cases, two additional processing steps occur. First, the original
query is rewritten into a form better suited for web search engines. Web
searches work differently from vector retrieval. A query that performs well
against an embedding index might be too specific or too abstract for a search
engine. The rewriter reshapes the query to match web search expectations.
Second, the raw web search results go through knowledge refinement. The
system extracts key points from the search results, discarding noise,
advertisements, and tangential information. What remains is a clean set of
bullet points that capture the essential facts. In ambiguous cases, these
refined web points are merged with the content from the best local document,
giving the language model a richer knowledge base to work from.
The complete pipeline, from query to evaluated retrieval to dynamic
routing to response generation, is shown in the following diagram.
--- PAGE 248 ---
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right. Stage 1: a speech bubble icon on the far left
representing the query. Stage 2: a cylinder icon labeled ‘Retrieve’. Stage 3: a shield icon labeled ‘Score’
with a small gauge indicator. Stage 4: a diamond shape labeled ‘Route’ with three forking arrows. The
top fork leads directly to Stage 5 (a lightbulb labeled ‘Generate’). The middle fork passes through both a
document icon and a globe icon merging before reaching Stage 5. The bottom fork passes through a
globe icon and a funnel icon labeled ‘Refine’ before reaching Stage 5.
Step 5: Generate the Response
With the appropriate knowledge source selected and refined, the language
model generates the final answer. The generation step itself is identical to
standard RAG. What changes is the quality and appropriateness of the
knowledge it receives. By dynamically choosing between local documents,
web results, or a combination, the system ensures the language model always
works with the best available information.
The core insight of Corrective RAG: retrieval is not a fire-and-forget
step. By evaluating what comes back and switching strategies when the
results fall short, the system turns a fragile one-shot retrieval into a
resilient, self-correcting process.
--- PAGE 249 ---
When to Use This
Best for:
Knowledge bases with uneven coverage, where some topics are well-
documented and others have gaps
Applications that need current information that may not yet be in the
local knowledge base
High-stakes systems where a confidently wrong answer is worse than a
slightly slower correct one
Domains where questions frequently fall outside the scope of the
indexed documents
Overkill when:
The knowledge base is comprehensive and regularly updated, covering
virtually all expected queries
External web access is not available or not permitted (regulated
environments)
Latency is the primary constraint and every query must return within
milliseconds
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Latency High: adds K evaluation calls plus potential
web search
Complexity Medium: requires relevance scoring, query
rewriting, and web search integration
Cost Medium to High: multiple LLM calls for
evaluation plus potential web API costs
Accuracy gain Significant for queries outside local knowledge
base coverage
Compared to standard RAG:
Standard RAG trusts whatever the vector store returns. Corrective RAG
verifies before trusting. The cost is additional latency from the evaluation
step and potential web search. The benefit is dramatically better answers
when the local knowledge base does not cover the query well. If your
--- PAGE 250 ---
knowledge base has comprehensive coverage and is regularly updated,
standard RAG may be sufficient. If there are coverage gaps or if queries
frequently touch on recent events, Corrective RAG provides a meaningful
safety net.
Compared to explainable retrieval (Chapter 19):
Explainable retrieval tells the user why documents were retrieved but does
not change what is retrieved. Corrective RAG changes what is used for
generation by dynamically switching sources. They solve different problems:
transparency versus accuracy. In systems where both matter, Corrective RAG
can ensure the right sources are selected, while explainable retrieval can
explain the selection to the user. The following diagram contrasts the
straight-line standard pipeline with the branching corrective pipeline.
A comparison diagram with two rows. Top row labeled ‘Standard’: query arrow to vector store cylinder
arrow to documents arrow to lightbulb, a straight line with no checkpoint. Bottom row labeled
‘Corrective’: query arrow to vector store cylinder arrow to a shield checkpoint arrow that branches
three ways, all converging at the lightbulb. The bottom row is visibly more robust with the branching
paths.
--- PAGE 251 ---
Key Takeaways
Corrective RAG adds a relevance evaluation step between retrieval and
generation, turning blind retrieval into assessed retrieval
A three-way routing mechanism directs the pipeline based on confidence:
use the local document, search the web, or combine both sources
The relevance evaluator provides deeper assessment than raw vector
similarity by checking whether the document actually addresses the
specific query
Web search acts as a fallback when local knowledge is insufficient, with
query rewriting and knowledge refinement to bridge the gap between
vector retrieval and web search formats
The technique is most valuable when knowledge base coverage is uneven
or when queries may require information not yet indexed locally
The main costs are additional latency from evaluation calls and the
infrastructure needed for web search integration
--- PAGE 252 ---
Chapter 21: Graph RAG
Building a knowledge graph from documents, clustering it into
communities, and using graph structure to answer both specific and
big-picture questions that chunk-based retrieval cannot handle.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 4 (proposition
chunking concepts)
The Problem
A policy analyst loads five hundred pages of internal reports into a RAG
system and asks: “What are the three biggest risks facing the organization
this year?” The system retrieves the five chunks most similar to that query. It
finds a paragraph mentioning cybersecurity threats, another about supply
chain delays, and three more that are vaguely related but not quite on point.
The generated answer covers cybersecurity and supply chains but misses the
budget shortfalls discussed in a different set of reports, the leadership
transitions mentioned in the HR documents, and the regulatory changes
scattered across legal memos.
The problem is structural. The answer to a big-picture question is never
sitting in any single chunk. It is distributed across dozens of documents,
woven into entity relationships that span the entire knowledge base. Standard
retrieval finds chunks that look similar to the query. It cannot synthesize
themes that emerge only when you connect information across many
documents.
The gap is equally painful for connection questions. “How is the marketing
team’s new campaign related to the product recall last quarter?” requires
tracing a chain of relationships: the recall affected customer trust, which
triggered a rebranding initiative, which the marketing team then built their
campaign around. Each link in that chain lives in a different document. No
single chunk contains the full story, and no amount of cosine similarity will
--- PAGE 253 ---
thread these pieces together. The following illustration contrasts the isolated-
chunk view with the connected view that reveals hidden relationships.
A split screen. Left side labeled ‘Chunks’: five separate gray rectangles floating independently with no
connections, a question mark above them. Right side labeled ‘Graph’: the same five rectangles now
connected by lines forming a network, with colored circles at the connection points representing
entities. A lightbulb icon above the graph side.
The Core Idea
Graph RAG transforms your document collection into a knowledge graph,
then uses the graph’s structure for retrieval instead of relying solely on vector
similarity. During indexing, a language model reads every chunk, extracts the
entities it mentions and the relationships between them, and assembles these
into a graph where entities are nodes and relationships are edges. The graph
is then partitioned into communities of densely connected entities, and each
community gets a pre-computed summary.
At query time, the system offers two modes. For specific questions about
particular entities, it locates those entities in the graph and traverses outward
--- PAGE 254 ---
to gather connected context. For broad questions about themes or patterns, it
retrieves the pre-computed community summaries and synthesizes them into
a comprehensive answer.
Think of it like the difference between exploring a city on foot and viewing
it from above. If you want to find a specific restaurant, you start at a known
intersection and walk the nearby streets, checking each block. That is local
search. But if someone asks “What kind of neighborhoods does this city
have?”, walking one street at a time will never give you the full picture. You
need an aerial view that shows how the city clusters into distinct districts,
each with its own character. Graph RAG builds both the street map and the
aerial view during indexing, so it can serve either type of question at query
time. The following illustration brings this city exploration analogy to life.
A visual analogy with a city theme. On the left, a small person figure walks along connected streets
between building icons, with a magnifying glass, representing local search through specific streets. On
the right, the same city is viewed from above as a map divided into three colored neighborhood zones in
muted blue, warm orange, and light gray, each with a small summary card, representing the aerial
community view. A label ‘Local’ sits under the left scene and ‘Global’ under the right scene.
--- PAGE 255 ---
How It Works
Step 1: Extract Entities and Relationships
The system processes each text chunk with a language model, asking it to
identify the entities mentioned and the relationships between them. An entity
is any named thing: a person, organization, location, concept, or event. A
relationship is a connection between two entities: “reports to,” “caused,” “is
located in,” “contradicts.”
For each entity, the model produces a name, a type, and a short
description. For each relationship, it produces the two connected entities and
a description of how they relate. When the same entity appears across
multiple chunks, the system merges the descriptions. When the same
relationship is extracted from different chunks independently, the connection
gains weight, signaling higher confidence.
The extraction uses multiple passes. After the first pass, the model is asked
whether it missed anything, and additional rounds capture entities that were
overlooked. This self-reflection step is important because a single pass on a
long chunk often drops entities mentioned only in passing. The following
diagram shows how a single chunk yields entities and relationships.
--- PAGE 256 ---
A concept close-up. On the left, a document rectangle with short horizontal lines representing text.
Three arrows emerge from the document pointing right. The top two arrows lead to colored circles: a
blue circle labeled ‘Entity’ and an orange circle labeled ‘Entity’. The bottom arrow leads to a line
connecting the two circles, labeled ‘Relation’. The overall visual shows a document decomposing into
graph elements.
Step 2: Build the Knowledge Graph
The extracted entities become nodes and the relationships become edges,
forming a graph that represents the structured knowledge contained in the
knowledge base. An entity that appears in thirty different chunks becomes a
single highly connected node. Two entities mentioned together in five
separate documents get a strongly weighted edge between them.
This graph captures something that flat vector stores cannot: the web of
connections between concepts. A chunk about a CEO and a chunk about a
product recall might never appear together in a similarity search, but in the
graph, the CEO node connects to the company node, which connects to the
product node, which connects to the recall event. The graph makes implicit
connections explicit.
--- PAGE 257 ---
Step 3: Detect Communities
With the graph built, the system applies a community detection algorithm to
partition it into clusters of densely interconnected entities. The Leiden
algorithm is a common choice for this step. It identifies groups of nodes that
have more connections to each other than to the rest of the graph.
The algorithm runs hierarchically. At the broadest level, it might find five
or six large communities representing the major topic areas of the knowledge
base. Within each of those, it finds sub-communities covering more specific
themes. Within those, even finer groupings. The result is a tree of
communities at multiple levels of granularity, from broad overviews down to
narrow subtopics.
For example, a knowledge base of company reports might yield a top-level
community covering “financial performance,” which contains sub-
communities for “revenue growth,” “cost reduction,” and “investment
strategy.” Each sub-community groups the specific entities and relationships
that belong to that theme. The following illustration shows how a flat graph
transforms into colored community clusters.
--- PAGE 258 ---
A before/after diagram. On the left labeled ‘Graph’, a network of circles connected by lines, all in the
same charcoal color, forming a tangled web. On the right labeled ‘Communities’, the same network but
now the circles are colored into three distinct groups: a blue cluster, an orange cluster, and a gray
cluster. Dotted outlines surround each cluster. The connections within each cluster are solid, while
connections between clusters are thinner and lighter.
Step 4: Summarize Each Community
For every community at every level of the hierarchy, the system generates a
natural language summary. The summary describes the key entities in that
community, the relationships between them, and the overall theme the
cluster represents.
These summaries are created during indexing, not at query time. This is a
crucial design choice. Summarizing an entire knowledge base on the fly for
each query would be prohibitively expensive. By pre-computing summaries at
multiple granularity levels, the system converts the impossible task of
“synthesize a million tokens right now” into the tractable task of “combine a
few dozen pre-computed summaries.”
--- PAGE 259 ---
Algorithm Sketch: Graph RAG Indexing
1. Split documents into text chunks
2. For each chunk, extract entities and relationships using LLM
3. Merge duplicate entities, aggregate relationship weights
4. Build knowledge graph (entities as nodes, relationships as edges)
5. Run community detection at multiple hierarchy levels
6. For each community at each level, generate a summary using LLM
7. Store graph, communities, and summaries for query-time retrieval
The following diagram visualizes this five-stage indexing pipeline, from raw
documents through entity extraction, graph construction, community
detection, and summary generation.
A horizontal pipeline diagram flowing left to right with five stages connected by arrows. Stage 1: a stack
of document icons labeled ‘Docs’. Stage 2: scattered entity nodes emerging from the documents, labeled
‘Extract’. Stage 3: the nodes connected into a dense network, labeled ‘Graph’. Stage 4: the network
partitioned into colored clusters within a dotted circle, labeled ‘Cluster’. Stage 5: three colored summary
cards in blue, orange, and gray, labeled ‘Summarize’.
Step 5: Query with Local or Global Search
--- PAGE 260 ---
At query time, the system chooses between two retrieval strategies based on
the nature of the question.
Local search handles questions about specific entities or their
immediate context. The system identifies the entities mentioned in the query,
finds them in the graph, and traverses outward along their edges. It gathers
the matched entities, their descriptions, the relationships connecting them,
and the original text chunks that produced those extractions. All of this
structured context feeds into the language model for answer generation.
Local search is like starting at a known address and exploring the
surrounding blocks.
Global search handles broad questions about themes, patterns, or
summaries across the entire knowledge base. It collects community
summaries at the appropriate hierarchy level and processes them through a
map-reduce pattern. In the map phase, the language model reads batches of
community summaries and generates partial answers, each scored for
relevance. In the reduce phase, the highest-scoring partial answers are
synthesized into a single comprehensive response. Global search is like
reading the neighborhood guides for an entire city to understand its overall
character.
The following diagram shows both search modes side by side.
--- PAGE 261 ---
A comparison diagram with two rows. Top row labeled ‘Local’: a speech bubble on the left, an arrow to
a small graph fragment showing three connected circles with one highlighted in blue, an arrow to a
document icon with connecting lines, an arrow to a lightbulb. The graph fragment shows entity
traversal. Bottom row labeled ‘Global’: a speech bubble on the left, an arrow to three colored cluster
shapes with summary cards, an arrow to a funnel icon, an arrow to a lightbulb. The clusters represent
community summaries being combined.
The key innovation of Graph RAG is not the knowledge graph itself. It is
the community summarization layer that pre-computes answers at
multiple granularity levels, making holistic questions over large
corpora tractable at query time.
When to Use This
Best for:
Corpora with complex relationships between entities across many
documents (legal cases, intelligence reports, organizational knowledge
bases)
--- PAGE 262 ---
Questions that require connecting information scattered across different
documents
Big-picture queries about themes, trends, or patterns across an entire
dataset
Scenarios where the same knowledge base will be queried many times,
amortizing the indexing cost
Overkill when:
Questions are simple fact lookups that a single chunk can answer
The knowledge base is small enough to fit in one context window (direct
summarization is simpler)
The budget does not allow for the high upfront indexing cost
Documents are independent and have few cross-references or shared
entities
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Indexing cost
Very High: LLM processes every chunk for
entity extraction, plus community
summarization
Query latency Low for local search, High for global search
(map-reduce over summaries)
Complexity High: requires graph construction, community
detection, and dual search modes
Accuracy gain Large for cross-document and holistic queries;
minimal for simple fact lookups
Compared to standard RAG:
Standard RAG excels at finding specific passages that are semantically
similar to the query. Graph RAG excels when the answer lives in the
connections between passages rather than in any single passage. If your users
mostly ask targeted questions and your knowledge base is well-organized,
standard RAG is simpler and cheaper. If your users ask “What are the main
themes?” or “How does X relate to Y across all these documents?”, Graph
RAG provides answers that standard RAG structurally cannot.
Compared to fusion retrieval (Chapter 12):
--- PAGE 263 ---
Fusion retrieval combines keyword and semantic search to cast a wider
net, but it still retrieves individual chunks. Graph RAG retrieves structured
subgraphs or pre-computed community summaries. Fusion retrieval
improves recall for single-chunk answers. Graph RAG enables answers that
require synthesizing information across the knowledge base.
Key Takeaways
Graph RAG extracts entities and relationships from every document
chunk, building a knowledge graph that makes implicit connections
explicit
Community detection partitions the graph into clusters of related entities
at multiple hierarchy levels, and each community gets a pre-computed
summary
Local search starts from query-mentioned entities and traverses the
graph outward, gathering structured context that spans multiple
documents
Global search uses a map-reduce pattern over community summaries to
answer broad thematic questions that no single chunk could address
The main cost is indexing: every chunk requires LLM-based extraction,
making it orders of magnitude more expensive than simple embedding-
based indexing
Graph RAG is most valuable for large corpora with rich entity
relationships where users ask both specific and big-picture questions
--- PAGE 264 ---
Chapter 22: RAG Evaluation
Measuring retrieval quality, generation faithfulness, and answer
relevance to systematically diagnose and improve every stage of a RAG
pipeline.
Prerequisites: Chapter 1 (basic RAG pipeline), Chapter 3 (verification
concepts)
The Problem
You have built a RAG system. It retrieves documents, feeds them to a
language model, and produces answers. In your manual testing, it seems to
work. But then a user reports an answer that is confidently wrong. Another
user says the system missed obvious information that is clearly in the
knowledge base. A third user gets a response that is technically correct but
does not address their actual question.
You open the logs and stare at the pipeline. The retriever returned five
chunks. The language model produced an answer. But where did things go
wrong? Did the retriever fetch the wrong documents? Did it fetch the right
documents but the language model hallucinated beyond what was provided?
Or was the answer faithful to the context but simply not relevant to what the
user asked?
This is the diagnostic gap. A RAG pipeline has multiple stages, and a
failure at any stage cascades into a bad final answer. Testing by reading a few
outputs and deciding “this looks right” does not scale and does not diagnose.
You need a systematic way to evaluate each stage independently and the
pipeline as a whole. Without it, improving one component might mask a
regression in another, and you cannot tell which of the twenty techniques in
this book actually helped your specific system.
Think of it like a restaurant health inspection. When a diner gets a bad
meal, the problem could be in the ingredient sourcing, the kitchen
--- PAGE 265 ---
preparation, or the final plating. A health inspector does not just taste the
dish and give a thumbs up. They check the supply chain, the cooking process,
and the presentation separately, because fixing the wrong stage wastes time
and leaves the real problem untouched. RAG evaluation works the same way.
The following illustration shows why evaluating only the final output leaves
critical failure points hidden.
Without systematic evaluation, a RAG pipeline appears to work but hides failures at each stage.
Evaluation adds an inspection layer that reveals which components are underperforming and where
the real problems lie.
The Core Idea
RAG evaluation breaks the pipeline into three relationships and measures
each one independently. These three measurements form what is known as
the evaluation triad.
Context relevance checks the relationship between the query and the
retrieved documents. Did the retriever actually fetch information that helps
--- PAGE 266 ---
answer the question?
Faithfulness checks the relationship between the retrieved documents
and the generated answer. Is every claim in the answer supported by the
context, or did the language model make things up?
Answer relevance checks the relationship between the query and the
generated answer. Does the response actually address what the user asked,
regardless of whether it is faithful to the context?
A RAG pipeline has one input (the query) and two outputs (the retrieved
context and the generated answer). The triad evaluates all three pairwise
connections between these elements. A system can score high on two pillars
and still fail on the third. Faithful and relevant answers built on irrelevant
context are lucky accidents. Relevant and well-grounded answers that do not
address the question waste the user’s time. The triad catches each failure
mode independently. The following diagram shows how the three evaluation
dimensions connect the pipeline’s components.
The evaluation triad measures three pairwise relationships between the query, retrieved context, and
generated answer. Retrieval quality connects the query to the context, faithfulness connects the context
to the answer, and relevance connects the query to the answer.
--- PAGE 267 ---
How It Works
Measuring Context Relevance
Context relevance answers the question: did the retriever do its job? For each
retrieved chunk, the evaluator determines whether that chunk contains
information useful for answering the query. The simplest approach uses a
language model to score each chunk as relevant or not relevant, then
computes the fraction of retrieved chunks that are actually useful.
Two classical retrieval metrics give sharper insight. Precision at K
measures what fraction of the top-K retrieved documents are relevant. If you
retrieve five documents and three are relevant, precision at five is 0.6. This
metric tells you how much noise the retriever introduces. Recall at K
measures what fraction of all relevant documents in the knowledge base were
actually retrieved. If ten documents could help answer the query and you
retrieved three of them, recall is 0.3. This metric tells you how much useful
information the retriever missed.
A more sophisticated metric, context precision, accounts for ranking
order. It penalizes systems that bury relevant documents below irrelevant
ones. Retrieving the same set of documents matters less than retrieving them
in the right order, because most generation prompts give more weight to
documents that appear first. The following illustration shows the difference
between high precision (little noise) and high recall (little missed
information).
--- PAGE 268 ---
Precision measures the fraction of retrieved documents that are actually relevant: three relevant out of
five retrieved gives 0.6. Recall measures the fraction of all relevant documents in the knowledge base
that were actually retrieved: three retrieved out of ten relevant gives 0.3.
Ranking Quality: MRR, MAP, and NDCG
Precision and recall tell you what was retrieved but not how well it was
ranked. Three metrics address ranking quality, and each answers a different
question.
Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) measures how quickly the first relevant
document appears. For each query, the score is one divided by the rank
position of the first correct result. If the first relevant document is at position
one, the score is 1.0. If it is at position three, the score is 0.33. MRR is
averaged across all test queries. Use MRR when there is typically one best
answer per query and you care most about surfacing it quickly.
Mean Average Precision (MAP) extends to queries with multiple
relevant documents. For each query, it computes precision at every rank
where a relevant document appears, averages those values, and then averages
across all queries. MAP rewards systems that rank all relevant documents
--- PAGE 269 ---
early, not just the first one. Use MAP when your queries have multiple
correct documents and you need all of them surfaced near the top.
Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) is the most
general of the three because it handles graded relevance. Instead of treating
documents as simply relevant or not, NDCG allows scores like “highly
relevant,” “somewhat relevant,” and “not relevant.” It computes a gain for
each retrieved document based on its relevance grade, applies a logarithmic
discount based on rank position (so documents ranked lower contribute less),
and normalizes against the ideal ranking. NDCG ranges from zero to one,
with one meaning the system produced a perfect ranking. Use NDCG when
relevance naturally falls on a spectrum rather than a binary, which is the
common case in RAG where some chunks are tangentially useful and others
are exactly on point.
The following illustration shows how the same set of retrieved documents
can have different ranking quality scores depending on where the relevant
items appear in the ranked list.
Ranking quality depends on where relevant documents appear in the result list. A good ranking clusters
all relevant results at the top positions, while a poor ranking scatters them among irrelevant ones, even
when both lists contain the same documents.
--- PAGE 270 ---
Measuring Faithfulness
Faithfulness answers the question: did the language model stick to what the
context actually says? This is the hallucination detector. A faithful answer
contains only claims that can be traced back to the retrieved documents. An
unfaithful answer introduces information the context never provided.
The most common measurement process works in two steps. First, the
generated answer is decomposed into individual claims. A sentence like “The
company was founded in 2005 and has offices in three countries” becomes
two claims: the company was founded in 2005, and the company has offices
in three countries. Second, each claim is verified against the retrieved context.
An LLM-based evaluator checks whether each claim can be logically inferred
from the provided documents.
The faithfulness score is the fraction of supported claims over total claims.
A score of 1.0 means every claim in the answer is grounded in the context. A
score of 0.7 means 30% of the claims are unsupported, which is a significant
hallucination rate. This metric isolates generation quality from retrieval
quality. If faithfulness is low but context relevance is high, the problem is in
the language model or the prompt, not the retriever.
Algorithm Sketch: Faithfulness Scoring
1. Receive the generated answer and the retrieved context
2. Decompose the answer into individual atomic claims
3. For each claim:
   a. Ask: "Can this claim be inferred from the context?"
   b. Label as supported or unsupported
4. Faithfulness = (supported claims) / (total claims)
An alternative approach to hallucination detection avoids claim
decomposition entirely. Sampling-based consistency checking generates
the answer multiple times with different random seeds. If the model
genuinely knows a fact, multiple samples will state it consistently. If a fact is
hallucinated, different samples will contradict each other. For each sentence
in the original answer, the method measures agreement across all sampled
versions. Sentences with low cross-sample consistency are flagged as likely
hallucinated. This approach requires no external knowledge base or retrieval
context, making it useful as a complementary check.
Measuring Answer Relevance
--- PAGE 271 ---
Answer relevance asks: does the response actually address the user’s
question? An answer can be perfectly faithful to the context but completely
miss the point. If a user asks “What is the return policy for electronics?” and
the system returns a faithful summary of the shipping policy, the answer is
grounded but irrelevant.
One approach uses reverse engineering. Given the generated answer, a
language model is asked to produce several hypothetical questions that the
answer seems to address. The metric then computes the similarity between
these generated questions and the original user query. If the answer truly
addresses the query, the reconstructed questions should closely match the
original. If the answer drifted off-topic, the reconstructed questions will
diverge.
A simpler approach uses direct LLM judgment. The evaluator receives the
query and the answer and scores how well the answer addresses the query on
a defined scale. Either approach catches the subtle failure where the retriever
finds relevant context and the generator faithfully summarizes it, but the
summary does not actually answer what was asked.
Measuring Answer Completeness
The triad tells you whether the answer is relevant and faithful, but not
whether it is complete. A response can correctly address part of a multi-
faceted question while ignoring the rest.
The most rigorous approach decomposes the user’s query into sub-
questions, classified by importance. Core sub-questions represent essential
aspects that must be addressed. Background sub-questions cover supporting
context that enriches the answer. Follow-up sub-questions capture
extensions that a thorough answer would anticipate. Completeness is then
measured as the fraction of sub-questions the answer actually addresses,
weighted by importance.
A lighter-weight approach compares the answer against a reference answer
at the claim level. The reference is decomposed into claims, and the evaluator
checks how many of those claims appear in the generated answer. This gives
a recall-like metric for answer content: of everything the answer should have
said, how much did it actually say?
Using a Language Model as Judge
--- PAGE 272 ---
All of the metrics above share a common mechanism: they use a language
model to evaluate another language model’s output. This is the LLM-as-judge
approach. A separate language model, often a different or more capable model
than the one generating answers, reviews the outputs and assigns scores.
The approach works because evaluation is simpler than generation. The
judge performs a focused classification task (is this claim supported? is this
document relevant?) rather than open-ended creation. Four practices
improve judge reliability.
First, use binary or low-granularity scoring rather than fine-grained scales.
A yes-or-no judgment on each claim is more consistent than asking for a
score from one to ten. Second, ask the judge to explain its reasoning before
giving a score. This chain-of-thought approach reduces snap judgments and
can improve correlation with human ratings by fifteen or more percentage
points. Third, evaluate one dimension at a time rather than asking a single
prompt to assess everything simultaneously. Fourth, be aware of known
biases: LLM judges tend to prefer longer answers, may favor their own
outputs, and can be influenced by the order in which options are presented.
Using a different model as judge and randomizing presentation order mitigate
these effects.
The goal of RAG evaluation is not to produce a single quality number. It
is to produce a diagnostic breakdown that tells you exactly which stage
of the pipeline needs attention and which can be left alone.
Reference-Based Text Metrics
When you have a golden reference answer, you can also use traditional text
comparison metrics. BLEU counts n-gram overlaps between the generated
and reference texts, measuring surface-level lexical precision. ROUGE
measures recall-oriented overlap, particularly useful for summarization.
BERTScore uses contextual embeddings to compute semantic similarity
between generated and reference text, capturing paraphrases and synonyms
that word-overlap metrics miss.
These metrics are fast, cheap, and require no LLM calls. Their limitation is
brittleness: a correct answer phrased differently from the reference can score
poorly on BLEU and ROUGE. BERTScore handles paraphrasing better and
shows meaningfully higher alignment with human judgments. In practice,
--- PAGE 273 ---
these metrics work best as a quick sanity check alongside the LLM-based
triad metrics, not as a replacement for them.
Testing Robustness and Noise Sensitivity
A system that works well on clean retrieval may fall apart when the retriever
returns noisy results, which happens regularly in production. Robustness
testing evaluates how gracefully the system degrades when context quality
deteriorates.
Four types of noise matter. Irrelevant noise is completely off-topic
content that should be easy to ignore. Distracting noise is semantically
similar to the query but contains wrong answers, the most dangerous type.
Low-quality noise includes poorly structured text, contradictory claims, or
minimal useful content. Inconsequential noise looks relevant at first
glance but adds no actual information.
The practical testing approach fixes the total number of retrieved
documents and systematically varies the ratio of relevant to noisy content: all
relevant, then eighty percent relevant, then sixty, and so on. This maps the
system’s degradation curve. Research shows that performance often holds
reasonably well up to about fifty percent noise, then exhibits a sharp collapse.
The following illustration shows how answer quality typically degrades as
noise increases.
--- PAGE 274 ---
Answer quality remains stable as retrieval noise increases, then collapses sharply past a critical
threshold. This cliff-like degradation means a system that handles moderate noise can fail suddenly
when conditions worsen even slightly.
Building an Evaluation Dataset
The metrics described above need test cases to run against. An evaluation
dataset is a collection of queries, each paired with the expected relevant
documents and a reference answer. Building this dataset well is as important
as choosing the right metrics.
Start with real user queries whenever possible. Synthetic questions
generated by a language model are useful for development speed, but they
often lack the ambiguity and edge cases that real users produce. A practical
workflow uses both. Generate synthetic test cases from your knowledge base
for rapid iteration during development. Then have domain experts review and
correct these synthetic cases, promoting the validated ones into a curated
golden dataset used for formal evaluation.
Cover the full range of expected queries. Include straightforward fact
lookups, multi-hop questions that require connecting information across
documents, questions where the knowledge base has no answer (the system
--- PAGE 275 ---
should gracefully decline rather than hallucinate), and adversarial queries
designed to trip up the retriever. A dataset of two hundred to five hundred
validated examples provides statistical significance for most use cases.
Component-Level Versus End-to-End Evaluation
The triad metrics provide component-level diagnostics. Context relevance
isolates the retriever. Faithfulness isolates the generator. Answer relevance
checks the overall alignment between input and output. But component-level
evaluation alone is not enough.
End-to-end evaluation treats the entire pipeline as a black box and
measures the final answer against ground truth. This catches interaction
effects that component-level metrics miss. A retriever might return slightly
noisy context that a strong language model handles gracefully, or a weak
language model might struggle even with perfect context. End-to-end metrics
like factual correctness (comparing the answer to a known reference) and
user satisfaction scores measure what actually matters: whether the user gets
a good answer.
The best practice is to use both. Component-level metrics are your
development tool for rapid iteration. End-to-end metrics are your acceptance
test for production. When an end-to-end score drops, the component-level
metrics tell you where to look. The following diagram contrasts the two
evaluation strategies.
--- PAGE 276 ---
Component-level evaluation measures each pipeline stage independently, revealing exactly where
failures occur. End-to-end evaluation treats the entire pipeline as a single unit, measuring only whether
the final answer meets quality standards.
Measuring Latency and Cost
Quality metrics are necessary but not sufficient. A system that scores 0.95 on
faithfulness but takes fifteen seconds and costs fifty cents per query may be
worse in practice than one scoring 0.90 at two seconds and five cents. Latency
and cost are first-class evaluation dimensions.
For latency, track time to first token (how quickly the user sees a response
begin), total response time (end-to-end from query to complete answer), and
per-component breakdown (embedding time, retrieval time, reranking time,
generation time). Knowing which component dominates helps you target
optimization. Track percentiles rather than averages: p50 shows typical
performance, p95 and p99 reveal worst-case behavior that affects user
experience.
For cost, track token consumption per query (input tokens from the
context plus output tokens for the answer), embedding cost per query, and
any reranking costs. Increasing the number of retrieved chunks (K) improves
--- PAGE 277 ---
recall but increases both latency and cost. Using a reranker improves
precision but adds a processing step. Every quality improvement has a cost
trade-off, and evaluation should capture both sides of that trade-off explicitly.
Production Evaluation and Monitoring
Offline evaluation on a golden dataset gates deployment. But once the system
is live, evaluation continues in a different form.
User feedback signals provide the most direct quality measure. Explicit
signals include thumbs up and down buttons, star ratings, and correction
submissions. Implicit signals include click-through rates on cited sources,
follow-up question patterns (many follow-ups may indicate the first answer
was incomplete), and task completion rates. Both types feed back into the
evaluation dataset, with flagged examples expanding the golden set over time.
Continuous monitoring runs the LLM-as-judge metrics on a sample of
production traffic on a regular schedule. Track faithfulness, relevance, and
retrieval quality over time. Set alerts for metric degradation below defined
thresholds. Monitor for embedding distribution drift, which can indicate that
user queries are shifting away from what the knowledge base covers.
A/B testing compares new retrieval strategies, prompt templates, or
model versions against the current production system on live traffic. The key
is isolating the variable being tested while keeping everything else constant,
then measuring the triad metrics on both variants with enough queries to
reach statistical significance.
The recommended production loop is: offline evaluation with golden
datasets before deployment, shadow testing with automated scoring, A/B
testing on a traffic subset, full deployment with continuous monitoring, and
periodic human evaluation on sampled production queries to recalibrate the
automated judges.
The following illustration shows the full evaluation workflow from test
queries through metric computation to diagnostic output.
--- PAGE 278 ---
The evaluation workflow runs test queries through the RAG pipeline, scores the output on context
relevance, faithfulness, and answer relevance, and compiles the results into a diagnostic report that
pinpoints which components need attention.
Safety Evaluation
For production systems, evaluation must extend beyond quality to safety.
Three concerns deserve specific measurement.
Hallucination as a safety issue. In regulated domains like healthcare,
legal, or finance, a hallucinated claim is not just a quality problem but a
liability. The faithfulness metric described earlier serves double duty as a
safety metric when tracked against a strict threshold.
Information leakage. RAG systems can surface sensitive content from
the knowledge base that should not be exposed to certain users. Evaluation
should include test cases with queries designed to elicit private information,
measuring how often the system leaks content it should withhold.
Adversarial robustness. Prompt injection attacks attempt to override
the system’s instructions through crafted queries. Evaluation should include
adversarial test cases that attempt common injection patterns, measuring the
system’s refusal rate. An important finding from recent research: safety
--- PAGE 279 ---
guardrails that work well for standalone language models can behave
differently when the model is conditioned on retrieved context. The retrieved
documents can inadvertently prime the model to bypass safety training. This
means safety evaluation must test the full RAG pipeline, not just the
language model in isolation.
When to Use This
Best for:
Any RAG system moving toward production use
Teams iterating on retrieval strategies or prompt templates who need
objective comparison
High-stakes domains where hallucination rates must be tracked and kept
below a threshold
Continuous monitoring of deployed systems to catch quality degradation
over time
Overkill when:
You are prototyping a proof of concept where qualitative spot-checking is
sufficient
The knowledge base has fewer than fifty documents and you can
manually verify answers
The system is used internally by a small team with high tolerance for
occasional errors
Tradeoffs:
Factor Impact
Setup cost Medium: requires building an evaluation
dataset and scoring infrastructure
Per-evaluation cost Medium: LLM-as-judge calls add token costs
for each test case
Complexity Medium to High: multiple metrics to track,
evaluation dataset to maintain
Diagnostic value Very High: precisely identifies which pipeline
stage needs improvement
--- PAGE 280 ---
Compared to manual testing:
Manual testing tells you whether individual answers look right. Systematic
evaluation tells you whether the system is reliable across hundreds of
queries, which specific component is underperforming, and whether your
latest change actually improved things. Manual testing is a flashlight.
Evaluation is a floodlight.
Key Takeaways
RAG evaluation measures three core relationships: context relevance
(retriever quality), faithfulness (hallucination rate), and answer
relevance (query alignment)
Ranking metrics like MRR, MAP, and NDCG go beyond precision and
recall to measure whether relevant documents appear at the top of the
ranked list, not just somewhere in it
Faithfulness scoring decomposes the answer into individual claims and
verifies each one against the retrieved context; sampling-based
consistency checking offers a complementary hallucination signal
Answer completeness evaluation checks whether the response addresses
all facets of the query, not just whether what it says is correct
Robustness testing with controlled noise ratios reveals how gracefully
the system degrades when retrieval quality drops, with performance
often collapsing sharply beyond fifty percent noise
LLM-as-judge is the standard automated evaluation mechanism; chain-
of-thought prompting and single-dimension scoring improve reliability
Production evaluation extends beyond offline metrics to include user
feedback signals, continuous monitoring, A/B testing, and safety checks
for hallucination, information leakage, and adversarial robustness
Latency and cost are first-class evaluation dimensions alongside quality;
every accuracy improvement has a cost trade-off that evaluation should
capture explicitly
--- PAGE 281 ---
Appendix A: Choosing Your Techniques: A Decision
Guide
Every technique in this book solves a specific problem. This appendix
helps you find the one you need.
Prerequisites: Chapters 1-22 (or read this appendix first as a map, then dive
into individual chapters)
How to Use This Appendix
There are three ways to navigate this guide depending on what you need right
now.
By pipeline stage. If you know which part of your RAG system needs
improvement, jump to the pipeline overview. It groups all twenty-two
techniques by the stage they affect, from indexing through verification.
By symptom. If something is going wrong but you are not sure where,
start with the symptom map. It lists six common failure patterns and points
you to the techniques that address each one.
