By scenario. If you are building a new system and want a proven
combination of techniques, skip to the common stacks section. It gives ready-
made recipes for six application types.
The RAG Pipeline at a Glance
Every RAG system follows a pipeline, and every technique in this book
improves one or more stages of that pipeline. The following diagram maps all
twenty-two chapters to six pipeline stages, giving you a bird’s-eye view of
where each technique fits.
--- PAGE 282 ---
The six stages of a RAG pipeline shown as colored bands flowing left to right: Index, Query, Retrieve,
Refine, Verify, and System. Each band contains circles representing the techniques that operate at that
stage.
Indexing prepares your documents before any query arrives. Proposition
chunking (Chapter 4), contextual chunk headers (Chapter 7), the context
enrichment window (Chapter 8), semantic chunking (Chapter 9), document
augmentation (Chapter 11), hierarchical indices (Chapter 14), multi-modal
RAG (Chapter 16), and graph RAG (Chapter 21) all improve how content is
split, annotated, and stored. Better indexing pays dividends on every future
query.
Query transforms the user’s question before it reaches the retriever.
Query transformations (Chapter 5), HyDE (Chapter 6), and adaptive retrieval
(Chapter 18) rewrite, expand, or route queries so the retriever has a better
target to match against.
Retrieval controls how candidates are selected from the index. Fusion
retrieval (Chapter 12) combines keyword and semantic search. Dartboard
retrieval with maximal marginal relevance (Chapter 15) balances relevance
against diversity.
--- PAGE 283 ---
Post-retrieval refines the candidate set after retrieval. Contextual
compression (Chapter 10) strips irrelevant content from each chunk.
Reranking (Chapter 13) reorders the results using a more powerful scoring
model.
Verification checks the final output for correctness. Reliable RAG
(Chapter 3) validates answers against the context. Explainable retrieval
(Chapter 19) traces each claim to its source. Corrective RAG (Chapter 20)
detects and repairs low-confidence answers.
System provides operational infrastructure. Retrieval with feedback loops
(Chapter 17) learns from user signals over time. RAG evaluation (Chapter 22)
measures pipeline quality at every stage.
The foundation beneath all of these is simple RAG (Chapter 1) for
unstructured text and CSV RAG (Chapter 2) for structured data. Every other
technique extends or refines this base.
What Symptom Are You Seeing?
When a RAG system misbehaves, the failure usually falls into one of six
patterns. Each pattern points to a different set of root causes and a different
set of techniques. The following diagram shows these six symptom families
as a quick visual reference.
--- PAGE 284 ---
Six symptom families for diagnosing RAG failures: wrong documents, missing info, wrong ranking,
wrong output, content gaps, and system-level issues. Each family maps to a different cluster of
techniques.
“The system retrieves the wrong documents.” The retriever returns
content that looks superficially related but does not actually help answer the
question. This is a matching problem. Contextual chunk headers (Chapter 7)
help by making each chunk carry its parent document’s context. Hierarchical
indices (Chapter 14) narrow the search space before matching. Semantic
chunking (Chapter 9) ensures chunks capture complete ideas rather than
arbitrary fragments. Proposition chunking (Chapter 4) further distills content
into atomic, searchable facts. HyDE (Chapter 6) bridges the gap between
question phrasing and document phrasing. Document augmentation (Chapter
11) attaches synthetic questions to each chunk so queries match more
naturally. Fusion retrieval (Chapter 12) casts a wider net by combining two
search strategies.
“The system misses relevant information.” The answer is partially
correct but leaves out important details that are clearly in the knowledge
base. The context enrichment window (Chapter 8) retrieves surrounding text
alongside each matched chunk. Fusion retrieval (Chapter 12) catches
--- PAGE 285 ---
documents that one search method would miss. Dartboard retrieval (Chapter
15) diversifies results so the system does not return five near-identical
chunks while ignoring a different angle on the topic.
“The retrieved documents are good, but in the wrong order.” The
right information is retrieved but buried below less useful results. Reranking
(Chapter 13) applies a powerful cross-encoder to reorder the initial
candidates. Contextual compression (Chapter 10) removes noise from each
chunk so the language model sees only the parts that matter.
“The final answer is wrong or made up.” The system produces
plausible-sounding text that either contradicts the source material or includes
claims the sources never made. Reliable RAG (Chapter 3) adds a verification
layer that checks the answer against the context. Corrective RAG (Chapter
20) detects low-confidence answers and triggers retrieval retries or web
search fallbacks. Explainable retrieval (Chapter 19) makes hallucinations
easier to catch by requiring each claim to cite its source.
“The system cannot handle my content type.” Standard text-based
RAG works for articles and reports but falls short when the knowledge base
includes images, charts, tables, or graph-structured relationships. Multi-
modal RAG (Chapter 16) processes images and other non-text content. Graph
RAG (Chapter 21) captures relationships between entities that flat documents
lose. CSV RAG (Chapter 2) handles structured tabular data.
“The system works but I cannot measure or improve it.”
Everything seems fine manually but you have no systematic way to know if a
change helped or hurt. Adaptive retrieval (Chapter 18) routes queries
intelligently based on their complexity. Retrieval with feedback loops
(Chapter 17) incorporates user signals to improve results over time. RAG
evaluation (Chapter 22) provides the metrics and methodology to measure
every pipeline stage.
Progressive Enhancement: Build in Layers
You do not need all twenty-two techniques at once. Think of RAG
improvement as five layers, each building on the last. Start at the foundation
and add layers only when you hit a specific wall. The following illustration
shows how these layers stack from broad foundation to specialized
operational capabilities.
--- PAGE 286 ---
Five horizontal layers stacked from a wide foundation at the bottom to a narrow operations layer at the
top, showing how techniques build progressively from basic RAG through quick wins, query
intelligence, advanced capabilities, and operations.
Layer 1: Foundation. Start with simple RAG (Chapter 1). Get a basic
pipeline working end to end with standard fixed-size chunking, embedding,
and retrieval. Do not optimize prematurely. Many use cases work well enough
at this level.
Layer 2: Quick wins. When basic RAG falls short, the two highest-
leverage improvements are semantic chunking (Chapter 9) and reranking
(Chapter 13). Semantic chunking improves what goes into the index.
Reranking improves what comes out of retrieval. Together they address the
two most common failure modes with minimal complexity.
Layer 3: Query intelligence. If the retriever still misses relevant
content, the problem is often on the query side. Query transformations
(Chapter 5) expand or decompose ambiguous questions. HyDE (Chapter 6)
generates a hypothetical answer to bridge the vocabulary gap. Fusion retrieval
(Chapter 12) combines keyword and semantic search for broader coverage.
Layer 4: Specialized capabilities. Now address content-specific or
trust-critical requirements. Multi-modal RAG (Chapter 16) for images and
--- PAGE 287 ---
charts. Graph RAG (Chapter 21) for entity relationships. Reliable RAG
(Chapter 3) and corrective RAG (Chapter 20) for applications where factual
accuracy is non-negotiable.
Layer 5: Operational maturity. Finally, add measurement and
learning. RAG evaluation (Chapter 22) gives you the metrics to quantify
improvement. Retrieval with feedback loops (Chapter 17) closes the loop
between user behavior and system performance.
The most common mistake is jumping to Layer 4 before getting Layers
1-3 right. A sophisticated verification layer cannot compensate for a
retriever that never finds the right documents in the first place.
Common Technique Stacks
Real applications rarely use a single technique in isolation. The following
stacks are battle-tested combinations tailored to six common scenarios. The
diagram below shows four of these stacks as columns of building blocks.
--- PAGE 288 ---
Four application scenarios shown as vertical columns of stacked colored blocks. Each block contains a
chapter number, and the columns represent different recommended technique combinations for
enterprise, support, technical docs, and research use cases.
Enterprise knowledge base (internal wikis, policy documents, HR
manuals): Semantic chunking (Chapter 9) handles the varied document
structure. Contextual chunk headers (Chapter 7) preserve which policy or
department each chunk belongs to. Fusion retrieval (Chapter 12) covers both
exact terminology searches and conceptual queries. Reranking (Chapter 13)
promotes the most authoritative source. Reliable RAG (Chapter 3) prevents
the system from inventing policy that does not exist.
Customer support (FAQs, troubleshooting guides, product
documentation): Contextual chunk headers (Chapter 7) track which product
or version each answer applies to. Document augmentation (Chapter 11)
attaches common phrasings customers use so queries match more naturally.
Reranking (Chapter 13) surfaces the most directly actionable answer. Query
transformations (Chapter 5) handle the wide variety of ways customers
phrase the same question.
Technical documentation (API references, code guides, specifications):
Proposition chunking (Chapter 4) breaks dense specification paragraphs into
--- PAGE 289 ---
discrete, searchable facts. Fusion retrieval (Chapter 12) catches exact method
names via keyword search alongside conceptual queries. Dartboard retrieval
(Chapter 15) diversifies results when a function appears in multiple contexts.
Reranking (Chapter 13) ensures the most specific match ranks first.
Research and scientific literature (papers, patents, clinical studies):
Hierarchical indices (Chapter 14) handle collections spanning thousands of
documents by narrowing the search space first. Graph RAG (Chapter 21)
captures citation networks and entity relationships that flat search misses.
Query transformations (Chapter 5) decompose complex multi-part research
questions. Corrective RAG (Chapter 20) triggers verification for high-stakes
claims. Reranking (Chapter 13) handles the nuanced relevance judgments
scientific queries require.
Legal and compliance (contracts, regulations, case law): Contextual
chunk headers (Chapter 7) preserve which statute, clause, or case each chunk
comes from. Proposition chunking (Chapter 4) isolates individual legal
provisions for precise retrieval. Reranking (Chapter 13) resolves the subtle
relevance distinctions legal queries demand. Reliable RAG (Chapter 3)
prevents the generation of legal guidance not grounded in the source
material. Explainable retrieval (Chapter 19) provides the citation trail that
legal and compliance work requires.
Multi-modal and product catalogs (e-commerce, visual
documentation, design assets): Multi-modal RAG (Chapter 16) processes
product images alongside descriptions. Semantic chunking (Chapter 9)
groups related product attributes. Fusion retrieval (Chapter 12) handles
searches by both product name and visual description. Reranking (Chapter
13) ensures the closest match surfaces first.

Ch22 Evaluation System measurement Medium Low
--- PAGE 291 ---
The following flowchart provides one more way to navigate: answer a few
diagnostic questions and arrive at the techniques most likely to help. Start at
the top and follow the branches.
A decision tree that walks through three diagnostic questions: Is your data preparation adequate? Is
your search returning good results? Is the final output correct? Each No branch points to the pipeline
stage that needs attention.
Key Takeaways
Every technique targets a specific pipeline stage. Diagnosing which stage
is underperforming is more productive than adding techniques at
random.
Start with the foundation (Chapter 1) and add complexity in layers.
Semantic chunking and reranking are the two highest-leverage additions
for most systems.
--- PAGE 292 ---
Match symptoms to techniques: wrong documents suggest indexing
improvements, wrong answers suggest verification layers, and missing
information suggests query or retrieval changes.
Real systems combine multiple techniques. The common stacks in this
appendix provide tested starting points for six application types.
Measure before and after every change. RAG evaluation (Chapter 22) is
not an optional final step but the feedback loop that tells you whether
your improvements actually work.
The cost tables help you make informed tradeoffs. Indexing techniques
have high upfront cost but zero per-query overhead, while verification
techniques are cheap to set up but add latency to every request.
--- PAGE 293 ---
Appendix B: Glossary
A quick-reference guide to every key term in this book, with pointers to
the chapters where each concept is explained in full.
A/B Testing Comparing two versions of a RAG pipeline on live traffic to
measure which performs better, isolating one variable at a time while keeping
everything else constant. (Chapter 22)
Adaptive Retrieval A technique that classifies each incoming query by
type — factual, analytical, opinion, or contextual — and routes it to a
specialized retrieval strategy, like a hospital triage system matching patients
to the right specialist. (Chapter 18)
Answer Completeness A measure of whether a generated response
addresses all facets of a multi-part question, not just whether what it says is
correct. Often evaluated by decomposing the query into sub-questions and
checking coverage. (Chapter 22)
Answer Relevance Whether the generated response actually addresses
what the user asked, regardless of whether it is faithful to the retrieved
context. One of the three pillars of the evaluation triad. (Chapter 22)
Asymmetry Problem The structural mismatch between questions
(short, interrogative) and document chunks (long, declarative) that causes
their embeddings to land far apart in meaning space even when they are
about the same topic. (Chapter 6)
BERTScore A text comparison metric that uses contextual embeddings to
compute semantic similarity between generated and reference text, capturing
paraphrases and synonyms that word-overlap metrics miss. (Chapter 22)
Bi-Encoder An architecture that processes the query and each document
independently through separate encoding passes, producing two vectors
compared via cosine similarity. Fast because document vectors can be
precomputed, but unable to detect fine-grained interactions between query
and document. (Chapter 13)
